Such a mechanism fails to impose explicit constraints on intermediate feature representations, which can cause structural collapse of the feature manifold after pruning. To address this limitation, we propose EGRT (Efficient and Global Interaction-aware Retraining-Free Token Pruning for ViTs). EGRT consists of two cooperative stages. In the Fisher-Prior State Initialization (FPSI) stage, we construct a high-quality pruning prior from the second-order moment of gradients, substantially reducing the exploration uncertainty of RL in high-dimensional discrete spaces. In the Global Mask Optimization (GMO) stage, we introduce a Manifold-Preserving Reward that explicitly penalizes topological discrepancies of features before and after pruning, compelling the agent to automatically maintain feature consistency during search. Extensive experiments on ViT-L, DeiT-B, DeiT-S, and DeiT-T demonstrate that, without any post-hoc parameter tuning, EGRT achieves a better trade-off among accuracy, inference speed, and GFLOPs, showing strong competitiveness.

title: The DFKI-SLT System for ESDD 2026: BiCrossMamba-ST with Attentive SSL Fusion
authors: Yassine El Kheir, Arnab Das, Enes Erdem Erdogan, Feidi Kallel, Tim Polzehl, Sebastian Möller
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distinguishing authentic from deepfake environmental sounds requires capturing subtle spectro-temporal artifacts across multiple scales. This paper presents our solution for the Environmental Sound Deepfake Detection (ESDD) Challenge 2026. We propose a unified framework that synergizes representations from large-scale Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) models using an attentive layer fusion mechanism to bridge hierarchical features. These representations are processed by a BiCrossMamba-ST backend, a bidirectional selective state space model optimized for temporal consistency. Our ensemble approach demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, securing 2nd place in Track 1 (0.80% EER) and 1st place in Track 2 (0.25% EER).

title: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Low-Frequency Repetitive TMS Inhibits Motor Activation During Online BCI Control
authors: Akhil Surapaneni, Hussein Alawieh, Minsu Zhang, Michael Solomon, Satyam Kumar, Uneeb Sajjad, John S. Kuo, José del R. Millán
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Maladaptive plasticity is a counterproductive neural reorganization in response to injury that hinders functional recovery. This may be corrected by inducing further plasticity in nodes of the dysfunctional network. Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) can focally alter cortical excitability. However, its effect on functional activation patterns is understudied, especially when activation patterns, such as the maladaptive ones, are reinforced. As a model case in healthy subjects, we investigate the effect of low-frequency rTMS on individualized, multivariate patterns of motor cortex activation decoded and reinforced by a brain-computer interface (BCI) with feedback. Low-frequency rTMS, delivered in open-loop, shows a trend towards inhibiting motor activation. But its effect is abrogated with continuous motor imagery BCI feedback. We developed a novel closed-loop BCI-triggered rTMS system that delivers low-frequency stimulation when maladaptive patterns are decoded. We demonstrate that closed-loop stimulation can inhibit motor activation during continuous motor imagery BCI control. Our results support the use of rTMS, open and closed-loop, for correcting maladaptive plastic changes underlying central nervous system disorders.

title: Revisiting Backdoor Threat in Federated Instruction Tuning From a Signal Aggregation Perspective
authors: Haodong Zhao, Jinming Hu, Gongshen Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning security research has predominantly focused on backdoor threats from a minority of malicious clients that intentionally corrupt model updates. This paper challenges this paradigm by investigating a more pervasive and insidious threat: backdoor vulnerabilities from low-concentration poisoned data distributed across the datasets of benign clients. This scenario is increasingly common in federated instruction tuning for language models, which often rely on unverified third-party and crowd-sourced data. We analyze two forms of backdoor data through real cases: 1) natural trigger (inherent features as implicit triggers); 2) adversary-injected trigger. To analyze this threat, we model the backdoor implantation process from signal aggregation, proposing the Backdoor Signal-to-Noise Ratio to quantify the dynamics of the distributed backdoor signal. Extensive experiments reveal the severity of this threat: With just less than 10% of training data poisoned and distributed across clients, the attack success rate exceeds 85%, while the primary task performance remains largely intact. Critically, we demonstrate that state-of-the-art backdoor defenses, designed for attacks from malicious clients, are fundamentally ineffective against this threat. Our findings highlight an urgent need for new defense mechanisms tailored to the realities of modern, decentralized data ecosystems.

title: From Base to Novel: Semantic-Guided Visual Concept Transfer in Few-Shot Image Classification
authors: Wen Chen, Yihua Tan, Xuanyu Xiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Few-shot image classification is important in many real-world applications where data are limited and expensive to collect. Humans can rapidly learn new categories by reactivating stored concepts in specific cortical regions, enabling recognition from sparse cues. Drawing on this insight, we suggest that effective few-shot image classification benefits from the stable activation of visual concepts in the feature space, enabling semantic information to reliably guide concept reactivation and transfer during novel-class prototype construction. To this end, we propose a Semantic-Guided Concept Transfer Network (SGCTNet). Specifically, a Visual Concept Extractor maps images into a concept space where visual concepts are activated along specific dimensions. In addition, a Semantic-Guided Concept Transfer Module integrates limited data, category description, and the distribution of base prototypes to construct more representative prototypes for novel classes. Extensive experiments on two attribute-labeled benchmarks, CUB and SUN, demonstrate that SGCTNet consistently outperforms recent methods.

title: An Enhanced Memory Attention and Content-Guided Model for Mini Led Anomaly Detection
authors: Lei Zhou, Long Chen, You Zhou, Zongze Wu, Wei Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: During the manufacturing process, the Mini Light-Emitting Diode (Mini LED) develops various defects. As new defect types continually emerge with limited samples, conventional supervised deep learning-based methods demonstrate poor performance in this scenario. To address this challenge, we propose an unsupervised anomaly detection model, named MACG, that only requires normal samples for training. First, we introduce Memory Attention (MA), which employs learnable parametric memory templates for efficient normal pattern learning. Second, we design a Content-Guided Decoder (CGD) to filter out anomalous features. Finally, we propose a Multi-Branch Downsampler (MBD) that minimizes feature information loss during downsampling through a parallel branch architecture. Extensive experiments show that MACG achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches on both our custom Mini LED datasets and the public MVTec LOCO dataset.

title: Video Hashing via Transformer and KAN for Retrieval
authors: Likai Yang, Nianqiao Li, Yihua Chen, Zhenjun Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-Supervised Video Hashing (SSVH) is a promising approach for large-scale video retrieval. However, existing methods often neglect fine-grained spatiotemporal details and the local clustering structure of hash codes, limiting retrieval performance. To address this, we propose a novel SSVH method called VHTK based on Transformer and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) architectures. A key contribution is the Wave-Transformer module, which decomposes frame features via the wavelet transform, employs attention mechanisms to highlight critical spatiotemporal regions, and adopts KAN for flexible nonlinear modeling, thereby enhancing video representation. Additionally, a novel triplet loss function is designed to optimize hash code distribution, promoting compact and semantically consistent binary codes. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that VHTK outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in terms of mean Average Precision.

title: Build With Precision: Bottom-Up Inference of Linear Dags
authors: Hamed Ajorlou, Samuel Rey, Gonzalo Mateos, Geert Leus, Antonio G. Marques
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Learning the structure of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational data is a central problem in causal discovery, statistical signal processing, and machine learning. Under a linear Gaussian structural equation model (SEM) with equal noise variances, the problem is identifiable and we show that the ensemble precision matrix of the observations exhibits a distinctive structure that facilitates DAG recovery. Exploiting this property, we propose BUILD (Bottom-Up Inference of Linear DAGs), a deterministic stepwise algorithm that identifies leaf nodes and their parents, then prunes the leaves by removing incident edges to proceed to the next step, exactly reconstructing the DAG from the true precision matrix. In practice, precision matrices must be estimated from finite data, and ill-conditioning may lead to error accumulation across BUILD steps. As a mitigation strategy, we periodically re-estimate the precision matrix (with less variables as leaves are pruned), trading off runtime for enhanced robustness. Reproducible results on challenging synthetic benchmarks demonstrate that BUILD compares favorably to state-of-the-art DAG learning algorithms, while offering an explicit handle on complexity.

title: Active Inference Framework for Closed-Loop Sensing, Communication, and Control in UAV Systems
authors: Guangjin Pan, Liping Bai, Zhuojun Tian, Hui Chen, Mehdi Bennis, Henk Wymeersch
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication is a core technology for 6G, and its application to closed-loop sensing, communication, and control (SCC) enables various services. Existing SCC solutions often treat sensing and control separately, leading to suboptimal performance and resource usage. In this work, we introduce the active inference framework into SCC-enabled uncrewed aerial vehicle systems for joint state estimation, control, and sensing resource allocation. By formulating a unified generative model, the problem reduces to minimizing variational free energy for inference and expected free energy for action planning. Simulation results show that both control cost and sensing cost are reduced relative to baselines.

title: Learning Dual Mixture-of-Experts Models for Unified Image Deraining
authors: Tianyi Yang, Mengke Zhang, Tianshuo Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite the remarkable progress achieved in image deraining, most existing approaches remain constrained to handling a single type of rain degradation or a specific rain pattern. In practice, however, rain manifests in diverse forms, which severely limits the applicability of current methods under real-world conditions. To overcome this limitation, we first construct a high-quality benchmark named Hybrid-Rain10K, which integrates three representative rain degradation patterns, including rain streaks, raindrops and rain accumulation. Building upon this benchmark, we propose a dual mixture-of-experts (MoE) model for unified image deraining, which adaptively handles various rain degradations and restores clean images. Where the encoder employs a Soft-MoE to assign collaborative weights across all experts, ensuring comprehensive degradation modeling, while the decoder adopts a Hard-MoE with top-k routing guided by high-frequency priors to enhance reconstruction quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our dataset and model.

title: BHSFlow: Low-Latency Flow Estimation with Block-Wise Huber Loss and Simplified Structure
authors: Tianyi Sun, Yanze Wang, Runkai Chen, Yuedi Chen, Jun Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Optical flow estimation provides motion information between two frames, acting as a key component in many visual tasks. Current methods either come with large computational complexity or suffer a severe accuracy drop to achieve real-time performance, especially when dealing with high-resolution frames. To address this trade-off, this paper proposes a low-latency recurrent pyramid network structure and a novel block-wise Huber loss applicable to all pyramid optical flow networks. By characterizing latency and FLOPs of various convolutional kernels, a fast yet effective pyramid structure is designed accordingly. Standard Huber loss mitigates the impact of small errors but risks allowing large errors to disproportionately influence the loss within a block in certain scenarios. Instead, our block-wise Huber loss first balances opposing errors within each block and selectively applies Huber loss only to the residual errors, ensuring stable optimization. Experiments show that our method achieves the best result among existing lightweight methods and demonstrates strong adaptability to frames in high resolution. Our code is available in https://github.com/cppppp/BHSFlow.

title: Enhancing Dialogue-Related Speech Tasks with Generated Spoken Dialogues
authors: Haitian Lu, Zhihao Bai, Yukun Liu, Xuyang Wang, Gaofeng Cheng, Yonghong Yan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), speech language models (SLMs) have enabled more natural spoken dialogue generation. However, to make generated dialogues useful for downstream tasks, key challenges remain in ensuring consistency between text and speech as well as providing accurate utterance timestamps. Building upon the SLIDE framework, this work introduces a method for generating dual-channel spoken dialogues with aligned transcriptions and utterance timestamps, and systematically investigates their effectiveness as augmentation data. In particular, we explore how such generated dialogues can be leveraged for downstream training in a task-specific manner—either alone or in combination with real data—across automatic speech recognition, end-to-end neural diarization, voice activity detection, and overlap speech detection. Extensive experiments on the Fisher and CALLHOME corpora demonstrate that our approach yields substantial performance gains across tasks, highlighting its effectiveness and practical value for speech processing applications.

title: SIREN: Spatially-Informed Reconstruction of Binaural Audio with Vision
authors: Mingyeong Song, Seoyeon Ko, Junhyug Noh
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Binaural audio delivers spatial cues essential for immersion, yet most consumer videos are monaural due to capture constraints. We introduce SIREN, a visually guided mono→binaural framework that explicitly predicts left and right channels. A ViT-based encoder learns dual-head self-attention to produce a shared scene map and end-to-end L/R attention – replacing hand-crafted masks. A soft, annealed spatial prior gently biases early L/R grounding, and a two-stage, confidence-weighted waveform-domain fusion (guided by mono reconstruction and interaural phase consistency) suppresses crosstalk when aggregating multi-crop and overlapping windows. Evaluated on FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo, SIREN yields consistent gains on time–frequency and phase-sensitive metrics with competitive SNR. The design is modular and generic, requires no task-specific annotations, and integrates with standard audio-visual pipelines.

title: MoRE: Multimodal Relationship Enhancement for Unbiased Scene Graph Generation
authors: Ruiqi Hu, Lida Huang, Bin Jiang, Chao Yang, Jianbo Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) extracts relational triplets 〈subject, predicate, object〉 from images, yet current benchmarks suffer from long-tailed distributions and semantic ambiguity, causing biased predictions toward generic relations (e.g., "on") and neglecting fine-grained ones (e.g., "parked on"). Existing balancing strategies reallocate predicates but largely ignore contextual semantics and underexploit missing relations. We propose Multimodal Relationship Enhancement (MoRE), a framework that improves relation prediction by addressing contextual inconsistency and annotation sparsity. MoRE introduces a Context-relation Bank to dynamically retrieve candidate predicates, and employs Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with context-driven prompts for semantically consistent relation selection. Experiments on VG and GQA demonstrate that MoRE achieves state-of-the-art performance on unbiased metrics, enhancing predicate diversity and prediction balance for SGG.

title: AIMRec: Aligning both Individuals and Modalities for Multimodal Recommendation
authors: Yihan Zhang, Ercan E. Kuruoglu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal recommendation requires learning expressive representations of users and items by integrating multimodal features such as text and images. While prior studies have extensively explored graph convolution-based frameworks and modality fusion strategies, a critical issue that remains underexplored is the alignment of representations across embedding spaces. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective model, AIMRec, which performs graph convolution separately on ID and feature embeddings before combining them into unified user and item representations. To enhance consistency between different representations, we introduce two auxiliary alignment losses: an ID–Feature Alignment loss that enforces coherence between ID and feature embeddings of individuals, and a Cross-Modal Alignment loss that aligns item representations across modalities from both local and global perspectives. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of AIMRec over baselines. Further ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each proposed component.

title: Syllable-Level Acoustic Modeling with a Stage-Aware Transformer for ALS Dysarthria Severity Estimation - ICASSP-2026 Sand Challenge
authors: Yuho Tamura, Mondher Bouazizi, Tomoaki Ohtsuki
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a syllable-level Transformer-based model for estimating dysarthria severity in patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) as part of the ICASSP-2026 SAND Challenge [1]. Our approach segments speech recordings into syllable-level acoustic units using Sylber and extracts eGeMAPS Low-Level Descriptors (LLDs). We then compute feature-level mutual information scores to rank and select the most discriminative features. After integrating features extracted from three distinct syllable recordings (/pa/, /ka/, and /ta/), we employ a stage-aware Transformer framework with separate models trained for each ALSFRS-R starting severity. Experimental results demonstrate that this syllable-level, stage-wise modeling improves the prediction of future ALS dysarthria severity.

title: Frontend Token Enhancement for Token-Based Speech Recognition
authors: Takanori Ashihara, Shota Horiguchi, Kohei Matsuura, Tsubasa Ochiai, Marc Delcroix
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Discretized representations of speech signals are efficient alternatives to continuous features for various speech applications, including automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech language models. However, these representations, such as semantic or phonetic tokens derived from clustering outputs of self-supervised learning (SSL) speech models, are susceptible to environmental noise, which can degrade backend task performance. In this work, we introduce a frontend system that estimates clean speech tokens from noisy speech and evaluate it on an ASR backend using semantic tokens. We consider four types of enhancement models based on their input/output domains: wave-to-wave, token-to-token, continuous SSL features-to-token, and wave-to-token. These models are trained independently of ASR backends. Experiments on the CHiME-4 dataset demonstrate that wave-to-token enhancement achieves the best performance among the frontends. Moreover, it mostly outperforms the ASR system based on continuous SSL features.

title: LLM4RIM: Leveraging Large Language Model for Radar-Based in-Vehicle Monitoring
authors: Chong Rao, Jiahang Guo, Xikang Jiang, Lei Li, Lin Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing need for non-invasive in-vehicle monitoring in the intelligent cabin underscores the potential of radar sensing. However, existing radar-based solutions often rely on task-specific approaches, lacking a unified framework across multiple downstream tasks. In this work, LLM4RIM, a large language model (LLM) multi-task fine-tuning framework is proposed for radar-based in-vehicle monitoring (RIM) tasks, including occupancy detection, drowsiness detection, gesture recognition, and vital sign monitoring. By utilizing the Mixture of Experts with Low-Rank Adaptation (MoE-LoRA) approach for fine-tuning, the framework leverages the cross-modal learning capabilities of LLMs to enable efficient multitask learning. Experiments conducted on four task-specific datasets demonstrate an average accuracy of 93.20%, outperforming the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by 13.48%. Our code is released at https://github.com/bupt-uwb/LLM4RIM.

title: Diversity is All You Need: Self-Supervised Hypergraph Learning for Mitigating Popularity Bias in Conversational Recommender System
authors: Yongsen Zheng, Ruilin Xu, Ye Ma, Guohua Wang, Liang Lin, Kwok-Yan Lam
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Popularity bias is the tendency of recommender systems (RSs) to favor popular items over personalized or niche options, reinforcing the popularity of well-liked items. Most existing methods aim to alleviate popularity bias in offline static environments, but this bias tends to intensify over time in online recommendations. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Self-Supervised Hypergraph Learning for Mitigating Popularity Bias in Conversational Recommender System (SHLCRS), which aims to reduce popularity bias in the dynamic user-system feedback loop by capturing diverse user preferences through hypergraphs. SHLCRS establishes a self-supervised learning paradigm to create paired hypergraphs and line graphs for exploring varied preferences. It then employs a Multi-Hypergraph-Oriented CRS to leverage these preferences for accurate item predictions and effective conversational responses. Extensive experiments on two public CRS benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SHLCRS in mitigating popularity bias.

title: A Benchmark Dataset and Baseline Framework for Action Recognition in Power Construction Safety
authors: Shuo Zhang, Xiaoqing Zheng, Weibin Zhang, Yaguang Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Unsafe behaviors of workers are a major cause of accidents in power engineering, while traditional manual inspections struggle to monitor complex, dynamic construction sites. Although video understanding has been widely applied to action recognition, power construction faces challenges such as large target scale variations, complex occlusions, and imbalanced class distributions. To address this, we introduce PCVA, a benchmark dataset for action recognition in power construction safety, and propose MSHCNet, a multi-scale spatio-temporal hierarchical collaborative network with an Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Hierarchical Module (ASHM) as a strong baseline. MSHCNet captures temporal dynamics and adaptively fuses multi-scale features for hierarchical action modeling. Experiments on PCVA show MSHCNet achieves 38.16 mAP, outperforming the previous best method by 7.54%, effectively supporting safety monitoring and accident prevention. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/ys-sy/MSHCNet.

title: Radiance Field Rendering with Adaptive Compact Kernel for Novel View Synthesis
authors: Binghao Wu, Haiyang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful explicit representation for novel view synthesis. However, its fixed Gaussian kernels introduce two key limitations: over-smoothed reconstructions due to limited adaptability, and truncation artifacts stemming from the kernel’s long-tailed and unbounded nature. To address these challenges, we present the Adaptive Compact Kernel (ACK), a shape-adaptive and boundary-aware representation that dynamically adjusts its spatial profile to capture both low-frequency structures and fine-grained details, while inherently eliminating truncation artifacts. In addition, we introduce a parameter compression scheme that reorganizes and quantizes primitives into compact PNG-based representations, achieving up to 82% storage reduction and 13% improvement in loading efficiency, with negligible degradation in rendering quality. Extensive experiments on four widely used benchmark datasets, including Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, Deep Blending, and NeRF Synthetic, demonstrate that our method achieves superior efficiency and fidelity over prior state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Research on Collaborative Interference Resource Allocation Based on Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Pengjiao Li, Haoran Gong, Yinshuai Meng, Yumeng Liang, Keding Yan
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: In the process of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) formation penetration, when multiple interference aircraft jointly suppress multiple radars, there are problems such as limited resources, high decision-making dimensions, and low optimization efficiency. This paper constructs a collaborative interference resource allocation model with the goal of maximizing interference effectiveness, comprehensively considering the power and time coordination mechanism as well as the constraints of interference targets and styles. On this basis, an improved genetic algorithm is proposed. By introducing sorting selection, small habitat elimination, and elite retention strategies, the algorithm's global search ability is enhanced and the convergence speed is accelerated. The results show that the proposed algorithm can converge to a better solution within approximately 60% of the iterations, significantly reducing the probability of enemy radar detection, increasing the positioning and tracking errors by 2 to 3 times, effectively compressing the radar exposure area, and providing a relatively safe flight corridor for UAV penetration.

title: BDRNet:Bidirectional Decomposed and Recalibrated Lightweight Network for Human Pose Estimation
authors: Jing Luo, Yanxia Wang, Xuping Liang, Wenwen Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Lightweight human pose estimation models often struggle to balance accuracy and efficiency in complex scenarios. Their limited receptive fields and feature representation capabilities lead to significant performance degradation under occlusions, crowded environments, etc. Existing methods typically address this issue by introducing multi-scale modules or global attention mechanisms, which often incur substantial computational costs or fail to effectively model the balance between long-range dependencies and local details. To alleviate this problem, this study proposes a lightweight cross-scale attention fusion network named BDRNet. The network innovatively integrates two key modules: the Bidirectional Decomposed Spatial Attention (BDSA) and the Multi-Scale Recalibrated Attention (MSRA). BDSA captures directional local details with minimal computational cost, while MSRA further efficiently models global contextual relationships and integrates local details through the Recalibrated Hybrid Attention (RHA) and Pointwise Shuffle Block (PWSB). Experimental results on the MSCOCO and MPII datasets demonstrate that BDRNet achieves competitive overall performance, excels in complex scenarios, approaches the performance of large-scale network models on average precision (AP), and outperforms most other lightweight models, striking a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency.

title: Unsupervised Landslide Detection from InSAR-Derived Time Series Using Change Point Analysis
authors: Ioana-Cristina Igret, Gabriel Iuhasz, Delia Teleagă, Valentin Poncos
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: Landslides pose dangerous risks to human safety and to infrastructure, especially in densely populated or geologically unpredictable regions. Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) technology, along with time series analysis has significantly improved the ability to monitor ground deformations associated with such hazards. This study explores the detection of change points in InSAR-derived ground motion time series from the Praid region in Romania, based on Sentinel-1 data collected over a period of five years. The methodology starts with clustering Persistent Scatterers (PS) with similar deformation patterns using the HDBSCAN algorithm, followed by the application of several detrending methods to enhance change point detection accuracy. Four algorithms, Pruned Exact Linear Time (PELT), Binary Segmentation (BS), Window Sliding and Bottom-Up Segmentation, are implemented to identify abrupt fluctuations in ground motion. The absence of ground truth data motivated the selection of a consensus-based on majority voting as evaluation system. More specifically, the validation of a detected change point was correlated with the number of algorithms that identified it independently, with a minimum of two algorithms out of the four implemented. The findings offer insights into deformation activity and highlight the value of change point analysis in landslide detection. Future work will include a more comprehensive analysis of data particularities, the integration of contextual environmental factors and finally, the application of advanced time series techniques, to further enhance detection accuracy and improve early warning and risk management. Our code is available at https://github.com/ioanaigret/unsupervised-change-point-analysis-based-on-InSAR-derived-time-series.git

title: Gen3d:Generating Domain-Free 3D Scenes From a Single Image
authors: Yuxin Zhang, Ziyu Lu, Hongbo Duan, Keyu Fan, Pengting Luo, Peiyu Zhuang, Mengyu Yang, Houde Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite recent advancements in neural 3D reconstruction, the dependence on dense multi-view captures restricts their broader applicability.Additionally, 3D scene generation is vital for advancing embodied AI and world models, which depend on diverse, high-quality scenes for learning and evaluation.In this work, we propose Gen3d, a novel method for generation of high-quality, wide-scope, and generic 3D scenes from a single image. After the initial point cloud is created by lifting the RGBD image, Gen3d maintains and expands its world model.The 3D scene is finalized through optimizing a Gaussian splatting representation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets demonstrate the strong generalization capability and superior performance of our method in generating a world model and Synthesizing high-fidelity and consistent novel views.

title: Enhancing Audio Question-Answering Performance Through Log-Likelihood Guided Reward Functions
authors: Sam Blouir, Ganesh Ramachandra Kini, Qingming Tang, Raphael Petegrosso, Chieh-Chi Kao, Ankur Gandhe, Chao Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has shown promise in training Large Language Models (LLMs) for text-to-text and multimodal tasks. Current RLVR approaches for audio question-answering often use format adherence and binary correctness signals. We propose a new reward framework for extracting learning signals from multiple-choice questions (MCQs). Our approach uses probability-ratio rewards to increase the likelihood difference between correct and incorrect answer choices, expanding beyond accuracy metrics. We conducted experiments on a standard audio-reasoning benchmark, where our probability-ratio reward framework performed better than the base model and existing RLVR methods. The results suggest that likelihood-based rewards can improve audio reasoning capabilities, offering an enhanced approach for multiple-choice audio question-answering systems. Specifically, we test our methods on MMAU [10], a recent complex audio QA benchmark that requires reasoning capability towards achieving human-level performance.

title: TASU: Text-only Alignment for Speech Understanding
authors: Jing Peng, Yi Yang, Xu Li, Yu Xi, Quanwei Tang, Yangui Fang, Junjie Li, Kai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in Speech Large Language Models (Speech LLMs) have paved the way for unified architectures across diverse speech understanding tasks. However, prevailing alignment paradigms rely heavily on large-scale audio-text paired data and computationally intensive training, yet often exhibit limited generalization to unseen domains or tasks. To address these limitations, we propose TASU (Text-only Alignment for Speech Understanding), a novel alignment paradigm that can leverage only unpaired text data to guide cross-modal alignment. Experiments show that TASU achieves competitive zero-shot speech recognition. Leveraging this property, it can further function as a pre-training stage in curriculum learning, enhancing domain generalization in speech recognition. Ultimately, TASU can extend its zero-shot generalization to a wide range of speech understanding tasks and notably outperforms prominent Speech LLMs including GLM-4-Voice and Step-Audio on the MMSU benchmark, establishing TASU as an efficient and scalable alignment paradigm for Speech LLMs.

title: TRACE: Tracking and Addressing Cross-Domain Conflict for Enhanced Semantic Segmentation
authors: Minhee Cho, Sumin Park, Dongbo Min
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-training has emerged as a dominant approach for cross-domain semantic segmentation, leveraging pseudo-labels on unlabeled target data to enhance model generalization. While recent methods attempt to utilize pseudo-labels based on prediction reliability, they predominantly rely on high-confidence regions and incorporate low-confidence areas in limited ways. This leads to sparse or imbalanced supervision, hindering the model’s ability to fully capture semantic context across the target domain. To address this, we propose TRACE, a framework for TRacking and Addressing Cross-domain conflict for Enhanced segmentation. TRACE explicitly monitors prediction consistency throughout training to distinguish between Domain-Aligned Regions (DAR) with high confidence and Potentially Conflicting Regions (PCR) with low confidence. The key idea is to leverage DAR as a stable supervisory signal to guide targeted learning on PCR via categorical learning and adaptive masking, enabling the model to better learn from uncertain regions without compromising robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TRACE, achieving new state-of-the-art results with 76.6% mIoU on GTA→Cityscapes, 68.5% mIoU on SYNTHIA→Cityscapes, 61.3% mIoU on Cityscapes→DarkZurich.

title: Few-Shot Bearing Fault Diagnosis Using Multi-Scale Feature Extraction and Attention-Based Prototype Matching
authors: Dingli Zhang, Wei He, Rong Cao, Tiantian Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Modern industrial systems rely on motors, where rolling bearings are critical yet prone to faults under harsh conditions. Accurate fault diagnosis with limited data remains a major challenge. This paper proposes an end-to-end few-shot fault diagnosis model with three key innovations. First, the Multi-scale Large-Kernel Attention Feature Extraction (MLKAFE) module captures both local and global patterns, improving fault feature representation. Second, the Spatial and Channel Attention Aggregation (SCAA) module refines multi-scale features by highlighting informative spatial and channel-wise cues. Finally, the Prototype Matching Module with Attention (PMMA) employs self- attention and cross-attention for robust similarity measurement, addressing the shortcomings of conventional Euclidean and cosine metrics. Experiments on the CWRU and PU datasets show that our model achieves higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods, especially under extremely limited data. These results demonstrate strong generalization and diagnostic performance, making the model well suited for industrial applications with constrained data availability.

title: PictOBI-20k: Unveiling Large Multimodal Models in Visual Decipherment for Pictographic Oracle Bone Characters
authors: Zijian Chen, Wenjie Hua, Jinhao Li, Lirong Deng, Fan Du, Tingzhu Chen, Guangtao Zhai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deciphering oracle bone characters (OBCs), the oldest attested form of written Chinese, has remained the ultimate, unwavering goal of scholars, offering an irreplaceable key to understanding humanity’s early modes of production. Current decipherment methodologies of OBC are primarily constrained by the sporadic nature of archaeological excavations and the limited corpus of inscriptions. With the powerful visual perception capability of large multimodal models (LMMs), the potential of using LMMs for visually deciphering OBCs has increased. In this paper, we introduce PictOBI-20k, a dataset designed to evaluate LMMs on the visual decipherment tasks of pictographic OBCs. It includes 20k meticulously collected OBC and real object images, forming over 15k multi-choice questions. We also conduct subjective annotations to investigate the consistency of the reference point between humans and LMMs in visual reasoning. Experiments indicate that general LMMs possess preliminary visual decipherment skills, and LMMs are not effectively using visual information, while most of the time, they are limited by language priors. We hope that our dataset can facilitate the evaluation and optimization of visual attention in future OBCoriented LMMs. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/OBI-Future/PictOBI-20k.

title: TAG: Structured Temporal Audio Generation via LLM-Guided Manual Scription and Control
authors: Hanwen Zhang, Jinshen Zhang, Cong Zhang, Shuhui Wang, Wei Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Although text-to-audio generation has made significant progress in recent years, generating customized especially temporally structured audio from complex prompts remains challenging. Current approaches construct structured information in an independent manner, ignoring model-specific capabilities and lacking flexibility for adaptation or extension. For structured temporal generation, existing methods either require high computational overhead or demonstrate limited adaptability like refining. In this paper, we propose a two-stage framework: first utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) to provide structured audio information while considering available capabilities of our generation model, then employing a training-free controllable generation framework with an attention control mechanism. We achieve precise temporal control via crossattention map manipulation, which can be easily integrated into various attention-based diffusion models and attention refinement applications. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods in temporal precision while preserving generation quality.

title: Leveraging Point Transformer for 3D Human Mesh Reconstruction with Incomplete Point Cloud
authors: Jingyi Wu, Han Yu, Juncen Guo, Jingyu Zhang, Lang Qian, Xiaoguang Zhu, Liang Song
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reconstructing 3D human models from point cloud is a fundamental problem in computer vision, but it remains challenging in anomalous data conditions. Existing methods often produce distorted or incomplete models because they lack the ability to robustly infer the geometry of hidden body parts. To address this, we propose a novel network with two branches that leverages pre-trained foundation models based on point transformer for collaborative full-body reconstruction, accurately recovering geometry even from anomalous inputs. The dense geometry branch, built upon a foundation model, leverages the global pose from visible parts to predict a complete, dense compactness vector field. Simultaneously, the sparse semantic branch robustly regresses a set of sparse anatomical keypoints, providing a strong structural prior for the global pose, making it robust against local anomaly. Finally, a parameterized body model is fitted by jointly optimizing its alignment with the completed dense surface and the stabilized sparse keypoints. Comparison with current leading methods highlights the superiority of our model. Especially in challenging scenarios, our solution can successfully reconstruct the human body with high precision.

title: Fabrication of Artificial Intelligence enabled Systematic Waste Segregation Model using Logical Sensors with Smart Controller
authors: Haritha V, C Nithiya, Chandrakala R, C Sadhana, P Vijayaraghavan, S Yuvaraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Automation and effective waste separation are important to a sustainable environmental management particularly in urban and industrial aspects. The current paper is a description of the design and implementation of an Artificial Intelligence (AI) based systematic waste segregation model, which is a combination of logical sensors and a smart Raspberry Pi controller. The system utilizes a blend of moisture sensors, metal detectors, IR sensors, gas sensors and weight sensors to categorize the waste into five different types namely, dry, wet, plastic, metal and hazardous. The intelligent classification process is made possible by a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on sensor data and images that are collected in the real world, and the actuation mechanisms are controlled by servo motors that are provided by the use of GPIOs. Experimental outcomes indicate that the classification can be made with a result of 95.2 % when dealing with wet waste, 94.2 % when dealing with dry waste, 95.3 % when dealing with metal waste, 92.7% when dealing with plastic waste, and 91.7 % when dealing with hazardous waste and the overall classification accuracy of the system has been achieved to be 93.82. The system saves more than 70 % of processing time, which is in comparison with manual sorting and makes the categorization of images more consistent. This model includes an OLED based real-time display and bin-level monitoring, as well. The proposed smart solution is a scalable, inexpensive, and real-time waste management structure that can be used in smart cities and industrial campuses. The extensions in the future can be IoT-enabled cloud connectivity and federated AI learning to track and report waste in a decentralized way.

title: Wavelet-Aware Anomaly Detection in Multi-Channel User Logs via Deviation Modulation and Resolution-Adaptive Attention
authors: Kaichuan Kong, Dongjie Liu, Xiaobo Jin, Shijie Xu, Guanggang Geng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Insider threat detection is a key challenge in enterprise security, relying on user activity logs that capture rich and complex behavioral patterns. These logs are often multi-channel, non-stationary, and anomalies are rare, making anomaly detection challenging. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework that integrates wavelet-aware modulation, multi-resolution wavelet decomposition, and resolution-adaptive attention for robust anomaly detection. Our approach first applies a deviation-aware modulation scheme to suppress routine behaviors while amplifying anomalous deviations. Next, discrete wavelet transform (DWT) decomposes the log signals into multi-resolution representations, capturing both long-term trends and short-term anomalies. Finally, a learnable attention mechanism dynamically reweights the most discriminative frequency bands for detection. On the CERT r4.2 benchmark, our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines in precision, recall, and F1 score across various time granularities and scenarios.

title: V2A-DPO: Omni-Preference Optimization for Video-To-Audio Generation
authors: Nolan Chan, Timmy Gang, Yongqian Wang, Yuzhe Liang, Dingdong Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper introduces V2A-DPO, a novel Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) framework tailored for flow-based video-to-audio generation (V2A) models, incorporating key adaptations to effectively align generated audio with human preferences. Our approach incorporates three core innovations: (1) AudioScore—a comprehensive human preference-aligned scoring system for assessing semantic consistency, temporal alignment, and perceptual quality of synthesized audio; (2) an automated AudioScore-driven pipeline for generating large-scale preference pair data for DPO optimization; (3) a curriculum learning-empowered DPO optimization strategy specifically tailored for flow-based generative models. Experiments on benchmark VGGSound dataset demonstrate that human-preference aligned Frieren and MMAudio using V2A-DPO outperform their counterparts optimized using Denoising Diffusion Policy Optimization (DDPO) as well as pre-trained baselines. Furthermore, our DPO-optimized MMAudio achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple metrics, surpassing published V2A models. Our demos and code are available at https://nolanchan23.github.io/V2A-DPO/.

title: Adaptive Task-Incremental Learning For Underwater Acoustic Recognition Based on Mixture-of-Experts Adapter
authors: Yang Zhang, Changjian Wang, Weiguo Chen, Yuan Yuan, Yingzhi Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Underwater acoustic target recognition employs acoustic signals to identify and classify marine objects. In practical applications, the frequent emergence of new tasks makes incremental learning a crucial research direction. Current mainstream methods largely depend on parameter isolation to alleviate catastrophic forgetting. However, these approaches require explicit task identification to activate corresponding sub-networks and often overlook potential correlations among different tasks. To tackle these limitations, we propose an adaptive task-incremental learning framework based on a Mixture-of-Experts Adapter that incorporates autonomous task perception. Our method combines a pre-trained acoustic model with Mixture-of-Experts structure to facilitate cross-task knowledge sharing through task-specific routing. Additionally, we designed a replay-aware task identification module where a lightweight network generates task-aware embeddings and leverages the replay data distribution for discrimination, thereby enabling automatic and adaptive task recognition. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance in terms of average forgetting rate compared to existing methods, while reducing parameter training overhead by more than 90% compared to full-parameter fine-tuning.

title: STD-Gaussians: Spatio-Temporal Decoupled Gaussian Splatting for Single-View Dynamic Scene Reconstruction
authors: Xian Wu, Ying Ye, Jintian Li, Junfeng Yao, Jinwen Li, Weixing Xie
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the prevalence of short-form videos on social platforms, accurately reconstructing dynamic scenes from a single-view input has become a critical and challenging task. Although 3D Gaussian Splatting–based methods have advanced reconstruction quality and efficiency, their reliance on multi-view information limits single-view applicability. To address these challenges, we propose a Spatio-Temporal Decoupled Gaussian Splatting framework for single-view dynamic scene reconstruction. Our method generates complete and stable initial point clouds using confidence-weighted depth priors. The spatio-temporal decoupled Gaussian deformation model differentiates the encoding of static and dynamic Gaussians, improving reconstruction quality while maintaining rendering efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce a strategy that enhances high-frequency details and large-motion regions through spatial weighting based on high-frequency subbands and motion-guided temporal supervision. Experimental results show that our method significantly improves rendering quality, accelerates rendering speed, and enables dynamic occluder removal.

title: Performance-Guided Reinforced Active Learning for Object Detection
authors: Zhixuan Liang, Xingyu Zeng, Rui Zhao, Ping Luo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Active learning (AL) strategies aim to train high-performance models with minimal labeling efforts, only selecting the most informative instances for annotation. Current approaches to evaluating data informativeness predominantly focus on the data’s distribution or intrinsic information content and do not directly correlate with downstream task performance, such as mean average precision (mAP) in object detection. Thus, we propose Performance-guided (i.e.mAP-guided) Reinforced Active Learning for Object Detection (MGRAL), a novel approach that leverages the concept of expected model output changes as informativeness. To address the combinatorial explosion challenge of batch sample selection and the non-differentiable correlation between model performance and selected batches, MGRAL skillfully employs a reinforcement learning-based sampling agent that optimizes selection using policy gradient with mAP improvement as reward. Moreover, to reduce the computational overhead of mAP estimation with unlabeled samples, MGRAL utilizes an unsupervised way with fast look-up tables, ensuring feasible deployment. We evaluate MGRAL’s active learning performance on detection tasks over PASCAL VOC and COCO benchmarks. Our approach demonstrates the highest AL curve with convincing visualizations, establishing a new paradigm in reinforcement learning-driven active object detection.

title: FocusFuzz: Towards Efficient and Directed Fuzzing for RTL Processor Designs
authors: He Wang, Ruomin Fang, Jingqian Zhang, Xu Chen, Qizhen Xu, Ruiping Yin, Liwei Chen, Gang Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As processor architectures evolve with the integration of new functional units, verifying the entire hardware design after each modification becomes increasingly inefficient. Although a significant portion of the modules remains unmodified between iterations, traditional verification methodologies still require re-testing of the entire process, failing to leverage this inherent modularity in hardware development.To address this inefficiency, we propose FocusFuzz, a novel module-directed hardware fuzzing framework tailored for processor verification. FocusFuzz integrates two key components: a Static-Dynamic Module Distance Profiler(SDMD) that identifies modules functionally close to the target, and a Module Feature-Guided Mutation Engine (MFGM) enhanced by a lightweight LSTM model to adapt mutation strategies to module-specific functional characteristics. We evaluated FocusFuzz on open-source RISC-V cores, where at the module level it improves time-to-target- coverage (TTTC) by up to 89× and achieves an average 6.94× speedup over DifuzzRTL, the famous currently available fuzzer.

title: APPLE: Attention-Promoted Prototype Learning for Federated Cross-Modal Hashing
authors: Xing Tian, Yifan Zeng, Zihao Zhan, Yuanyuan Li, Wing W. Y. Ng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep cross-modal hashing has been widely adopted for large-scale data retrieval due to its efficiency and compact storage. In practice, data are scattered in data silos, privacy constraints, and data transfer overhead often render centralized training infeasible. Consequently, recent studies turn to federated learning for deep cross-modal hashing. However, most methods underutilize global knowledge to guide the local training, leading to degraded retrieval performance on non-IID data. Therefore, an Attention-Promoted Prototype LEarning method (APPLE) is proposed for federated cross-modal hashing retrieval. In APPLE, a global prototype training strategy and a client-side feature augmentation module are employed to bridge the semantic gap between global and local representations. Without exposing raw data, APPLE learns more separable global prototypes and adaptively injects their semantics into local code generation, thereby mitigating cross-client semantic bias caused by non-IID effects. Experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that APPLE consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines.

title: Joint Reproduction Number and Spatial Connectivity Structure Estimation via Graph Sparsity-Promoting Penalized Functional
authors: E. Lasalle, B. Pascal
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: During an epidemic outbreak, decision makers crucially need accurate and robust tools to monitor the pathogen propagation. The effective reproduction number, defined as the expected number of secondary infections stemming from one contaminated individual, is a state-of-the-art indicator quantifying the epidemic intensity. Numerous estimators have been developed to precisely track the reproduction number temporal evolution. Yet, COVID-19 pandemic surveillance raised unprecedented challenges due to the poor quality of worldwide reported infection counts. When monitoring the epidemic in different territories simultaneously, leveraging the spatial structure of data significantly enhances both the accuracy and robustness of reproduction number estimates. However, this requires a good estimate of the spatial structure. To tackle this major limitation, the present work proposes a joint estimator of the reproduction number and connectivity structure. The procedure is assessed through intensive numerical simulations on carefully designed synthetic data and illustrated on real COVID-19 spatiotemporal infection counts.

title: Mind the Noise, Align the Fine: Confidence-Aware Masked Image Modeling for Text-based Person Re-Identification
authors: Zhengbing Li, Jiaying Chen, Yurong Qian, Huiyong Lv
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Text-based Person Re-identification (TBPR) aims to retrieve person images from natural language descriptions. However, existing approaches face two major challenges. First, training data from real-world scenarios often contain samples with inaccurate or semantically ambiguous image-text correspondences. Second, previous works insufficiently model the visual information. To address these issues, we propose a novel TBPR framework named FiMi, which integrates two key components: the Confidence Modeling Module (CMM) and the Cross-modal Masked Image Modeling (CroMIM) module. CMM models the global alignment loss distribution of image-text pairs using a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), and estimates the posterior probability of each pair belonging to the high-confidence group to generate adaptive confidence weights, thereby achieving soft-label supervision. CroMIM introduces a lightweight cross-modal reconstruction task by randomly masking image regions and recovering them using both textual and unmasked visual cues, thereby enhancing fine-grained alignment. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our FiMi framework.

title: MMFast: Rethinking Vision-Language Interaction in Efficient MLLMs
authors: Shengyi Xiong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable advancements, yet the mechanisms underlying vision-language interaction remain underexplored, often leading to inefficient architectures. This work investigates the fusion dynamics within auto-regressive MLLMs and reveals that critical fine-grained interactions occur predominantly in intermediate layers, while early and late layers exhibit significant redundancy. Motivated by these insights, we propose MMFast, a novel MLLM architecture that achieves a superior trade-off between efficiency and performance. MMFast selectively propagates a subset of visual tokens ("Salient Tokens") as a global representation and injects fine-grained visual details only into the critical middle layers for alignment. This hierarchical fusion strategy significantly reduces computational overhead without sacrificing performance. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MMFast surpasses existing baselines under comparable computational constraints, delivering state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.

title: De-Biasing Facial Age Estimation: A Dual-Stage Disentanglement Framework for Cross-Racial Generalization
authors: Shuo Gao, Zhenhua Tan, Mingzhao Xie, Li Li, Li Tan, Yingyou Wen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Facial age estimation often suffers from racial bias due to limited and imbalanced racial representation in training data. In this paper, we propose a cross-race age estimation method based on monoracial samples training, aiming to mitigate the racial bias correlated with age information. Specifically, the proposed method mainly consists of two parts, i.e., Mixed-Feature Extraction module and De-Bias module. We first introduce the Mixed-Feature Extraction module to explore the facial mixed features, which exist in the form of pixels in facial images. Then, the De-Bias module is designed to mitigate racial bias from the extracted mixed features, thereby achieving cross-race age estimation. Particularly, all experiments evaluation followed a clear and fair evaluation protocol to ensure the reliability of results. Extensive experiments on the popular datasets have validated that the proposed method is more effective on cross-race age estimation.

title: Semantic Relation-Enhanced Clip Adapter for Domain Adaptive Zero-Shot Learning
authors: Jiaao Yu, Mingjie Han, Jinkun Jiang, Junyu Dong, Tao Gong, Man Lan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The high cost of data annotation has spurred research on training deep learning models in data-limited scenarios. Existing paradigms, however, fail to balance cross-domain transfer and cross-category generalization, giving rise to the demand for Domain-Adaptive Zero-Shot Learning (DAZSL). Although vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) have inherent advantages in the DAZSL field, current studies do not fully exploit their potential. Applying CLIP to DAZSL faces two core challenges: inefficient cross-category knowledge transfer due to the lack of semantic relation guidance, and degraded cross-modal alignment during target domain fine-tuning. To address these issues, we propose a Semantic Relation-Enhanced CLIP (SRE-CLIP) Adapter framework, integrating a Semantic Relation Structure Loss and a Cross-Modal Alignment Retention Strategy. As the first CLIP-based DAZSL method, SRE-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on the I2AwA and I2WebV benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches.

title: CVaR-Aware Network Slicing for Tail Latency Under Tiered Deadlines
authors: Shujia Niu, Qixin Peng, Zhenli He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Real-time distributed computing must satisfy tiered deadlines on shared, heterogeneous infrastructure, where policies that optimize averages often leave tail latency uncontrolled and vulnerable to bursts. We present a conditional value-at-risk (CVaR)-aware network slicing framework that provides end-to-end resource isolation and explicitly optimizes the tail of the delay distribution while enforcing hard-deadline reliability targets. The controller operates in two regimes: when resources are sufficient, it minimizes the CVaR of soft-deadline exceedance and exploits problem monotonicity with a safeguarded hybrid solver that blends adaptive Newton steps with Illinois backstops for global convergence; when resources are scarce, we propose a Priority-Guided Saturation Search (PGSS) that decomposes the Lagrangian conditions into analytically solvable subproblems, allocating additional capacity to delay-sensitive slices without black-box search. Across high-load simulations, our approach compresses extreme tail delays and maintains hard-deadline violation probabilities below target bounds, outperforming CVaR-TD3, PSO-based, Lagrangian-search, and proportional baselines. The framework demonstrates effective tail risk control capability, making it attractive for safety-critical and mission-critical services.

title: Variational Bayesian Filtering Using Gaussian Mixtures
authors: Felix Gustafsson, Roland Hostettler
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper introduces a variational Bayes filtering framework that employs Gaussian mixture models as the variational distribution to approximate complex and multimodal posterior distributions in state estimation problems. Unlike traditional Kalman-based filters or particle filters, the proposed method enables parametric estimation of non-Gaussian posteriors. The proposed approach optimizes the evidence lower bound using Monte Carlo approximations and the reparameterization trick. Experimental results on a nonlinear toy example and a stochastic oscillator demonstrate that the proposed filter significantly improves posterior approximation and reduces Kullback–Leibler divergence between the estimate and the true posterior compared to single-Gaussian variational Bayes and Unscented Kalman Filters, achieving performance comparable to dense grid approximations.

title: FCSTG-INCEPTIONNET: Temporal Lag and Mesoscale Spatio-Temporal Features Modeling for EEG-Based Diagnostics
authors: Xiwen Yang, Yunfei Han, Xun Deng, Zhibin Li, Feng Tan, Yu-An Huang, Zhuhong You, Lun Hu, Le Tong, Pengwei Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Analyzing complex Electroencephalography (EEG) signals for brain function research is challenging due to temporal lags between brain regions and difficulties in capturing mesoscale spatio-temporal features. To address this, we introduce FCSTG-InceptionNet, a model designed to capture these intricate dependencies. It integrates a Fully-Connected Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network for time-lagged relationships with a multi-path attention mechanism and 3D electrode spatial mapping to effectively capture local, mesoscale, and global features. Validated on the SEED and CHB-MIT datasets for emotion classification and epilepsy detection, FCSTG-InceptionNet demonstrates superior and robust performance over baselines in subject-dependent, cross-session, and cross-subject experiments. These results highlight the model’s potential for advancing EEG-based diagnostics by effectively modeling complex spatio-temporal dynamics.

title: CAD-Judge: Toward Efficient Morphological Grading and Verification for Text-to-CAD Generation
authors: Zheyuan Zhou, Jiayi Han, Liang Du, Naiyu Fang, Lemiao Qiu, Shuyou Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models are widely used across industrial design, simulation, and manufacturing processes. Text-to-CAD systems aim to generate editable, general-purpose CAD models from textual descriptions, significantly reducing the complexity and entry barrier associated with traditional CAD workflows. However, rendering CAD models can be slow, and deploying VLMs to review CAD models can be expensive and may introduce reward hacking that degrades the systems. To address these challenges, we propose CAD-Judge, a novel, verifiable reward system for efficient and effective CAD preference grading and grammatical validation. We adopt the Compiler-as-a-Judge Module (CJM) as a fast, direct reward signal, optimizing model alignment by maximizing generative utility through prospect theory. To further improve the robustness during testing phase, we introduce a simple yet effective agentic CAD generation and adopt the Compiler-as-a-Review Module (CRM), which efficiently verifies the generated CAD models, enabling the system to refine them accordingly. Extensive experiments on challenging CAD datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining superior efficiency.

title: Evaluating Software Platforms for Digital Twin Creation: Features, Capabilities, and Tradeoffs
authors: Daniel Flores-Martin, Aranzazu Rodriguez-Cabanillas, Javier Berrocal
publish: IT Professional
abstract: The increasing adoption of digital twin technologies across sectors has led to many platforms offering diverse capabilities, architectures, and integration models. However, selecting the most suitable platform remains a challenge due to inconsistent documentation, varying levels of abstraction, and heterogeneous toolchains. We propose a structured six-phase methodology, requirements analysis, functionality assessment, evaluation criteria definition, technology mapping, evaluation metrics, and implementation and evaluation to enable reproducible, like-for-like comparisons. To validate this methodology, a complex real-world scenario about urban traffic simulation for Madrid’s M-30 was implemented across three prominent platforms: Azure Digital Twins, AWS IoT TwinMaker, and Eclipse Ditto. Each platform was rigorously assessed in terms of scalability, data management, protocol compatibility, and security. The work highlights key tradeoffs between cloud-native, proprietary solutions and open source alternatives, supporting informed platform selection aligned with technical needs and project constraints. The proposed methodology proves to be effective and adaptable for guiding both academic studies and industrial decision making in the adoption of digital twins.

title: Channel-Adaptive Robust Aggregation for Over-the-Air Federated Learning in Heterogeneous Networks
authors: Zubaida Fatima, Zubair Shaban, Yusuf Jamal, Nazreen Shah, Ranjitha Prasad, B. N. Bharath
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing demand for privacy-preserving, data-intensive applications such as IoT, augmented reality, and autonomous systems positions Federated Learning (FL) as a key enabler in 6G networks. Over-the-Air FL (OTA-FL) leverages the superposition property of the wireless multiple access channel for efficient aggregation via simultaneous transmissions. Existing methods rely on fixed aggregation schedules and do not jointly address noise, fading, and client heterogeneity. We propose CHARGE-FL (CHannel-Adaptive Robust agGrEgation), a framework that adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness. By combining a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism, CHARGE-FL mitigates channel distortion and bias from partial updates, achieving superior accuracy, stability, and convergence under realistic wireless conditions. Empirical results under realistic wireless conditions show that CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-ofthe-art OTA-FL methods, particularly in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.

title: KANSeg-BT: Adaptive Kolmogorov–Arnold Segmentation Network for Brain Tumor MRI
authors: Ankit Kumar, Shreya Awasthi, Manish Kumar, Mukul S. Sutaone
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: The segmentation of brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has been proven to be one of the most difficult and challenging tasks. The difficulties arise from irregular tumor boundaries, inconsistent intensity levels, and low contrast with nearby tissues. Currently, traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) perform well on standard segmentation tasks but struggle to capture subtle structural and intensity variations present in tumor boundaries. To identify these limitations, we proposed a novel, adaptive model for segmentation, built on the Kolmogorov Arnold network (KAN), called KANSegBT. Unlike traditional CNNs, KANSeg-BT learns flexible feature representations through B-spline-based activations. KANSeg-BT introduces two custom modules: the Kolmogorov-Arnold Enhanced Convolution Block (KAN-ECB) and the KAN Bottleneck Block (KAN-BB), which help capture both local and global structural patterns in MRI data. Finally, we evaluated the model on two datasets. On the BraTS 2020 dataset, the model recorded a Dice score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9327 \pm 0.014$</tex>, along with a loss value of 0.0087. It also achieved a sensitivity of 0.9282, a specificity of 0.9984, and an overall accuracy of 0.9966, indicating highly consistent segmentation results. When tested on the BraTS 2022 dataset, the model showed a slight improvement, obtaining a Dice score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9396 \pm 0.012$</tex> with a loss of 0.0085, a sensitivity of 0.9224, a specificity of 0.9989, and an accuracy of 0.9968. KANSeg-BT outcome surpasses U-Net and attention-based models without extra computation.

title: Can Large Audio Language Models Understand Audio Well? Speech, Scene and Events Understanding Benchmark for LALMs
authors: Han Yin, Jung-Woo Choi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recently, Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have progressed rapidly, demonstrating their strong efficacy in universal audio understanding through cross-modal integration. To evaluate LALMs’ audio understanding performance, researchers have proposed different benchmarks. However, key aspects for real-world interactions are underexplored in existing benchmarks, i.e., audio signals typically contain both speech and non-speech components, and energy levels of these components can vary significantly across different scenarios. Moreover, most benchmarks do not consider the joint understanding of speech, scene, and events within the same audio clip. In this work, we introduce SSEU-Bench, the first versatile audio understanding benchmark that explicitly accounts for energy differences between speech and non-speech audio, with both independent and joint understanding settings for speech, scene, and events. Furthermore, we demonstrate that some LALMs tend to underperform on certain tasks in a joint understanding setting. To address this issue, we introduce Chain-of-Thought, which effectively improves LALMs’ joint audio understanding performance by decomposing complex tasks into simpler reasoning steps <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: Boosting Contextual Adaptive Policy Learning with Foundation Model Guidance
authors: Yuanfei Wang, Hao Dong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Realistic multi-agent environments require agents to cooperate or compete with unfamiliar partners or opponents, which demands efficient exploration and the construction of informative interaction contexts to model and adapt to others. Reinforcement learning methods often struggle to learn such contextual adaptive policies, as environments do not directly reward the long-horizon exploration necessary for successful modeling and adaptation. While recent approaches leverage foundation models to build generalizable and adaptive agents, invoking these models during interaction is both computationally expensive and time-consuming. To address these challenges, we propose FORCAP, a novel framework that performs offline reasoning to generate context functions, from which an expert policy and a reward function are derived to accelerate RL training. FORCAP harnesses the rich knowledge of foundation models while minimizing inference cost. Experiments on both cooperative and competitive multi-agent benchmarks demonstrate that FORCAP significantly improves both training efficiency and performance.

title: METASPDB: A Shared-Personalized Dual-Branch Framework for Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition
authors: Hexi Li, Ling Wu, Jiacai Zhang, Fangyi Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a powerful tool for emotion recognition(ER), but its practical application is severely hampered by inter-subject variability. To address this challenge, we introduce MetaSPDB, a lightweight yet powerful meta-learning framework featuring a Shared-Personalized Dual-Branch architecture. Our design decouples representation learning into a shared branch for capturing universal emotional features and a personalized branch for subject-specific traits. This dual-branch structure maintains high expressive power while ensuring each component remains computationally tractable for meta-optimization. Through training, MetaSPDB learns a globally optimal initialization that can rapidly adapt to unseen subjects. Cross-subject evaluations on the SEED and SEED-IV datasets validate its efficacy, achieving accuracies of 88.36% and 81.88% in 1-shot setting, respectively. Notably, this performance is achieved with a highly efficient model of only 0.96M parameters, successfully bridging the gap between model expressiveness and meta-learning feasibility.

title: ActiveParam: Selective Parameterization for Efficient and Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Siqiang Li, Xifan Liu, Peng Peng, Shouqiang Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating external knowledge. However, parametric RAG (PRAG) methods often suffer from redundancy and noise sensitivity, leading to inefficiency and degraded robustness. We propose ActiveParam, a framework for selective knowledge parameterization. ActiveParam introduces a Knowledge Probing stage, where the LLM computes an ignorance score to identify knowledge gaps for each document. Only documents with high scores, indicating novel information, are parameterized through lightweight adapters. Experiments on four question-answering benchmarks and three LLMs show that ActiveParam consistently improves F1 scores by 2-5 points over baselines like Naive Combine, while simultaneously reducing the number of required parameter adapters. This work thus establishes that selective parameterization, guided by our novel ignorance score, offers a more efficient and robust path for knowledge augmentation.

title: Decision Fusedconv: Efficient Offline Reinforcement Learning Via Fused State-Reward Encoding and Hybrid Temporal Convolution
authors: Zhuojing Tian, Yushu Chen, Haoyu Ma, Wenlai Zhao, Zhao Liu, Hongkun Yu, Wusheng Zhang, Shiming Song, Dianxi Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) represents return-to-go, state, and action as independent tokens, resulting in inflated sequence length and quadratic attention cost. To address this inefficiency, we propose Decision FusedConv (DFC), which jointly encodes return and state to shorten sequences and employs a gated hybrid convolutional module that integrates global uniform and local heterogeneous convolutions. This design effectively captures both long-range and modality-specific temporal dependencies while avoiding the overhead of self-attention.
