Experiments on MuJoCo and navigation benchmarks show that DFC consistently outperforms DT and recent variants, achieving up to a 64.9% reduction in FLOPs compared to DT, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained settings. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Drama1234/Decision-FusedConv

title: MHAN: A Multi-Module Hybrid Attention Network for Robust Classification of Appendiceal Cancer and Appendicitis
authors: Rengmin Wu, Dan Liang, Jiaqiang Li, Yongtao Zhang, Wenzheng Hu, Hui Zhou, Guangming Li, Xinhua Wei, Tianfu Wang, Baiying Lei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal disease, but appendiceal cancer is a rare malignancy. The similarities between the two diseases often lead to missed diagnosis in clinical practice, resulting in serious consequences. To address this problem, we propose a multi-module hybrid attention network (MHAN) to tackle the challenge of classifying appendiceal cancer and appendicitis in CT images. Our framework comprises four innovative modules. The multi-level feature extraction (MFE) enhances the multi-scale representation of small lesions. The hypergraph-based feature interaction (HFI) captures high-order cross-layer dependencies. The agent attention fusion (AAF) supports cross-layer recursive semantically guided enhancement. Finally, a reinforcement learning-based Dynamic loss optimization (RDLO) improves robustness in imbalanced data problem. We validate the proposed model on both internal and external datasets, and the results demonstrate significant improvements in lesion representation and diagnostic performance.

title: Precision Matters: Comparing Deep Models for Visual Marker Detection on Eyewear-Mounted Calibration Objects
authors: Adrian-Pavel Măduța
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of localizing hazard-shaped calibration markers mounted on eyeglass frames, a task relevant to augmented reality, head pose estimation and optical distance calculations. The goal is to accurately detect these markers, which lie in the upper portion of the face, within a rectangular region provided by a face detector. We revisit this problem and ask: how much precision can be reliably extracted, and at what computational cost? Beyond a controlled comparison of heatmap-based segmentation models (HRNet modified for multiscale outputs, MobileNetV3) and object detection with YOLOv8 variants, we introduce two contributions that improve accuracy while retaining real-time performance: (i) a temperature-scaled soft-argmax readout for subpixel localization with calibrated spatial confidence, and (ii) a lightweight Detection → Refinement cascade that couples YOLO proposals with a 7-layer heatmap refiner operating on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64 \times 64$</tex> crops. Experiments are conducted on both synthetic and real datasets spanning diverse capture distances. Our cascade reduces mean pixel error by 17 % over YOLOv8-n while running at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 8 ~ m s}$</tex> per frame (includes additional latency such as image loading, resizing, and patch extraction, inference time alone is 75 ms per frame), and outperforms HRNet by 4.7% AUC@2px at a fraction of its latency. Evaluation includes distance-based error metrics, uncertainty calibration (ECE), and both quantitative and qualitative analyses. Results show a strong correlation between confidence and localization error, enabling practical deployment.

title: Place Anywhere: Learning Spatial Reasoning for Occlusion-Aware Image Composition
authors: Zan Li, Bingnan Wang, Wencheng Wang, Fei Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Image composition aims to seamlessly blend objects into new environments, which is a fundamental task in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods often cannot fully understand the 3D contents. They only allow to insert an object into part of the image, where there are no occlusion issues. To address this limitation, occlusions between foreground and background objects must be carefully identified to freely generate a reasonable composited image. We propose Place Anywhere, a framework with strong spatial reasoning capability that learns to resolve occlusion relationships in arbitrary compositions. Given a foreground object and a background scene, our model predicts a visibility mask for the foreground, indicating which regions should remain visible after considering occlusion. This is achieved through our carefully designed feature extraction, fusion, and depth-guided modules, along with a novel method for constructing training data that incorporates spatial relationships. Experimental results show that Place Anywhere can generalize well, learn robust 3D spatial reasoning, and produce realistic compositions without explicit placement constraints. The source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Zannnn/autoComposition.

title: Spiking Self-Organizing Maps with Convergence Guarantees for Unsupervised Radar Signal Deinterleaving
authors: Qi Sun, Shumo Yang, Zhen Cao, Shiqing Yang, Biao Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In high-density, multi-source radar pulse environments, signal deinterleaving requires both high precision and real-time performance. Traditional pulse descriptor word (PDW)-based statistical or rule-based methods are limited in complex scenarios and incur high computational costs, making them unsuitable for edge deployment. We propose a Spiking Self-Organizing Map (Spiking-SOM) that integrates LIF spiking neurons with SOM unsupervised learning rules, enabling BMU selection and weight updates in the spike domain while avoiding floating-point distance computation. Theoretically, we prove that BMU firing rates are monotonically increasing and weight updates satisfy the Robbins-Monro conditions, ensuring topological stability and convergence. During inference, Spiking-SOM leverages event-driven accumulation for similarity computation and sparse weight updates, with arithmetic complexity and memory access decreasing linearly with input sparsity. Experiments demonstrate that Spiking-SOM achieves high accuracy while enabling sparse, low-power computation, making it suitable for deployment on FPGA and other edge hardware.

title: Toward Embodied Intelligence: An Architecture for Natural Dialogue and Action Execution in Assistive Robots
authors: Karameldeen Omer, Andrea Monteriù
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This work presents a new architecture for an assistive mobile robot designed to support the elderly and individuals with disabilities in performing daily indoor tasks. The proposed framework integrates multimodal perception, language-based reasoning, and safety-aware action planning to enable natural and effective two-way communication between humans and robots. At its core, the system utilizes large language models (LLMs) for dialogue management, contextual understanding, and reasoning over fused sensory inputs, including vision, speech, and proprioceptive data. By combining speech recognition, object detection, and local memory modules, the robot not only interprets explicit user commands but also infers implicit intentions, predicts missing information, and requests clarifications when necessary. A dedicated safety layer filters and validates action sequences before execution, ensuring reliability and user safety. The architecture further incorporates short- and long-term memory structures, enabling the robot to maintain a dialogue history and semantic knowledge of the environment. This bidirectional interaction model allows the robot to generate both natural conversational responses and executable action plans in a context-aware manner. Preliminary implementation and testing demonstrate promising performance, bridging the gap between conversational AI and embodied robotic action in real-life assistive scenarios.

title: Current-Tracking Technique Improves RF Amplifier Efficiency in Lower HF Frequency Band
authors: Aleksandar Kiričenko, Vedran Kos
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Over the past decade, a prominent trend in mobile radio communications has been the increasing adoption of envelope-tracking techniques to enhance the efficiency of radio frequency (RF) power amplifiers. An envelope-tracking amplifier is a hybrid linear/non-linear circuit where the supply voltage to the linear amplifier is dynamically adjusted by a nonlinear (switched-mode) power supply circuit to match the instantaneous amplitude (envelope) of the input signal. Inspired by this principle of operation, the presented research investigates a more straightforward approach: feeding the antenna directly with current generated by a highly efficient switched-mode source. The viability of the principle has been verified by simulations and measurement results on a demonstrator in the lower HF frequency range. The results have confirmed the substantial efficiency increase, in the operating frequency range of non-linear circuit.

title: A Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework for Ethical Decision Automation and Regulatory Compliance Management in Organizations
authors: Maha Lakshmi S, Nandini D, Priya R, Hubin Bens L, Thuseenth M, Vimalyabesh A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: With the advent of smart automation, organizational governance systems are being under more pressure to make morally upright and legally correct decisions within dynamic environments. This paper gives a new Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework that incorporates structural causal modeling (SCM), counterfactual reasoning, digital twin simulation, explainable reinforcement learning (XRL), Bayesian trust scoring, and federated auditable ledgers (FAL) in order to achieve transparency, accountability, and regulatory alignment. The model represents the policy effects on virtual simulations, selects the optimal decision paths through XRL, and automatically revises the trust scores of the data sources and service constituents. These experimental findings show a 97.5 prediction accuracy in the Ethics, convergence of the trust score in 6 iterations and 94 % compliance fidelity in the case of simulated governance. Also, the counterfactual engine successfully identified 60 % of ethically suboptimal decisions prior to implementation. The proposed model beats the traditional rule-based compliance engines in terms of ethical foresight, interpretability, and adaptiveness. The article provides a regulatory-conscious, scaled, and explainable governance infrastructure that can be used in areas like finance, healthcare, and government. The future work will introduce and include multi-jurisdictional alignment of regulatory ontology and real-time feedback of human stakeholders to further improve ethical negotiation in conflicting governance situations and assist in hybrid human-AI regulatory symbiosis.

title: Dissecting Performance Degradation in Audio Source Separation under Sampling Frequency Mismatch
authors: Kanami Imamura, Tomohiko Nakamura, Kohei Yatabe, Hiroshi Saruwatari
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audio processing methods based on deep neural networks are typically trained at a single sampling frequency (SF). To handle untrained SFs, signal resampling of the input signal is commonly employed, but it can degrade performance, particularly when the input SF is lower than the trained SF. This paper investigates the causes of this degradation through two hypotheses: (i) the lack of high-frequency components introduced by up-sampling, and (ii) the greater importance of their presence than their spectral contents. To examine these hypotheses, we compare conventional resampling with three alternatives: post-resampling noise addition, which adds Gaussian noise to the resampled signal; noisy-kernel resampling, which perturbs the kernel with Gaussian noise to enrich high-frequency components; and trainable-kernel resampling, which adapts the interpolation kernel through training. Experiments on music source separation show that noisy-kernel and trainable-kernel resampling alleviate the degradation observed with conventional resampling. We further demonstrate that noisy-kernel resampling is effective across diverse models, highlighting it as a simple yet practical option to alleviate the performance degradation.

title: A Decomposition-Based State Space Model for Multivariate Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Shunya Nagashima, Shuntaro Suzuki, Shuitsu Koyama, Shinnosuke Hirano
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multivariate time series (MTS) forecasting is crucial for decision-making in domains such as weather, energy, and finance. It remains challenging because real-world sequences intertwine slow trends, multi-rate seasonalities, and irregular residuals. Existing methods often rely on rigid, hand-crafted decompositions or generic end-to-end architectures that entangle components and underuse structure shared across variables. To address these limitations, we propose DecompSSM, an end-to-end decomposition framework using three parallel deep state space model branches to capture trend, seasonal, and residual components. The model features adaptive temporal scales via an input-dependent predictor, a refinement module for shared cross-variable context, and an auxiliary loss that enforces reconstruction and orthogonality. Across standard benchmarks (ECL, Weather, ETTm2, and PEMS04), DecompSSM outperformed strong baselines, indicating the effectiveness of combining component-wise deep state space models and global context refinement.

title: Random Matrix-Driven Graph Representation Learning For Bioacoustic Recognition
authors: Biaohang Yuan, Jiangzhao Wang, YuKai Hao, Ruzhen Chen, Yan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Ecoacoustic monitoring underpins global biodiversity assessment. However, the scarcity of data presents challenges in acoustic signal processing, leading to limited model accuracy due to inadequate training samples. In this work, we propose a Random Matrix-Driven Graph Representation Learning (RM-GRL) framework that utilizes time-frequency hypergraphs and the distance-preserving properties of random matrix theory. By introducing a learnable scaling factor, denoted as α, the framework dynamically adjusts cross-channel weights to effectively capture global and local time-frequency features. Additionally, the inclusion of superedges maintains harmonic structure by connecting time-frequency nodes within the same harmonic component. Our method employs a three-channel Mel spectrogram (log-Mel, delta, delta-delta) and addresses data imbalance by explicitly modeling the harmonic repetition period of the acoustic signal and the temporal irregularity of transient events. The RM-GRL framework is evaluated on the Birdsdata dataset for tasks involving natural sound detection and bullfrog call detection. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance across four groups of imbalanced positive and negative samples and significantly outperforms four selected baselines.

title: Multimodal Variational Graph Network for Multimodal Sentiment Analysis
authors: Yuzhi Ren, Qiang Lu, Yunfei Long, Zhenfang Zhu, Jing Meng, Hongli Pei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) aims to integrate heterogeneous modalities (visual, acoustic and text) for recognizing human sentiment states. Existing methods primarily use non-textual information to enhance text representation, but they still face issues of context misalignment between different modalities and from failing to capture complex dependencies. Therefore, we propose a Multimodal Variational Graph Network (MVGNet) to tackle asynchronous alignment and dependency modeling in MSA. Specifically, first, the Adaptive Cross-modal Graph Interaction Module (ACGIM) dynamically models global–local dependencies by combining a Cross-modal Attention Graph Construction Strategy (CAGS) with a Gated Residual Graph Convolution Strategy (GRGCS). Second, the Modality-Weighted Variational Encoding Module (MWVEM) effectively alleviates semantic ambiguity via variational guidance and modality-weighted fusion, thereby promoting robust multimodal alignment. MVGNet establishes a progressive feature alignment – structural fusion paradigm, significantly improving sentiment analysis robustness. Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI benchmark datasets demonstrate that MVGNet outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

title: Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoding for Irregular Microphone Arrays
authors: Chaoqun Zhuang, Xue Wen, Lin Ma, Lizhong Wang, Liang Wen, Jaehyun Kim, Gangyoul Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Ambisonic encoding from irregular microphone arrays is challenging due to spatial aliasing and limited coverage of the sound field. Existing deep learning approaches typically operate on fixed time–frequency representations (e.g., STFT), which restricts the network’s ability to fully exploit high level spatial correlations. In this work, we propose a Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoder, a fully end-to-end deep neural network (DNN) that estimates a nonlinear transformation in a learned latent space. Explicit spatial clues (IPD, ILD) are fused with deep spectral features to guide the model toward physically consistent mappings. Unlike prior work, the transformation is applied to latent feature maps instead of shallow spectra, enabling adaptive representation learning and more accurate reconstruction. Experiments on real commercial mobile phone microphone arrays in multi-source reverberant scenes demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms least squares and recent neural ambisonic encoders across spatial similarity, spectral error, and SI-SDR metrics, while requiring fewer parameters than competing neural methods.

title: Hierarchical Sparse Vector Transmission for Ultra Reliable and Low Latency Communications
authors: Yanfeng Zhang, Xi’An Fan, Jinkai Zheng, Xiaoye Jing, Weiwei Yang, Xu Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse vector transmission (SVT) is a promising candidate technology for achieving ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC). In this paper, a hierarchical SVT scheme is proposed for multi-user URLLC scenarios. The hierarchical SVT scheme partitions the transmitted bits into common and private parts. The common information is conveyed by the indices of non-zero sections in a sparse vector, while each user’s private information is embedded into non-zero blocks with specific block lengths. At the receiver, the common bits are first recovered from the detected non-zero sections, followed by user-specific private bits decoding based on the corresponding non-zero block indices. Simulation results show the proposed scheme outperforms state-of-the-art SVT schemes in terms of block error rate.

title: AURA: A Stegaformer-Based Scalable Deep Audio Watermark with Extreme Robustness
authors: Linxi Li, Liwei Jin, Yechen Wang, Houmin Sun, Zi Hu, Carsten Maple
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current deep learning-based audio watermarking are suffering from multiple limitations: insufficient robustness, audible noise, and limited capacity. To address these limitations, we propose Adaptive Universal Robust Audiomark(AURA), an audio watermark framework utilizing our novel Stegaformer module. Stegaformer synergizes a Conformer backbone with Feature-wise Linear Modulation to achieve a deep and robust fusion of the audio and watermark.AURA establishes a new state-of-the-art(SOTA), significantly surpassing existing methods in robustness, particularly against aggressive transformations on diverse audio content, and imperceptible to human ears. Furthermore, this work presents the first scaling law study in audio watermarking, revealing unique architectural principles for effectively scaling model capacity and performance for this challenging task.

title: Power Consumption Reduction in ELAA-Assisted ISAC Systems
authors: Xiaomin Cao, Mohammadali Mohammadi, Hien Quoc Ngo, Michail Matthaiou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper, we consider power consumption reduction in extremely large antenna arrays (ELAAs) for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) applications. Although ELAAs are critical for achieving high-resolution near-field sensing, fully activating all antenna elements in conventional digital architectures leads to prohibitive power demands. To address this, we propose an energy-efficient subarray activation framework that selects an optimal subset of subarrays to minimize the total power consumption, subject to quality-of-service (QoS) constraints for both sensing and communication. We formulate a novel optimization problem and solve it using a successive convex approximation (SCA)-based iterative algorithm. The simulation results confirm that the proposed method significantly reduces power consumption while maintaining dual-function performance.

title: Enhance Balance between Generalization and Personalization for Vision-Language Models in Federated Learning
authors: Ziyun Cai, Yizhou Lu, Yawen Huang, Jie Song, Ye Liu, Xiao-Yuan Jing
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose FLORA, a novel Federated Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning framework that combines orthogonal low-rank adaptation for global prompt with attention-guided visual adapter to address the balance between generalization and personalization under data heterogeneity. Specifically, each client personalizes the global prompt through orthogonal low-rank adaptation term with an additional orthogonal loss, thereby achieving efficient local adaptation while maintaining the generalization of the global prompt. Furthermore, we design a lightweight attention-guided visual adapter for the image encoder to enhance cross-modal alignment, further balancing generalization and personalization. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our FLORA achieves superior performance in balancing generalization and personalization over state-of-the-art methods.

title: Inverse Rendering for High-Genus 3D Surface Meshes from Multi-View Images with Persistent Homology Priors
authors: Xiang Gao, Xinmu Wang, Yuanpeng Liu, Yue Wang, Junqi Huang, Wei Chen, Xianfeng Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reconstructing 3D objects from images is inherently an ill-posed problem due to ambiguities in geometry, appearance, and topology. This paper introduces collaborative inverse rendering with persistent homology priors, a novel strategy that leverages topological constraints to resolve these ambiguities. By incorporating priors that capture critical features such as tunnel loops and handle loops, our approach directly addresses the difficulty of reconstructing high-genus surfaces. The collaboration between photometric consistency from multi-view images and homology-based guidance enables recovery of complex high-genus geometry while circumventing catastrophic failures such as collapsing tunnels or losing high-genus structure. Instead of neural networks, our method relies on gradient-based optimization within a mesh-based inverse rendering framework to highlight the role of topological priors. Experimental results show that incorporating persistent homology priors leads to lower Chamfer Distance (CD) and higher Volume IoU compared to state-of-the-art mesh-based methods, demonstrating improved geometric accuracy and robustness against topological failure.

title: CG-DMER: Hybrid Contrastive-Generative Framework for Disentangled Multimodal ECG Representation Learning
authors: Ziwei Niu, Hao Sun, Shujun Bian, Xihong Yang, Lanfen Lin, Yuxin Liu, Yueming Jin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate interpretation of electrocardiogram (ECG) signals is crucial for diagnosing cardiovascular diseases. Recent multimodal approaches that integrate ECGs with accompanying clinical reports show strong potential, but they still face two main concerns from a modality perspective: (1) intra-modality: existing models process ECGs in a lead-agnostic manner, overlooking spatial–temporal dependencies across leads, which restricts their effectiveness in modeling fine-grained diagnostic patterns; (2) inter-modality: the existing methods directly align ECG signals with clinical reports, introducing modality-specific biases due to the free-text nature of the reports. In light of these two issues, we propose CG-DMER, a contrastive-generative framework for disentangled multimodal ECG representation learning, powered by two appealing designs: (1) Spatial-temporal masked modeling is designed to better capture fine-grained temporal dynamics and inter-lead spatial dependencies by applying masking across both spatial and temporal dimensions and reconstructing the missing information. (2) Representation disentanglement and alignment strategy is designed to further mitigate unnecessary noise and modality-specific biases by designing modality-specific and modality-shared encoders, which ensures a clearer separation between modality-invariant and modality-specific representations. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that CG-DMER achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse downstream tasks.

title: CorrEctor: An Execute-to-Correct Paradigm for Efficient LLM Secure Inference
authors: Dong Liu, Bingzheng Wang, Yifan Zeng, Minghui Pan, Jiang Zhou, Xiaoyan Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) provide hardware-level isolation that protects sensitive user data during Large Language Model (LLM) inference. However, mainstream CPU-based TEEs lack the capability to perform largescale matrix operations efficiently. To mitigate this, many approaches offload computations to untrusted GPUs via parameter encryption or differential privacy, but these typically introduce substantial communication overhead or accuracy degradation. To achieve efficient LLM inference while protecting user request privacy without compromising accuracy, we propose an execute-to-correct paradigm for efficient LLM secure inference, namely CorrEctor. The core mechanism of CorrEctor involves generating a sequence of candidate tokens from user inputs protected by differential privacy on untrusted GPUs, followed by verification and correction within a TEE. Experimental results on mainstream LLMs show that CorrEctor outperforms CPU-based TEE baselines by 1.36× to 3.55× in inference acceleration.

title: Alignment-Aware Unrolling Network for Multimodal MRI Reconstruction
authors: Yi Du, Tingting Wang, Ziyi Wang, Sunzhe Yang, Faming Fang, Guixu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep unrolling networks are effective for multimodal MRI reconstruction but struggle to capture cross-modal relationships, limiting reference modality utilization. Moreover, patient motion may induce spatial misalignments, exacerbating the reconstruction difficulty. To address these challenges, we propose an Alignment-aware Unrolling Network (AUNet) that employs semi-implicit cross-modal modeling to capture structural correlations between target and reference images. Specifically, we utilize convolutional sparse coding (CSC) with a shared dictionary to jointly represent structural features of both modalities in the aligned latent space. Furthermore, we introduce a Bidirectional Deformable Sampler (BDS) within the unrolling stages to accurately estimate cross-modal displacement, thereby mitigating spatial misalignment and enhancing reference feature extraction. Our experimental results show that AUNet outperforms existing methods, effectively handling misalignment and improving reconstruction quality. The complete code has been made publicly available at: https://github.com/Corsilent/AUNet.

title: UAFD: Unified Adaptive Frequency-Domain Detector for Generalizable Deepfake Detection
authors: Jialei Zhou, Hongnan Ma, Wenlong Wang, Shuxia Ren, Kexue Fu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the rapid development of diffusion-based image generation, distinguishing highly realistic synthetic images from authentic ones has become increasingly crucial. Despite their visual realism, generative models inevitably introduce inconsistencies due to their fundamentally imitative nature. To address this, we propose UAFD (Unified Adaptive Frequency-Domain Detector), a detection framework that maps nuance, illumination, and reconstruction inconsistencies into a unified frequency-domain representation space. Specifically, nuance inconsistencies are captured via multi-scale Haar wavelet decomposition across different subbands; illumination inconsistencies are modeled through gradient analysis combined with multi-directional Gabor filtering to extract directional lighting cues; and reconstruction inconsistencies are redefined as residual differences between the original and diffusion-reconstructed image spectrums. Furthermore, a spectrum-adaptive weighting mechanism dynamically adjusts the importance of each modality based on frequency energy distribution and spectral complexity. This unified design enhances robustness across generative methods while improving generalization, interpretability, and efficiency.

title: Experimental Evaluation of Synthetic GAN based Data Augmentation Scheme to Predict Improved Rare Diseases from Medical Imaging
authors: L Pattathurani, Shanthashalini, Yashashwini S, S Priya, Soumya T R, M Arun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Medical imaging can pose a vital challenge to rare disease detection because of the scarcity of medical imaging data and imbalance in the classes. This paper presents a synthetic augmentation framework, Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN), which can be used to solve these problems and enhance the work of diagnostic systems in various categories of rare diseases. Open datasets of MRI, CT, and X-ray modalities that were obtained and published in Kaggle were curated, and annotated classes of muscular dystrophies, rare brain lesions, and pediatric skeletal anomalies. It implemented a piping system that consists of preprocessing, synthetic image generation via GANs and the downstream CNN classifier (ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet). The outcomes prove that GAN-enhanced datasets outperform in classification and recall significantly. In particular, the ResNet50 has an accuracy of 91.4 that is higher compared to real-only (84.1) and classical augmented (86.9) models. Optimal performance was also observed at a synthetic to real ratio of 5: 1 as found out in ablation studies. The FID (36.3), Inception Score (3.78), and SSIM (0.76) quantitative quality measurements were used to determine the realism and diversity of synthetic samples. The article demonstrates the potential of synthetic augmentation to enhance the ability to detect underrepresented pathologies and offers a repeatable design of improving rare disease classifiers. The approach can be applied in the medical fields with limited labeled data but early diagnosis is a critical factor.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Optimized Adaptive Sampling
authors: Ryan Pai, John Lipor, Peter Gerstoft
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Level set estimation (LSE)–––finding all points in a domain whose function value lies above or below a known threshold–––is an effective and demonstrated approach to understand the spatial variability of the geoacoustic properties of the ocean floor. The vast scales involved with estimating seabed properties using autonomous underwater vehicles make naïve approaches to spatial sampling expensive or infeasible. Modeling the seabed variation as a Gaussian process (GP) enables an efficient framework that informs adaptive sampling policies. Existing approaches to adaptive sampling utilize heuristics aimed at reducing various forms of uncertainty. We train a reinforcement learning agent using the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) method, learning a policy that considers the entire the sampling trajectory. Instead of using the GP to determine subsequent samples, we use it as observational input for the SAC agent. Results on synthetic GP fields and realistic seabed data show that our approach obtains superior accuracy, uncertainty reduction, and a lower computational cost than existing approaches.

title: Self-Attention Decomposition for Training Free Diffusion Editing
authors: Tharun Anand, Mohammad Hassan Vali, Arno Solin, Green Rosh, B H Pawan Prasad
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models achieve remarkable fidelity in image synthesis, yet precise control over their outputs for targeted editing remains challenging. A key step toward controllability is to identify interpretable directions in the model’s latent representations that correspond to semantic attributes. Existing approaches for finding interpretable directions typically rely on sampling large sets of images or training auxiliary networks, which limits efficiency. We propose an analytical method that derives semantic editing directions directly from the pretrained parameters of diffusion models, requiring neither additional data nor fine-tuning. Our insight is that self-attention weight matrices encode rich structural information about the data distribution learned during training. By computing the eigenvectors of these weight matrices, we obtain robust and interpretable editing directions. Experiments demonstrate that our method produces high-quality edits across multiple datasets while reducing editing time by 60% over current benchmarks.

title: Semanticshield: LLM-Powered Audits Expose Shilling Attacks in Recommender Systems
authors: Kaihong Li, Huichi Zhou, Bin Ma, Fangjun Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recommender systems (RS) are widely used in e-commerce for personalized suggestions, yet their openness makes them susceptible to shilling attacks, where adversaries inject fake behaviors to manipulate recommendations. Most existing defenses emphasize user-side behaviors while overlooking item-side features such as titles and descriptions that can expose malicious intent. To address this gap, we propose a two-stage detection framework that integrates item-side semantics via large language models (LLMs). The first stage pre-screens suspicious users using low-cost behavioral criteria, and the second stage employs LLM-based auditing to evaluate semantic consistency. Furthermore, we enhance the auditing model through reinforcement fine-tuning on a lightweight LLM with carefully designed reward functions, yielding a specialized detector called Semantic-Shield. Experiments on six representative attack strategies demonstrate the effectiveness of SemanticShield against shilling attacks, and further evaluation on previously unseen attack methods shows its strong generalization capability. Code is available at https://github.com/FrankenstLee/SemanticShield.

title: Robust Federated Fine-Tuning Over Heterogeneous and Unreliable Communication Networks
authors: Yanmeng Wang, Zhiwen Dai, Jian Zhou, Fu Xiao, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) has attracted growing attention as it leverages both server- and client-side data resources to improve global model generalization while preserving privacy. However, its performance is often hindered by unreliable server-client connections and heterogeneous client data. Existing strategies typically assume uniform network conditions or require prior knowledge of connection failures, which is impractical in real-world networks with diverse network standards and heterogeneous failure types. To address this, we propose FedAuto, a novel FFT scheme that mitigates the impact of connection failures through adaptive aggregation, without requiring prior knowledge of network conditions or infrastructure modifications, enabling seamless plug-and-play deployment. We also establish rigorous convergence guarantees for FedAuto. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedAuto consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under diverse connection failure scenarios and even surpasses strategies that rely on complex resource optimization.

title: VISA: Virtual Identity for Secure Face Anonymization
authors: Xianhan Zeng, Xiaoxiao Hu, Sheng Li, Xinpeng Zhang, Zhenxing Qian
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper, we propose VISA (Virtual Identity for Secure face Anonymization), a novel framework for generating anonymized yet identifiable faces. VISA binds each face to a virtual identity, enabling reliable verification in virtual spaces without revealing the real identity. To ensure strong privacy protection, VISA enforces that anonymized faces are not only visually and statistically distinct from their originals but also different from all identities in a specified reference database. Technically, VISA builds on a pretrained Style-GAN inversion encoder and StyleGAN generator, enhanced with a lightweight latent transformer for efficient identity transformation. We further design a training strategy based on a multi-queue feature bank and tailored loss functions to balance anonymity, identifiability and utility. Experiments demonstrate that VISA achieves effective face anonymization while preserving recognition and authentication utility.

title: MPM-QIR: Measurement-Probability Matching for Quantum Image Representation and Compression via Variational Quantum Circuit
authors: Chong-Wei Wang, Mei Ian Sam, Tzu-Ling Kuo, Nan-Yow Chen, Tai-Yue Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present MPM-QIR, a variational-quantum-circuit (VQC) framework for classical image compression and representation whose core objective is to achieve equal or better reconstruction quality at a lower Parameter Compression Ratio (PCR). The method aligns a generative VQC’s measurement–probability distribution with normalized pixel intensities and learns positional information implicitly via an ordered mapping to the flattened pixel array, thereby eliminating explicit coordinate qubits and tying compression efficiency directly to circuit (ansatz) complexity. A bidirectional convolutional architecture induces long-range entanglement at shallow depth, capturing global image correlations with fewer parameters. Under a unified protocol, the approach attains PSNR ≥ 30 dB with lower PCR across benchmarks: MNIST 31.80 dB / SSIM 0.81 at PCR 0.69, Fashion-MNIST 31.30 dB / 0.91 at PCR 0.83, and CIFAR-10 31.56 dB / 0.97 at PCR 0.84. Overall, this compression-first design improves parameter efficiency, validates VQCs as direct and effective generative models for classical image compression, and is amenable to two-stage pipelines with classical codecs and to extensions beyond 2D imagery.

title: Vision KAN: Towards an Attention-Free Backbone for Vision with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
authors: Zhuoqin Yang, Jiansong Zhang, Xiaoling Luo, Xu Wu, Zheng Lu, Linlin Shen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Attention mechanisms have become a key module in modern vision backbones due to their ability to model long-range dependencies. However, their quadratic complexity in sequence length and the difficulty of interpreting attention weights limit both scalability and clarity. Recent attention-free architectures demonstrate that strong performance can be achieved without pairwise attention, motivating the search for alternatives. In this work, we introduce Vision KAN (ViK), an attention-free backbone inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks. At its core lies MultiPatch-RBFKAN, a unified token mixer that combines (a) patch-wise nonlinear transform with Radial Basis Function-based KANs, (b) axis-wise separable mixing for efficient local propagation, and (c) low-rank global mapping for long-range interaction. Employing as a drop-in replacement for attention modules, this formulation tackles the prohibitive cost of full KANs on high-resolution features by adopting a patch-wise grouping strategy with lightweight operators to restore cross-patch dependencies. Experiments on ImageNet-1K show that ViK achieves competitive accuracy with linear complexity, demonstrating the potential of KAN-based token mixing as an efficient and theoretically grounded alternative to attention. Code: https://github.com/SeriYann/ViK

title: Multisource Localization Using Multimarginal Optimal Transport
authors: Henri Hentilä, Filip Elvander, Visa Koivunen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The problem of localizing multiple non-cooperative signal-emitting sources is studied. Using a set of receivers with known locations, the direction of arrival (DoA) and time difference of arrival (TDoA) of the signals are measured, and these measurements used to perform the localization. Due to the non-cooperative nature of the sources, it is not known which measurement is associated with which source, leading to a joint localization and association problem. This joint problem is solved via a multimarginal version of optimal transport (OT). By combining DoA and TDoA measurements, it is shown that the proposed solution can achieve localization accuracies much higher than when using only one of the two, and close to those achievable when the associations between measurements and sources are known.

title: UNMIXX: Untangling Highly Correlated Singing Voices Mixtures
authors: Jihoo Jung, Ji-Hoon Kim, Doyeop Kwak, Junwon Lee, Juhan Nam, Joon Son Chung
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We introduce UNMIXX, a novel framework for multiple singing voices separation (MSVS). While related to speech separation, MSVS faces unique challenges: data scarcity and the highly correlated nature of singing voices mixture. To address these issues, we propose UNMIXX with three key components: (1) musically informed mixing strategy to construct highly correlated, music-like mixtures, (2) cross-source attention that drives representations of two singers apart via reverse attention, and (3) magnitude penalty loss penalizing erroneously assigned interfering energy. UNMIXX not only addresses data scarcity by simulating realistic training data, but also excels at separating highly correlated mixtures through cross-source interactions at both the architectural and loss levels. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that UNMIXX greatly enhances performance, with SDRi gains exceeding 2.2 dB over prior work.

title: VividTalker: A Modular Framework for Expressive 3D Talking Avatars with Controllable Gaze and Blink
authors: Hangyu Xiong, Jinyi Zhang, Zheng Wang, Tianlun Pan, Qingzheng Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The synthesis of believable 3D talking avatars is critically hampered by two fundamental obstacles: fragmented, inefficient pipelines and the absence of realistic nonverbal behaviors, leading to a lifeless "dead-eye" gaze. We present VividTalker, a unified framework that systematically addresses these twin challenges. First, we introduce a principled modular architecture that ensures reproducibility while achieving a 35.5% efficiency gain over state-of-the-art methods. Second, we propose a novel, physiologically-grounded model that injects lifelike eye dynamics by synthesizing saccadic movements, conversational blink patterns (15–20 BPM), and head-eye coordination. Extensive multilingual evaluations confirm our approach, showing a 45% increase in gaze naturalness, an absolute MOS gain of +2.5 points in blink realism (from 2.1 to 4.6), and a 62% user preference, setting a new benchmark for expressive digital humans.

title: Robust Colonoscopy Polyp Tracking via Online Coreset Appearance Clustering
authors: Xiao Yang, Zirui Liu, Dongming Yu, Guangzhi Ma, Enmin Song
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In dynamic endoscopic polyp tracking, conventional appearance-based data association relies on redundant historical buffering or rigid feature fusion, limiting their adaptability to rapid viewpoint changes and artifacts. To address these limitations, we propose a novel appearance modeling framework based on online coreset clustering. Instead of a single feature or a redundant buffer, our approach maintains a compact, dynamically updated coreset of historical appearances for each trajectory, designed to preserve feature diversity. This point-to-set matching paradigm evaluates new detections against the representative subset, improving robustness to transient artifacts and viewpoint changes. The compact coreset also enables similarity computation with constant-bounded time complexity. Extensive evaluations on both an internal clinical dataset and the public SUN-SEG benchmark demonstrate competitive performance, achieving 76.3% and 98.7% MOTA respectively, with significant gains in identity preservation metrics.

title: MotionPLLM: LLM-Based Generator for Part-Level Controllable Human Motion in Quantized Latent Space
authors: Jiaqi Zhou, Haofei Wang, Jian Wu, Ju Dai, Lili Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Text-driven human motion generation has advanced with deep models, yet most large language models (LLMs) methods reason over holistic full-body representations, overlooking part-level semantics and limiting control and diversity. We present MotionPLLM, an LLM-based framework that models motion at the part level and encodes dynamics in a quantized latent space. A hierarchical MotionPart-Representation decomposes motion into semantically meaningful body parts, enabling localized control while preserving global coherence. Built on this representation, MotionPart-VQVAE discretizes part-wise dynamics into a structured codebook, supporting efficient tokenization and robust representation learning. Extensive experiments show that MotionPLLM surpasses recent LLM-based baselines in motion quality, generation diversity, and text–motion alignment, offering precise part-level control without sacrificing semantic consistency.

title: Scalable and Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Model Checker for Computation Tree Logic
authors: Ghalya Alwhishi, Jamal Bentahar, Amine Andam, Ahmed Elwhishi, Mustapha Hedabou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Formal verification using temporal logics such as computation tree logic (CTL) is essential for validating safety and correctness in complex systems. However, traditional model-checking techniques face severe scalability limitations due to the state explosion problem and their reliance on exhaustive symbolic traversal. Moreover, existing learning-based verification methods often lack formal guarantees and interpretability. These challenges create a pressing need for scalable, learning-based verification methods that preserve verification reliability while improving computational efficiency. This article introduces a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based model checking framework that learns to verify CTL formulas directly through interaction with system models. Unlike traditional symbolic model checkers such as NuSMV, the proposed DRL-CTL checker trained using proximal policy optimization (PPO) interprets CTL semantics over system models represented as Kripke structures without performing symbolic state-space traversal at inference time. Reward functions are designed for individual CTL operators, and fixed-point reasoning is incorporated to handle global temporal properties such as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$AG(\phi)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$EG(\phi)$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves near-constant inference time of approximately 2 ms per formula on an Intel Core i9-13900K CPU (24 cores, 3.0 GHz), 64 GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU (24 GB VRAM), reduces verification time by up to 90% compared with traditional model checkers, and scales to models with more than <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{1192}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> reachable states. The framework also produces witnesses and counterexamples and yields verification outcomes identical to those of symbolic checkers in our experiments. These results highlight the potential of DRL to serve as a scalable, efficient, and explainable alternative to classical CTL model checking.

title: MLRUI-R: Multilabel Feature Selection Considering Relative Uncertainty Information
authors: Weiyi Huang, Xin Lu, Jianhua Dai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Granular computing, which simulates human cognition by partitioning objects into multiple granules, serves as a valuable approach for handling data with uncertainty and has been widely applied to multilabel feature selection. However, existing methods based on granular computing typically assume equal importance across all labels, which limits their ability to capture the relative recognition of features within the label space. In this article, we address this limitation by analyzing label space dependencies through the lens of rough set theory. In addition, a novel method for multilabel feature selection is introduced, which incorporates relative uncertainty information. First, we propose an object grid-based acceleration method to speed up the computation of relationships in the process of constructing neighborhood granularity, and provide a new definition of neighborhood granularity on this basis. Second, based on the constructed neighborhood granularity, we define uncertainty measures under multilabel data and analyze their corresponding theoretical properties. Finally, by analyzing the dependencies within the label space, we derive an importance matrix for the labels and, by combining it with the defined uncertainty measure, develop a multilabel feature selection method that incorporates relative uncertainty. The experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Editorial
authors: Issouf Fofana
publish: IEEE Electrical Insulation Magazine
abstract: Through my involvement with the IEEE Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation Society (DEIS), I have seen firsthand how dielectrics and insulating materials—the backbone of modern power systems—ensure safety, reliability, and efficiency across high-voltage transformers, cables, gas-insulated systems, power electronics, renewable integration, and electrified transport. DEIS's conferences, workshops, standards development, publications, and collaborative research address pressing challenges—asset aging, reliability, sustainability, and technology integration—shaping international best practices and guiding utilities and manufacturers worldwide.

title: Hierarchical Optimization for Time-Varying Resource Allocation of Networked Uncertain Robotic Agents With Inconstant Parameters
authors: Zi-Heng Yi, Ming-Feng Ge, Teng-Fei Ding, Zhi-Wei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: The existing time-invariant resource allocation methods for general multi-agent systems are difficult to cope with dynamic and time-varying practical application scenarios such as unmanned ocean exploration and ground disaster rescue. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a reliable and safe strategy to meet the optimal distributed time-varying resource allocation (DTVRA) requirements of multi-robot systems in this context. This paper investigates the DTVRA problem of networked uncertain robotic agents (NURAs) with inconstant parameters. We propose a novel hierarchical optimization strategy including the layers of a distributed resource-optimization-based (DROB) estimator and a local adaptive controller. Specifically, the tools of Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions and gradient descent are used to construct a distributed Lagrange multiplier subsystem in the estimator layer, guaranteeing that the estimator states converge to the optimal solutions of DTVRA problem. Furthermore, only the Lagrange multipliers and their derivatives are exchanged instead of the physical states through the interaction topology to address the DTVRA problem in a distributed manner. In addition, a local adaptive controller is constructed so that the physical states of NURAs can be driven to track the estimated values obtained by the DROB estimator, such that the global cost function is minimized under the constraints of the time-varying resources. By employing Lyapunov stability theory, several sufficient conditions on the convergence of the closed-loop system are formally derived. Finally, the simulation examples are carried out to verify the performance of the designed hierarchical optimization algorithm.

title: Trajectory Prediction-Based Adaptive Takeover Method for Mobile Robot Teleoperation Systems with Communication Delays
authors: Ruoshan Wang, Pengfei Li, Yun-Sheng Zhao, Yun-Bo Zhao, Yu Kang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: As a typical application of the Internet of Things, mobile robot teleoperation systems play a crucial role in dangerous tasks or special scenarios. The passive takeover mechanism provides effective safety assurance for such systems, relying on a takeover request (TOR) generator to issue warnings and an authority transfer controller to manage control handover. However, variable communication delays can degrade the safety and smoothness of the passive takeover process. To address this issue, we propose a trajectory prediction-based adaptive takeover method that redesigns both the TOR generator and the authority transfer controller. The TOR generator issues early warnings based on the predicted robot trajectory in autonomous control mode. The authority transfer controller uses the control input that tracks the predicted human-intended trajectory as a reconstruction of the delayed human input, and adaptively adjusts the weights between the autonomous input and the reconstructed human input based on the risk indicator and the delay impact level. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through the human-in-the-loop simulation. The results show that our method ensures better safety and smoothness in the takeover process compared to other methods.

title: Star-shaped Multi-person Interaction Graph Model for Group Skeleton-based Action Recognition
authors: Hongjun Li, Yuehan Jiang, Liping Teng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Skeleton-based human action recognition has attracted increasing attention in recent years. However, most existing methods focus on single-person scenarios and struggle with complex behaviors in multi-person groups. In particular, they lack the capability to automatically identify and model core person. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a star-shaped group interaction model for skeleton-based action recognition. Firstly, the character importance scoring system analyzes both individual and group aspects: it evaluates each person’s individual importance based on motion intensity and motion complexity, and assesses their significance within the group using centrality and interactivity. This process enables accurate identification of the core person in the video. Secondly, a core-star interaction graph is constructed with the core person as the center node and other individuals as peripheral nodes. The relationships among individuals are categorized into self-connections, centripetal connections, and centrifugal connections. For each type of connection, we design differentiated data augmentation strategies to fully exploit diverse action and interaction features. Finally, the structured skeleton data is fed into the star-shaped spatio-temporal graph convolutional network for efficient feature extraction and action classification. Experiments on several public benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, achieving accuracies of 79.1%, 96.1%, and 93.1% on the NBA, Volleyball, and Volleyball-weak datasets, respectively.

title: Application of IEEE Std. 1459-2010 and Hjorth Descriptors for the Identification of Stator Faults in Induction Motors
authors: Arthur C. Santos, Marcelo F. Castoldi, Wesley A. Souza, Alessandro Goedtel
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: Stator interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults represent a significant fraction of failures in three-phase induction motors (TIMs), with industrial studies reporting occurrences of up to 36%. Current-based diagnostic techniques are widely employed; however, their effectiveness degrades under inherent supply asymmetries, manufacturing tolerances, and moderate voltage imbalances, which introduce negative-sequence and harmonic components that mask incipient fault signatures and can produce current components similar to those generated by internal electromagnetic asymmetries. Diagnostic capability is investigated using two families of features extracted from stator electrical signals: power quantities defined by IEEE Std. 1459-2010 and Hjorth time-domain descriptors. These feature families correspond to distinct physical manifestations observable in the measured currents: macroscopic redistribution of electrical power under nonsinusoidal and unbalanced operating conditions, and local variations in waveform geometry associated with circulating currents and early flux asymmetries. Model performance is evaluated through stratified <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-fold cross-validation using five supervised classifiers applied to a labeled laboratory dataset comprising multiple ITSC severities (healthy, 1%, 3%, 5%, and 10%), controlled voltage unbalance, multiple mechanical loading conditions, and supply-frequency variation. Hjorth parameters present higher sensitivity to low-severity faults and achieve classification accuracies above 99%, whereas IEEE Std. 1459-2010 quantities yield accuracies between 78% and 95%, reflecting reduced sensitivity to weak distortions but intrinsically phase-invariant behavior. A reduced hybrid feature set combining selected IEEE Std. 1459-2010 descriptors with the Hjorth parameters Activity and Mobility reduces phase dependence and produces classification accuracies exceeding 99.5% across all evaluated classifiers. Correlation analysis and feature-importance rankings indicate limited redundancy between descriptor groups and reveal complementary diagnostic information associated with electrical power redistribution and waveform-geometry variations. The obtained results indicate that early-stage ITSC faults are identifiable using a compact descriptor space derived from physically interpretable electrical quantities, without dependence on high-dimensional time-frequency representations or deep learning models.

title: Inferring Spatial Reasoning by Observing Human Interactions With Smart IoT Objects
authors: Subir Biswas, Amit Kumar Bhuyan, Hrishikesh Dutta, Saptarshi Das, Amy Noelle Parks, Kiley McKee, Danielle Rothschild Doyle, David H Uttal
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Magazine
abstract: This paper presents a proof-of-concept for a new paradigm for analyzing human-object interactions with high spatiotemporal resolution. The system comprises a set of smart IoT objects, equipped with sensors to monitor their spatial states and surroundings, including other smart objects. Each object is able to detect, store, and wirelessly report its orientation, rotation, adjacency, and various types of movements, both its own and those of neighboring objects. By collating this data, an analytical model can reconstruct a participant’s spatiotemporal handling of the objects with millisecond resolution, thereby inferring their spatial reasoning process. The objective is to establish a IoT echo systems for monitoring, characterizing, and enhancing human spatial reasoning through this novel approach of observing interactions between humans and objects with agency. The paper delves into the engineering details of the IoT system, followed by a series of spatial reasoning-related experiments. Subsequently, analytical methods are developed to gain insights into participants’ spatial reasoning within the context of two well-known tasks: the Tower of Hanoi and the Wechsler Block Design Task. Finally, the results are presented to demonstrate the practicality of the developed IoT-style smart object system for quantitatively understanding spatial reasoning in a data-driven manner.

title: Complete Autonomous Robotic Nasopharyngeal Swab System with Evaluation on a Stochastically Moving Phantom Head
authors: Peter Q. Lee, John S. Zelek, Katja Mombaur
publish: IEEE Transactions on Robotics
abstract: The application of autonomous robotics to close-contact healthcare tasks, such as the nasopharyngeal (NP) swab test, has clear potential for reducing infection risks to staff and improving efficiency. We propose a control system that performs the NP swab test with a collaborative manipulator arm, guided by an instrumented end-effector that measures force and visual information. We assume a scenario where the patient is unrestrained, with hardware general enough for other types of close contact tasks. The system employs visual servo control to align the swab with the nostrils. A compliant joint velocity controller inserts the swab into the nasal cavity, following a planned trajectory adjusted with force-feedback. Fuzzy logic systems are designed to detect when the swab reaches the nasopharynx and enforce safety criteria. We validate the system using a second robotic arm that holds a nasal cavity phantom and simulates natural head motions. Extensive experiments identify controller configurations capable of effectively performing the NP swab test even with significant head motion.

title: LCCST: Loosely Coupled Closed-loop Student-Teacher Framework for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Yajun Liu, Zenghui Zhang, Jiang Yue, Dongying Li, Wenxian Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Semi-supervised learning methods based on the Mean Teacher paradigm have shown strong performance in medical image segmentation, yet they still suffer from representation homogenization: as training progresses, the teacher and student models become tightly coupled, limiting the student’s ability to learn diverse and complementary features. To alleviate this issue, we propose a Loosely Coupled Closed-loop Student-Teacher (LCCST) framework. It consists of one teacher network (T) and two student networks (S1 and S2). The teacher is updated as the exponential moving average of S1, as in conventional Mean Teacher schemes, but its predictions on unlabeled data are not used directly. Instead, they are passed through a Semantic-Support Rectification Module (SSRM), which leverages labeled-support features to produce reliability-weighted pseudo-labels that supervise the independent student S2. In parallel, S1 and S2 interact via a Double Mutual Learning Mechanism (DMLM) that blends pixel-wise probability alignment with global, image-conditioned structural regularization. This closed-loop routing establishes a loosely coupled relation between S1 and T while enabling rich information exchange between S1 and S2 at both local and global levels, thereby improving segmentation accuracy and robustness. Experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate that LCCST consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/PHPJava666/ LCCST.

title: Spoofing Detection and Mitigation in GNSS Using Cyclostationarity and Sparse Array
authors: Mojtaba Khaki, Mohamad Farzan Sabahi, Saeed Gazor, Zhang Zhenkai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Spoofing attacks (SAs) in Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) involve transmitting counterfeit timing and location information and pose serious threats to many critical infrastructures. Target GNSS receivers are vulnerable to lowpower SAs, which may lead to unavailability, navigation errors, or entirely spurious position/time solutions. Traditional SA detectors typically assume a single SA originating from a specific direction with higher power than the authentic signals. These methods rely on eigendecomposition of the spatial covariance matrix to identify the spoofer's direction and often fail to detect lowpower spoofers or multiple simultaneous spoofers. In this paper, we introduce a spatio-temporal method to distinguish spoofers from authentic signals using a sparse array antenna in the pre-correlation stage of the GNSS receiver. We employ high-resolution direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation followed by a power-independent cyclostationarity detector. For each direction, the received signal consists of either a single authentic satellite signal or multiple counterfeit (spoofer) signals. The proposed detector decides between these two hypotheses by estimating the number of impinging signals, and we construct an asymptotically optimal detector using a suitable separating function. In addition, we propose a spatial filter to obtain a spooferfree signal, thereby enhancing the robustness of GNSS receivers against lowpower and multiSAs. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed sparsearray approach in separating and localizing multiple individual spoofers—even in multipath environments—each transmitting multiple counterfeit signals.

title: EvoGraphCoder: An Evolutionary Graph-Reasoning Framework for Self-Adaptive Software Engineering
authors: Karthik Ramamurthy, Rahul Kumar Konduru, Nurmyrat Amanmadov
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Modern software evolves rapidly, accumulates architectural debt, and develops cross-module dependencies that complicate reliable maintenance. We present EvoGraphCoder, an evolutionary graph-reasoning framework for self-adaptive software engineering. EvoGraphCoder represents source code, tests, commit history, dependencies, performance signals, and review feedback as a relational software graph. It combines Adaptive Evolutionary Code Reasoning (AECR) with a multi-agent design consisting of the Innovator, Critic, and Historian to generate, evaluate, and refine candidate repairs over multiple cycles rather than emitting a single patch. The framework further introduces EvoGraph Memory for persistent cross-release learning and Self-Reflexive Validation for explainable pre-merge verification. Experiments on repository-level repair benchmarks show that EvoGraphCoder improves patch quality and robustness over strong baselines, while maintaining positive improvement across repeated repair cycles. These results suggest that graph-driven evolutionary reasoning with persistent memory offers a practical path toward reliable and explainable AI-assisted software maintenance.
