title: Multi-Scale Spatial-Temporal Representation Learning for mmWave Radar 3D Human Pose Estimation
authors: Yaxin Li, Jun Wang, Changshun Yuan, Xiaoming Yuan, Yuquan Luo, Song Liang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar has emerged as a promising sensing modality for 3D human pose estimation in ubiquitous Internet of Things (IoT) applications, owing to its privacy-preserving nature and robustness under challenging illumination conditions. However, accurate skeletal reconstruction remains difficult due to the inherent sparsity, non-uniform distribution, and instability of radar point clouds. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MS-STPoseNet, a unified multi-scale spatio-temporal learning framework for robust mmWavebased human pose estimation. For spatial representation, MS-STPoseNet employs a hierarchical multi-scale spatial encoder built upon PointNet++, which leverages multi-scale grouping to effectively capture body structures across different spatial resolutions, from fine-grained joint regions to limb- and torso-level configurations, under irregular radar observations. For temporal modeling, a multi-branch Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with different dilation rates is introduced to model multi-rate motion dynamics, enabling effective representation of both rapid limb movements and smoother torso motions. An attention mechanism is further incorporated to enhance informative temporal features while suppressing noise. The entire framework is trained end-to-end to estimate per-frame 3D poses by exploiting short-term temporal context, thereby improving robustness under noisy sensing conditions. Extensive experiments conducted on a self-collected dataset and two public benchmarks demonstrate that MS-STPoseNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of pose estimation accuracy and cross-subject generalization, achieving an MPJPE of 3.08 cm on the self-collected dataset. In addition, the proposed framework exhibits favorable computational efficiency and a compact model size, highlighting its potential applicability to practical IoT sensing systems.

title: GQL Rules: Deterministic and Safe Rule-Based Materialization for Property Graphs
authors: Dominik Tomaszuk
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Property-graph query languages have matured, but controlled materialization of reusable derived graph knowledge has remained comparatively under-specified. In many practical workloads, transient query answers are not enough: applications need persistent derived relationships, defaults, alerts, and provenance that can be reused and audited across downstream queries and services. We addressed this gap by presenting GQL Rules, a restricted rule layer for property graphs that combined a Cypher/GQL-style MATCH/WHERE body with an inflationary head based on MERGE and additive ENRICH. The framework targeted static finite graphs, preserved first-class edge identity, exposed explicit diagnostics and per-rule policies for imperfect inputs, and limited negation to stratified NOT EXISTS. We formalized a stratified fixpoint semantics and gave sufficient conditions for safety, finite generation, and deterministic saturated outcomes, together with a semi-naive evaluation strategy for recursive strata as part of the formal proposal. We also implemented a Neo4j/APOC wrapper-based proof of concept and reported preliminary results on representative workloads; these showed correct saturation behavior, deterministic final states under the stated conditions, and naive-fixpoint recursive-scaling trends that motivate the proposed semi-naive strategy.

title: From PowerSGD to PowerSGD+: Low-Rank Gradient Compression For Distributed Optimization With Convergence Guarantees
authors: Shengping Xie, Chuyan Chen, Kun Yuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-rank gradient compression methods, such as PowerSGD, have gained attention in communication-efficient distributed optimization. However, the convergence guarantees of PowerSGD remain unclear, particularly in stochastic settings. In this paper, we show that PowerSGD does not always converge to the optimal solution and provide a clear counter-example to support this finding. To address this, we introduce PowerSGD+, which periodically updates the projection subspace via singular value decomposition, ensuring that it remains aligned with the optimal subspace. We prove that PowerSGD+ converges under standard assumptions and validate its effectiveness through empirical evaluation on large language model tasks.

title: Reliable and Compact Graph Fine-Tuning Via Graph Sparse Prompting
authors: Bo Jiang, Hao Wu, Beibei Wang, Jin Tang, Bin Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Recently, graph prompt learning has garnered increasing attention in adapting pre-trained GNN models for downstream graph learning tasks. However, existing works generally conduct prompting over all graph elements (e.g., nodes, edges, node attributes, etc.), which is suboptimal and obviously redundant. To address this issue, we propose exploiting sparse representation theory for graph prompting and present Graph Sparse Prompting (GSP). GSP aims to adaptively and sparsely select the optimal elements (e.g., certain node attributes) to achieve compact prompting for downstream tasks. Specifically, we propose two kinds of GSP models, termed Graph Sparse Feature Prompting (GSFP) and Graph Sparse multi-Feature Prompting (GSmFP). Both GSFP and GSmFP provide a general scheme for tuning any specific pre-trained GNNs that can select some desired attributes for prompting by employing sparsity-guided prompt learning. A simple yet effective algorithm has been designed for solving GSFP and GSmFP models. Experiments on 16 widely-used benchmark datasets validate the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed GSFPs. Our code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wuhaoxz/GSFP</uri>.

title: Magnetic Hyperthermia Based on Hysteresis Heating Alone
authors: Kevin O’Grady, Vijay Patel, Iestyn Jones, Gonzalo Vallejo-Fernandez
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: In this work we describe a new approach to magnetic hyperthermia based on hysteresis alone. This is achieved by encapsulating magnetite nanoparticles in polymer spheres to remove dynamic effects which cause unpredictable and uncontrollable heating. This also prevents aggregation of the nanoparticles. To generate significant hysteresis and hence heat, we have used nanoparticles grown by the standard coprecipitation process which are used as seeds and grown to a larger size by the addition of further Fe<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2+</sup> salts in an alkaline solution and heating. We define this as a Controlled Growth Process (CGP). The CGP particles are found to have a much higher saturation magnetisation than particles produced by coprecipitation enhancing their magnetic shape anisotropy. Via magnetic measurements and complementary studies of the heat generated when the polymer spheres are exposed to an ac field, we show that the heat generated is due to hysteresis losses with potentially residual contributions from dynamic effects. At 111 kHz and a maximum applied field of 180 Oe, a specific absorption rate (SAR) value of ∼35 W/gFe was determined from calorimetry measurements. This value agrees within error with the losses calculated from the hysteresis loop. When the same particles are not encapsulated, significant viscous heating is present.

title: Privacy-Preserving and Verifiable Federated Learning Framework for Biometric Authentication at the Edge
authors: Hao Zhou, Lin Li, Hua Dai, Geng Yang, Fusen Guo, Chao Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has emerged as a pivotal privacy-preserving paradigm for training models across decentralized biometric datasets. However, integrating secure biometric verification and data integrity in FL remains challenging, especially under non-IID distributions and resource constraints in edge environments. This paper introduces BPVFL, a novel Biometric Privacy-preserving and Verifiable Federated Learning framework, which synergizes differential privacy, cryptographic signatures, and photoplethysmogram (PPG)-based biometric recognition. Distinct from prior works, BPVFL supports both verifiable model updates and privacy-preserving identity authentication through a dual-layer protection scheme. We propose a customized privacy noise mechanism tailored for biometric features and a lightweight signature protocol to trace and validate local contributions. Experimental evaluations on three biometric datasets (SigD, BIDMC, TBME) demonstrate that BPVFL achieves over 92.7% accuracy under strong privacy guarantees, while reducing communication costs via deep network modularization. To substantiate verifiability beyond provenance, we further integrate an optional Correctness Verification Layer (CVL) based on remote attestation, which makes accepted updates protocol-correctness verifiable (i.e., generated by an approved clipping/noising/training pipeline) at a small constant overhead. We provide an empirical comparison to representative verifiable FL designs along the axes of client cryptographic time, extra uplink, and end-to-end round latency, clarifying the engineering trade-offs for edge biometric services.

title: Remote Sensing Monitoring of Glacier Changes in the Puruogangri Glacier Over the Past Four Decades
authors: Boao Li, Xiaoyan Wang, Shi Liang, Hao Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Puruogangri Glacier represents the largest glacier system on the central Tibetan Plateau. Existing glacier-identification algorithms are often susceptible to cloud contamination and frequently underutilize cloud-free pixels, leading to considerable uncertainties in extraction results. The Snow Cover Occurrence Index (SCOI) based method reduces cloud effects and seasonal snow, providing reliable glacier information for long-term change analysis. In this study, 2300 Landsat images from 1986 to 2024 are used in the SCOI method to extract the glacier boundaries of the Puruogangri Glacier over nearly four decades. Results show an overall glacier retreat from 1986 to 2024, with area decreasing from 416.22 ± 9.84 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> to 380.66 ± 8.99 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, a total loss of 35.56 km<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (0.24% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). The most significant glacier recession occurred during 1988–1994 (0.58% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>) and 1997–2003 (0.57% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). In contrast, a slight glacier expansion was observed between 1994 and 1997 (0.85% a<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>). Glacier changes were primarily concentrated in glacier-terminus regions at elevations below 5500 m. Among eight glaciers exhibiting notable changes, seven underwent continuous retreat, while one advanced slightly. During the same period, both temperature and precipitation in the region displayed increasing trends. Glacier area change correlated negatively with both temperature and precipitation, with a stronger correlation observed for temperature.

title: Test Time Optimization for Highly-Reliable Redundant Systems with Single-machine Similarity and Non-Uniform Labor-hours
authors: Chengjie Zhang, Zhigeng Fang, Rui Shi, Youpeng Liu, Zhongwen Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: Reliability demonstration of highly-reliable redundant systems traditionally requires large-sample, long-duration tests, resulting in prohibitively lengthy test cycles and excessive resource consumption. However, existing approaches have not fully exploited system redundancy structure and similarity to reduce test time. To address this gap, this paper integrates redundancy structure, single-machine similarity, and non-uniform labor-hour information into a system-level test time minimization framework. First, an analytical expression for test time is derived by leveraging the sample-size expansion effect of redundant structures. Second, a similarity conversion coefficient is defined to incorporate single-machine similarity into the optimization model. Third, Bayesian methods are utilized to integrate prior test information. Through system-level optimization that optimally allocates reliability targets to individual units, this work achieves joint optimization of redundancy, similarity, and prior information for the first time. A case study demonstrates that the proposed approach reduces test time by up to 74% compared with conventional methods that use similarity solely for prior construction. This study provides an efficient solution for rapid reliability verification of aerospace systems, and the proposed framework is applicable to other exponential lifetime systems with redundant architectures.

title: A Herd Effect based Incentive Scheme for Age of Information Minimization in Mobile Crowdsensing_supp1-3685315.pdf
authors: Anfeng Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Mobile Crowdsensing (MCS) has emerged as an effective paradigm for large-scale data collection, yet it faces two inherently conflicting challenges: minimizing platform cost and reducing the Age of Information (AoI). Existing approaches primarily focus on task assignment optimization or assume fully rational worker behavior, which often limits their effectiveness in achieving timely data updates without incurring substantial costs. To address these challenges, a Herd Effect-based AoI-aware (HEA) data collection scheme is proposed to minimize AoI under budget constraints. Specifically, the HEA scheme incorporates insights from behavioral economics to influence workers’ mobility decisions by exploiting the herd effect. By incentivizing a subset of pioneer workers to adopt a high-effort mobility mode (i.e., the Boosted Mode), the observable behaviors of these pioneers induce subsequent workers to adjust their decisions accordingly, thereby forming a self-reinforcing herd behavior. This mechanism enables timely data collection while effectively controlling the overall incentive expenditure. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme is validated through rigorous theoretical analysis and extensive experiments conducted on multiple real-world datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that HEA consistently outperforms representative baseline schemes, achieving significant AoI reductions with marginal additional cost and substantially improving the trade-off between data timeliness and budget efficiency.

title: Decentralized Federated Learning with Period Gradient Tracking over Time-Varying Networks_supp1-3685244.pdf
authors: Yuehong Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: To address the communication challenges associated with Federated Learning (FL), Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) eliminates the central server and trains the model with decentralized method, enabling each client to only communicate with its neighbors. However, per our analysis, model trained with DFL experiences performance degradation because of data-heterogeneity and time-varying topologies. To address these issues, we propose a Dynamic K-step Gradient Tracking (DKGT) method to enhance the performance of DFL over time varying networks. Specifically, DKGT employs K-step local updates and gradient tracking to reduce the communication cost and the variance from heterogeneous data distribution, and we use dynamic gradient tracking parameter to correct gradient over time varying graph. Theoretically, we derive a universal convergence rate for smooth and non-convex problem at the rate of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{\left(f(\textbf{x}_0)-f(\textbf{x}^*)\right)}{\sqrt{T}(L\sqrt{KN})^{-1}-\tau(pKL\sqrt{TKN})^{-1}}+\frac{\sigma^2}{KTN\tau(pK-\tau)}\right)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, that τ and p respectively represent the time window length and the connectivity of time-varying networks. Experimentally, we illustrate the robustness and effectiveness of this heterogeneity correction on extensive non-convex neural network training tasks over different topologies and dynamic network settings.

title: From Hallucination to Articulation: Language Model-Driven Losses for Ultra Low-Bitrate Neural Speech Coding
authors: Jayeon Yi, Minje Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: "Phoneme Hallucinations (PH)" commonly occur in low-bitrate DNN-based codecs. It is the generative decoder’s attempt to synthesize plausible outputs from excessively compressed tokens missing some semantic information. In this work, we propose language model-driven losses (LM loss) and show they may alleviate PHs better than a semantic distillation (SD) objective in very-low-bitrate settings. The proposed LM losses build upon language models pretrained to associate speech with text. When ground-truth transcripts are unavailable, we propose to modify a popular automatic speech recognition (ASR) model, Whisper, to compare the decoded utterance against the ASR-inferred transcriptions of the input speech. Else, we propose to use the timed-text regularizer (TTR) to compare WavLM representations of the decoded utterance against BERT representations of the ground-truth transcriptions. We test and compare LM losses against an SD objective, using a reference codec whose three-stage training regimen was designed after several popular codecs. Subjective and objective evaluations conclude that LM losses may provide stronger guidance to extract semantic information from self-supervised speech representations, boosting human-perceived semantic adherence while preserving overall output quality. Demo samples, code, and checkpoints are available online<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: AG-Fusion: Adaptive Gated Multimodal Fusion for 3D Object Detection in Complex Scenes
authors: Sixian Liu, Chen Xu, Yiwen Li, Qiang Wang, Donghai Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal camera-LiDAR fusion technology has found extensive application in 3D object detection, demonstrating encouraging performance. However, existing methods exhibit significant performance degradation in challenging scenarios characterized by sensor degradation or environmental disturbances. We propose a novel Adaptive Gated Fusion (AG-Fusion) approach that selectively integrates cross-modal knowledge by identifying reliable patterns for robust detection in complex scenes. Specifically, we first project features from each modality into a unified BEV space and enhance them using a window-based attention mechanism. Subsequently, an adaptive gated fusion module based on cross-modal attention is designed to integrate these features into reliable BEV representations robust to challenging environments. Furthermore, we construct a new dataset named Excavator3D (E3D) focusing on challenging excavator operation scenarios to benchmark performance in complex conditions. Our method not only achieves competitive performance on the standard KITTI dataset with 93.92% accuracy, but also significantly outperforms the baseline by 24.88% on the challenging E3D dataset, demonstrating superior robustness to unreliable modal information in complex industrial scenes.

title: Residual Vector Quantization for Communication-Efficient Multi-Agent Perception
authors: Dereje Shenkut, B.V.K. Vijaya Kumar
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-agent collaborative perception (CP) improves scene understanding by sharing information across connected agents such as autonomous vehicles, unmanned aerial vehicles, and robots. Communication bandwidth, however, constrains scalability. We present ReVQom, a learned feature codec that preserves spatial identity while compressing intermediate features. ReVQom is an end-to-end method that compresses feature dimensions via a simple bottleneck network followed by multi-stage residual vector quantization (RVQ). This allows only per-pixel code indices to be transmitted, reducing payloads from 8192 bits per pixel (bpp) of uncompressed 32-bit float features to 6-30 bpp per agent with minimal accuracy loss. On DAIR-V2X real-world CP dataset, ReVQom achieves 273× compression at 30 bpp to 1365× compression at 6 bpp. At 18 bpp (455×), ReVQom matches or outperforms raw-feature CP, and at 6-12 bpp it enables ultra-low-bandwidth operation with graceful degradation. ReVQom allows efficient and accurate multi-agent collaborative perception with a step toward practical V2X deployment.

title: Deco3D: Decoupled Semantic and Geometric Learning for Sparse Supervision in 3D Object Detection
authors: Yubo Ai, Xujing Tao, Lifan Wu, Tianzhu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse supervision for 3D object detection has gained increasing attention, yet most existing methods rely on bounding-box pseudo-labels to supervise both semantics and geometry, which amplifies errors and noise. We propose Deco3D, a task-decoupled and collaborative learning sparse-supervised 3D detection framework. Deco3D employs coarse pseudo-labels for semantic learning and sparse accurate ground truths for geometric learning. To this end, we introduce the Cluster-Level Label Generation (CLLG) module, which leverages SAM to produce large-scale cluster-level pseudo-labels. We further propose the Cross-Scene Object Mining (CSOM) module, where learnable queries capture intra-modal semantics and activate potential target regions across scenes. Finally, the Collaborative Learning Module (CoLM) module integrates consistency-based label assignment and jointly trains with cluster- and box-level labels. Experiments on KITTI and Waymo demonstrate that Deco3D consistently surpasses prior sparse supervision methods under extremely low annotation cost.

title: Isometric Immersion Learning with Riemannian Geometry for Distortion-Free Representation
authors: Zihao Chen, Wenyong Wang, Qinye Zhu, Yu Xiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Rooted in Riemannian geometry, the concept of isometric immersion offers a foundational paradigm for geometry-preserving representation learning. However, effective approaches tailored to discrete non-Euclidean datasets remain underexplored. To address this gap, we propose an unsupervised framework that decouples isometry and immersion into two dedicated modules, with a parameterized design that induces Riemannian metrics capable of preserving local geometric structures in discrete circumstance. Empirical results show that our method improves geometric preservation by over 90% in isometry evaluation compared to state-of-the-art manifold learning approaches. Furthermore, experiments on downstream task, such as intelligent aerodynamic prediction, confirm the practical effectiveness of the learned Riemannian metrics, enabling faithful and minimally distorted latent representations.

title: Heterogeneous Spatial Temporal Graph Neural Network for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Yue Zhang, Zhiqiang Li, Zhu Chen, Yimin Qi, Ning Gui
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multivariate time series (MVTS) forecasting requires modeling complex spatial, temporal, and cross-dimensional relations. However, existing methods often focus on homogeneous relations, neglecting heterogeneous interactions. To fill this gap, we propose HSTGNN, a Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network that represents MVTS as a heterogeneous graph with distinct node types and both homogeneous and heterogeneous edges. A graph structure learning module refines latent dependencies, while a heterogeneous attention layer integrates relation-specific representations. Experiments on real-world datasets show HSTGNN outperforms 11 state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines and captures meaningful dynamics, achieving strong accuracy and interpretability.

title: CAMA: Character-Aware Masking and Alignment for Self-Supervised STR
authors: Xi Gong, Yang Xue
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Scene Text Recognition (STR) combines visual and linguistic information, yet existing self-supervised methods ignore global language structure due to lack of annotations. We propose CAMA, a novel framework that integrates linguistic awareness into pre-training by synergizing masked image modeling (MIM) and contrastive learning (CL). Our key innovation is character-aware masking: guided by self-supervised segmentation, we mask entire characters instead of random patches, forcing the model to use contextual semantics during reconstruction. A lightweight linguistic alignment branch enforces consistency of [CLS] representations across augmented views, promoting view-invariant text understanding. CAMA achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard and challenging benchmarks, with notable gains (+3.7%) on contextless text, demonstrating effective integration of structural priors without textual labels.

title: Tuning-Free Fidelity-Constrained Decoding for Faithful Legal Reasoning with Open-Domain Large Language Models
authors: Kepu Zhang, Weijie Yu, Jun Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to legal tasks to assist judges and lawyers in decision-making, where outputs must remain faithful to case facts and legal elements to ensure reliable legal interpretations and accurate predictions. Existing approaches—ranging from prompt engineering to task-specific fine-tuning—either demand substantial human effort or lack explicit mechanisms to guarantee such fidelity. To address this issue, we propose Fidelity-Constrained Decoding (FCD), a tuning-free decoding framework that directly constrains generation to preserve strict alignment with both factual details and legal elements. Experiments on three legal datasets with two open-domain LLMs show that FCD consistently improves performance across diverse legal reasoning tasks while avoiding additional training overhead. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/ke-01/FCD.

title: KGER: Knowledge Graph Error Detection and Refinement with Reinforcement Learning
authors: Aishan Maoliniyazi, Chaohong Ma, Xiaofeng Meng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are essential for AI applications. However, pervasive KG errors substantially impact their performance. Existing error correction methods often distinguish detection from refinement, underutilize KG embeddings, and largely depend on scarce labeled data for generalization and scalability. This paper introduces KGER, a novel approach for knowledge graph error detection and refinement with reinforcement learning. KGER frames KG detection and refinement as a unified reinforcement learning process. It employs a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent that integrates KG embeddings and uses a staged training approach. This allows it to learn robust correction policies applicable across varying levels of supervision, from supervised to unsupervised settings. Evaluations on various datasets show that KGER effectively identifies and refines erroneous KG triples, enhances knowledge graph quality, and outperforms existing methods. The code and data are available at https://github.com/kazgu/KGER.

title: Impact of Antenna Polarization on the Frequency Dependent Pathloss Characteristics of Anomalous Reflectors Assisted Indoor Wireless Links
authors: Atli Lemma Gebretsadik, Raju Malleboina, Debdeep Sarkar
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: In this paper, we demonstrate the impact of antenna polarization on the wide-band pathloss characteristics for anomalous reflector (AR)-assisted indoor wireless communication channels. First, we designed the two 2 -bits coded ARs having angles of reflection as 30° and 45° for normal incidence at the operating frequency of 10 GHz. Both the ARs have aperture area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17 \lambda \times 17 \lambda$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda$</tex> is the free-space wavelength at 10 GHz. Next using full-wave simulations, we compute their angular RCS (Radar Cross Section) patterns for plane-wave illumination. Furthermore, we construct an indoor communication link using standard-gain ridged horn antenna as transmitter (Tx) and an inhouse wide-band printed monopole antenna as a receiver (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R x}$</tex>), and measure corresponding pathloss over the frequency range 8-12 GHz by moving the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R x}$</tex> antenna for fixed Tx antenna location. Specifically, we investigate the effect on pathloss for respective Tx-Rx antenna polarization states as VV, VH, HV and HH (here V is vertical and H is horizontal). The measurement campaign showcases: (i) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-20 \text{dB}$</tex> difference between the copolar (VV, HH) and cross-polar (VH, HV) configurations, (ii) significant difference between frequency dependent pathloss for co-polar (VV, HH) configurations. The results of this paper will be useful for realization of RIS-assisted 6G wireless networks.

title: Fed-Game: Personalized Federated Learning with Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts for Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Yi Li, Han Liu, Mingfeng Fan, Guo Chen, Chaojie Li, Biplab Sikdar
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) on graphs shows promise for distributed time-series forecasting. Yet, existing methods rely on static topologies and struggle with client heterogeneity. We propose Fed-GAME, a framework that models personalized aggregation as message passing over a learnable dynamic implicit graph. The core is a decoupled parameter difference-based update protocol, where clients transmit parameter differences between their fine-tuned private model and a shared global model. On the server, these differences are decomposed into two streams: (1) averaged difference used to updating the global model for consensus (2) the selective difference fed into a novel Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME) aggregator for fine-grained personalization. In this aggregator, shared experts provide scoring signals while personalized gates adaptively weight selective updates to support personalized aggregation. Experiments on two real-world electric vehicle charging datasets demonstrate that Fed-GAME outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL baselines.

title: Let More Experts Speak: Balancing Exploration and Exploitation in PEFT for Mixture-of-Experts Models
authors: Yi-Zeng Fang, Juinn-Dar Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models achieve remarkable performance across a wide range of NLP tasks, but their increasing scale makes full finetuning prohibitively expensive. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) offers a lightweight alternative; however, most existing studies focus on dense transformer architectures, leaving its effectiveness on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models largely unexplored. We identify a substantial performance gap between PEFT and full fine-tuning in MoE models, primarily caused by imbalanced expert activation and suboptimal capacity utilization. To bridge this gap, we introduce ExpertTUNE, a two-stage PEFT framework that explicitly balances exploration and exploitation in expert routing. The Warm-up Routing stage encourages broader expert activation, while the Focused Routing stage concentrates on refining the experts. Combined with LoRA, ExpertTUNE improves expert balance and consistently outperforms existing PEFT approaches. Specifically, it delivers gains of up to +3.17% on Switch Transformers-32 and +2.01% on OLMoE. Our results underscore the importance of exploration-aware PEFT strategies for MoE models.

title: MSF-Mamba: Multi-Scale Frequency Mamba For Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
authors: Xuesen Ma, Ran Liu, Yan Qiao, Min Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recently, a new state-space model (SSM) called Mamba has been introduced, and Mamba shows great potential in balancing forecasting performance and computational efficiency. However, when applied to long sequences in the time domain, Mamba struggles to effectively separate periodic patterns from noise, leading to suboptimal feature extraction efficiency. To this end, we propose MSF-Mamba (Multi-Scale Frequency Mamba), which integrates frequency-domain analysis with multi-scale modeling to enhance feature extraction and overcome the limitations of time-domain approaches. Specifically, MSF-Mamba employs dual-path Fast Fourier Transform to decompose time-series data into the frequency-domain, effectively isolating periodic patterns from noise and capturing cross-variable dependencies. Furthermore, the multi-scale architecture of MSF-Mamba enables simultaneous modeling of both global and local temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on seven real-world datasets demonstrate that our MSF-Mamba model achieves superior performance in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: Auxiliary Tasks Benefit Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: Yuheng Yang, Quanyi Yu, Na Ta, Jing Hong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Skin lesion segmentation is a compelling and fundamental problem in artificial intelligence. The task is inherently challenging due to noise and occlusion that frequently occur in dermoscopic images. Current state-of-the-art methods typically learn pathological structures directly from these corrupted images, leading to unsatisfactory segmentation results. To address this issue, we propose a novel learning framework that incorporates auxiliary tasks into the image modeling network. Specifically, the auxiliary tasks corrupt the input images by adding noise and mask, followed by requiring the network to recover these corrupted images, facilitating more robust boundary features learning and being adaptive to incomplete lesion morphology. Additionally, we mathematically derive an information-theoretic loss function to supervise the auxiliary tasks, ensuring feature consistency and spatial alignment between the recovered and the original images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC2017 and the ISIC2018 datasets.

title: Identifying Common Backbones of Interactions Underlying Food Webs via Non-Deterministic Alignments
authors: Yifan Xu, Carlos Taveras, Lydia Beaudrot, César A. Uribe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Climate change reshapes food webs by altering species distributions and interactions, making it essential to identify structural backbones of interactions that persist across ecosystems. Deterministic alignment methods are computationally slow and restricted to one-to-one correspondences. We introduce a scalable, non-deterministic alignment framework inspired by optimal transport that captures overlapping species roles via many-to-many mappings. Framed via motif-role profiles as a Gromov–Wasserstein transport problem, our method is both efficient and interpretable. We apply the proposed method to a large continental-scale data set of 129 mammal food webs in Sub-Saharan Africa. Pairwise alignments are identified, and we uncover robust backbones with greater connectivity and transitivity than those expected under null models. The proposed approach provides a formal, reproducible tool for forecasting ecosystem reorganization and conservation efforts.

title: Fake Image Detection on Noise Residual Spectra Via Random-Feature Single-Layer Neural Networks
authors: Emanuele Mele, Angelo Coluccia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Discriminating between real and AI-generated images in the frequency domain (noise residual spectra) is typically addressed via deep neural networks. This paper investigates instead the use of a single-layer neural network with random features, so-called Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), in which the weights between input and hidden layer are randomly initialized and not updated during training. Compared to deep networks, this offers significant advantages in terms of complexity, while maintaining good accuracy. We show that, based on asymptotic formulas from random matrix theory, hyperparameter optimization can be conducted in closed form, so greatly reducing its computational cost. In terms of classification results, our experiments with popular generative AI algorithms show competitive performance. Ablation study confirms that detection based on noise residual spectra are much more accurate compared to pixel-domain detection.

title: Multi-Classifier and Deep Learning Framework for Robust Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: Swathi D R, Kalaiarasan T, Sanjai C, Vinotheni M S, Mariammal K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Credit card fraud remains a major concern in the financial sector, leading to significant financial losses and eroding customer trust. Real-time detection is challenging due to highly imbalanced datasets and constantly changing fraud behaviors. This work presents a unified fraud detection framework that combines supervised learning models such as Random Forest, Stochastic Gradient Descent Classifier, Support Vector Machine and Logistic Regression with unsupervised techniques including Isolation Forest, One-Class Support Vector Machine and Density-Based Spatial Clustering for Applications with Noise to identify both known and previously unseen fraud patterns. Reinforcement learning methods such as Q-Learning and Deep Q-Learning enable adaptive detection strategies that improve over time, while deep learning architectures such as Multilayer Perceptron and Long Short-Term Memory networks capture complex sequential and nonlinear transaction patterns. A key contribution is the extraction and conversion of model parameters into FPGA compatible formats using Vitis High-Level Synthesis, enabling real-time hardware-accelerated inference with significantly reduced latency. Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets demonstrates superior accuracy, recall, F1-score and ROC-AUC with lower false-positive rates compared to individual models, making the proposed framework scalable, adaptive and effective for real-time credit card fraud detection..

title: Noisy Soft Syndrome Decoding of Quantum LDPC Codes for the Quantum Erasure Channel
authors: Gayathri R., Shobhit Bhatnagar, P Vijay Kumar
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: Erasure decoding of quantum low-density paritycheck (QLDPC) codes has recently received a lot of interest as qubit erasures occur in many physical systems such as neutralatom systems, trapped-ion systems etc. However, decoding of QLDPC codes heavily relies on syndrome information, which may be unreliable as syndrome extraction circuits are prone to errors in practice. While syndrome errors are usually modeled as flipping of a binary syndrome bit, typically, measurement outcomes yield analog voltage/current values, which are then discretized to obtain the binary syndrome. Prior work on correcting qubit errors in the presence of syndrome noise shows that this soft (i.e., analog) information can be exploited to achieve performance improvement. In this paper, we consider the case of qubit erasures and present an iterative decoding algorithm, based on the hardware-friendly min-sum algorithm, that incorporates this soft information to perform check node updates. We show via simulations on hypergraph product codes and lifted product codes that our decoder achieves performance very close to that in the case of noiseless syndrome for a certain noise regime.

title: KinGuard: Hierarchical Kinship-aware Fingerprinting to Defend Against Large Language Model Stealing
authors: Zhenhua Xu, Xiaoning Tian, Wenjun Zeng, Wenpeng Xing, Tianliang Lu, Gaolei Li, Chaochao Chen, Meng Han
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Protecting the intellectual property of large language models requires robust ownership verification. Conventional backdoor fingerprinting, however, is flawed by a stealth-robustness paradox: to be robust, these methods force models to memorize fixed responses to high-perplexity triggers, but this targeted overfitting creates detectable statistical artifacts. We resolve this paradox with KinGuard, a framework that embeds a private knowledge corpus built on structured kinship narratives. Instead of memorizing superficial triggers, the model internalizes this knowledge via incremental pre-training, and ownership is verified by probing its conceptual understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate KinGuard’s superior effectiveness, stealth, and resilience against a battery of attacks including fine-tuning, input perturbation, and model merging. Our work establishes knowledge-based embedding as a practical and secure paradigm for model fingerprinting.

title: Implementation of the Quine-McCluskey Algorithm in LabVIEW for Minimization of Logical Functions
authors: Ivanka Krasteva, Vladimira Ganchovska, Borislav Milenkov
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: This article proposes an automated approach for the minimization of logical functions using the QuineMcCluskey method. A graphical user interface (GUI) has been developed based on the LabVIEW programming environment. The primary objective of this development is to increase processing speed and to visualize all intermediate stages involved in solving such problems. This tool can be especially useful for students and educators by providing clear visualization of the individual steps of the Quine-McCluskey algorithm.

title: MAG: Multi-Modal Aligned Autoregressive Co-Speech Gesture Generation Without Vector Quantization
authors: Binjie Liu, Lina Liu, Sanyi Zhang, Songen Gu, Yihao Zhi, Tianyi Zhu, Lei Yang, Long Ye
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This work addresses full-body co-speech gesture generation, where prior methods often rely on autoregressive models with vector-quantized tokens, causing information loss and reducing realism. To overcome this, we propose MAG, a multi-modal framework that avoids discrete tokenization and preserves the natural continuity of human motion. MAG consists of two main components: (1) the Motion-Text-Audio-Aligned Variational Autoencoder (MTA-VAE), which leverages pre-trained WavCaps text and audio embeddings to enhance semantic and rhythmic alignment, producing more realistic and contextually appropriate gestures; (2) the Multi-modal Masked Autoregressive Gesture generation model (MMAG), which applies diffusion in continuous motion embedding space to eliminate vector quantization, and incorporates a hybrid granularity audio-text fusion block for strong multi-modal consistency. (3) MAG achieves state-of-the-art performance on two benchmark datasets, generating realistic, diverse, and well-synchronized gestures.

title: Selective Hub Fusion with Modality-Heterogeneous Experts for Multimodal Emotion Recognition
authors: Huan Zhao, Ling Xiong, Kehan Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Humans typically process and integrate multimodal information from vision, audio, text, and other sources to perceive and identify emotions. Existing multimodal models primarily rely on multi-modal data fusion, leveraging complementarity between modalities to enhance classification performance. However, when leveraging this complementarity, the acoustic and visual streams in video often carry dense, fine-grained content with substantial noise. Furthermore, these approaches neglect the differential contributions of each modality to emotion recognition outcomes. To address these issues, we proposes a Selective Hub Fusion with Multimodal Heterogeneous Mixture of Experts (SH-MHMoE) for multimodal emotion recognition, achieving intermodal complementarity. The Selective Hub–Mediated Fusion module imposes path constraints that curb cross-modal propagation of redundant information, thereby mitigating noise. A subsequent multimodal heterogeneous MoE enhances the emotion-specific expressiveness of each unimodal representation. Experimental results on the publicly available multimodal sentiment analysis datasets CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI demonstrate that the SH-MHMoE model outperforms most existing methods.

title: Online Test-Time Adaptation for Shadow Segmentation
authors: Di Wu, Haifeng Xia, Libo Sun, Wenhu Qin, Siyu Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Shadow segmentation is sensitive to illumination shifts and scene diversity. Test-time adaptation provides a source-free solution, yet most methods assume large batches or stationary distributions, limiting online use. We present an online test-time adaptation framework for shadow segmentation, where data arrive sequentially in small batches without revisiting. Our method applies dual-stage filtering: image-level domain scoring with a frozen supervisor, followed by pixel-level soft masking guided by confidence, consistency, and structural cues. A brightness-guided module exploits illumination priors to refine shadow regions, while curriculum-based thresholding and exponential moving average teacher–student updates stabilize adaptation and reduce error accumulation. Experiments across six cross-domain settings on SBU, CUHK-Shadow, and ISTD demonstrate consistent gains over baselines in accuracy and boundary preservation, achieving robust generalization under diverse shifts.

title: Development and Validation of High-Accuracy Models for Low-Voltage Distribution Networks
authors: Dražen Jovanović, Stevan Rakočević, Martin Ćalasan, Mihailo Micev
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: This paper presents a methodology for developing high-accuracy models of low-voltage (LV) distribution networks with integrated photovoltaic (PV) systems. The modelling approach incorporates detailed cable parameters, unbalanced network topology, and realistic inverter behavior to reflect real operating conditions. The developed unbalanced <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L V$</tex> network model was verified using two industry-standard simulation tools: DIgSILENT PowerFactory 2021 and ETAP 16.0. Two representative operating points were analyzed: a midday snapshot (12:00) with high PV generation and low residential demand, and an evening snapshot (21:00) with zero PV output and peak residential loading. These conditions capture the daily transition from passive to active <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{L V}$</tex> network behavior, including bidirectional power flows and significant voltage and loading variations. The results demonstrate the suitability of the proposed modelling procedure for analyzing LV networks with high PV penetration and support its application in future distribution-system studies.

title: Prototype-Guided Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning for Continual Audio-Visual Sound Separation
authors: Wanrong Ma, Hongyu Wen, Zijian Gao, Qisheng Xu, Kele Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Continual audio-visual sound separation aims to progressively isolate sound sources from mixtures using visual cues, adapting to new categories without degrading performance on previous ones. This task is challenged by catastrophic forgetting, where new learning overwrites prior knowledge, and cross-modal interference, where features from different categories or modalities entangle in the representation space, reducing discriminability. In this paper, we propose Prototype-guided Cross-modal Contrastive Learning (PGCCL) to address these issues. PGCCL builds category-level prototypes as stable anchors to structure the multimodal representation space, enhancing inter-class separability and intra-class consistency, while an Exponential Moving Average mechanism mitigates feature drift. Experiments show that PGCCL achieves superior adaptation to novel categories while preserving earlier performance, providing a robust and scalable solution for continual multimodal learning.

title: A Light-Weight PRNU-Based Camera-Device Authentication Based on Device-Specific Image Downsampling
authors: Kotaro Naruse, Nagisa Nishimura, Hikari Sasaki, Yoshihiro Midoh, Jun Shiomi, Yuichi Tanaka, Minoru Kuribayashi, Noriyuki Miura
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a light-weight camera-device authentication scheme based on Photo Response Non-Uniformity (PRNU), defined as a unique sensor fingerprint caused by manufacturing imperfections. In the proposed method, a step-wise downsampling with PRNU-based weighted averaging is performed to preserve the device-specific nature of PRNU even in low-resolution images. This light-weight security framework is highly suitable for resource-constrained distributed sensing systems, such as continuous infrastructure monitoring where the network cameras must operate over unprotected open fields under limited energy source and communication bandwidth constraints. In this work, as a proof of concept, the proposed framework was demonstrated in the continuous monitoring system built with > 100 network camera devices. The proposed PRNU-preserving downsampling successfully maintained average F1 scores of > 0.98 even with 1/16 downsampled images. The proposed method significantly saves required energy and is beneficial for scalable physical-attack resilient security, essential for practical use of infrastructure monitoring systems.

title: AgentCite: Trustworthy AI Agents for Information Verification Across Referenced Documents
authors: Eymen Yigit, Ramazan Aygun
publish: 2025 Conference on AI x Software Engineering (AIxSE)
abstract: This paper presents AgentCite, an Agentic AI system designed to autonomously verify information found in documents against their sources. Our work leverages OpenAI’s GPT-4 model and Agents SDK to build the framework. The AgentCite system involves three key components: a Negotiator agent to facilitate communication between parties, a Main Document Agent to access a chosen document, and a Source Documents Agent to access sources referenced in the main document. The workflow is orchestrated through the following steps. First, the system retrieves information from the main document based on a user’s request. Next, it retrieves the same information from the source and analyzes the context. This step is repeated if multiple references are found. Finally, the system concludes whether the information can be verified against the sources and returns its findings to the user. In this paper, we present a case study on a simple document, provide solutions, and discuss the challenges of verification using Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: Cross-Modality Self-Supervision for Unimodal Physiological Signals Representation Learning
authors: Xiaomao Fan, Shuangdu Li, Jikui Liu, Jinzhou Cao, Jingyan Jiang, Yumeng Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning methods for physiological signals offer significant promise for medical diagnosis, health monitoring, and treatment guidance. However, their development is hampered by the reliance on large, high-quality annotated datasets. Self-supervised learning (SSL) provides a viable path forward by learning representations from unannotated data. Although existing SSL methods using token vocabularies have advanced representation learning for unannotated physiological data, their reliance on a single signal modality restricts the generality and performance of the resulting models. To overcome this limitation, we present a Cross-Modal Self-Supervision (CM-SSL) Learning framework designed to learn robust representations from single-modality physiological signals. This work specifically focuses on the prevalent modalities of Electrocardiogram (ECG) and Photoplethysmogram (PPG). The core advantage of CM-SSL is its construction of a shared token vocabulary from cross-modal signals, which enhances the quality and generality of the learned unimodal representations. Extensive evaluation across a diverse suite of downstream tasks demonstrates that the CM-SSL based unimodal model achieves superior performance over baseline models. The source code can be available at URL(https://github.com/ximosss/CM-SSL.git).

title: A Design of Intelligent Video Surveillance System for Deep-Face Detection and Emotion Analysis Using Artificial Intelligence Based Anu-Net
authors: R. Sivakumar, Vishnu Kumar V C, Mohamed ArsathFarves K A, Praveen V T, Surendar E
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: An Intelligent Video Surveillance System (IVSS) is AI- and computer-vision-based monitoring system that analyses live video streams, detects security breaches, recognizes faces, and identifies threats without human intervention. Though, privacy issues are raised due to the uninterrupted monitoring and storage of sensitive data. The paper introduces an IVSDFE model fusing facial analysis, behavior monitoring, and weapon detection for on-the-spot security. The model is tailored to various scenarios by training on such datasets as UCF101, AVA, VGGFace2, CASIA-Web Face, and FIRE. It first pre-processes the data using deep convolutional detectors, then performs detailed facial analysis with the Deep Face framework, and finally puts more emphasis on behavior recognition by features from UCF101 and AVA. It detects weapons and the Attention U-Net++ framework is used to locate the most important visual areas and speed up learning. The Monarch Butterfly Optimization (MBO) algorithm is used for parameter tuning. Accuracy of the system is measured by employing visualization methods like heat maps and detection-frequency plots.

title: Turn the Black-Box White: Inferring Aggregation Rules in Federated Learning Through Multi-Trigger Geometry-Aware Backdoors
authors: Yang Li, Adams Wai Kin Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing federated learning attacks either align with benign updates to stay hidden or assume a fixed server aggregation rule. In black-box settings, where the server configuration is unknown, such attacks often fail due to mismatched assumptions. We show that black-box conditions do not inherently ensure security: the aggregation rule leaves discernible preferences that can be systematically revealed. To this end, we propose Backdoor Cartography, a novel attack framework that infers the server’s aggregation behavior by injecting geometry-aware, multi-trigger backdoors with distinct trigger shapes and target labels into client updates. By observing which backdoors persist after aggregation, the attacker can infer the server’s preference for update norm and direction. With this knowledge, future attacks can be tailored to exploit the revealed preference. Experiments show that Backdoor Cartography reveals aggregation preferences and highlights the need for adaptive defenses.

title: Active Sensing Based Beam Alignment for Backscatter Communication
authors: Xiyu Wang, Xiao Cai, Hei Victor Cheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper addresses the beam alignment problem for mono-static full-duplex backscatter communication systems. This is crucial for an access point (AP) to acquire data from a backscatter device (BD) without either information on the BD location or channel state information. Conventional beam alignment methods, including beam sweeping and channel estimation, require long preambles to achieve acceptable performance. To address this challenge, this work proposes an active sensing based method in which the AP actively interacts with the environment and uses historical observations collected thus far to adaptively adjust the beamforming vectors, thereby maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio (SINR) of the BD signal. Specifically, a long-short-term memory (LSTM) network is exploited to capture correlations over different observations, and deep neural networks are employed to map the hidden state to beamforming vectors. Simulation results demonstrate that the active sensing based method effectively optimizes the beamforming vectors that maximize SINR with only a few interactions with the environment, thus facilitating subsequent BD data transmission.

title: CodecSlime: Temporal Redundancy Compression of Neural Speech Codec via Dynamic Frame Rate
authors: Hankun Wang, Yiwei Guo, Chongtian Shao, Bohan Li, Kai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current mainstream neural speech codecs are fixed-frame-rate (FFR). However, speech is inherently non-uniform in temporal information density. As a result, many tokens are wasted on steady-state segments like long vowels and silences. To address this mismatch, we present CodecSlime, a plugin-style method for compressing temporal redundancy through supporting dynamic frame rate (DFR) on neural speech codecs. Our method is unsupervised and architecture-agnostic, combining two key innovations, ScheDFR and Melt-and-Cool, for adapting inference and training, respectively. When integrated into a typical VQ-GAN codec backbone and operating at 40 Hz DFR (≈600 bps), the reconstruction WER of CodecSlime is reduced by up to 32% relative to conventional FFR baselines with the same model architecture and similar bitrates, while other metrics are also competitive. CodecSlime also enables flexible trade-offs between reconstruction quality and bitrate: a single model supports inference at multiple frame rates and consistently outperforms FFR models at the corresponding frame rates. Audio samples and more results are available at https://x-lance.github.io/codecslime/.

title: LightCSeg: Lightweight Crack Segmentation Network With Adaptive Sobel And Local Enhancement
authors: Zilong Zhao, Chenyu Zhang, Zhengming Ding, Feng Guo, Pei Niu, Zhicheng Lin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Real-time crack segmentation is a key technology for infrastructure health monitoring. Existing methods fail to address the challenges of lightweight design and practical applicability. To tackle this challenge, we propose a lightweight real-time crack segmentation network, LightCSeg. First, we designed the Sobel-Guided Adaptive Edge Aware (SAE) module, which fully leverages the edge feature extraction capability of the Sobel operator and the adaptive capacity of convolutional layers. Through a simple yet effective design, it significantly enhances the response intensity of crack textures. Second, we designed the Local Enhanced Multi-Scale Fusion (LEF) module, which employs bilinear interpolation for upsampling while leveraging the advantages of neighborhood attention for local modeling, ensuring high-quality feature fusion. Experiments on benchmark datasets for crack segmentation demonstrate that our method achieves SOTA-comparable performance while significantly reducing both parameters and FLOPs.

title: Penguin: General Vital Sign Reconstruction from PPG with Flow Matching State Space Model
authors: Shuntaro Suzuki, Shuitsu Koyama, Shinnosuke Hirano, Shunya Nagashima
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Photoplethysmography (PPG) plays a crucial role in continuous cardiovascular health monitoring as a non-invasive and cost-effective modality. However, PPG signals are susceptible to motion artifacts and noise, making accurate estimation of vital signs such as arterial blood pressure (ABP) challenging. Existing estimation methods are often restricted to a single-task or environment, limiting their generalizability across diverse PPG decoding scenarios. Moreover, recent general-purpose approaches typically rely on predictions over multi-second intervals, discarding the morphological characteristics of vital signs. To address these challenges, we propose PENGUIN, a generative flow-matching framework that extends deep state space models, enabling fine-grained conditioning on PPG for reconstructing multiple vital signs as continuous waveforms. We evaluate PENGUIN using six real-world PPG datasets across three distinct vital sign reconstruction tasks (electrocardiogram reconstruction, respiratory monitoring, and ABP monitoring). Our method consistently outperformed both task-specific and general-purpose baselines, demonstrating PENGUIN as a general framework for robust vital sign reconstruction from PPG.

title: Rochester Institute of Technology’s Open Community eXperiment (ROCX) 2025
authors: John P. Kerekes, Nina G. Raqueno, Dean N. Riley
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Graph Distribution-Valued Signals: A Wasserstein Space Perspective
authors: Yanan Zhao, Feng Ji, Xingchao Jian, Wee Peng Tay
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We introduce a novel framework for graph signal processing (GSP) that models signals as graph distribution-valued signals (GDSs), which are probability distributions in the Wasserstein space. This approach overcomes key limitations of classical vector-based GSP, including the assumption of synchronous observations over vertices, the inability to capture uncertainty, and the requirement for strict correspondence in graph filtering. By representing signals as distributions, GDSs naturally encode uncertainty and stochasticity, while strictly generalizing traditional graph signals. We establish a systematic dictionary mapping core GSP concepts to their GDS counterparts, demonstrating that classical definitions are recovered as special cases. The effectiveness of the framework is validated through graph filter learning for prediction tasks, supported by experimental results.

title: Virtual Consistency for Audio Editing
authors: Matthieu Cervera, Francesco Paissan, Mirco Ravanelli, Cem Subakan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Free-form, text-based audio editing remains a persistent challenge, despite progress in inversion-based neural methods. Current approaches rely on slow inversion procedures, limiting their practicality. We present a virtual-consistency based audio editing system that bypasses inversion by adapting the sampling process of diffusion models. Our pipeline is model-agnostic, requiring no fine-tuning or architectural changes, and achieves substantial speed-ups over recent neural editing baselines. Crucially, it achieves this efficiency without compromising quality, as demonstrated by quantitative benchmarks and a user study involving 16 participants.

title: ASRC-SNN: Adaptive Skip Recurrent Connection Spiking Neural Network
authors: Shang Xu, Jiayu Zhang, Ziming Wang, Runhao Jiang, Rui Yan, Huajin Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In recent years, Recurrent Spiking Neural Networks (RSNNs) have shown promising potential in long-term temporal modeling. Many studies focus on improving neuron models and also integrate recurrent structures, leveraging their synergistic effects to improve the long-term temporal modeling capabilities of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). However, these studies often place an excessive emphasis on the role of neurons, overlooking the importance of analyzing neurons and recurrent structures as an integrated framework. In this work, we consider neurons and recurrent structures as an integrated system and conduct a systematic analysis of gradient propagation along the temporal dimension, revealing a challenging gradient vanishing problem. To address this issue, we propose the Skip Recurrent Connection (SRC) as a replacement for the vanilla recurrent structure, effectively mitigating the gradient vanishing problem and enhancing long-term temporal modeling performance. Additionally, we propose the Adaptive Skip Recurrent Connection (ASRC), a method that can learn the skip span of skip recurrent connection in each layer of the network. Experiments show that replacing the vanilla recurrent structure in RSNN with SRC significantly improves the model’s performance on temporal benchmark datasets. Moreover, ASRC-SNN outperforms SRC-SNN in terms of temporal modeling capabilities and robustness.

title: Phase Consistency Enhanced Complex-Valued Neural Network for Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification
authors: Baihe Chen, Jing Xiao, Zeqi Shao, Wenrui Ding, Yufeng Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification (RFFI) is promising for device authentication in IoT, wireless communications, and defense, yet most deep learning methods rely on real-valued networks that underuse phase information. To address this, we propose PCE-CVNN, a complex-valued framework that enforces relative phase consistency to mitigate global phase shifts, leverages complex supervised contrastive learning to transfer discriminative phase features from high-SNR to low-SNR samples, and incorporates a complex confidence decision module to refine classification boundaries. Experiments on 32 ZigBee devices across SNRs from -20 dB to 20 dB show that it achieves 94.19% overall accuracy, maintaining 71.06% at -20 dB, demonstrating the robustness of explicit phase-aware modeling for emitter identification.

title: Universal Denoising Patterns for Diffusion Image Detection
authors: Yurui Qian, Qi Cai, Yingwei Pan, Ting Yao, Yan Chen, Tao Mei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The proliferation of photorealistic, AI-generated images threatens public trust, creating an urgent need for detectors that can reliably distinguish them from real content. However, existing detectors, typically trained on limited known generators, struggle to generalize to unseen ones. The rapid evolution of diffusion models has intensified this problem, creating a "diffusion fog" that limits practical applicability of existing detectors. To pierce this fog, we posit that the inherent patterns in the denoising process of all diffusion models are key to generalizable detection. To isolate these patterns, we create minimal fake training images by applying a single-step denoising operation to slightly perturb real images, forcing the detector to learn the subtle artifacts of denoising. We then blend these minimal fakes with their real counterparts, erasing superficial cues from the perturbation operation to prevent detector from learning shortcuts. Considering that the fake images closely resemble real ones, we propose a feature separation loss to enhance detector’s discrimination capacity. For rigorous evaluation, we constructed a new benchmark, DiffuGen, comprising 65K synthetic images from 13 modern diffusion models and 5K real images. Empirical results demonstrate that our detector achieves significantly improved generalization, reaching an average accuracy of 99% on DiffuGen.

title: Faclip : Learnable Fine-Grained Prompts and Multi-Scale Fusion for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection
authors: Shixin Zhao, Sheng Liu, Yuan Feng, Yiheng Yu, Zhelun Jin, Min Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection (ZSAD) enables detecting anomalies without defective samples and has gained increasing attention, current methods based on Vision-Language Models (VLM) like CLIP demonstrates limited sensitivity to fine-grained anomalies, and its prompt templates exhibit constraints when dealing with specific anomaly types. To address these challenges, we propose a novel ZSAD framework called FACLIP, it eliminates the need for fixed text prompts by incorporating learnable anomaly prompts generated with the help of large language models, and it employs a cross-modal multi-scale attention fusion module to integrate multi-scale local visual features across modalities. A local differences filter module is introduced to integrate local visual discrepancies extracted from the original image into the anomaly map. Furthermore, a global-adapter aligns global visual features with text information. FACLIP achieves advanced performance across multiple datasets and demonstrates superior ZSAD ability compared to other approaches.

title: Inverse Halftoning via Weighted Sobel Conditioned Diffusion Model
authors: Shengshang Shen, Jiefeng Yao, Denghui Zhang, Keke Tang, Dawei Zhao, Zhaoquan Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Halftoning is widely used in image compression and encryption for its efficiency and fidelity. However, existing halftoning algorithms often lose color and fine detail when dithering images with binary dots, making the restoration of original images challenging. This paper proposes DiffSo, a weighted Sobel-conditioned diffusion model for high-fidelity inverse halftoning. Unlike traditional noise-focused diffusion models that emphasize diversity, our method employs a deterministic residual diffusion process with dual forward paths, thus enabling fast sampling. The weighted operator provides strong structural cues from highly degraded halftone patterns, enabling accurate detail recovery. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate that DiffSo outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and perceptual quality, exhibiting remarkable generalization across various halftoning techniques and ill-posed conditions.

title: Transferable Audio Lottery Tickets: Gradient Accumulation for Extreme Sparsity
authors: Hyunjae Kim, Juhan Nam, Kyung Myun Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large-scale neural networks have achieved impressive performance across diverse audio domains, but their growing size raises the need for lightweight alternatives. The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH) offers a compelling direction by revealing sparse subnetworks that can match full network performance, yet its potential across various audio subdomains remains underexplored. In this work, we examine this potential by evaluating sparse subnetworks on diverse audio classification tasks, spanning speech, music, and environmental sound. In particular, we propose a simple modification to the training process that incorporates momentum-like gradient accumulation during subnetwork search. We show that this strategy enables finding extremely sparse subnetworks with less than 1.0% of the initial parameters remaining, while still retaining up to 90% of dense model performance without layer collapse even under severe unstructured pruning. Furthermore, these sub-networks were effectively transferred across different audio subdomains while sustaining their sparsity-robust characteristics.

title: Acoustic Feedback Cancellation in Hearing Aids Exploiting an Inertial Sensor
authors: Sina Miran, Henning Schepker, Ivo Merks, Martin McKinney
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Acoustic feedback is a fundamental problem in hearing aids limiting the maximum applicable gain and causing artifacts such as whistling and howling. Adaptive feedback cancellation is one solution to increase the maximum applicable gain and reduce artifacts, where different adaptive filtering solutions can be used, e.g., the NLMS algorithm. Real-time adjustment of the adaptation speed, i.e., the step size, in these filters is crucial in obtaining both a small steady-state error and rapid convergence under significant feedback path changes. Existing step-size adjustment methods only operate on the audio signals in the hearing aid, and hence, are heavily susceptible to biases caused by characteristics of input audio. In this work, we propose to modify the adaptation speed based on head movement acceleration measured by an inertial measurement unit (IMU) in the hearing aid. Experimental results show that feedback path changes can be detected reliably from inertial measurements, and our approach outperforms audio-based adjustment methods in particular under steady-state conditions. Thus, our study encourages a multi-modal approach to feedback cancellation in hearing aids combining both inertial and audio signals.

title: SSG-DIT: A Spatial Signal Guided Framework for Controllable Video Generation
authors: Peng Hu, Yu Gu, Liang Luo, Fuji Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Controllable video generation synthesizes content aligning with user conditions like text and images. However, existing models struggle with semantic consistency, as they focus on general text-to-video alignment while overlooking spatial information in prompts, causing semantic-spatial misalignment where objects exist but lack proper positions, scales, and interactions. We propose SSG-DiT (Spatial Signal Guided Diffusion Transformer), an efficient framework for high-fidelity controllable video generation. Our two-stage approach uses Spatial Signal Prompting to generate spatially aware visual prompts via pre-trained CLIP. Combined with original text, these form joint conditions injected into frozen DiT through our lightweight SSG-Adapter. This dual-branch attention design enables the model to harness generative priors while being steered by external spatial signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate SSG-DiT achieves state-of-the-art performance on VBench, particularly excelling in spatial relationship control and consistency, outperforming existing models across multiple key metrics.

title: VChangeCodec: An Ultra Low-Complexity Neural Speech Codec with Built-In Voice Changer for Customized Real-Time Communication
authors: Xusheng Yang, Wei Xiao, Bang Yang, Shidong Shang, Yuexian Zou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present VChangeCodec, an ultra low-complexity neural speech codec with built-in timbre adaptation capabilities designed for customized real-time communication (RTC). Unlike cascaded pipelines that combine neural speech codecs (NSCs) with separate voice conversion (VC) systems, which have high latency, VChangeCodec integrates VC into the NSC itself and can seamlessly switch between original and voice change modes. Specifically, we design the NSC by proposing a fully causal convolutional network with scalar quantization to generate compact tokens. In addition, we introduce a lightweight causal projection network that adapts the tokens with target speaker embeddings for decoding into the converted voice, enabling seamless timbre conversion without the need for an additional VC system. Experiments show that, compared to the state-of-the-art speech codec, VChangeCodec reduces parameters by 96.3%, while maintaining competitive speech quality. More importantly, while the cascaded pipeline suffers from more than 100 ms latency, VChangeCodec achieves integrated timbre customization with only 40 ms, making it well-suited for RTC scenarios.

title: DDSR-Net: Robust Multimodal Sentiment Analysis via Dynamic Modality Reliability Assessment
authors: Jianwen Hou, Enguang Zuo, Chaorui Shi, Kurban Ubul
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) aims to understand human emotions by integrating text, vision, and audio. In real-world scenarios, unaligned data exhibit dynamic and uneven modality quality, posing challenges for robust feature learning and fusion. Existing methods rely on static fusion or feature disentanglement, but generally cannot assess modality reliability at the sample level. To address this, we propose DDSR-Net, a framework for Dynamic-quality guided Disentanglement and Selective Reconstruction Network. DDSR-Net first evaluates modality quality per sample, then disentangles features into shared and specific components, and performs Cross-Modal Synergistic Repair, where reliable modalities enhance low-quality ones.
