Finally, a dynamic focusing attention mechanism fuses enhanced features adaptively. This evaluate–repair–focus pipeline allows robust handling of noise and asymmetry in unaligned multimodal data. Experiments on MOSI and MOSEI show that DDSR-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness and robustness.

title: A Comprehensive Study Comparing Alternative Ranking Methods and Criteria Weighting Techniques: A Case Study in Sewing Machine Ranking
authors: Dinh Mai Huong, Branislav Dudić, Nguyen Thi Tham
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Ranking alternative solutions when multiple criteria, which characterize each solution, must be considered to identify the optimal choice is a necessary task across all various domains. This process is known as Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM). This study conducts a comparison between two groups of methods within the MCDM framework: alternative ranking techniques and criteria weighting techniques. Four alternative ranking methods were investigated in this research: Simple Additive Weighting (SAW), Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), MultiObjective Optimization by Ratio Analysis (MOORA), and Preference Index Value (PIV). Five criteria weighting methods were examined: Rank Sum (RS) method, Rank Reciprocal (RR) method, Rank Order Centroid (ROC) method, a method calculating the arithmetic mean of the three methods (RS, RR, ROC) termed the AVER method, and a method calculating the relative weight of the three methods (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{RS}, \text{RR}, \text{ROC}$</tex>) termed the PRO method. The comparison was carried out through the task of ranking eight types of sewing machines. The criterion used to perform these comparisons was the degree of stability in the ranking of alternatives under varying scenarios. This research identified MOORA as the method exhibiting the best performance among the four ranking techniques explored, and RS as the method with the highest efficacy among the five weighting techniques assessed.

title: FTIN: Frequency-Time Integration Network for Inertial Odometry
authors: Shanshan Zhang, Qi Zhang, Siyue Wang, Liqin Wu, Tianshui Wen, Ziheng Zhou, Ao Peng, Xuemin Hong, Lingxiang Zheng, Yu Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Inertial odometry (IO) leverages inertial measurement unit (IMU) signals for cost-effective localization. However, high IMU sampling rates introduce substantial redundancy that impedes IO’s ability to attend to salient components, thereby creating an information bottleneck. To address this challenge, we propose a cross-domain IO framework that fuses information from the frequency and time domains. Specifically, we exploit the global context and energy-compaction properties of frequency-domain representations to capture holistic motion patterns and alleviate the bottleneck. To the best of our knowledge, this is among the first attempts to incorporate frequency-domain feature processing into IO. Experimental results on multiple public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed frequency–time-domain fusion strategy.

title: LAKALMANTracker: Robust Learning-Aided Kalman Filtering for Multi-Object Tracking
authors: Jian Song, Wei Mei, Yunfeng Xu, Qiang Fu, Renke Kou, Lina Bu, Yucheng Long
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Kalman filter (KF)-based multi-object tracking methods assume linear object motion; however, they fall short in accurately predicting nonlinear object motion due to model mismatch. To address this limitation, we explore data-driven motion estimators and thus propose Patching-KalmanNet (PKNet), inspired by KalmanNet, which learns the Kalman gain (KG) via deep neural networks. PKNet takes multi-step historical state patches as input and leverages two key components for KG learning: variate-independent patch embedding (capturing heterogeneous motion features across different variates) and attention mechanisms (highlighting critical historical information for feature refinement). With a KF-alike structure, it can integrate directly into existing trackers as a replacement for the KF, forming the Learning-Aided Kalman Tracker (LAKalmanTracker). Experiments on SoccerNet, DanceTrack, and other benchmarks with highly nonlinear object motion show that PKNet significantly outperforms both the KF and existing KalmanNets in boosting tracker performance and robustness.

title: Reinforced Active Learning for Change Point Detection
authors: Stefano Bertolasi, Diego Carrera, Diego Stucchi, Pasqualina Fragneto, Luigi Amedeo Bianchi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Change Point Detection algorithms often rely on hyperparameters that are difficult to tune and highly sensitive to the specific characteristics of the time series. Active Learning offers a promising solution for tuning these hyperparameters by minimizing annotation costs through selective querying of the most informative samples. While classical active learning methods rely on heuristic strategies and recent data-driven approaches employ Reinforcement Learning to optimize query policies, their use in Change Point Detection remains underexplored. In this paper, we propose ReAL4CPD, a novel reinforcement learning based active learning algorithm designed to efficiently guide the change point detection in time series. Our approach improves the generalization of query strategies across diverse datasets, outperforming existing uncertainty-based active learning methods. Experimental results validate that ReAL4CPD effectively reduces labeling effort while enhancing change point detection accuracy.

title: Contrastive Timbre Representations for Musical Instrument And Synthesizer Retrieval
authors: Gwendal Le Vaillant, Yannick Molle
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficiently retrieving specific instrument timbres from audio mixtures remains a challenge in digital music production. This paper introduces a contrastive learning framework for musical instrument retrieval, enabling direct querying of instrument databases using a single model for both single- and multi-instrument sounds. We propose techniques to generate realistic positive/negative pairs of sounds for virtual musical instruments, such as samplers and synthesizers, addressing limitations in common audio data augmentation methods.The first experiment focuses on instrument retrieval from a dataset of 3,884 instruments, using single-instrument audio as input. Contrastive approaches are competitive with previous works based on classification pre-training. The second experiment considers multi-instrument retrieval with a mixture of instruments as audio input. In this case, the proposed contrastive framework outperforms related works, achieving 84.2% top-1 and 96.4% top-5 accuracies for three-instrument mixtures.

title: PG-SE: Predictive Acceleration and Correction for Generative Speech Enhancement
authors: Yikai Huang, Jinjiang Liu, Zijian Lin, Xiang Li, Renjie Yu, Zhiyong Wu, Shiyin Kang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong potential for speech enhancement. However, they remain limited by inferior noise suppression compared to predictive approaches and the high computational burden arising from numerous neural function evaluations (NFEs) in reverse sampling. To overcome these challenges, we propose PG-SE, a novel Predictive-Generative integrated framework for speech enhancement. In this framework, predictive models are introduced at both the pre- and post-stages of diffusion inference to play complementary roles: the pre-stage predictive model provides priors to guide and accelerate score-based conditional diffusion sampling, while the post-stage model refines the generated speech by correcting residual errors, thereby improving both perceived quality and intelligibility of the enhanced speech. Experimental results demonstrate that PG-SE reduces inference NFEs by over 80% while delivering superior performance compared to full-step baselines, enabling low-latency deployment without compromising perceptual quality.

title: Large System Analysis of SURE based Hyper- Parameter Optimizing in Sparse Bayesian Learning
authors: Fangqing Xiao, Jichao Chen, Dirk Slock
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper studies stationary points arising from outputdomain Stein’s unbiased risk estimator (SURE) based hyperparameter optimization in sparse Bayesian learning from a large-system perspective. By analyzing the coordinatewise stationary conditions and leveraging tools from random matrix theory, we show that the high-dimensional problem admits a deterministic decoupling, leading to a distributional fixed-point characterization of stationary solutions. This framework reveals that multiple stationary points may naturally coexist. We further provide a local stability interpretation, clarifying which stationary solutions are observable under coordinatewise optimization. The analysis focuses on stationary-point structure rather than specific algorithms or finite-dimensional performance, and provides a principled theoretical understanding of the stationary landscape of SURE-based hyper-parameter learning in sparse Bayesian learning.

title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Correlation Meta-Network for Few-Shot Remote Sensing Image Classification
authors: Anurag Kaushish, Ayan Sar, Sampurna Roy, Sudeshna Chakraborty, Prashant Trivedi, Tanupriya Choudhury, Kanav Gupta
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Few-shot learning in remote sensing remains challenging due to three factors: the scarcity of labeled data, substantial domain shifts, and the multi-scale nature of geospatial objects. To address these issues, we introduce Adaptive Multi-Scale Correlation Meta-Network (AMC-MetaNet), a lightweight yet powerful framework with three key innovations: (i) correlation-guided feature pyramids for capturing scale-invariant patterns, (ii) an adaptive channel correlation module (ACCM) for learning dynamic cross-scale relationships, and (iii) correlation-guided meta-learning that leverages correlation patterns instead of conventional prototype averaging. Unlike prior approaches that rely on heavy pre-trained models or transformers, AMC-MetaNet is trained from scratch with only ~ 600K parameters, offering 20× fewer parameters than ResNet-18 while maintaining high efficiency (< 50ms per image inference). AMC-MetaNet achieves up to 86.65% accuracy in 5-way 5-shot classification on various remote sensing datasets, including EuroSAT, NWPU-RESISC45, UC Merced Land Use, and AID. Our results establish AMC-MetaNet as a computationally efficient, scale-aware framework for real-world few-shot remote sensing.

title: PHPTrigger: Multi-Engine Assisted Vulnerability Auditing and Verification in PHP Applications
authors: Yayuan Zhang, Zhisheng Yang, Jiangyong Shi, Yongjun Wang, Jianzhe Yang, Xi Xuan, Hao Peng, Zhiyuan Jiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the widespread adoption of web applications and IoT firmware, PHP vulnerabilities have become a frequent and high-impact attack vector. Existing PHP vulnerability auditing approaches, which mainly rely on rule-based detection or precise causal modeling, often struggle to balance high recall with practical exploitability verification.To address this challenge, we propose PHPTrigger, an intelligent auditing framework that integrates multi-engine analysis with large language model reasoning. Without requiring complete causal modeling of vulnerabilities, PHPTrigger combines inter-procedural semantic reasoning with cross-modal semantic generation to automatically locate vulnerabilities and generate environment-adaptive PoCs.We implemented a prototype of PHPTrigger and systematically evaluated it on 150 vulnerabilities collected from 7 open-source projects. Experimental results show that, compared with the state-of-the-art tool ZIPPER, PHPTrigger improves recall by 46% and precision by 62%. For vulnerability verification, PHPTrigger achieved 100% executable PoC rate on 147 detected vulnerabilities. Moreover, PHPTrigger discovered 46 0-day vulnerabilities, of which 34 were assigned CVE identifiers. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and practical value of our approach in real-world vulnerability auditing and verification.

title: Graph Hodge-Laplacian Particle Filtering for Communication-Efficient Distributed Tracking
authors: Dayu Qin, Ercan E. Kuruoglu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We revisit communication-efficient distributed particle filtering through a simplicial lens. Our method, GHLA-DPF, encodes the node log-likelihood signals and compresses them in the L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> Hodge Laplacian basis, exchanging only a small set of spectral coefficients via standard gossip algorithm. This keeps the same communication paradigm as graph Laplacian approaches while explicitly suppressing curl on triangular loops and improving spectral locality for potential-like fields. Experiments on bearings-only tracking with circulation bias show consistently better accuracy–communication trade-offs than GLA-DPF and a selective-gossip algorithm baseline, demonstrating the value of edge-aware compression for distributed tracking.

title: Simple Aggregation is Not Enough: Temporal Knowledge Graph Forecasting Via Decentralized Multi-Chain Reasoning
authors: Rikui Huang, Shengzhe Zhang, Xiaoye Qu, Wei Wei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Forecasting (TKGF) aims to predict future events based on historical events. To achieve interpretable reasoning, explicit multi-hop path methods attract increasing attention. However, existing methods focus on generating a single reasoning path, which may be insufficient to support inference. An intuitive yet marginally beneficial approach is to aggregate information from multiple reasoning chains. We find that an excessive focus on a single high-quality reasoning chain results in a centralized distribution of reasoning chains, hindering multi-chain reasoning. To tackle this problem, we propose TimeFlow, a decentralized multi-chain reasoning TKGF framework. Specifically, we propose a target flow expectation maximization to jointly optimize multiple reasoning chains. Moreover, to generate high-quality reasoning chains, we leverage the temporal context of the query as guidance for the path generator. Experimental results demonstrate that TimeFlow effectively generates a decentralized distribution of reasoning chains and significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models.

title: AnyRIR: Robust Non-Intrusive Room Impulse Response Estimation in the Wild
authors: Kyung Yun Lee, Nils Meyer-Kahlen, Karolina Prawda, Vesa Välimäki, Sebastian J. Schlecht
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We address the problem of estimating room impulse responses (RIRs) in noisy, uncontrolled environments where non-stationary sounds such as speech or footsteps corrupt conventional deconvolution. We propose AnyRIR, a non-intrusive method that uses music as the excitation signal instead of a dedicated test signal, and formulate RIR estimation as an ℓ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf>-norm regression in the time–frequency domain. Solved efficiently with Iterative Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) and Least-Squares Minimal Residual (LSMR) methods, this approach exploits the sparsity of non-stationary noise to suppress its influence. Experiments on simulated and measured data show that AnyRIR outperforms ℓ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-based and frequency-domain deconvolution, under in-the-wild noisy scenarios and codec mismatch, enabling robust RIR estimation for AR/VR and related applications.

title: FLAME: Empowering Frozen LLMS for Knowledge Graph Completion
authors: Bo Xue, Yi Xu, Bolei Ma, Yunchong Song, Jiaxin Ding, Luoyi Fu, Xinbing Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Traditional knowledge graph completion (KGC) methods rely solely on structural information and struggle with sparsity, while Large Language Models (LLMs) address these limitations through rich world knowledge and strong context modeling. Fine-tuning LLMs is effective but costly, while non-fine-tuned LLMs are efficient but suboptimal. To address this trade-off, we propose FLAME, a framework that extracts context-aware hidden states from intermediate layers of frozen LLMs to train data-efficient KGC classifiers. We bridge LLM-KG semantic gaps via subgraph-based entity descriptions and employ sliced mutual information (SMI) to quantify task-relevant information in representations. Experiments demonstrate that FLAME achieves 47% improvement over non-fine-tuned LLM baselines and, to our knowledge, is the first to achieve fine-tuned performance with 188× memory efficiency and 26.11× speedup.

title: Dynamic Adaptive Wavelet State Space Model for Efficient Low-Light Image Enhancement
authors: Yinghui Fang, Xiang Chen, Jinshan Pan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement (LLIE) is a fundamental yet challenging task due to the coexistence of severe noise, low visibility, and loss of structural details. Despite recent progress, existing methods still suffer from inefficient feature representation and inadequate texture restoration. In this paper, we propose DAW-Mamba, a wavelet-guided state space model tailored for LLIE. Specifically, we adopt a multi-scale wavelet-based UNet backbone, where the low-frequency state space block is introduced to effectively model global dependencies in low-frequency components via a dynamic adaptive scanning block (DASB). Unlike manually designed scanning strategies, DASB dynamically learns optimal scanning trajectories, enabling more effective feature aggregation. Furthermore, we propose the high-frequency content-aware block, which leverages structural cues from low-frequency components under dual-attention guidance to restore fine textures in high-frequency regions. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves favorable performance against state-of-the-art approaches, while maintaining high computational efficiency.

title: SpeakerRPL V2: Robust Open-Set Speaker Identification Through Enhanced Few-Shot Foundation Tuning and Model Fusion
authors: Zhiyong Chen, Shuhang Wu, Yingjie Duan, Xinkang Xu, Xinhui Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper proposes an improved approach for open-set speaker identification based on pretrained speaker foundation models. Building upon the previous Speaker Reciprocal Points Learning framework (V1), we first introduce an enhanced open-set learning objective by integrating reciprocal points learning with logit normalization (LogitNorm) and incorporating adaptive anchor learning to better constrain target speaker representations and improve robustness. Second, we propose a model fusion strategy to stabilize and enhance the few-shot tuning process, effectively reducing result randomness and improving generalization. Furthermore, we introduce a model selection method to ensure optimal performance in model fusion. Experimental evaluations on the VoxCeleb, ESD and 3D-Speaker datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method under diverse conditions. On a newly proposed Vox1-O–like test set, our method reduces the EER from 1.28% to 0.09%, achieving a relative reduction of approximately 93%.

title: RecSum: Reconstruct Complementary and Consistent Information in Multiplex Graph for Unsupervised Social Summarization
authors: Ruifang He, Lamu Songlang, Tanglong Zhao, Sen Yao, Bo Wang, Xiaohong Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Social summarization aims to obtain concise summaries that describe the core content of a collection of social texts (called posts) on a specific topic. Post content is short, and recent work has alleviated insufficient information in individual posts by introducing multiple relationships among them. However, contacting posts’ representations from different relationships directly can lead to redundancy and overlook the private information inherent in each relationship, which can be mitigated by extracting and fusing common and private information from different relationships. In this paper, we introduce RecSum, an unsupervised model for social summarization. RecSum reconstructs the clean common information of posts, as well as more-complementary and less-noisy private information from multiplex graph to obtain the post representation. It is then applied to a sparse reconstruction extractor that selects the most representative posts as summarization. Experiments on two real-world social media datasets demonstrate that RecSum outperforms other alternatives.

title: Individual Risky Action Warning of Wearable Sensors on Personalized Health Profiles via LLM
authors: Yiyin Xie, Yuhan Jing, Haifeng Sun, Qi Qi, Bo He, Chengsen Wang, Jingyu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current action recognition methods for health monitoring are often generic, failing to provide personalized warnings and struggling to scale to complex risky behaviors. This paper introduces a two-stage personalized risky action warning system to overcome these limitations. In the first stage, a Large Language Model (LLM) processes user medical histories to generate a tailored set of risky actions and decomposes complex behaviors, significantly enhancing recognition scalability. The LLM then formats this knowledge into structured guidance. In the second stage, an action recognition model, guided by the LLM’s output, utilizes wearable sensor data for a comprehensive risk assessment across action category, intensity, and temporal patterns. This enables precise, real-time warnings. Tested on real-world data for 10 different physical conditions, our system achieves an early warning F1-score of over 90%.

title: SF-MVD: Sensor Failure-Aware Multi-Modal Vehicle Detection with Lidar-Radar Fusion in Foggy Weather
authors: Jiake Tian, Yi Zou, Dacheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Autonomous driving perception systems face severe challenges under adverse weather, where cameras and LiDAR often degrade or fail completely. Radar sensors are more robust but produce sparse point clouds with unreliable height estimation, limiting the effectiveness of multimodal fusion. To address this, we propose the Sensor Failure-aware Multi-modal Vehicle Detection framework, termed SF-MVD, which for robust vehicle detection under sensor failures. SF-MVD integrates sensor-failure augmentation with a teacher–student collaborative learning paradigm, where both models share identical architectures and incorporate a radar height query module. This module leverages LiDAR data to compensate for radar height deficiencies, enhancing fusion accuracy. The student model is trained on failure-augmented data under teacher consistency supervision, enabling robust knowledge transfer.. Experiments on the Oxford Radar RobotCar dataset show that the teacher model outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving 95.8%, 94.2%, and 81.5% at intersection over union thresholds of 0.5, 0.65, and 0.8. Even under sensor failures, the student model maintains strong performance (90.0%, 88.2%, and 74.6%).

title: G-SFADA: Gradient-Inspired Source-Free Active Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation
authors: Yibin Wen, Defeng Gu, Juepeng Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Domain adaptation addresses the challenge of transferring a pre-trained model from a source domain to an unannotated target domain. However, most works in semantic segmentation assume that source domain data is accessible, which neglects the potential privacy issues associated with the source images. To address this, we propose G-SFADA, a method that combines source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) and active learning (AL). In SFDA, a teacher network generates pseudo-labels for the target domain, while a student network engages in self-supervised learning through the use of gradient-inspired masked images. AL selectively annotates target images based on adaptability, uncertainty, and diversity to improve the target model’s performance. Our experiments show that G-SFADA achieves an improvement of 5.3% to 13.6% compared to other methods on the GTA5 → Cityscapes task.

title: First Results on RIS-Enabled Multi-Layer Localization: A Joint Terrestrial and Non-Terrestrial Method
authors: Daniel Egea-Roca, Alda Xhafa, José A. López-Salcedo, Gonzalo Seco-Granados
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Positioning with centimeter-level accuracy and robust coverage in non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions is critical for many applications such as autonomous vehicles and industrial IoT. Terrestrial networks (TNs) provide high accuracy, but are limited by coverage gaps, whereas non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) offer global reach with reduced accuracy in urban environments. This paper presents a hybrid TN-NTN localization framework aided by the Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS) called the Multi-Layer Network (MuLa), which employs a single RIS to enhance integration of terrestrial and satellite downlink signals. The results demonstrate substantial accuracy and robustness improvements, underscoring RIS-enabled integration as a viable approach to meet such stringent localization requirements.

title: Mitigating Domain Shift in Ultrasonic wavefield Pattern Analysis Through Test-Time Training
authors: Jiaxing YE, Takumi Kobayashi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This study addresses the critical challenge of domain shift in physics signal analysis of ultrasonic wavefield imaging data for non-destructive testing using neural networks. In real-world, variable environments, measurement data acquired on-site often exhibit statistical discrepancies from training data, leading to severe degradation in inference performance. To mitigate such domain shift at deployment, we propose an efficient framework based on test-time training (TTT) that enables deployed model to adapt to the input data on-the-fly. The primary contributions are threefold: (1) a benchmarking dataset of annotated real ultrasonic wavefield images has been developed to facilitate rigorous evaluation under diverse domain shift settings, including variations in specimen structures, transducer positions, and defect conditions; (2) an enhanced TTT approach is devised to progressively adapt feature representations of deployed models in a self-supervised manner during inference; (3) comprehensive comparisons and ablation studies validate the proposed framework. This research demonstrate that post-deployment learning could be vital for robust neural network-based signal analysis in real applications. The code and dataset can be accessed online<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: CSGAN-VLP: Swin-Transformer Enhanced Gan and Contrastive Alignment for Robust Cross-Scene Passive Visible Light Positioning
authors: Wei Sun, Ziwei Liu, Yimao Sun, Liangyin Chen, Binyu Yan, Yanbing Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Passive visible light positioning (VLP) has attracted significant attention due to its device-free nature and convenience. However, existing methods often struggle to generalize across diverse scenes, where variations in environmental conditions and data distributions hinder practical deployment. To address this challenge, we propose CSGAN-VLP, a robust cross-scene VLP framework that leverages sparse data through generative modeling and feature alignment. Specifically, we employ a Swin-Transformer-based Cycle-GAN to generate realistic cross-scene samples from limited data, effectively capturing distribution shifts while preserving intrinsic spatial correlations. A dilated convolutional autoencoder is then used to extract compact Received Signal Strength representations, while a feature alignment module jointly optimizes generated and original samples to unify domain-specific characteristics. This integration enables the localization network to maintain reliable performance even under sparse cross scenes. Extensive real-world experiments validate the effectiveness of the CSGAN-VLP, achieving an average Root Mean Square Error of 16 cm, which is a fourfold reduction compared to traditional methods.

title: Latent-Space Metrics for Complex-Valued VAE Out-Of-Distribution Detection under Radar Clutter
authors: Y. A. Rouzoumka, E. Terreaux, C. Morisseau, J.-P. Ovarlez, C. Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We investigate complex-valued Variational AutoEncoders (CVAE) for radar Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) detection in complex radar environments. We proposed several detection metrics: the reconstruction error of CVAE (CVAE-MSE), the latent-based scores (Mahalanobis, Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD)), and compared their performance against the classical ANMF-Tyler detector (ANMF-FP). The performance of all these detectors is analyzed on synthetic and experimental radar data, showing the advantages and the weaknesses of each detector.

title: DNS: Data-Driven Nonlinear Smoother for Complex Model-Free Process
authors: Fredrik Cumlin, Anubhab Ghosh, Saikat Chatterjee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose data-driven nonlinear smoother (DNS) to estimate a hidden state sequence of a complex dynamical process from a noisy, linear measurement sequence. The dynamical process is model-free, that is, we do not have any knowledge of the nonlinear dynamics of the complex process. There is no state-transition model (STM) of the process available. The proposed DNS uses a recurrent architecture that helps to provide a closed-form posterior of the hidden state sequence given the measurement sequence. DNS learns in an unsupervised manner, meaning the training dataset consists of only measurement data and no state data. We demonstrate DNS using simulations for smoothing of several stochastic dynamical processes, including a benchmark Lorenz system. Experimental results show that the DNS is significantly better than a deep Kalman smoother (DKS) and an iterative data-driven nonlinear state estimation (iDANSE) smoother.

title: Unsupervised Sentence Stress Detection in L2 Spoken English Via Iterative Adaptation of Whisper ASR Framework
authors: Jhansi Mallela, Abin Roy, Fathima N, Reni K Cherian, Chiranjeevi Yarra
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sentence stress refers to the emphasis placed on specific words within an utterance to convey meaning beyond the literal message. Accurate detection of sentence stress is crucial for spoken language understanding systems, yet existing approaches are limited by the lack of annotated corpora, particularly for non-native speakers. In literature, large pre-trained speech models like Whisper offer a promising foundation for this task but remain untested on non-native data. And their adaptation in low-resource non-native settings is challenging due to the absence of huge annotated data. To address this gap, we propose a novel unsupervised framework that leverages noisy labels derived from energy-based heuristic features to iteratively fine-tune a Whisper-based classification model. We evaluate our approach on low-resource non-native English speech from Italian speakers using F1-score as the metric. Experimental results show that our method performs on par with supervised baselines, achieving absolute improvements of ~10% over the best existing unsupervised models.

title: Speaking Clearly: A Simplified Whisper-Based Codec for Low-Bitrate Speech Coding
authors: Xin Zhang, Lin Li, Xiangni Lu, Jianquan Liu, Kong Aik Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech codecs serve as bridges between continuous speech signals and large language models, yet face an inherent conflict between acoustic fidelity and semantic preservation. To mitigate this conflict, prevailing methods augment acoustic codecs with complex semantic supervision. We explore the opposite direction: a semantic-first approach that starts from a semantically-capable model and adapts it for high-fidelity acoustic reconstruction. Through empirical analysis, we discover that targeted architectural simplification can unlock the acoustic modeling potential of Whisper, a text-aligned Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) model. Based on this finding, we propose SimWhisper-Codec, a novel codec that balances the semantic and acoustic preservation by leveraging a frozen, simplified Whisper encoder without requiring external supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that SimWhisper-Codec achieves superior performance in both semantic preservation and acoustic quality compared to semantically-supervised codecs such as Mimi Codec and SpeechTokenizer at similar bitrates, validating the effectiveness of our semantic-first approach. Code is available at https://github.com/ZhangXinWhut/SimWhisper-Codec.

title: Self-Supervised Note Tracking and Multi-Pitch Estimation Via Reconstruction-Based Learning
authors: Heng-Hsiu Hu, Li Su
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The limited availability of large, high-quality annotated datasets in music information retrieval (MIR) remains a key challenge for advancing automatic music transcription (AMT). To address this limitation, we introduce a fully self-supervised framework for note-level AMT that integrates multi-pitch estimation (MPE) and onset detection without labeled data. Our method extends the reconstruction/transcription paradigm by incorporating spectral flux and local group delay features. Experiments show consistent gains over prior self-supervised baselines in MPE and promising results in note-level transcription. Although note tracking still trails supervised systems, analysis highlights the role of reconstruction losses in capturing instrument-sensitive pitch/onset information. This work advances self-supervised AMT methods that generalize across diverse musical domains with limited labeled resources.

title: An Energy-Efficient and Compact AES S-Box Architecture Optimized for IoT Applications in Advanced Technology Nodes
authors: Ruby Mishra, Manish Okade, Kamalakanta Mahapatra
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: This paper proposes novel hardware architectures exhibiting improved performance in terms of power, throughput, energy and other metrics with regard to cipher designs. The optimization strategy revolves around a generic power-efficient selector module (PESM) that utilises high-speed multiplexers. The efficiency of the proposed PESM has been exemplified by including it in the AES S-box cipher architectures as a use case. The PESM module can be deployed in any digital architecture, requiring low power and optimized area. The high-speed multiplexers here refer to the novel structures of the multiplexers customized to target a particular architecture. The proposed architectures have been evaluated on both FPGA and ASIC platforms at an RFID operating frequency of 13.56 MHz, proving them beneficial for applications deployed in IoT environments. Performance analysis demonstrates a reduction of 87.87% in power and 59% in critical delay for the AES S-box use case compared to state-of-the-art architectures. An ASIC layout of the proposed AES S-box, synthesized using 32 nm standard cell libraries, is also presented, along with detailed performance analyses. Furthermore, the reduction in dynamic power contributes to lower information leakage probabilities, thereby enhancing the architecture's resilience against sidechannel attacks.

title: Pilot Clustering in Cell-Free mMIMO Systems Under Mixed LoS/NLoS Channels
authors: Ashish Pratap Singh, Anubhab Chowdhury, Ribhu Chopra
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: The problem of pilot contamination arises in cell-free massive multiple input multiple output (CF-mMIMO) systems due to a large number of user equipments (UEs) being served simultaneously by multiple access points (APs) under constraints on pilot length. Reduction of pilot contamination becomes challenging in the context of CF-mMIMO systems mainly because of two reasons: (i) all the APs serving a subset of UEs need to obtain good local channel estimates; and (ii) there is no inherent AP-UE clustering that can facilitate local orthogonality among the clustered UEs. In this paper, we cast the joint problem of pilot length and contamination minimization for a CF-mMIMO system with a more practical mixed LoS/NLoS channel model and analyze it using tools from stochastic geometry. We first characterize the probability of a given UE having an LoS link as a function of the AP density, and use this to find the number of UEs whose accurate channel state information (CSI) is required. Following this, we recast a pilot allocation strategy based on graph coloring in our system model and analyze the values taken by various parameters using tools from stochastic geometry. We validate our results using Monte-Carlo simulations and evaluate the performance of the graph coloring-based pilot allocation algorithm.

title: GCAF-Net: Geometry-Informed Cross-Attention Fusion Graph Neural Network for Eeg-Based Diagnosis of Alzheimer’s Disease and Frontotemporal Dementia
authors: Shiang Hu, Xiaoliang Sheng, Yifan Hu, Fei Ruan, Juan Hou, Zhao Lv, Pedro A. Valdes-Sosa
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) are frequently misdiagnosed due to overlapping early symptoms. Electroencephalography (EEG) provides a non-invasive, cost-effective modality for diagnosis, yet existing methods often lack global perspective in feature extraction and fusion, while neglecting the complex spatial topology of brain networks. Here, we propose GCAF-Net, a geometry-informed cross-attention fusion graph neural network. Its backbone integrates functional connectivity and spectral features with electrode spatial coordinates and enables the spatial topology to be dynamically updated throughout training. It leverages the Bidirectional Cross-Attention Block (BCAB) and Conditional Local Feature Encoding (CLFE) modules to capture and enhance spatial–functional interactions, thereby generating more discriminative representations. Our method outperforms existing deep learning approaches, achieving 97% accuracy on the ds004504 dataset and 98.69% accuracy on the BrainLat dataset. These results demonstrate that GCAF-Net provides a promising, generalizable framework for EEG-based dementia classification.

title: Diffusion-Based Natural Adversarial Perturbations Towards Segment Anything Model
authors: Haodong Xiao, Wenbo Yu, Hao Fang, Shuoyang Sun, Bin Chen, Xuan Wang, Shu-Tao Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Segment Anything Model (SAM) is widely used in many applications and serves as a foundation model in computer vision. Meanwhile, the security issues of SAM have also attracted widespread attention. Although different adversarial attacks targeting SAM have been proposed, they have not paid sufficient attention to the stealthiness of malicious images crafted by attackers. In this paper, we introduce a diffusion-based approach that generates natural adversarial samples targeting SAM, such that the perturbed images remain imperceptibly natural to human observers while leading to incorrect segmentation. By leveraging gradients derived from the diffusion model’s guidance mechanism, our method generates adversarial samples to fool SAM by perturbing both foreground and background semantics. Additionally, we propose an image high-frequency region selection strategy based on the Sobel operator, which efficiently identifies regions where applying perturbations can minimize the effect on image naturalness, thus achieving an optimal trade-off between adversarial attack effectiveness and visual imperceptibility. Extensive experimental results across diverse models and datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior overall performance in balancing attack efficacy and visual stealth compared to existing approaches.

title: Secondary Source Placement for Sound Field Control Based on Ising Model
authors: Shihori Kozuka, Shoichi Koyama, Hiroaki Itou, Noriyoshi Kamado
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: A method for optimizing secondary source placement in sound field control is proposed. Sound field control techniques that aim to synthesize the desired spatial sound using multiple loudspeakers (or secondary sources) rely heavily on the placement of sources. Methods for optimizing secondary source placement are typically formulated as combinatorial optimization problems that select the configuration from discrete candidate points on the basis of a certain measure. Whereas the mean-square-error (MSE)-based source placement method has been shown to be effective, a simple greedy algorithm cannot guarantee the near optimality of the solution due to the complexity of its cost function. Furthermore, it also requires significant computation time for sequentially evaluating the cost function. We thus propose a secondary source placement method based on the Ising model. By reformulating the MSE-based cost function as a binary quadratic form, its minimization problem can be efficiently solved by Ising machines. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method yields better solutions, particularly at low frequencies, with significantly less computation time than the greedy algorithm.

title: Beamspace Model and Beam Alignment Method for Reconfigurable Holographic Surface Antenna Systems
authors: Ashna K K, Visa Koivunen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reconfigurable holographic surface (RHS) is a cost-effective and power-efficient multi-antenna solution to the emerging 6G and ISAC systems. Traditional beamforming methods can be challenging for RHS systems. Beamspace processing generates deterministic orthogonal beams that are allocated to different spatial processing tasks or form demanding beampatterns. In this paper, a novel approach for generating a beamspace of orthogonal beams in RHS systems is proposed. Moreover, a method for dynamically aligning these beams with actual target/user equipment (UE) directions is developed. The beam alignment problem operates on the Stiefel manifold to ensure orthogonality and reduce interference among the beams. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed algorithm efficiently aligns the beamspace beams to the target/UE directions while maintaining the beams as orthogonal as possible. The algorithm provides significant signal-to-interference-noise-ratio (SINR) improvement for misaligned targets/UEs.

title: Ranking The Impact of Contextual Specialization in Neural Speech Enhancement
authors: Peter Leer, Svend Feldt, Zheng-Hua Tan, Jan Østergaard, Jesper Jensen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We systematically investigate neural speech enhancement systems, ranging from very small (~10k parameters) to medium-large (~2-5M parameters), which specialize to acoustic conditions using contextual information such as speaker identity, noise type, speaker gender, spoken language, and SNR. By fine-tuning generalist models on specific data subsets, we find that specializing to a speaker’s identity consistently yields the largest gains in estimated speech intelligibility and quality. In contrast, specializing to SNR, noise type, or gender offers only marginal benefits. Crucially, we show that a small model specialized to both a specific speaker and a specific noise type can match or exceed the performance of a generalist model ten times its size. Further, cross-lingual tests reveal that models specialized to a target language outperform multilingual generalists, suggesting that language is a salient feature for specialization. These findings highlight the potential of small, adaptive models for resource-constrained applications like hearing aids, which specialize on-the-fly to contextual information.

title: MTS-CR: Contrastive Representation Learning for Real-Time Qos Degradation Detection in Media Cloud Shared Instances
authors: Zhijiao Xiao, Yunyun Qiu, Pengwei Liu, Weipeng Cao, Zhong Ming
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In media cloud environments, shared instances often suffer from unpredictable QoS degradation, leading to impaired user experience in real-time multimedia services such as HD video streaming. Existing methods struggle to model the high-dimensional, nonlinear telemetry dynamics that precede performance drops. To address this, we propose MTS-CR, a novel framework that combines contrastive learning and representation learning for real-time QoS degradation detection. MTS-CR employs a Linear Attention-based Encoder enhanced by Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) to extract discriminative temporal representations from 73 system-level metrics (CPU, memory, I/O, network). A lightweight MLP-based Predictor then forecasts imminent QoS anomalies. Evaluated on real-world cloud telemetry, MTS-CR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both detection accuracy and generalization, offering cloud operators an actionable signal for dynamic resource orchestration — ultimately preserving end-user QoE.

title: Scalable Bayesian Fine-Tuning of LLMs for Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization
authors: Haotian Xiang, Heng Zhang, Qin Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As the default choice for surrogate modeling in multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO), Gaussian processes (GPs) struggle with irregular high-dimensional variables and non-stationary spaces. To alleviate these challenges, we adapt the prevailing large language model (LLM) as the surrogate model given its powerful capability in feature extraction based on large-scale pre-training. Specifically, we rely on the Bayesian last layer (BLL) framework with the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) of the LLM as a common feature extractor across objectives, which is amenable to scalable variational (V) training with tractable posterior inference. The resulting LoRA-VBLL-MO surrogate can not only accommodate computation- and storage-efficient recursive Bayesian updates across fine-tuning steps, but can also be coupled with Thompson sampling-based expected hypervolume improvement for acquisition function design. Experimental results on MO molecular optimization benchmarks demonstrate superior performance over baselines.

title: Temporal Context and Architecture: A Benchmark for Naturalistic EEG Decoding
authors: Mehmet Ergezer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study how model architecture and temporal context interact in naturalistic EEG decoding. Using the HBN movie-watching dataset, we benchmark five architectures, CNN, LSTM, a stabilized Transformer (EEGXF), S4, and S5, on a 4-class task across segment lengths from 8 s to 128 s. Accuracy improves with longer context: at 64 s, S5 reaches 98.7%±0.6 and CNN 98.3% ± 0.3, while S5 uses ∼20 × fewer parameters than CNN. To probe real-world robustness, we evaluate zero-shot cross-frequency shifts, cross-task OOD inputs, and leave-one-subject-out generalization. S5 achieves stronger cross-subject accuracy but makes over-confident errors on OOD tasks; EEGXF is more conservative and stable under frequency shifts, though less calibrated in-distribution. These results reveal a practical efficiency–robustness trade-off: S5 for parameter-efficient peak accuracy; EEGXF when robustness and conservative uncertainty are critical.

title: MT-HuBERT: Self-Supervised Mix-Training for Few-Shot Keyword Spotting in Mixed Speech
authors: Junming Yuan, Ying Shi, Dong Wang, Lantian Li, Askar Hamdulla
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Few-shot keyword spotting aims to detect previously unseen keywords with very limited labeled samples. A pre-training and adaptation paradigm is typically adopted for this task. While effective in clean conditions, most existing approaches struggle with mixed keyword spotting—detecting multiple overlapping keywords within a single utterance—a capability essential for real-world applications. We have previously proposed a pre-training approach based on Mix-Training (MT) to tackle the mixed keyword detection problem and demonstrated its efficiency. However, this approach is fully supervised, unable to utilize vast unlabeled data. To this end, we propose Mix-Training HuBERT (MT-HuBERT), a self-supervised learning (SSL) pre-training framework that implements the MT criterion during pre-training. MT-HuBERT predicts, in a self-supervised manner, the clean acoustic units of each constituent signal from contextual cues, in contrast to predicting compositional patterns of mixed speech. Experiments conducted on the Google Speech Commands (GSC v2) corpus demonstrate that our proposed MT-HuBERT consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in few-shot KWS tasks under both mixed and clean conditions.

title: Gromov-Wasserstein Graph Coarsening
authors: Carlos A. Taveras, Santiago Segarra, César A. Uribe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study graph coarsening in the Gromov-Wasserstein geometry. Specifically, we propose two algorithms that leverage a novel representation of the distortion induced by merging pairs of nodes. The first method, Greedy Pair Coarsening (GPC), iteratively merges pairs of nodes that locally minimize a distortion measure until the desired size is reached. The second method, termed k-means Greedy Pair Coarsening (KGPC), leverages clustering based on pairwise distortion metrics to directly merge node clusters. We provide conditions that guarantee optimal coarsening for our methods and validate their performance across six large-scale datasets and a downstream clustering task. Results show that the proposed methods outperform existing approaches on a wide range of parameters and scenarios.

title: Learning Piezoelectric Hysteresis in In-Ear MEMS Loudspeakers from Acoustic Measurements
authors: Oliviero Massi, Alessandro Ilic Mezza, Riccardo Giampiccolo, Alberto Bernardini
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Piezoelectric MEMS loudspeakers represent a viable technology for in-ear audio applications, offering several key advantages for the audio market geared towards miniaturization. Yet, their nonlinearities, particularly hysteretic effects in electro-mechanical transduction, pose challenges for accurate modeling and distortion compensation. We address this by introducing a hybrid MEMS loudspeaker equivalent circuit model that embeds a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to represent hysteretic transduction as a nonlinear pre-distortion block, driving a Wave Digital Filter (WDF) implementation of a linear equivalent circuit model of the loudspeaker mechanical and acoustic domains. The RNN is trained end-to-end within the differentiable WDF simulation using voltage–pressure measurements. Experiments confirm close agreement with measured responses and highlight the model’s potential for MEMS loudspeaker linearization applications.

title: Efficient Plug-and-Play Method for Dynamic Imaging Via Kalman Smoothing
authors: Benjamin Hawkes, Mike Davies, Víctor Elvira, Audrey Repetti
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: State-space models (SSM) are common in signal processing, where Kalman smoothing (KS) methods are state-of-the-art. However, traditional KS techniques lack expressivity as they do not incorporate spatial prior information. Recently, [1] proposed an ADMM algorithm that handles the state-space fidelity term with KS while regularizing the object via a sparsity-based prior with proximity operators. Plug-and-Play (PnP) methods are a popular type of iterative algorithms that replace proximal operators encoding prior knowledge with powerful denoisers such as deep neural networks. These methods are widely used in image processing, achieving state-of-the-art results. In this work, we build on the KS-ADMM method, combining it with deep learning to achieve higher expressivity. We propose a PnP algorithm based on KS-ADMM iterations, efficiently handling the SSM through KS, while enabling the use of powerful denoising networks. Simulations on a 2D+t imaging problem show that the proposed PnP-KS-ADMM algorithm improves the computational efficiency over standard PnP-ADMM for large numbers of timesteps.

title: Compression meets Sampling: LZ78-SPA for Efficient Symbolic Music Generation
authors: Abhiram Gorle, Connor Ding, Sagnik Bhattacharya, Amit Kumar Singh Yadav, Tsachy Weissman
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in symbolic music generation rely on deep learning models that achieve high-quality results but demand heavy compute, limiting scalability. We introduce LZMidi, a lightweight framework based on an LZ78-induced sequential probability assignment (SPA) that enables efficient CPU-only generation with minimal training and inference cost. First, beyond universal convergence results, we also provide finite-sample performance guarantees for LZ78-SPA. Second, on the Lakh MIDI dataset, we benchmark LZMidi against classical and deep generative baselines: achieving competitive Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD) and Wasserstein Distance (WD) while significantly reducing compute: with up to 30× faster training, 300× faster generation, and orders-of-magnitude lower energy. These results position universal compression as a scalable, theoretically grounded alternative to resource-intensive generative models.

title: LogPTR: Variable-Aware Log Parsing with Pointer Network
authors: Yifan Wu, Bingxu Chai, Siyu Yu, Ying Li, Pinjia He, Wei Jiang, Jianguo Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Due to the sheer size of software logs, developers rely on automated log analysis. Log parsing, which parses semi-structured logs into a structured format, is a prerequisite of automated log analysis. However, existing log parsers are unsatisfactory when applied in practice because they 1) ignore categories of variables, and 2) need labor-intensive model tuning. To address these limitations, we propose LogPTR, a variable-aware log parser that can extract the static and dynamic parts in logs, and further identify categories of variables. The key of LogPTR is formulating log parsing as a text summarization problem and using a pointer mechanism to copy words from the log message and label tokens indicating categories of variables. The experimental results on widely-used benchmark datasets show that LogPTR outperforms state-of-the-art log parsers on both general log parsing that extracts log templates and variable-aware log parsing that further identifies categories of variables.

title: β-AVSDNET: A Novel End-To-End Neural Network Architecture For Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
authors: Chang Huai You
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose β-AVSDnet, a novel end-to-end audio-visual speaker diarization (AVSD) system that unifies visual and acoustic processing in a single trainable architecture. A novel ROI-delta visual representation is first constructed to capture both spatial appearance and short-term lip motion. These visual inputs are then processed by a modified LeNet encoder combined with a shared Conformer, which extracts compact visual embeddings. The visual embeddings together with speaker embedding are subsequently passed to a dedicated β-AV embedding subnet. Finally, the resulting embedding representations are integrated with audio features into BLSTMs to perform speaker diarization. Model training is guided by a dual-target strategy. Compared with the multimodal information based speech processing (MISP) baseline, β-AVSDnet reduces parameters by over 50% while improving accuracy: on the MISP far-field development set with single-channel audio, DER decreases from 15.38% to 12.20%, and RTTM-level multi-channel fusion further reduces it to 10.98%.

title: AMFN:Adaptive Multi-view Fusion Network Framework
authors: Guanzheng Jiang, Zhimin Feng, Hui Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-view learning aims to fuse data from diverse sources or feature sets to obtain more discriminative representations. However, existing methods struggle with view heterogeneity, dynamic weight assignment, and noise robustness. To address these challenges, we propose an Adaptive Multi-view Fusion Network (AMFN), which enables efficient representation learning and fusion via learnable view-importance weights and joint optimization. AMFN first employs autoencoder-based modules preserve view-specific information, then integrates views using a cross-modal attention mechanism, and finally enhances feature discriminability through graph-based contrastive learning. Experiments on six benchmark datasets—including Prokaryotic, Synthetic3d, YouTubeFace, and the Caltech series—demonstrate that AMFN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods clustering accuracy, normalized mutual information, and purity. Notably, it achieves 0.9421 accuracy on Caltech-5V (8.46% higher than the best baseline) and improves accuracy on YouTubeFace by 7.25%. These results confirm the effectiveness, robustness, and scalability of AMFN, offering new insights for multi-view learning research.

title: Frequency-Aware Dynamic Graph Learning Via Pseudo-Spectral Decomposition for Metro Flow Forecasting
authors: Yanzhe Xie, Tuohang Li, Heng Wang, Yujun Ge, Li Huang, Qiang Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate metro flow forecasting requires modeling highly dynamic and multi-scale correlations across different metro stations. Conventional graph-based approaches typically rely on static or heuristic adjacency structures, which fail to capture the temporal variability and spectral diversity inherent in flow signals. This study proposes FDGP, a Frequency-aware Dynamic Graph learning framework grounded in Pseudo-spectral decomposition. Instead of operating purely in the time domain, FDGP leverages Fourier-based analysis on high-dimensional embeddings to obtain pseudo-frequency representations that reveal latent periodicities and multi-band dependencies. By jointly exploiting magnitude and phase spectra, frequency-domain convolution, and spectral attention, FDGP adaptively generates dynamic adjacency matrices that capture both global periodic trends and local fluctuations. Experiments on two real-world metro flow datasets demonstrate that FDGP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, highlighting the effectiveness of frequency-domain modeling in capturing spatio-temporal dependencies among flow signals.

title: Bayesian-Optimized BiLSTM with Tensor-Reshaped Structured Data for Robust Breast Cancer Risk Prediction
authors: Adaleta Gicić, Dženana Đonko
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Deep learning has become increasingly significant in clinical medicine, including breast cancer detection, offering significant potential to improve patient outcomes. However, recurrent architectures like LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) and BiLSTM (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory) remain underutilized for breast cancer prediction using structured tabular data, primarily due to the absence of explicit temporal dependencies, which are unsuitable for sequence-based modeling. This work presents a novel approach that redefines how LSTM architecture can be applied to the publicly available non-sequential Wisconsin Diagnostic Breast Cancer (WDBC), consisting of 569 samples and 30 features. The flat tabular input is reshaped into a fixed-length 3D tensor using a sliding window approach to adapt the data for sequence modeling. This transformation enables the model to leverage LSTM's sequential processing capabilities in a fundamentally new way, capturing implicit feature interactions across structured attributes without temporal context. Furthermore, Bayesian hyperparameter optimization techniques are applied to enhance the model's performance. The proposed model is evaluated against standard LSTM and state-of-the-art tabular Transformer architectures (FT-Transformer and SAINT). Results show that BiLSTM achieves the best overall performance (AUC 0.9985, accuracy 0.9824, RMSE 0.0964), while the LSTM baseline also surpasses both Transformerbased tabular models (AUC 0.9958, accuracy 0.9719). Performance gains are consistent across seven evaluation metrics, with statistical significance confirmed via paired t-tests <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$({p}<0.05)$</tex>. These findings demonstrate that, when appropriately adapted, recurrent architectures can outperform even advanced self-attention models in structured clinical prediction tasks.

title: Audio Classification Models are Vulnerable to Filter Perturbations
authors: Justin Dettmer, Annelot Bosman, Igor Vatolkin, Holger Hoos
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning models are known to suffer from a lack of robustness to adversarial attacks. While the majority of research on the robustness of neural networks stems from the image classification domain, audio classifiers have also come under scrutiny, as their use in tasks such as speech processing is increasing. We introduce a novel adversarial attack method that creates band-pass filters as perturbations for mel spectrograms. These perturbations are closer to real-life conditions than conventionally created adversarial examples, as they can simulate the use of different recording devices or other acoustic properties. We demonstrate that state-of-the-art audio classifiers lack robustness against these adversarially-created filters, and we show that adversarial training with our attack method can help mitigate this effect on the widely studied NSynth, ESC-50 and SpeechCommands datasets.

title: FIG: Frequency-Based Integrated Gradients for Robust Feature Attribution
authors: Kun Yang, Zhizhong Niu, Fangmin Xie, Ziyao Tang, Guoqiang Xiao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Attribution methods identify critical input features driving model predictions, particularly crucial for high-stakes vision tasks (e.g., medical imaging). While path-integrated gradient techniques (e.g., Integrated Gradients) provide axiomatically justified attributions, they often produce spurious feature attributions that are irrelevant to the model’s predictions. To address these limitations, we propose Frequency-based Integrated Gradients (FIG), a novel attribution framework that enhances the robustness of explanation maps. FIG introduces two key innovations: (1) Frequency-based Path (FBP) constructs baseline-free trajectories through sequential FFT, spectral masking, and IFFT operations to eliminate path artifacts; (2) Path-wise smoothing suppresses noise while preserving discriminative regions during gradient accumulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FIG outperforms baseline methods (e.g., BlurIG, Guided IG, IIA) in both qualitative localization precision and quantitative metrics. The source code of our FIG is available at https://github.com/kunflv/FIG.

title: Boosting Knowledge Distillation Via Local Categories Similarity Scaling
authors: Dingyao Chen, Shanshan Zhang, Keyan Jin, Yuxin Zhang, Long Lan, Xiao Teng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers knowledge from a pre-trained teacher network to a lightweight student model. While most KD methods adjust global category similarities by softening the teacher’s output distribution, they struggle to regulate similarities among local categories. This often leads to overconfident predictions and reduced discriminability for similar classes. To address this, we propose Local Categories Similarity Scaling (LCSKD), a plug-and-play framework that enhances KD by refining local similarity transfer. Specifically, we partition the predicted category probabilities into local groups and distill intra-group relations. A temperature adjustment strategy based on distribution "smoothness" is further introduced to calibrate local similarities effectively. LCSKD introduces negligible computational overhead and is compatible with existing KD methods. Experiments on visual tasks validate its effectiveness.

title: MedMoVE-HRMLLM: A Mixture-of-Vision-Experts MLLM for Fine-Grained High-Resolution Medical Image Understanding
authors: Yujie Bai, Juanjuan Zhao, Yapeng Zhao, Bing Gu, Yan Qiang, Qi Chen, Huilan Zhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The multimodal interpretation of high-resolution medical images is hindered by difficulties in modeling global anatomical and pathological semantics and aligning visual details with textual semantics, exhibiting bottlenecks especially in detecting millimetre-scale lesions and fine textures. We present MedMoVE-HRMLLM, a mixture-of-vision-experts(MoVE) multimodal large language model(MLLM) combining a medical knowledge expert and a high-resolution fine-grained expert. The former enhances the modelling of global organ and pathological concepts by distilling clinical semantic priors and large-scale medical image-text contrastive learning; the latter applies variable-resolution encoding and hierarchical windows to capture minute lesions and complex textures. A text-guided, channel-wise soft routing mechanism enables dynamic fusion of both representations. On multiple medical benchmarks, MedMoVE-HRMLLM (13B) achieves average performance close to 40B-scale models, with strong advantages in high-resolution, detail-sensitive tasks. Accuracy reaches 92.97% on PathVQA and 85.23% on SLAKE. The code will be made publicly available on GitHub.

title: OCTIP: Compact Geography-Aware IP embeddings for Nearest-Neighbor IP Signal Retrieval
authors: Hao Feng, Chaokun Wang, Fang Niu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study Nearest-Neighbor IP Signal Retrieval, a problem that retrieves semantically related IP signals directly in the IP address space without relying on precise geographic coordinates, traffic logs, or active measurements. To address this challenge, we propose OCTIP, a compact OCTet-level IP encoder for IPv4, together with IPSPRE, a reproducible framework for geography-preserving evaluation. OCTIP maps four octets to a unit vector using lightweight embeddings and a two-layer MLP, and learns with a ranking objective that aligns distance orderings in the IP space with those induced by a geography-aware text space derived from public IP-to-location records. The text supervision is powered by G2PTL, a geography-graph pretrained encoder, so that coarse attribution strings carry explicit geographic knowledge into training. IPSPRE measures an order-preserving rate, a Spearman rank correlation, a Mapping Consistency Index, and a Shuffle Loss Ratio; fixed sampling ensures robust replicability. On benchmarks built from public IPto-location data, OCTIP yields stronger order preservation and higher geography-oriented correlation than structured bit-level encoders under identical supervision, while remaining architecturally simple and compatible with large-scale approximate nearest-neighbor indices. This approach enables fast nearest-neighbor IP signal retrieval for downstream tasks such as region-aware clustering and attribution.

title: Nonconvex Regularization for Feature Selection in Reinforcement Learning
authors: Kyohei Suzuki, Konstantinos Slavakis
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This work proposes an efficient batch algorithm for feature selection in reinforcement learning (RL) with theoretical convergence guarantees. To mitigate the estimation bias inherent in conventional regularization schemes, the first contribution extends policy evaluation within the classical least-squares temporal-difference (LSTD) framework by formulating a Bellman-residual objective regularized with the sparsity-inducing, nonconvex projected minimax concave (PMC) penalty. Owing to the weak convexity of the PMC penalty, this formulation can be interpreted as a special instance of a general nonmonotone-inclusion problem. The second contribution establishes novel convergence conditions for the forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) algorithm to solve this class of problems. Numerical experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach substantially outperforms state-of-the-art feature-selection methods, particularly in scenarios with many noisy features.

title: Enhancing Knowledge base Question Answering with Reinforced Hop-Wise Logical form Generation
authors: Jinghua Tang, Jian Cao, Jianqi Gao, Ranran Bu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Knowledge-Based Question Answering (KBQA) focuses on extracting accurate answers from structured knowledge bases (KBs) to address natural language queries. The semantic parsing approach has become a dominant methodology in this domain, delivering impressive results by converting user questions into formal query structures, specifically logical forms (LFs). Despite the adoption of large language models (LLMs) for LF generation, these representations may remain non-executable owing to model hallucinations and the inherent complexity of KBQA tasks. To solve the above problems and inspired by autoregressive token-wise generation and boosting strategy, we propose a novel learning framework, ReSON (Reinforced Hop-wise Search for Logical Form Generation). ReSON first generates a hop-by-hop search path (preliminary logical form, PLF) for each topic entity, similar to autoregressive token-wise content generation. It then effectively integrates each PLF to generate a final refined query statement (refined logical form, RLF). Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that ReSON significantly improves the performance of LLMs on KBQA tasks.

title: Soft Graph Transformer for MIMO Detection
authors: Jiadong Hong, Lei Liu, Xinyu Bian, Wenjie Wang, Zhaoyang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose the Soft Graph Transformer (SGT), a soft-input–soft-output neural architecture designed for MIMO detection. While Maximum Likelihood (ML) detection achieves optimal accuracy, its exponential complexity makes it infeasible in large systems, and conventional message-passing algorithms rely on asymptotic assumptions that often fail in finite dimensions. Recent Transformer-based detectors show strong performance but typically overlook the MIMO factor graph structure and cannot exploit prior soft information. SGT addresses these limitations by combining self-attention, which encodes contextual dependencies within symbol and constraint subgraphs, with graph-aware cross-attention, which performs structured message passing across subgraphs. Its soft-input interface allows the integration of auxiliary priors, producing effective soft outputs while maintaining computational efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that SGT achieves near-ML performance and offers a flexible and interpretable framework for receiver systems that leverage soft priors.

title: SFEF: Transformer-based LiDAR-Camera Object Detection for Autonomous Driving
authors: Sotirios Spanogianopoulos
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Precise 3D object detection is crucial for self-driving cars, but it is tricky. Deep convolutional networks have shown promise in combining Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) and camera data for this task. However, existing methods often use simple network designs and fixed 3D bounding boxes, which don't fully capture the relationships and variations in the size of 3D objects. In response, we introduce a novel fusion algorithm named Scalable Feature Extraction Scalable Fusion (SFEF) method, which considers both long-range dependencies in the features and also different object sizes by considering two scalable units termed intra-domain feature extraction and inter-domain fusion units. The results show the superiority of the proposed method in detecting several objects and achieves overall comparable results in the nuScenes leaderboard.

title: Make Your Move: Make Your 3D Contents by Adapting Multi-View Diffusion Models to External Editing
authors: Weitao Wang, Haoran Xu, Jun Meng, Haoqian Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As 3D generation techniques continue to flourish, the demand for generating personalized content is rapidly rising. Users increasingly seek to apply various editing methods to polish the generated 3D content, aiming to enhance its color, style, and lighting without compromising the underlying geometry. However, most existing editing tools focus on the 2D domain, and directly feeding their results into 3D generative methods (like multi-view diffusions) will introduce information loss, degrading the quality of the final 3D assets. In this paper, we propose a training-free, plug-and-play scheme that aligns edited assets with their original geometry in a single inference run. Central to our approach is a geometry preservation module that guides the edited multi-view generation with original input normal latents. Besides, an injection switcher is proposed to deliberately control the supervision extent of the original normals, ensuring the alignment between the edited color and normal views. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently improves both the multi-view consistency and mesh quality of edited 3D assets, across multiple combinations of multi-view diffusions and editing methods.

title: Design and Application of Virtual Disassembly and Quick-Change Tool for Retired Power Battery Modules
authors: Licheng Fang, Yunhan Li, Yuhang Jiang, Jiayu Han, Lihao Xu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: To address the issues of low disassembly efficiency and high labor costs associated with retired power battery modules, this study designed a robotic quick-change tool system for virtual disassembly of retired power battery modules based on the RobotStudio platform. Standardized battery modules were constructed using a modular hierarchical structure, and three types of quick-change tools were developed for screw removal, casing removal, and cell disassembly. Combined with Smart components and RAPID programming, the system enables automatic tool switching and collaborative operations. The entire process-from tool loading and component disassembly to material handling-was simulated in a virtual environment, encompassing robotic arm motion trajectory planning, tool loading/unloading logic control, and multi-sensor information integration. Experimental results demonstrate that this system effectively reduces disassembly time and lowers labor costs. Virtual disassembly of simplified retired power battery modules validated the feasibility of combining robotic arms with quickchange tools for practical disassembly, providing valuable insights for green recycling and intelligent disassembly of power batteries.

title: 3D Mesh Steganography Algorithm Based on Non-Additive Distortion Minimization
authors: Shuai Ren, Shaoming Wu, Wenhui Deng, Chen Wu, Mo Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper proposes a 3D mesh steganography algorithm using non-additive distortion minimization (NADM). Conventional additive models treat vertex modifications independently, but this overlooks geometric correlations and nonlinear cumulative effects in 3D meshes.
