Our approach introduces a non-additive distortion function that explicitly models inter-vertex interactions, capturing how neighboring vertices collaboratively influence embedding quality rather than treating them as isolated modifications. The method also incorporates adaptive bit-plane selection using a stability index for blind extraction. Experiments on multiple datasets show NADM outperforms existing additive methods in imperceptibility and robustness, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of the non-additive modeling approach.

title: FlipCon: Flipped Contrastive Learning for Fine-Grained DOA Representation
authors: Davin Kim, Hyun-Soo Kim, Da-Hee Yang, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In supervised contrastive learning, samples within the same class often exhibit fine-grained variations that must be captured to learn robust representations. However, conventional contrastive losses struggle to preserve these subtle intra-class distinctions, as in direction-of-arrival estimation, where signals may share the same angular label but still differ in their acoustic characteristics. We trace the root cause of this limitation to a structural weakness: as the similarity between positive embeddings increases, the gradient with respect to their angular distance rapidly vanishes. This phenomenon, which we term the double gradient vanishing problem. To address this issue, we propose FlipCon, a principled reformulation of the contrastive loss that inverts the cosine similarity term. This simple modification not only preserves meaningful gradients even for highly aligned positives but also integrates seamlessly into existing frameworks, yielding consistent performance improvements across multiple contrastive learning variants. Furthermore, we extend our formulation with an additive angular margin, originally introduced for classification, and demonstrate additional gains in direction-of-arrival estimation. These results establish FlipCon as an effective and general solution to the limitations of standard contrastive loss.

title: Robust Multi-Model Kalman Filtering with Adaptive Noise Tuning for Maneuvering Source DOA Estimation in MIMO Radar
authors: Vijay Kumar N. V. S. V, Rajesh Kumar P
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Dynamic target tracking in MIMO radar systems demands algorithms capable of adapting to varying motion regimes and degraded SNR conditions. This paper develops an adaptive Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) filter specifically designed for Direction of Arrival (DOA) estimation under Rayleigh fading propagation. Six algorithmic improvements are proposed: (1) extension of the Constant Acceleration model to three states for direct acceleration observability, (2) SNR-adaptive process noise scaling (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5-5.0$</tex> base values with motion-specific multipliers), (3) SNR-dependent measurement noise parameterization (0.5-3.5), (4) optimized motion-specific model transition matrices with self-transition probabilities ranging from (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.70-0.90$</tex>), (5) robust multi-point weighted least squares initialization, and (6) Joseph-form updates integrated with Rauch-Tung-Striebel smoothing for enhanced numerical stability. Performance assessment across eight scenarios-Constant Velocity, Constant Acceleration, Coordinated Turn, and Mixed maneuvers at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+10 d B$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-5 d B$</tex> SNR—reveals 28.6 % average and 38.1 % peak RMSE reduction relative to single-model Kalman filtering. The enhanced filter achieves stable tracking performance ((0.2-0.5) error) in contrast to the oscillating error behavior (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.2-1.2$</tex>) exhibited by conventional approaches.

title: MVP4EI: A Multi-View Persistent Spatio-Temporal Feature Learning for EEG Imputation
authors: Guanjian Liu, Huan Liu, Yuhan Shi, Yuzhe Zhang, Yugui Xie, Dalin Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals frequently encounter significant data loss, posing substantial challenges for downstream analysis. While deep learning-based imputation methods have shown promise in handling complex missing patterns, they often overlook the inherent persistence of EEG signals. To address this gap, we propose MVP4EI, a Multi-View Persistent spatio-temporal feature learning framework for EEG Imputation. Our approach introduces a tri-branch learnable codebook mechanism to explicitly capture persistent features across temporal, spatial, and spatio-temporal dimensions, fused via an SE network. These features are then integrated with the corrupted signal through a spatio-temporal attention module to achieve accurate reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MVP4EI significantly outperforms existing methods in both imputation accuracy and downstream task performance. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/XJTU-EEG/MVP4EI.

title: M2FNet: Multi-Level Modality-Fused Network for Robust Fingerprint and Finger Vein Recognition
authors: Wenyang Miao, Xionghan Zhao, Hengyi Ren, Xing Li, Jinting Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal fingerprint recognition combining fingerprint and finger vein is a promising solution for enhancing security and reliability due to their complementary and stable features. However, most existing methods rely on shallow or single-layer fusion, which limits complementarity and is prone to noise. To address this, we propose M2FNet, a hierarchical fusion framework. It first extracts modality-specific features to avoid semantic drift and conflicts, then progressively integrates details, textures, and semantics across spatial and frequency domains, and finally employs an adaptive complementation module to recalibrate features for enhanced discrimination and robustness. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate that M2FNet outperforms existing methods in accuracy and generalization and achieves better accuracy and robustness.

title: GRASP: Group-Shapley Feature Selection for Patients
authors: Yuheng Luo, Shuyan Li, Zhong Cao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Feature selection remains a major challenge in medical prediction, where existing approaches such as LASSO often lack robustness and interpretability. We introduce GRASP, a novel framework that couples Shapley value–driven attribution with group-L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21</inf> regularization to extract compact and non-redundant feature sets. GRASP first distills group-level importance scores from a pre-trained tree model via SHAP, then enforces structured sparsity through groupL<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21</inf>–regularized logistic regression, yielding stable and interpretable selections. Extensive comparisons with LASSO, SHAP, and deep learning–based methods show that GRASP consistently delivers comparable or superior predictive accuracy, while identifying fewer, less redundant, and more stable features.

title: Unit-Based Agent for Semi-Cascaded Full-Duplex Dialogue Systems
authors: Haoyuan Yu, Yuxuan Chen, Minjie Cai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Full-duplex voice interaction is crucial for natural human computer interaction. We present a framework that decomposes complex dialogue into minimal conversational units, enabling the system to process each unit independently and predict when to transit to the next. This framework is instantiated as a semi-cascaded full-duplex dialogue system built around a multimodal large language model, supported by auxiliary modules such as voice activity detection (VAD) and text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis. The resulting system operates in a train-free, plug-and-play manner. Experiments on the HumDial dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, which ranks second among all teams on the test set of the Human-like Spoken Dialogue Systems Challenge[1] (Track 2: Full-Duplex Interaction). Code is available at the GitHub repository https://github.com/yu-haoyuan/fd-badcat.

title: Enhancing Domain Generation Through Pluggable Style Randomization
authors: Bin Xiao, Jiahao Xu, Mo Yang, Min Wang, Xingpeng Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Domain generalization is an effective approach to mitigating distribution shifts in image data and improving model robustness. However, the limited diversity of styles in the source domain often fails to capture real-world distributions. To address this, we propose Feature Style Randomization (FSR) to sensitivity to style-domain information. FSR introduces noise and integrates a novel style-transfer strategy to enrich the stylistic diversity of training data. To reduce distortions from style augmentation, we design a Style Consistency and Classification (SCC) module that employs dual classifiers to ensure reliable outputs for augmented samples, while introducing a consistency loss to align predictions between augmented and original data. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: EduGesture: A Dataset of Teachers’ Hand Gestures Toward Pedagogical Intentions
authors: Jiawen Li, Yuqing Chen, Fei Jiang, Aimin Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Teacher gestures are of great importance for nonverbal communication with students during the class. However, existing researches of teachers’ gesture analysis mainly focus on gesture recognition with predefined categories, which are hardly to capture instructional intentions expressed by these gestures. To address such limitation, we present EduGesture, the first dataset of teachers’ hand gestures with explicit instructional intentions description. Specifically, EduGesture comprises 5k+ images curated from award-winning lessons across various subjects and grades. Each image is annotated with a functional label derived from educational research and an expert-refined natural language description that expresses instructional intentions. This dual annotation framework links categorical supervision with teaching semantics, extending gesture recognition from morphology to pedagogical meaning. Then, we build baselines for teacher hand gesture classification and teaching intention understanding for further research on multimodal teaching analysis. Finally, we demonstrate the extra values of EduGesture with two applications, i.e., instructional hands generation and realistic gesture motion generation of virtual teachers.

title: From Intent to Invocation: A Reasoning-First Framework for Natural Language to Penetration Testing Commands
authors: He Kong, Jingguo Ge, Zheng Liu, Die Hu, Hui Li, Liangxiong Li, Bingzhen Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The automated translation of natural language into penetration testing commands represents a critical challenge in cybersecurity. Although Large Language Models demonstrate considerable potential in this domain, they frequently generate syntactically invalid commands or hallucinate non-existent parameters. To address these limitations, we propose Penetron, a framework that enhances command generation accuracy through a novel two-stage training paradigm. In the first stage, supervised fine-tuning is performed on a curated Chain-of-Thought dataset to cultivate fundamental reasoning capabilities. In the second stage, the model is further optimized via reinforcement learning with Generative Reward Policy Optimization, guided by a composite reward function that jointly evaluates structural validity and semantic correctness. To facilitate systematic evaluation, we introduce NL2PEN-KALI, a benchmark specifically designed for penetration testing scenarios. Experimental results show that Penetron achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art models on both NL2PEN-KALI and the public NL2SH-ALFA benchmark, producing commands with higher syntactic validity and contextual relevance.

title: H3GM: History-Guided Global Geometric Metric for Single Image to 3D Scene Generation
authors: Yuki Katayama, Ren Togo, Keisuke Maeda, Takahiro Ogawa, Miki Haseyama
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose H3GM, a novel no-reference metric for "single image to 3D scene generation". H3GM detects History-Dependent Contradiction (HDC)—incoherence that arises when a previously observed viewpoint is revisited—and quantifies global geometric coherence using only the generated frames. Quantitative detection of HDCs has been difficult for existing no-reference metrics, which are typically limited by either a reliance on 2D appearance or a confinement to local, adjacent-frame evaluation. We address these limitations by sequentially reconstructing the generated sequence to build a single common coordinate system. By then restricting our evaluation to geometrically overlapping frame pairs, we robustly measure the scene’s global geometric coherence. In this work, we advocate for HDC as a critical aspect for the quantitative evaluation of generated 3D scenes.

title: Sensor Array and Camera Fusion Via Unbalanced Optimal Transport for 3D Source Localization
authors: Ilyes Jaouedi, Gilles Chardon, José Picheral
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We address the problem of localizing multiple sources in 3D by combining sensor array measurements with camera observations. We propose a fusion framework extending the covariance matrix fitting method with an unbalanced optimal transport regularization term that softly aligns sensor array responses with visual priors while allowing flexibility in mass allocation. To solve the resulting large-scale problem, we adopt a greedy coordinate descent algorithm that efficiently updates the transport plan. Its computational efficiency makes full 3D localization feasible in practice. The proposed framework is modular and does not rely on labeled data or training, in contrast with deep learning–based fusion approaches. Although validated here on acoustic arrays, the method is general to arbitrary sensor arrays. Experiments on real data show that the proposed approach improves localization accuracy compared to sensor-only baselines.

title: C&F-WSVAD: Towards High-Performance Coarse and Fine-Grained Weakly-Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
authors: Yang Wang, Jiaogen Zhou, Jihong Guan, Shuigeng Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Weakly-supervised video anomaly detection (WSVAD) performs frame-level anomaly detection using only video-level labels. However, existing methods mainly rely on visual data, ignoring multimodal cues that could enhance performance — particularly for fine-grained WSVAD (FG-WSVAD), which identifies specific anomaly types, unlike coarse-grained WS-VAD (CG-WSVAD) that only detects anomaly presence. This paper proposes C&F-WSVAD, a novel method leveraging video-text multimodal data and multiscale temporal dependencies to overcome WSVAD limitations. Our approach enhances both coarse-grained (CG) and fine-grained (FG) WSVAD by fusing video-text features and modeling multiscale temporal dependencies. We also propose a pseudo-labeling strategy and a center loss to maximize inter-class separation while minimizing intra-class distance. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance in both FG-WSVAD and FG-WSVAD.

title: LongSpeech: A Scalable Benchmark for Transcription, Translation and Understanding in Long Speech
authors: Fei Yang, Xuanfan Ni, Renyi Yang, Jiahui Geng, Qing Li, Chenyang Lyu, Yichao Du, Longyue Wang, Weihua Luo, Kaifu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in audio-language models have demonstrated remarkable success on short, segment-level speech tasks. However, real-world applications such as meeting transcription, spoken document understanding, and conversational analysis require robust models capable of processing and reasoning over long-form audio. In this work, we present LongSpeech, a large-scale and scalable benchmark specifically designed to evaluate and advance the capabilities of speech models on long-duration audio. LongSpeech comprises over 100,000 speech segments, each approximately 10 minutes long, with rich annotations for ASR, speech translation, summarization, language detection, speaker counting, content separation, and question answering. We introduce a reproducible pipeline for constructing long-form speech benchmarks from diverse sources, enabling future extensions. Our initial experiments with state-of-the-art models reveal significant performance gaps, with models often specializing in one task at the expense of others and struggling with higher-level reasoning. These findings underscore the challenging nature of our benchmark. Our benchmark will be made publicly available to the research community.

title: An Intelligent Prognostic Framework for Lithium-Ion Batteries Using IoT-Enabled Learning Models
authors: Sathishkumar S, Yogesh Rajkumar R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The adoption of electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, and portable electronic systems, which are based on lithium-ion batteries has increased the necessity of reliable prognostic methods, which would determine safety, durability, and efficiency in the application of the battery in the products. The traditional battery management systems tend to adopt the rule-based models or physics-based models that are difficult to adapt to the dynamic operating conditions which show complicated degradation patterns. The problem of successful battery health prediction, which is tackled in this paper, is that while relying on Internet of Things (IoT)-powered data collection is desirable, it is crucial to employ sophisticated learning algorithms as well. It aims at obtaining accurate determination of battery State of Health (SoH) and Remaining Useful Life (RUL) using real-time operational data. The method here is that continuous voltages, current and temperature are sensed and supplemented by nonlinear degradation behavior capturing data-based learning models. An effective model of the long-term aging is provided by feature extraction and learning in the long term. Experimental analysis of experimental evaluation based on benchmark lithium-ion battery datasets, it can be seen that the proposed framework results in better prediction accuracy than traditional machine learning and conventional prognostic methods based on baseline prediction, and it reduces significantly to prediction error and enhanced robustness with the changing load. The findings confirm the fact that intelligent prognostics with the assistance of IoT can have a significant impact on the process of battery monitoring and predictive maintenance. This paper adds to the scaleable and effective solution to next-generation battery management systems and safer operation and informed decision-making in activities where energy is a critical factor.

title: Precise Segmentation of Pathological Liver in 3D CT Scans Using Adaptive Shape Guidance from Prior Appearance
authors: Ahmed Semida, Ahmed Sharafeldeen, Hanan M. Amer, Ali Mahmoud, Rasha Karam, Basma A. Elged, Usama Shiha, Ahmad M. Mounir, Fatma Taher, Mohammed Ghazal, Guruprasad A. Giridharan, Sohail Contractor, Ayman El-Baz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel approach for extracting pathological liver regions from 3D computed tomography (CT) images. The proposed method leverages deep learning techniques to precisely segment pathological livers from 3D CT images. This is particularly challenging due to the inhomogeneity and diffused boundaries in the appearance of pathological liver tissue, as well as anatomical complexities such as shape and size variations and the liver’s close proximity to other organs. The proposed approach begins with 3D affine-based registration to roughly maximize the overlap between the subject to be segmented and the created liver atlas. Next, a 3D probabilistic shape map of the liver is generated by adaptively matching the appearance of each voxel with the prior liver atlas. This is achieved by selecting cubic neighbors of varying sizes around the location of each voxel. Finally, the original CT data, along with their corresponding 3D shape maps, are input into the 3D UX-Net. The proposed system is evaluated on 40 patients (20 with normal livers and 20 with pathological livers) using various metrics, including Dice similarity coefficient, overlap coefficient, absolute volume difference, and Hausdorff distance. It achieved scores of 94.66±1.1%, 89.88 ± 1.96%, 3.26± 2.66, and 7.18 ±2.65, respectively, outperforming different deep learning-based segmentation systems.

title: DPO-Regularized Regression for Age Prediction
authors: Mahsa Zamani, Rita Singh, Bhiksha Raj
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker age estimation is a challenging regression task that is typically approached as a pointwise prediction problem, where models are trained to minimize Mean Squared Error (MSE). While effective for reducing absolute error, MSE fails to incorporate the ordinal nature of age. To address this, we propose a hybrid training approach that combines MSE with a pairwise Direct preference optimization (DPO) loss, encouraging the model to prefer bins that are relatively closer to the true age by learning from pairwise comparisons between candidate bins. We conducted experiments on the TIMIT dataset for age prediction from voice and showed that incorporating DPO can lead to improvements in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to the MSE-only baseline.

title: FastAV: Efficient Token Pruning for Audio-Visual Large Language Model Inference
authors: Chaeyoung Jung, Youngjoon Jang, Seungwoo Lee, Joon Son Chung
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this work, we present FastAV, the first token pruning framework tailored for audio-visual large language models (AV-LLMs). While token pruning has been actively explored in standard large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (LVLMs), its application to AV-LLMs has received little attention, even though multimodal integration substantially increases their token demands. To address this gap, we introduce a pruning strategy that utilizes attention weights to identify tokens emphasized at different stages and estimates their importance. Building on this analysis, FastAV applies a two-stage pruning strategy: (1) global pruning in intermediate layers to remove broadly less influential tokens, and (2) fine pruning in later layers considering the impact on next token generation. Notably, our method does not rely on full attention maps, which makes it fully compatible with efficient attention mechanisms such as FlashAttention. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FastAV reduces FLOPs by more than 40% on two representative AV-LLMs, while preserving or even improving model performance.

title: UA-TTRL: Uncertainty-Aware Test-Time Reinforcement Learning
authors: Zixuan Liu, Siavash Khajavi, Guangkai Jiang, Xinru Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are deployed online, they increasingly must adapt at inference time, where explicit supervision is unavailable and user inputs may deviate substantially from the training distribution. Test-Time Reinforcement Learning (TTRL) addresses this by repeatedly sampling outputs and treating majority vote (MV) as a pseudo-label for policy updates. However, under out-of-distribution (OOD) shift, MV can be unreliable, causing reinforcement of incorrect answers and unstable learning. We propose Uncertainty-Aware TTRL (UA-TTRL), a simple, robust method that quantifies the reliability of MV and uses it to modulate test-time updates. UA-TTRL estimates a lower confidence bound (LCB) on pairwise log-odds between the MV winner and its rivals, converts these statistics into a soft confidence weight, and integrates this weight into PPO as a per-prompt scaling of the loss. We evaluate UA-TTRL on three challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks and observe consistent gains over TTRL baselines that treat MV as a hard label. These results show that uncertainty-aware weighting yields a more faithful proxy for correctness, improves robustness under OOD shift, and reduces reliance on human annotations, enabling more reliable, continual adaptation of LLMs at test time.

title: Direct Preference Optimization For Speech Autoregressive Diffusion Models
authors: Zhijun Liu, Dongya Jia, Xiaoqiang Wang, Chenpeng Du, Shuai Wang, Zhuo Chen, Haizhou Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Autoregressive diffusion models (ARDMs) have recently been applied to speech generation, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in zero-shot text-to-speech. By autoregressively generating continuous speech tokens with next-token diffusion, these models offer a promising alternative to next-token prediction, avoiding the technical complexities associated with discrete speech tokenization. As a relatively new paradigm, research on reinforcement learning (RL)-based fine-tuning of speech ARDMs remains limited. In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Diffusion-Direct Preference Optimization (ARDM-DPO) to advance this research. By fine-tuning the recently proposed zero-shot text-to-speech model DiTAR with DPO, we achieve significant improvements in terms of speech expressiveness and robustness for long texts.

title: A Graph Neural Network-Based Predictive Planning Model for Resilient Supply Chain and Disruption-Aware Management Systems
authors: Prabakaran S, Karthik R, Dinesh Chandran R, Prince Jinuson A, Sasindhar K, Sundaravel S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Resilient and disruption-aware supply chain planning has become increasingly critical in the wake of frequent global disturbances such as pandemics, geopolitical tensions, and environmental events. This study presents a novel Graph Neural Network-based Predictive Planning Model that transforms heterogeneous supply chain entities into structured graph representations, enabling real-time disruption forecasting and resilient decision-making. The proposed architecture integrates Relational GCN, Temporal Graph Attention Networks (TGAT), Bayesian GNNs, and a hybrid GNN-LSTM framework to capture both structural and temporal patterns. Additionally, a Graph-based Deep QNetwork (Graph-DQN) facilitates adaptive policy decisions, while GNN-GAN modules simulate adversarial disruptions for robustness testing. Experiments on benchmark and simulated datasets reveal high prediction accuracy across diverse disruption types- 94.2 % for port congestion, 91.6 % for supplier failures, and an average of 92.1 % across ten disruption categories. The Graph-DQN planner also maintains resilience scores above 81 % under critical disruptions and reduces recovery latency by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0 - 3 5 \%}$</tex> compared to baselines. This model demonstrates strong generalization and low decision latency, making it suitable for dynamic, high-stakes environments. The framework opens new avenues for proactive supply chain risk mitigation by integrating causality, uncertainty quantification, and adversarial robustness into a single predictive planning pipeline.

title: Performance Evaluation of PBCH Equalization in the Presence of CFO for B5G and 6G Systems
authors: Abhinav Anand, Adarsh Ravi, Preetam Kumar
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: The 5G NR initial access (IA) process begins with detecting the synchronisation signal block (SSB), which contains the primary and secondary synchronisation signals (PSS and SSS) as well as the physical broadcast channel (PBCH). However, at millimetre-wave FR2 frequencies, oscillator drift, Doppler rotation, and phase noise significantly impair PBCH decoding. This work evaluates FR2 synchronisation robustness under carrier frequency offset (CFO) using a third-generation partnership project technical report 38.901-compliant link level framework based on the clustered delay line urban macro model. The framework models the full physical-layer synchronisation chain, including SSB based cell search, channel propagation, CFO estimation and compensation, and PBCH demodulation. By comparing compensated and uncompensated receivers, we quantify the sensitivity of PBCH decoding to CFO. Results show that combining PSS based coarse estimation with cyclic-prefix fine refinement keeps the residual CFO below 150 Hz and holds the PBCH EVM near 0.55 %, even for frequency offsets up to 50 kHz in either direction. In contrast, without CFO removal the EVM exceeds 60 %, causing complete PBCH decoding failure despite correct beam alignment. These findings provide concrete evidence that CFO dominates PBCH robustness in FR2 and establish practical tolerance limits for beyond-5G (B5G) and 6G IA under non-ideal oscillator conditions.

title: Genetic Algorithm for Computing an Upper Bound on Undirected Unicast Network Information Flow
authors: Vikas Dangi, Satyajit Thakor
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: The multicommodity network flow problem involves finding an optimal flow assignment in a given network. This problem has a linear objective function and constraints and, hence, can be solved as a linear program. Information flow is a generalization of commodity flow. For multiple-unicast in undirected networks, there is an upper bound on information flow called the partition bound. This bound has a complex combinatorial structure, and the known procedures to compute it have exponential time complexity. This makes evaluation of the bound practically infeasible for large networks. In this paper, we present a genetic algorithm that provides a high-quality solution to the partition bound computation problem, thus rendering an upper bound and enabling new insights into information flow in large networks.

title: Benchmarking Heuristic and Cooperative Agents in AbstractSwarm Logistics Scenarios
authors: SM Mehedi Hasan, Doina Logofătu
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: The long-term goal of artificial intelligence (AI) is to develop systems that can generalize across diverse domains and solve complex coordination problems. Logistics scenarios provide an effective benchmark for this objective because they require decentralized decision-making, temporal scheduling, and dynamic cooperation. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of the AbstractSwarm Multi-Agent Logistics Competition, held at GECCO 2023, which employed the AbstractSwarm simulation system as a testbed for cooperative agents. Six heterogeneous scenarios were used to model challenges ranging from patient scheduling and ward coordination to traffic control, warehouse storage, and last-mile delivery. Agents submitted by participants were executed for 100 runs with 20 repetitions per scenario, and performance was measured using normalized waiting times with scenario-specific baselines. The results, summarized in detailed tables, reveal a trade-off between specialization and generalization: some agents excelled in structured scheduling tasks such as Treatments and X-Ray Scheduling, while others demonstrated moderate robustness across all scenarios but struggled in dynamic environments such as Crossroads or sparse-reward tasks like Storage. These findings highlight both the promise and limitations of swarm intelligence for logistics and motivate future research into hybrid methods, reinforcement learning strategies, and scalable coordination mechanisms for real-world applications.

title: A Low-Complexity Equalizer Design for OTFS Modulation in Doubly-Dispersive Channels
authors: Rabah Ouchikh, Thierry Chonavel, Abdeldjalil Aïssa-El-Bey, Mustapha Djeddou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulation has emerged as a promising technique for reliable communication over rapidly time-varying channels, thanks to its robustness against Doppler effects and delay spread. However, the design of efficient equalizers for OTFS systems remains a key challenge due to the high computational complexity involved. In this paper, we propose a low-complexity linear equalizer for OTFS modulation that exploits the inherent structure of the effective channel matrix. Specifically, the proposed method leverages the block-circulant property and the sparse nature of the OTFS channel matrix and applies a Cholesky-based decomposition to significantly reduce computational cost. Simulation results confirm that the proposed equalizer achieves notable improvements in both bit error rate performance and computational efficiency, outperforming existing state-of-the-art techniques.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Feature Fusion and Multi-Level Domain Adaptation Network for Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition
authors: Chuwen Xie, Rongtao Chen, Zhuoyi Huang, Jiahui Zhang, Lina Qiu, Jiahui Pan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based emotion recognition has emerged as a crucial technique in affective computing, providing an objective measure of emotions. However, inter-subject variability in EEG data distribution causes substantial domain shifts, severely limiting model generalization across subjects. To address this challenge, we propose M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net, a novel network that integrates multi-dimensional feature fusion and multi-level domain adaptation for cross-subject EEG emotion recognition. M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net employs a multi-branch parallel architecture to extract spatial, temporal, and frequency features, which are then adaptively fused via a cross-attention mechanism to form robust, domain-invariant representations. Furthermore, a hierarchical adversarial alignment module is introduced to simultaneously adapt both global marginal and class-aware conditional distributions. For precise conditional alignment, we design a dual-standard pseudo-labeling strategy combining confidence and feature-center proximity to generate reliable supervisory signals. Experiments on SEED and SEED-IV show that M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net achieves state-of-the-art accuracies of 95.56%±3.81% and 87.38%±7.62%. Source code is available at: https://github.com/xiechuwen1/M-2Net.

title: Cross-View Change Detection via Self-Supervised Contrastive Representation Learning
authors: Ghassen Graja, Olfa Besbes, Takoua Abdellatif
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Change detection (CD) is a fundamental task in remote sensing. Existing CD methods often require precise co-registration, geometric correction, and large annotated datasets, limiting their scalability in heterogeneous or time-critical scenarios. We address cross-view heterogeneous CD between archived satellite imagery and newly acquired UAV data under annotation scarcity. We propose a self-supervised contrastive learning framework that learns robust representations from unlabeled bi-temporal pairs. Our method integrates CutMix-based data augmentation and a cross-attention module within a Swin Transformer backbone to enhance multi-scale, cross-modal feature fusion. Experiments on a challenging cross-view dataset demonstrate that our approach outperforms strong baselines with minimal supervision, highlighting its potential for efficient and reliable real-world change detection.

title: Unigeo: A Unified 3d Indoor Object Detection Framework Integrating Geometry-Aware Learning and Dynamic Channel Gating
authors: Xing Yi, Jinyang Huang, Feng-Qi Cui, Anyang Tong, Ruimin Wang, Liu Liu, Dan Guo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing adoption of robotics and augmented reality in real-world applications has driven considerable research interest in 3D object detection based on point clouds. While previous methods address unified training across multiple datasets, they fail to model geometric relationships in sparse point cloud scenes and ignore the feature distribution in significant areas, which ultimately restricts their performance. To deal with this issue, a unified 3D indoor detection framework, called UniGeo, is proposed. To model geometric relations in scenes, we first propose a geometry-aware learning module that establishes a learnable mapping from spatial relationships to feature weights, which enabes explicit geometric feature enhancement. Then, to further enhance point cloud feature representation, we propose a dynamic channel gating mechanism that leverages learnable channel-wise weighting. This mechanism adaptively optimizes features generated by the sparse 3D U-Net network, significantly enhancing key geometric information. Extensive experiments on six different indoor scene datasets clearly validate the superior performance of our method.

title: Adversarial Defense via Generative Speech Enhancement Module
authors: Chi-Tao Chen, Chun-Shien Lu, Jia-Ching Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning models excel in speech processing but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, posing critical risks in safety-sensitive applications. We introduce a defense framework that integrates Gaussian noise injection with a speech enhancement module to mitigate adversarial perturbations while preserving perceptual quality and semantic integrity. Evaluations on SC09, VCTK, and QKWS demonstrate that our approach achieves superior robustness against diverse white-box and black-box attacks, including PGD with varied steps, norm constraints, EOT budgets, and the query-based FakeBob. Additionally, our framework offers significantly faster inference and a compact 2M-parameter footprint, enabling deployment in real-time and resource-constrained settings.

title: IGCNET: Dual-Branch Implicit Feature and Global Context Network for Agricultural Parcel Delineation
authors: Ye Li, Liruizhi Jia, Chao Liu, Bo Kong, Yuan Liu, Shengquan Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The delineation of farmland parcels is fundamental for agricultural monitoring and resource management, yet existing methods often suffer from boundary incompleteness, irregular shapes, and global inconsistency. To address these issues, we propose the Implicit–Global Context Network (IGCNet), which integrates two key branches: one for implicit feature interactions to enhance nonlinear local representations, and another for global context modeling that captures directional long-range dependencies through a state-space mechanism. Experiments on the Netherlands and FHAPD-JS datasets show that IGCNet achieves superior boundary accuracy and structural integrity under diverse farmland conditions. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of the parallel dual-branch design in balancing fine-grained boundary refinement with global consistency. These results demonstrate that IGCNet provides a reliable and adaptable solution for farmland parcel segmentation, supporting applications in crop monitoring, yield estimation, and precision agriculture. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Prodigal-lqg/IGCNET/.

title: Combining Multi-Order Attention and Multi-Resolution Discriminator for High-Fidelity Neural Vocoder
authors: Yan Shi, Jin Shi, Minchuan Chen, Ziyang Zhuang, Peng Qi, Shaojun Wang, Jing Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generative Adversarial Network (GAN-based) vocoders have gained significant attention for generate high-quality audio with fast inference speed. A typical issue with GAN-based models is phase and artifacts in synthesized speech. In this work, we propose a scalable hierarchical architecture incorporating stacked multi-order spatial-channel attention to enhance feature representation learning. This design enables progressive refinement of acoustic features through attention-guided processing. Moreover, we propose a multi-resolution full-band discriminator, which simultaneously captures local spectral details and global waveform coherence with multiple resolutions, effectively mitigating blurring artifacts that commonly occur in conventional discriminators. Additionally, our discriminator explicitly integrates both the magnitude and complex-domain representations by jointly processing the magnitude, real, and imaginary components. The experimental results show that our proposed method outperforms the baseline models in objective and subjective metrics. Audio samples are available online <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: Magnitude Difference Conditioned All-in-One Image Restoration
authors: Jungwoo Bae, Jitae Shin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent all-in-one image restoration systems often rely on heavy spatial conditioning, inflating parameters and latency, which hinders real-world deployment. We present MDIR, a lightweight framework that conditions a backbone using a compact frequency-domain signature: a 1D Fourier magnitude difference vector with log-spaced radial bins and sinusoidal embeddings. At test time, a small magnitude predictor estimates the clean magnitude directly from degraded inputs for on-the-fly conditioning. This conditioning is injected via adaptive layer normalization, applied strongly in the decoder and lightly in late encoder/skip blocks. The backbone replaces heavy self-attention with efficient spatial propagation and a simplified residual design. Across dehazing, deraining, denoising, deblurring, and low-light enhancement, MDIR delivers substantial latency and memory savings while matching or surpassing heavier schemes, offering a practical solution for real-world deployment. Our codes will be available at: https://github.com/BaeJungWoo/MDIR

title: Bounding Box-Based 3D Mapping with UGV–UAV Collaboration for Precision Agriculture
authors: Accacio F. dos Santos Neto, Manuel B. Couto, Marcelo R. Petry, António Paulo Moreira, Paolo Mercorelli
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Building 3D maps in agricultural environments is challenging due to dense vegetation, irregular terrain, lack of landmarks, and unreliable GPS. This paper proposes a Bounding Box-Based 3D Mapping method using collaboration between an Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV) and an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV). The method simplifies crop rows and tree canopies by enclosing their point clouds in 3D bounding boxes, fused with original UAV and UGV data, producing compact maps that preserve essential structures for autonomous navigation and trajectory planning. Evaluation in a simulated Orchard scenario shows that the method could reduce map size by up to 60% while maintaining 83.6% coverage. Multi-robot collaboration proved crucial, with the UGV contributing 74% and the UAV 26% of the merged map. Overall, the proposed method demonstrates potential and deserves further investigation in more complex agricultural scenarios.

title: A New Method and Dataset for Classroom Teaching Stage Segmentation
authors: Shihao Yang, Nan Zhang, Yue Jiang, Ziyi Zhang, Shuhua Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current integrations of Artificial Intelligence (AI) with classroom teaching primarily focus on individual scenarios or simple tasks, with few studies addressing the segmentation of classroom teaching stages. However, an effective classroom teaching process requires complete teaching stages, which is particularly crucial for pre-service teacher training. This paper is the first study on the issue of teaching stage segmentation. First, we construct a teaching stage segmentation dataset, which includes 1,928 lessons. The classroom teaching is divided into five stages, namely, review, introduction, explanation, summary, and assignment based on the logical structure of the teaching content by educational experts. The dataset includes three modalities: transcribed text, audio, and video. Furthermore, we propose a multimodal classroom teaching stage segmentation method with two key innovations: a general multimodal clustering-separation loss framework and An entropy-based dynamic weighting strategy for multimodal fusion. The experiment verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Collaborative Graph Contrastive Network for Semi-Supervised Graph Node Classification
authors: Xiaoyong Huang, Heli Sun, Guanlan Yue, Pan Lou, Liang He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Semi-supervised node classification reduces reliance on labeled data. While GNNs (e.g., GCN, GAT) exploit local structures, they often miss global topology; contrastive methods (e.g., MVGRL) face rigid embeddings or poor view design. We propose the Collaborative Graph Contrastive Network (CGCN) to jointly capture local and global semantics via a dual-encoder framework with cross-view alignment. A fine-grained collaborative constraint preserves node similarity by softly regularizing cross-view correlation matrices, avoiding over-compression. Additionally, a category-aware contrastive loss enhances class compactness and separation with minimal labels. Experiments on six benchmarks show CGCN outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Ablation studies validate each component and demonstrate robustness under complex topologies.

title: BMDC: A Hybrid Bigbird and Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution Model For Seizure Detection From Scalp EEG
authors: Hao Geng, Jinjie Guo, Guanglong Zhang, Guixia Kang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate seizure detection from electroencephalogram (EEG) remains challenging due to long temporal spans and complex dynamics. EEG signals contain both temporal dependencies and spatial correlations, requiring joint modeling for reliable detection. Existing methods often employ Transformers for spatial representation and LSTMs for temporal dynamics, but suffer from quadratic attention complexity and sequential inefficiency. We propose BMDC, a hybrid model combining BigBird sparse attention with Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution. BigBird captures global and local dependencies with linear complexity, while Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution extracts multi-scale temporal features in parallel. Evaluated on the Temple University Seizure Corpus (TUSZ), BMDC consistently outperforms Transformer–LSTM and other baselines in AUROC, accuracy and Weighted F1-score, while achieving faster inference, demonstrating its scalability and robustness for clinical seizure detection.

title: Adversarial Label Recovery with Multi-Modal Fusion and Dual-Task Contrastive Learning
authors: Haoran Fu, Wenhui Cheng, Chundong Wang, Lijun Shu, Zhiyong Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Adversarial examples pose serious threats to the reliability of AI systems, especially in safety-critical applications. Existing detection methods often exhibit poor robustness, undermining their trustworthiness under adversarial attacks. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-modal feature-fusion framework for adversarial label recovery. Our framework integrates visual–semantic embeddings from CLIP, spatial representations from VGG16, gradient-based structural features, and frequency-domain signatures. These heterogeneous features are unified through a transformer architecture that captures cross-modal dependencies, yielding a more discriminative and robust representation space. The framework is optimized with a dual-task contrastive learning scheme, jointly performing image classification for label recovery and adversarial detection. The detection module is further reinforced with contrastive learning to improve discriminability. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art label recovery accuracy on CIFAR-10, significantly surpassing existing approaches.

title: DPAnet: Dual Pyramid Attention Network for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Qianyang Li, Xingjun Zhang, Shaoxun Wang, Wei Jia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) is hampered by the challenge of modeling complex dependencies that span multiple temporal scales and frequency resolutions. Existing methods, including Transformer and MLP-based models, often struggle to capture these intertwined characteristics in a unified and structured manner. We propose the Dual Pyramid Attention Network (DPANet), a novel architecture that explicitly decouples and concurrently models temporal multi-scale dynamics and spectral multi-resolution periodicities. DPANet constructs two parallel pyramids: a Temporal Pyramid built on progressive downsampling, and a Frequency Pyramid built on band-pass filtering. The core of our model is the Cross-Pyramid Fusion Block, which facilitates deep, interactive information exchange between corresponding pyramid levels via cross-attention. This fusion proceeds in a coarse-to-fine hierarchy, enabling global context to guide local representation learning. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks show that DPANet achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming prior models. Code is available at https://github.com/hit636/DPANet.

title: TGME-Net: Text-Guided Multi-Scale Enhancement Network for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Feng Li, Chen Sun, Bing Wang, Jian Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pseudo labels quality is pivotal to the performance of semi-supervised medical image segmentation. Recent studies have explored the use of vision language models (VLM) to improve the quality of pseudo labels by enhancing their ability to describe lesion regions. Nevertheless, existing methods often exhibit limitations such as excessive reliance on textual modalities, performance plateaus, and insufficient integration of multi-grained information. To address these issues, we propose a Text-Guided Multi-scale Enhancement (TGME) network, which enhances pseudo labels through the accumulation of multi-scale cross-modal supervision. Specifically, TGME establishes cross-modal semantic alignments between multi-scale image patches and text tokens, thereby preserving both fine-grained and coarse-grained informative features. Additionally, we introduce a novel learning paradigm named Conflict Feature Supervised Learning (CFSL) to achieve spatial perception of text-described regions within feature. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: DynaPredict: Alternating Predictive and Real Iteration for Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning Training
authors: Yiran Liu, Peng Qiao, Rongchun Li, Zhouyu He, Tao Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sample-inefficient in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) impedes scalability and practical deployment. While model-based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) improves efficiency through environmental simulation, it suffers from model bias and high computational costs. Conversely, conventional optimizers adapt poorly to DRL’s non-stationary optimization landscapes. To bridge this gap, we introduce DynaPredict, a novel optimization framework that transposes the concept of environmental simulation from observation space to parameter space. Instead of predicting observations, DynaPredict employs a lightweight predictive component to forecast the trajectory of policy parameter updates, thereby implicitly encoding environmental feedback. Our method alternates between predictive and real update cycles, drastically reducing environment interactions while avoiding model bias. Empirically, DynaPredict achieves consistent performance gains across the Atari benchmark, reducing sample complexity by up to 60% and accelerating wall-clock convergence time, demonstrating benefits orthogonal to specific algorithms or parallel architectures.

title:
MH-A
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    : Metropolis-Hastings Anomaly-Aware Augmentation For Contrastive Graph Anomaly Detection
authors: Maoji Wen, Chaohao Yuan, Jiacheng Hou, Ercan Engin Kuruoglu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Contrastive unsupervised graph anomaly detection (UGAD) hinges on augmented views. Fixed-strength, heuristic perturbations often erase rare signals or yield easy negatives: they tune a deletion budget rather than the node-perceived impact, and they do not adapt diversity as the encoder evolves. MH-A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> reframes augmentation as sampling from a target over views via a Metropolis–Hastings sampler. A K-hop message-accumulation statistic aligns each candidate’s effective strength to a desired level, preserving anomaly evidence; a global uncertainty signal sets the target’s variance, widening exploration when the model is unsure and tightening it when confident to avoid easy negatives. Sampling is coupled with learning in an accept-once, learn-once loop, keeping views matched to the current encoder. The plug-in is backbone-agnostic and leaves inference unchanged; attached to CoLA, ANEMONE, SL-GAD and GRADATE, it lifts ROC–AUC up to +1.84 with an average +0.93 across five benchmarks and yields hard-but-valid contrasts under standard UGAD protocols.

title: WATEMP: Acoustic-Based Non-Contact Water Temperature Measurement System Using Smartphones
authors: Younho Nam, Su Yeon Shim, Young-Joo Suh
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Measuring water temperature is necessary for real-world applications, including proper infant formula concentration, stable aquarium conditions, and food safety. However, existing methods often pose a risk of contamination or require dedicated hardware. To address these limitations, we propose WaTemp, a non-contact water temperature measurement system that utilizes acoustic signals with only a commodity smartphone. Our framework leverages a domain adversarial neural network that learns temperature-relevant yet domain-invariant representations, delivering robust performance across varied containers and volumes. Furthermore, a few-shot learning enables simple calibration to the user’s own containers. Our experiments demonstrate that the system achieves a mean absolute error of 1.55°C within a 25-70°C range.

title: Dataset-Driven Channel Masks in Transformers for Multivariate Time Series
authors: Seunghan Lee, Taeyoung Park, Kibok Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Capturing channel dependency (CD) is essential for modeling multivariate time series (TS), and attention-based methods have been widely employed for this purpose. Nonetheless, these methods primarily focus on modifying the architecture, often neglecting the importance of dataset-specific characteristics. In this work, we introduce the concept of partial channel dependence (PCD) to enhance CD modeling in Transformer-based models by leveraging dataset-specific information to refine the CD captured by the model. To achieve PCD, we propose channel masks (CMs), which are integrated into the attention matrices of Transformers via element-wise multiplication. CMs consist of two components: 1) a similarity matrix that captures relationships between the channels, and 2) dataset-specific and learnable domain parameters that refine the similarity matrix. We validate the effectiveness of PCD across diverse tasks and datasets with various backbones.

title: Photo Shielding: Robust Protection Against AI Manipulation of Images
authors: Amit Chaulwar, Mohammad Haseeb, Vanraj Vala, Ravi Sankar Guntur
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In the era of generative AI, malicious photo manipulation which are shared on social media poses significant risks. Existing methods embed imperceptible noise to protect images but are vulnerable to counterattacks such as image compression and adversarial purification. Their high computational costs also limit practical on-device implementation, a key concern for users prioritizing privacy. To address these issues, we introduce Photo Shielding. Our method employs a novel adversarial perturbation calculation tailored for on-device use, ensuring noise persistence post-compression. It achieves a 5x speedup over prior techniques and demonstrates effectiveness through qualitative and quantitative results, balancing protection and computational efficiency.

title: Artificial Intelligence Enabled Smart Sensors Associated Real-Time Human Fall Detection and Reporting System
authors: M Dinesh, N R Rejin Paul, Lakshmi N, P Lavanya, Rathika S, Ramachandran A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Elderly and physically impaired falls are a pressing issue of importance within the community, as they may result in severe injuries, follow-up hospitalization, and even death unless appropriate help is delivered to them. In this paper, a sophisticated real-time fall detection and alert system using artificial intelligence (AI), and smart embedded sensor systems is discussed. The proposed architecture incorporates tri-axial accelerators and gyroscopes with CNN-LSTM-based classification model running on edge devices such as Raspberry Pi, and thus allows low-latency inference without using cloud computing. The reliability of motion analysis is improved with the help of a sophisticated preprocessing pipeline such as signal smoothing, feature extraction and normalization. Experimental verification with SisFall data set and real-life experiments show that the system is able to recognize fall instances with high accuracy and legitimacy and daily activities. This model has an average accuracy of 97.8, F1-score of 96.8, and a false alarm rate of only 2.1, which is much better in performance compared to the traditional models based on threshold and machine learning based models. When the fall is detected, the system will automatically send alerts through SMS, mobile app, and locally located by GPS on a cloud dashboard. The outcomes are in line with the solution as a viable and effective solution to proactive health surveillance. Offline feature, rapid response, and scalability make it one of the promising additions to smart healthcare and assistive technology worlds.

title: An Improved Objective-Oriented ICT Framework for Strengthening Communications Over Healthcare Supply Chain Management System
authors: S. Surendharan, Krithika M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The increased complexity of healthcare logistics requires strong, secure and interoperable communication systems to handle real time supply coordination particularly in critical situations like pandemics or disruption in the supply chain. In this work, we propose an Improved Objective-Oriented ICT Framework capable of incorporating new computational approaches to become more resilient, traceable, and make better decisions throughout healthcare supply chains. Through the framework, Federated Healthcare Ontology Mapping (FHOM) and Transformational Interlingua Translation (TIT) has been used to provide semantic interoperability between heterogeneous systems. Quantum Blockchain Mesh (QBM) improves the integrity of the data and Bio-inspired Fuzzy Multi-Agents Systems allow prioritizing the working process. The T-CapsNet is capable of predictive detecting the bottlenecks in communication and Causality-Aware Graph Reinforcement Learning is able to optimize routing policies. Also, there is the homomorphic Federated Tensor Factorization (HFTF) that guarantees privacy-conscious collaboration among data, and Neuro-Symbolic Probabilistic Logic Networks (NS-PLN) that can provide context-aware alerts. Cross-experimental results were gained at 20 hospitals and 10 vendors where overall semantic mapping accuracy was found to be 94.8, blockchain communication success rate was 96.6, and bottleneck prediction accuracy was 95.3, and alert precision was greater than 95.2. The suggested system is more responsive, transparent, and trustworthy than the current models, which creates a baseline to be continued with scalable, ethical, AI-driven healthcare logistics infrastructures.

title: Text2Try3D: Text-Guided 3D Garment Generation on Parametric Human Models
authors: Weilong Peng, Xi Chen, Meng Xing, Keke Tang, Meie Fang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Text-driven 3D human modeling has emerged as a powerful paradigm for controllable avatar creation, with growing applications in virtual try-on, gaming, and digital content production. However, existing generative methods often entangle identity, pose, and clothing representation, leading to limited garment controllability and weak structural consistency. We introduce Text2Try3D, a modular framework that disentangles appearance synthesis and geometry refinement for customizable clothed human avatars. Our approach leverages a UV-structured diffusion prior to generate high-fidelity textures from text, and applies score-based optimization to align mesh geometry with the synthesized appearance. Built upon parametric models like SMPL, it ensures compatibility with animation pipelines while enabling realistic and structured garment generation. Experiments demonstrate strong performance in appearance quality, geometric alignment, and prompt consistency.

title: Frequency-Modulated Differential Transformer for Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images
authors: Xin Li, Feng Xu, Jue Zhang, Xin Lyu, Fan Liu, Lingqiang Meng, Yiwei Fang, André Kaup
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing images (HRRSIs) is crucial for a wide range of geospatial applications. However, complex textures, ambiguous boundaries, and high background redundancy often degrade model performance. In this paper, we propose FMDFormer, a novel Frequency-Modulated Differential Transformer designed to enhance segmentation precision by integrating spatial differential attention with frequency-domain modulation. Specifically, we introduce the FMDFormer Block, a dual-branch architecture that combines spatial-domain differential attention with frequency-enhanced features derived via Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). This design enables effective suppression of irrelevant activations while emphasizing high-frequency cues such as boundaries and contours. A gated fusion mechanism adaptively integrates both branches to enhance feature diversity and localization capability. Extensive experiments on ISPRS Potsdam and LoveDA datasets demonstrate that FMDFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing existing CNN and transformer-based methods. Comprehensive ablations validate the complementary advantages of the proposed dual-domain attention mechanism.

title: AdaEvol: Dynamic Adapter Merging for Effective Continual Learning and Knowledge Transfer in Large Language Models
authors: Ran Liu, Min Yu, Jianguo Jiang, Gang Li, Rongsheng Li, Mingqi Liu, Zhen Xu, Weiqing Huang, Ming Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) allows large language models (LLMs) to adapt efficiently, yet existing methods for continual learning often require sequential fine-tuning from scratch and rely on auxiliary routing or selection modules, increasing training complexity. Meanwhile, many open-source LoRA adapters already encode transferable knowledge. To this end, we introduce AdaEvol, a continual learning framework that reuses and evolves LoRAs for efficient adaptation and knowledge transfer. For each new task, AdaEvol rapidly identifies and updates the most compatible LoRA, while at inference it integrates multiple LoRAs without a routing mechanism. Both theoretical analysis and empirical results confirm the effectiveness of AdaEvol. It surpasses training from scratch in single-task adaptation and joint fine-tuning in multi-task continual learning. Moreover, it uniquely achieves positive backward transfer on related tasks, a capability absent in prior state-of-the-art methods.

title: PMSS-NeXt: An Efficient Polarized Multi-Scale Synergy Network for Complex Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Qingxue Zhao, Di Wu, Jiakang Gao, Siqi Wang, Jun Tian
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficient and accurate encoding-decoding frameworks are critical for medical image segmentation, especially under limited computational resources. While traditional U-Net architectures struggle to capture global multi-scale context, and hybrid CNN-Transformer models are often too computationally expensive, a significant performance-efficiency gap remains. To address this, we propose PMSS-NeXt, a novel network that integrates an efficient encoding and decoding framework. PMSS-NeXt enhances feature maps using multi-scale grouped convolutions and incorporates a specialized polarization channel attention mechanism alongside large-kernel depthwise separable convolution gating. This design synergistically integrates complex global multi-scale context with a strong focus on local details. We conducted rigorous evaluations on nine diverse 2D medical image segmentation datasets. Results show that PMSS-NeXt consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) models. Notably, compared to the highest-performing competitor, our model achieves a remarkable reduction of 99.67% in parameters and 99.71% in computational complexity (FLOPs), establishing a new benchmark in high-performance, lightweight medical image segmentation. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

title: Dual-Guided Generative Frame Interpolation
authors: Yiying Wei, Hadi Amirpour, Christian Timmerer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video frame interpolation (VFI) aims to generate intermediate frames between given keyframes to enhance temporal resolution and visual smoothness. While conventional optical flow–based methods and recent generative approaches achieve promising results, they often struggle with large displacements, failing to maintain temporal coherence and semantic consistency. In this work, we propose dual-guided generative frame interpolation (DGFI), a framework that integrates semantic guidance from vision-language models and flow guidance into a pre-trained diffusion-based image-to-video (I2V) generator. Specifically, DGFI extracts textual descriptions and injects multimodal embeddings to capture high-level semantics, while estimated motion guidance provides smooth transitions. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-guided method over the state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Empirical Analysis of Approximate Message Passing Under non-I.I.D. Measurements with Comparison to State Evolution
authors: Tomoharu Furudoi, Takumi Takahashi, Hideki Ochiai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper investigates a denoiser design that enhances the robustness of approximate message passing (AMP) while retaining high estimation performance. The minimum mean squared error (MMSE)-based Bayesian denoiser, naturally derived from the belief propagation framework, is the most widely used in AMP. While this choice ensures optimal convergence under the ideal i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) measurements in the large-system limit, its aggressiveness increases the sensitivity of AMP to model mismatch. To address this issue, we design a denoiser that adaptively regulates the convergence behavior to improve robustness. We further show that its convergence dynamics can be empirically predicted under non-i.i.d. measurements by heuristically modifying the state evolution equations according to the asymptotic spectrum of measurement matrices.

title: Empowering Transformers Spectrally: Towards Comprehensive Pattern Learning for Image Demoiréing
authors: Sungjun Hong, Seung Woo Kwak, Sangyun Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Digital screen photography frequently suffers from moiré patterns caused by frequency aliasing between camera sensors and display pixels. Removing moiré artifacts is challenging due to their varying color, shape, and scale. Existing methods primarily operate in the spatial domain and thus overlook crucial frequency-domain information inherent in moiré patterns. To address this, we propose Spectrally Empowered Demoiréing Transformers (SEDT), a U-shaped Transformer integrating a Frequency Domain Modulator (FDM) within each Transformer block. The FDM performs spectral filtering through learnable modulation of frequency components, such as channel-wise affine transformations and band-wise frequency masking, enabling effective extraction of moiré-related features across multiple scales and frequency ranges. Evaluations on the TIP-2018 dataset demonstrate that SEDT significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in both PSNR and SSIM, highlighting the potential of frequency-domain enhancements in Transformer architectures for advanced image restoration tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/sylee-skhu/SEDT.

title: TrafficGS: Sparse-View Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Roadside Traffic Scene Modeling and Streaming
authors: Renwei Hai, Haolin Zhang, Pengfei Dong, Shitao Chen, Jingmin Xin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent neural rendering techniques, particularly Gaussian Splatting, have achieved remarkable progress in dynamic scene modeling. However, these methods typically rely on dense multi-view inputs, which are impractical for roadside sensor setups. To address this, we propose TrafficGS, a novel method for dynamic traffic scene modeling based on fixed, sparse-view roadside sensors. TrafficGS first generates globally consistent depth maps by fusing LiDAR and camera data through a depth completion network, which also initializes the first frame for Gaussian primitives. We then employ multi-resolution hash encoding to capture the traffic flow across frames and introduce a scene-completion module that repairs missing regions using a ground-plane decoupling mask. Additionally, we construct a dataset specifically designed for evaluating cross-view rendering in roadside scenes. Experiments conducted on this dataset demonstrate that TrafficGS outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving substantial improvements in traffic scene reconstruction and rendering.

title: QP-SAM: Query-Based Prompt Generation for Segment Anything Model in Urban Village Identification
authors: Xinyuan Guo, Huijie Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Urban village identification is vital for sustainable development, yet complex morphology and dense structures challenge conventional segmentation methods. While the Segment Anything Model (SAM) demonstrates strong cross-scene segmentation, its heavy reliance on external prompts directly affects reliability and deployment costs. To address this, we propose Query-based Prompt-SAM (QP-SAM), which reformulates prompt generation as a set prediction problem for efficient domain adaptation of SAM. Specifically, with SAM parameters fully frozen, QP-SAM employs a query-based prompt predictor that directly generates sparse and effective bounding box prompts with a few learnable queries, employing the Hungarian algorithm for precise instance matching. Furthermore, with instance- and image-level supervision alongside mask–box consistency constraints, our approach ensures prompt plausibility and segmentation integrity. Experiments on urban village remote sensing datasets show that QP-SAM introduces only minimal additional parameters while achieving higher segmentation accuracy than existing state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/woshixinyuan/Query-based-Prompt-SAM.

title: Dual-Branch Spatial-Lighting Network for Photometric Stereo
authors: Xiaoyan Tian, Ye Jin, Zhao Zhang, Peng Liu, Fenglei Ni, Dansong Cheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Photometric stereo recovers surface geometry by analyzing intensity variations under varying lighting conditions, offering high precision in fine-detail reconstruction. However, existing methods face challenges in unconstrained environments: per-pixel models lack global context, whereas all-pixel approaches often lose high-frequency detail. This study addresses the challenge of reconciling global contextual understanding with pixel-wise detail preservation in photometric stereo measurements. A novel dual-branch feature extraction neural architecture augmented by a multisuper-visory gradient propagation scheme is introduced. The proposed framework employs multiscale encoding and decoding mechanisms to integrate global and local representations, enabling end-to-end mapping from the input image sequences to surface normal estimations. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms nearly 20 state-of-the-art methods, highlighting robustness and practical applicability. The code and pretrained model weights are publicly available at https://github.com/zz-ux/ICASSP_2026.

title: RadarEye: Robust Liquid Level Tracking Using Mmwave Radar in Robotic Pouring
authors: Hongyu Deng, He Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transparent liquid manipulation in robotic pouring remains challenging for perception systems: specular/refraction effects and lighting variability degrade visual cues, undermining reliable level estimation. To address this challenge, we introduce RadarEye, a real-time mmWave radar signal processing pipeline for robust liquid level estimation and tracking during the whole pouring process. RadarEye integrates (i) a high-resolution range–angle beamforming module for liquid level sensing and (ii) a physics-informed mid-pour tracker that suppresses multipath to maintain lock on the liquid surface despite stream-induced clutter and source container reflections. The pipeline delivers sub-millisecond latency. In real-robot water-pouring experiments, RadarEye achieves a 0.35 cm median absolute height error at 0.62 ms per update, substantially outperforming vision and ultrasound baselines.

title: GTCL: Graph-Text Contrastive Learning Meets Log Anomaly Detection
authors: Xiao Tan, Jiaxiang Liu, Yujie Wu, Mingkun Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Log analysis is a fundamental technique for diagnosing issues in large-scale modern computing systems. Existing log anomaly detection methods are often unimodal, struggling to effectively integrate both structural information and deep semantic features inherent in log events. To address this issue, we propose a new Graph-Text Contrastive Learning (GTCL) framework tailored for log anomaly detection. GTCL employs a GNN-based graph encoder and an LM-based text encoder to capture complementary features, and unifies them via a graph–text contrastive loss. Across multiple public log benchmarks, GTCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, it remains stable across a wide range of sequence window sizes, suggesting robustness to hyperparameter choices and workload variability typical of real-world logging pipelines.
