Our Depth-Adaptive Anchor Graph (DAAG) then selects depth and density-aware anchors via Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) and Determinantal Point Process (DPP) and propagates context with a lightweight GCN, after which a prediction head regresses the image-level count. On three of the representative crowd-counting benchmarks, DAAGNet delivers significant improvements over existing counting methods.

title: Distribution-Aware Mobility-Assisted Decentralized Federated Learning
authors: Md Farhamdur Reza, Reza Jahani, Richeng Jin, Huaiyu Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) eliminates reliance on a central server by enabling peer-to-peer model aggregation, but suffers from limited connectivity and non-IID data. In many applications, some clients are mobile, yet the role of mobility in DFL remains underexplored. This work shows that introducing even a small fraction of mobile clients, moving randomly, significantly improves information flow and accuracy. To further enhance performance, we propose structured distribution-aware mobility strategies, where mobile clients navigate the network using knowledge of data distributions and static client locations. These strategies mitigate the impact of data heterogeneity and accelerate convergence. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of induced mobility in DFL and demonstrate the superiority of our proposed mobility patterns over random movement.

title: Numerical Spectrum Linking: Identification of Governing PDE via Koopman-Chebyshev Approximation
authors: Phonepaserth Sisaykeo, Shogo Muramatsu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: A numerical framework is proposed for identifying partial differential equations (PDEs) governing dynamical systems directly from their observation data using Chebyshev polynomial approximation. In contrast to data-driven approaches such as dynamic mode decomposition (DMD), which approximate the Koopman operator without a clear connection to differential operators, the proposed method constructs finite-dimensional Koopman matrices by projecting the dynamics onto a Chebyshev basis, thereby capturing both differential and nonlinear terms. This establishes a numerical link between the Koopman and differential operators. Numerical experiments on benchmark dynamical systems confirm the accuracy and efficiency of the approach, underscoring its potential for interpretable operator learning. The framework also lays a foundation for future integration with symbolic regression, enabling the construction of explicit mathematical models directly from data.

title: Ultralight IPM-DAE: An Ultra-Lightweight ECG Denoising Autoencoder Via Parallel Mamba and Multi-Scale Fusion
authors: Ruizhe Wang, Zhanyi Liu, Honghao Li, Jiaqi Liang, Jie Sun, Min Xiang, Xiaolin Ning
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) is an important tool for diagnosing cardiovascular diseases. Clinically acquired ECG signals are often contaminated by various types of noise, severely impairing subsequent analysis. However, traditional methods are limited in denoising long-term complex ECG signals and often struggle to balance performance and efficiency. Therefore, we propose an ultra-lightweight denoising autoencoder (Ultralight IPM-DAE) for efficiently removing mixed noise in ECG signals. Specifically, Ultralight IPM-DAE integrates a parallel Mamba based on independent state matrices and a multi-scale information fusion module. The former is used to enhance the model’s ability to capture long-range dependencies in ECG signals, while the latter facilitates the integration and complementation of multi-scale information for more comprehensive feature representation. Experimental results demonstrate that Ultralight IPM-DAE achieves superior denoising performance with lower computational complexity, indicating higher clinical acceptability and validating the potential of Mamba as an ultra-lightweight backbone for electrophysiological signal analysis. The source code is available at: https://github.com/RZWangbuaa/Ultralight-IPM-DAE.

title: Test-Time Adaptation for Speech Enhancement via Mask Polarization
authors: Tobias Raichle, Erfan Amini, Bin Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Adapting speech enhancement (SE) models to unseen environments is crucial for practical deployments, yet test-time adaptation (TTA) for SE remains largely under-explored due to a lack of understanding of how SE models degrade under domain shifts. We observe that mask-based SE models lose confidence under domain shifts, with predicted masks becoming flattened and losing decisive speech preservation and noise suppression. Based on this insight, we propose mask polarization (MPol), a lightweight TTA method that restores mask bimodality through distribution comparison using the Wasserstein distance. MPol requires no additional parameters beyond the trained model, making it suitable for resource-constrained edge deployments. Experimental results across diverse domain shifts and architectures demonstrate that MPol achieves very consistent gains that are competitive with significantly more complex approaches.

title: Disentangled Authenticity Representation for Partially Deepfake Audio Localization
authors: Siding Zeng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Partially deepfake audio, where only short segments of speech are manipulated, has emerged as a significant challenge to audio security, since reliable localization of manipulated regions is critical for authenticity verification. This task is difficult due to domain shifts between training and evaluation data and the subtle transitions at manipulation boundaries. We propose Disentangled Authenticity Representation (DisAR), a dual-branch framework that separates authenticity cues from nuisance factors such as speaker identity and recording conditions. To retain full information and improve robustness, DisAR incorporates a gated fusion-reconstruction module, while a local temporal contrastive loss enhances boundary sensitivity by contrasting embeddings across real-fake transitions. Experiments on PartialSpoof and ADD 2023 Track 2 show that DisAR achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1-scores of 96.54% and 76.74% respectively, outperforming recent baselines under both in-domain and cross-domain settings.

title: EHLM-Gen: Explicit Hierarchical And Localized Visual Context Modeling For Histopathology Report Generation
authors: Chengxin Ye, Guangli Li, Shiying Zeng, Kaichun Hu, Jingqin Lv, Qingzhong Zhou, Hongbin Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Histopathology report generation (HRG) from whole slide images (WSIs) is crucial for computer-aided cancer diagnosis, where multiple instance learning (MIL) serves as a dominant framework in this domain. Although Transformer-based MIL methods excel at capturing global dependencies, their capacity for modeling local spatial context is often limited. Moreover, integrating multi-scale information and bridging the semantic gap between pathological patterns and diagnostic texts remain challenging. To address these issues, we propose EHLM-Gen, a novel HRG framework featuring a Explicit Hierarchical and Localized Modeling (EHLM) module. EHLM jointly employs hierarchical graph attention along with two novel tissue-aware attention mechanisms to efficiently capture multi-scale global dependencies and fine-grained local spatial context within WSIs. A cross-modal prototypical memory module is also introduced to enhance vision-language alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art results on natural language generation metrics while remaining competitive with existing approaches in human evaluation.

title: I-DCCRN-VAE: An Improved Deep Representation Learning Framework for Complex VAE-Based Single-Channel Speech Enhancement
authors: Jiatong Li, Simon Doclo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recently, a complex variational autoencoder (VAE)-based single-channel speech enhancement system based on the DCCRN architecture has been proposed. In this system, a noise suppression VAE (NSVAE) learns to extract clean speech representations from noisy speech using pretrained clean speech and noise VAEs with skip connections. In this paper, we improve DCCRN-VAE by incorporating three key modifications: 1) removing the skip connections in the pretrained VAEs to encourage more informative speech and noise latent representations; 2) using β-VAE in pretraining to better balance reconstruction and latent space regularization; and 3) a NSVAE generating both speech and noise latent representations. Experiments show that the proposed system achieves comparable performance as the DCCRN and DCCRN-VAE baselines on the matched DNS3 dataset but outperforms the baselines on mismatched datasets (WSJ0-QUT, Voicebank-DEMEND), demonstrating improved generalization ability. In addition, an ablation study shows that a similar performance can be achieved with classical fine-tuning instead of adversarial training, resulting in a simpler training pipeline.

title: Ziv-Zakai Bound For Distributed-Array-Based Doa Estimation
authors: Zongyu Zhang, Zhiguo Shi, Maria Sabrina Greco, Fulvio Gini
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The existing Ziv–Zakai Bound (ZZB) for multi-target direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation is not directly applicable to distributed array systems. The main challenge lies in incorporating unknown subarray position offsets into the ZZB derivation. Moreover, the high sidelobes in the beam-pattern make the approximations used in conventional ZZB derivations overly relaxed. In this work, we address these challenges and derive the ZZB for multi-target distributed DOA estimation. Specifically, we first establish the conditional ZZB given the subarray position offsets, where a new approximation method is introduced to account for the effect of sidelobes in the beampattern, yielding a tighter ZZB than those in existing studies. The final ZZB is then obtained by averaging the conditional ZZB over the unknown subarray position offsets. Simulation results provide insights into distributed array configurations from the perspective of the ZZB and validate the effectiveness of the proposed approximation.

title: A Dynamic Dual-Backbone Model for Adaptive Vehicle-Pedestrian Detection
authors: Wenbo Liu, Haoran Liu, Chunyu Zhao, Tao Deng, Fei Yan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As a cornerstone of decision-making in intelligent transportation systems, vehicle-pedestrian detection requires both accuracy and efficiency, yet static models struggle to balance them. Therefore, we present DVPNet, a dynamic dual-backbone detection framework that adapts computation to scene complexity. The model integrates an Efficient Spatial Perception Attention Module (ESPAM) to enhance feature perception, and employs a differentiable neural architecture search–based selector for difficulty-aware path routing. Experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that DVPNet achieves superior robustness and accuracy while reducing redundant overhead, offering a practical solution for real-time vehicle-pedestrian detection.

title: Deformable Attention Graph Representation Learning for Histopathology Whole Slide Image Analysis
authors: Mingxi Fu, Xitong Ling, Yuxuan Chen, Jiawen Li, Fanglei Fu, Huaitian Yuan, Tian Guan, Yonghong He, Lianghui Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate classification of Whole Slide Images (WSIs) and Regions of Interest (ROIs) is a fundamental challenge in computational pathology. While mainstream approaches often adopt Multiple Instance Learning (MIL), they struggle to capture the spatial dependencies among tissue structures. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a solution to model inter-instance relationships, yet most rely on static graph topologies and overlook the physical spatial positions of tissue patches. Moreover, conventional attention mechanisms lack specificity, limiting their ability to focus on structurally relevant regions. In this work, we propose a novel GNN framework with deformable attention for pathology image analysis. We construct a dynamic weighted directed graph based on patch features, where each node aggregates contextual information from its neighbors via attention-weighted edges. Specifically, we incorporate learnable offsets informed by the real coordinates of each patch, enabling the model to adaptively attend to morphologically relevant regions across the slide. This design significantly enhances the contextual field while preserving spatial specificity. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmark datasets (TCGA-COAD, BRACS, gastric intestinal metaplasia grading, and intestinal ROI classification), demonstrating the power of deformable attention in capturing complex spatial structures in WSIs and ROIs.

title: GEIA: Generative Enhancement Inversion Attack Targeting Machine Unlearning
authors: Yaoxuan Ma, Xiaoyang Xu, Juan Wang, Wenzhe Yi, Yaxin Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Machine unlearning aims to empower models to selectively expunge designated data upon request, thereby safeguarding individual privacy. However, recent studies have indicated that unlearning methods may not fully mitigate privacy risks, as disparities between original and unlearned models might inadvertently expose excised data details. Nonetheless, existing attack methods frequently fail to capture the pixel-level details of unlearned samples, resulting in feature loss and excessive pixelation. In this paper, we introduce a novel attack framework targeting the feature recovery of unlearned samples, named Generative Enhancement Inversion Attack (GEIA). Unlike prior works, GEIA employs a two-stage strategy for high-fidelity feature recovery. The first stage uses latent semantic modeling to extract intrinsic patterns, while the second integrates a novel hierarchical architecture for multi-scale semantic detail reconstruction, effectively recovering local textures and global structures. Extensive experiments confirm GEIA’s superiority over the state-of-the-art methods, exposing vulnerabilities in machine unlearning.

title: Gaussian Processes for Sensor Repositioning in PDE-Driven Systems
authors: Maan Pandya, Bianca Giovanardi, Raj Thilak Rajan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Field estimation in spatio-temporally evolving environments remains challenging, particularly when limited sensor resources must capture dynamic features while contending with modeling errors and measurement noise e.g., in environmental monitoring using aerial vehicles, where system dynamics interact with practical sensing limitations. In this work, we consider a scenario where a network of mobile sensor nodes measure an advection-diffusion field, where the sensor locations can be dynamically optimized based on PDE residuals e.g., sensors on-board drones. Our novel two-stage framework strategically integrates Gaussian Process regression with PDE constraints. An initial inference stage estimates key parameters (e.g., advection velocity, diffusion coefficient) through stationary sensor measurements and finite-difference derivative approximations, while a subsequent mobility stage employs forward-Euler time-stepping to dynamically relocate the sensors toward regions of high PDE residual. Simulations based on a 2D advection-diffusion field experiment reveals upto an order magnitude improvement in field reconstruction error, as compared to information theoretic deployments. We conclude with future directions of extending our work and suggest applications.

title: DiffusionCom: Structure-Aware Multimodal Diffusion Model for Multimodal Knowledge Graph Completion
authors: Wei Huang, Peining Li, Meiyu Liang, Xu Hou, Yawen Li, Junping Du, Zhe Xue, Zeli Guan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal knowledge graphs (MKGs) are widely used in recommendation systems, information retrieval, and question answering, but they remain highly incomplete, driving the need for multimodal knowledge graph completion (MKGC) methods. Most existing approaches rely on discriminative models that maximize conditional likelihood, neglecting the underlying data distribution and limiting their ability to capture complex real-world relations. To address this, we propose DiffusionCom, a structure-aware multimodal diffusion model for MKGC. DiffusionCom frames MKGC as generating the joint probability distribution of (head, relation) pairs and candidate tail entities from noise. We also propose Structure-MKGformer as its encoder, which captures structural information via a multi-modal graph attention network (MGAT) and adaptively fuses it with entity representations. Trained with both generative and discriminative losses, DiffusionCom outperforms state-of-the-art models on FB15k-237-IMG and WN18-IMG datasets—notably, achieving a 27.5% relative improvement in Hits@1 on FB15k-237-IMG.

title: Object-Aware Restoration Diffusion: Progressive and Interactive Framework for Blind Compressed Image Restoration
authors: Lijie Yang, Juan Song, Mingtao Feng, Hang Zhong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: JPEG compression is widely adopted for image storage and transmission, yet aggressive compression introduces artifacts such as blocking and texture loss, degrading visual quality and hindering downstream tasks. Blind Compressed Image Restoration (CIR) aims to mitigate compression artifacts caused by unknown quality factors. Existing CIR methods typically treat the image holistically, ignoring object-specific texture and compression sensitivity. To address this, we introduce Object-aware Restoration Diffusion (ORD), a progressive and interactive framework for blind compressed image restoration that incorporates semantic object control into a multi-stage diffusion pipeline. ORD first employs an initial artifact removal module (e.g., FBCNN) to remove compression artifacts without hallucination. It then extracts object semantics using vision-language models and progressively refines object-wise details via Object Restoration with Attention Guidance (ORAG), an IRControlNet-based module that integrates semantic masks and textual prompts. Additionally, an interactive click-to-restore mode enables user-driven, selective restoration for efficient enhancement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that proposed ORD achieves state-of-the-art performance in both perceptual quality and semantic fidelity under aggressive compression. The code is available at https://github.com/yang-xidian/ORD.

title: A Novel Self-Correcting Direct Position Determination in Asynchronous Sensor Networks
authors: Yuan Zhang, Zihan Yao, Guizhou Wu, Fucheng Guo, Xi Li, Min Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a novel self-calibration direct position determination (SC-DPD) method in asynchronous sensor networks without the aid of anchor sources. By leveraging the consistency of asynchronous clock bias across different intercepted signal batches, the localization cost function is derived to effectively mitigate asynchronous effects. Simulation results demonstrate enhanced performance of the proposed method over existing studies: (1) superior localization accuracy, particularly in low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) conditions, (2) lower system complexity through anchor-free operation, (3) reduced computational complexity without clock bias estimation in the asynchronous sensor networks. The proposed method bridges the gap between theoretical DPD frameworks and practical deployment constraints, particularly for large-scale sensor networks.

title: Multilayer LTCC Bandpass Filter Using Broadside Coupled Stripline with Microstrip Feed
authors: Sayyad Shahwaz Ali, Dillibabu E
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: A compact three-layer broadside coupled stripline resonator is used to design an interdigital bandpass filter with a microstrip feed. Multi-folds structure of the resonator is used to introduce strong capacitive coupling to reduce the overall size of the filter. A comparison of the resonant frequency analysis between a single-layer resonator and a three-layer resonator is carried out using EM simulation and further verified by analyzing with electrical equivalent circuits. Filter prototype is fabricated using low temperature co-fired ceramic (LTCC) technology. Measured results of the filter show return loss better than 10 dB and maximum insertion loss of 3 dB over the band over the band from 1 GHz to 1.4 GHz.

title: Chairs and Program Committees: SYNASC 2025
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)

title: Enhancing the Performance of Event-Related Potential-Based Brain&#x2013;Computer Interfaces under Cognitive Distraction: A Multiwindow Adaptive Approach_supp1-3685282.docx
authors: Sung-Phil Kim
publish: N/A

title: SAKA: Spatially-Adaptive and Keyframe-Anchored Graph Network for Continuous Sign Language Recognition
authors: Weiyi Ye, Xu-Hua Yang, Gang-Feng Ma, Dong Wei, Sheng Liu, Yuan Feng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Continuous Sign Language Recognition (CSLR) technology facilitates communication for individuals with hearing impairments by converting dynamic sign language videos into text sequences. However, current methods are often constrained by the inadequate spatial feature extraction of CNNs from critical regions and the inefficient modeling of long-range temporal dependencies. To address these limitations, we propose SAKA, a novel CSLR framework leveraging spatially-adaptive and keyframe-anchored graph networks. In the spatial domain, our adaptive graph construction strategy dynamically builds adjacency by predicting content-aware coordinate offsets, thereby enhancing discriminative features in information-rich areas while suppressing background noise. For temporal modeling, we propose a keyframe-anchoring mechanism that identifies semantically significant keyframes through motion saliency and establishes sparse connections to the current frame, capturing long-range temporal dependencies with low computational overhead. Extensive experiments on PHOENIX14, PHOENIX14-T, and CSL-Daily datasets demonstrate that SAKA outperforms state-of-the-art approaches without any additional annotations.

title: Meg-YOLO Powered by Hypergraph: Multiscale Neighborhood Modeling in Medical Image Detection
authors: Shuai Luo, Jixun Ye, Jiayu Huang, Ge Jiao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Object detection in medical images often faces challenges such as small targets, blurred boundaries, and significant scale variations. Existing methods still fall short in integrating multi-level features and capturing cross-region dependencies. To this end, this paper proposes Meg-YOLO, which introduces Hypergraph-based Correlation Adaptive Fusion (HyperCAF) during the feature fusion stage. HyperCAF explicitly models multi-level dependencies by constructing hypergraphs of cross-scale features, and enables global semantic interactions through graph propagation. Additionally, a Feature Allocation Refiner (FAR) is designed within the backbone network to enhance fine-grained feature representations. During the fusion stage, a Feature Refinement Unit (FRMU) and Context-Guided Modulator (CGM) are introduced to facilitate cross-level integration and contextual information supplementation. Experimental results demonstrate that Meg-YOLO yields gains of 1.9%, 0.8%, 2.6%, and 1.3% on Tuberculosis, BCCD, Br35H, and Kvasir-SEG, respectively.

title: Conditional Random Fields for Interactive Refinement of Histopathological Predictions
authors: Tiffanie Godelaine, Maxime Zanella, Karim El Khoury, Saïd Mahmoudi, Benoît Macq, Christophe De Vleeschouwer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Assisting pathologists in the analysis of histopathological images has high clinical value, as it supports cancer detection and staging. In this context, histology foundation models have recently emerged. Among them, Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide strong yet imperfect zero-shot predictions. We propose to refine these predictions by adapting Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to histopathological applications, requiring no additional model training. We present HistoCRF, a CRF-based framework, with a novel definition of the pairwise potential that promotes label diversity and leverages expert annotations. We consider three experiments: without annotations, with expert annotations, and with iterative human-in-the-loop annotations that progressively correct misclassified patches. Experiments on five patch-level classification datasets covering different organs and diseases demonstrate average accuracy gains of 16.0% without annotations and 27.5% with only 100 annotations, compared to zero-shot predictions. Moreover, integrating a human in the loop reaches a further gain of 32.6% with the same number of annotations. The code is available on https://github.com/tgodelaine/HistoCRF.

title: Frequency-Aware Contrastive Learning and Spectral Disentanglement for Unsupervised Image Deraining
authors: Tao Jiang, Zikang Chen, Xiaowan Hu, Wang Zhang, Huaqiu Li, Haoqian Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We reformulate unsupervised image deraining as a signal separation task, addressing the practical challenge of acquiring paired training data. Existing methods often neglect the intrinsic physical prior of the rain signal: its directional high-frequency energy in the Fourier amplitude spectrum. Based on this insight, our method is driven by two core innovations. First, a novel frequency amplitude loss acts as an unsupervised spectral separation constraint, penalizing any spectral overlap to ensure the recovered background is verifiably free of rain’s unique signature. Second, a dual-domain contrastive learning strategy trains a rain streak extractor that accurately models the joint spatio-spectral priors of rain streaks and learns their consistent characteristics. This robust modeling allows our framework to surpass all unsupervised benchmarks and demonstrate superior generalization over state-of-the-art supervised methods on real-world datasets, validating the superiority of our signal prior-based approach. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Windrain7/DDCL.

title: Deep Unfolded Superiorized POCS for Robust Joint Transmission under Phase Misalignment
authors: Maximilian Klemm, Jochen Fink, Ezer Melzer, Doron Ezri, Sławomir Stańczak
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Joint transmission (JT) is a promising technology to increase the spectral efficiency in next-generation Wi-Fi systems by serving users coherently via multiple access points (APs) in the downlink. However, in practical systems, phase misalignment between APs can degrade the performance of JT schemes. The recently proposed robust joint transmission (ZI-RJT) scheme addresses this problem through closed-form precoders that are restricted to a certain subspace. Since the ZI-RJT precoders are derived assuming a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), we propose a new precoding strategy which takes into account the noise variance. We propose an iterative algorithm based on superiorization and projections onto convex sets (POCS) which aims at maximizing the spectral efficiency. Moreover, we propose a deep-unfolded variant (DU-S-POCS) of this algorithm, which achieves superior performance compared to ZI-RJT with just one iteration.

title: CFAI-Net: Cross-Stage Frequency-Aware Interaction Network For Fine-Grained Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Yang Liu, Ge Jiao, Guangcheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Edge blurring and structural detail loss remain major obstacles in medical image segmentation. Although Vision Mamba models long-range dependencies efficiently, they insufficiently capture high-frequency information, leading to degraded boundary reconstruction. We propose CFAI-Net (Cross-Stage Frequency-Aware Interaction Network), which integrates two key modules for cross-stage frequency modeling. The Cross-stage Frequency Interaction Module (CFIM) adaptively decouples and enhances high- and low-frequency components during feature fusion, while the Wavelet Frequency Refinement Block (WFRB) performs frequency-band decomposition and multi-scale aggregation for precise boundary reconstruction. Experiments on Synapse and ACDC datasets show that CFAI-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving average Dice by up to 1.35%, validating its effectiveness in detail preservation and segmentation accuracy. Code is available at: https://github.com/ipika9/CFAI-NET.

title: Listen, But Don't Leak: Sensitive Data Protection for Privacy Aware Automatic Speech Recognition with Acoustic Triggers
authors: Trinita Roy, Ngoc Thang Vu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the widespread adoption of automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems, concerns about unintentional transcription of sensitive or private information have grown significantly. In this study, we introduce Protective Acoustic Triggering (PAT), a novel method for embedding privacy redaction directly into ASR models. We propose a privacy-preserving strategy that leverages a high-frequency acoustic trigger, originally conceptualized as an adversarial attack on the ASR model, as a controlled mechanism to enforce transcription redaction. When activated, this trigger forces the ASR system to redact entire utterances or sensitive phrases by substituting them with a dedicated <Redacted> token, thereby embedding privacy awareness directly into the ASR pipeline rather than relying on post-hoc filtering. We fine-tune several Whisper variants on LibriSpeech as well as a synthetically generated privacy-sensitive data that allows Whisper to appropriately redact content while preserving transcription accuracy on insensitive speech. Our experiments show that in the utterance-level setup, the model achieves a 99.47% redaction success rate, and, for phrase-level redaction, the model successfully protected 97.7% of utterances (90.6% of sensitive spans), while maintaining near-baseline speech recognition utility on insensitive segments.

title: pTSE-T: Presentation Target Speaker Extraction Using Unaligned Text Cues
authors: Ziyang Jiang, Jiahe Lei, Xueyan Chen, Yifan Zhang, Zexu Pan, Wei Xue, Xinyuan Qian
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Target Speaker Extraction (TSE) aims to extract the clean speech of the target speaker in an audio mixture, eliminating irrelevant background noise and speech. While prior work has explored various auxiliary cues including pre-recorded speech, visual information, and spatial information, the acquisition and selection of such strong cues are infeasible in many practical scenarios. Differently, in this paper, we condition the TSE algorithm on semantic cues extracted from limited and unaligned text contents, such as condensed points from a presentation slide. This method is particularly useful in scenarios like meetings, poster sessions, or lecture presentations, where acquiring other cues in real time may be challenging. To this end, we design two different networks. Specifically, our proposed Text Prompt Extractor Network (TPE) fuses audio features with content-based semantic cues to facilitate time-frequency mask generation to filter out extraneous noise. The experimental results show the efficacy in accurately extracting the target speaker’s speech by utilizing semantic cues derived from limited and unaligned text, resulting in SI-SDRi of 12.16 dB, SDRi of 12.66 dB, PESQi of 0.830 and STOIi of 0.150. The dataset and source code will be publicly available at https://slideTSE.github.io/

title: Learning Class Similarities for Enhanced Image Out-Of-Distribution Detection
authors: Dimitrios Spanos, Nikolaos Passalis, Anastasios Tefas
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OoD) detection is critical for deploying deep learning models in safety-sensitive signal applications such as image recognition. Conventional classification pipelines, which rely on one-hot labels and cross-entropy loss, often suffer from feature collapse, where semantically diverse or ambiguous inputs are mapped to indistinguishable representations. This limits the reliability of uncertainty estimation and hampers OoD detection. In this work, we address these limitations by introducing a framework that explicitly models class similarities during training. An auxiliary neural network is employed to refine labels dynamically, enabling the feature space to better capture semantic relationships and mitigating the risk of collapse. Our approach utilizes soft labels that arise by the inherent similarities of the classes identified by the neural network and integrates seamlessly into existing pipelines. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves OoD detection performance as well as calibration error metrics.

title: Event-Aided Semantic Scene Completion
authors: Shangwei Guo, Hao Shi, Song Wang, Xiaoting Yin, Kailun Yang, Kaiwei Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in Semantic Scene Completion (SSC) for autonomous driving underscore the limitations of RGB-based approaches, which struggle under motion blur, poor lighting, and adverse weather. Event cameras, offering high dynamic range and low latency, address these challenges by providing asynchronous data that complements RGB inputs. We present DSEC-SSC, the first real-world benchmark specifically designed for event-aided SSC, which includes a novel 4D labeling pipeline for generating dense, visibility-aware labels that adapt dynamically to object motion. Our proposed RGB-Event fusion framework, EvSSC, introduces an Event-aided Lifting Module (ELM) that effectively bridges 2D RGB-Event features to 3D space, enhancing view transformation across SSC models. Evaluations on DSEC-SSC and simulated SemanticKITTI-E show that EvSSC is adaptable to both transformer-based and LSS-based SSC architectures, achieving up to a 52.5% relative improvement in mIoU under degraded conditions on the corrupted SemanticKITTI-C dataset. The established datasets and our codebase will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Pandapan01/EvSSC.

title: Spike-Driven Low-Power Speech Bandwidth Extension
authors: Donghyun Kim, Sangho Han, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent speech bandwidth extension (BWE) systems attain impressive fidelity through increasingly deep and complex architectures, but these gains often come with increased computation which costs that limit practical use under power and resource constraints. Therefore, we introduce a spike-driven BWE framework that replaces conventional artificial neural networks (ANNs) with spiking neural networks (SNNs) to bridge gap between performance and implementation complexity. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed spike-driven model surpasses the evaluated baselines on speech-quality and intelligibility metrics (e.g., PESQ, ESTOI, and LSD) while maintaining lower estimated energy usage, and superior efficiency. Demo is available at here.

title: MINDCINE: Multimodal Eeg-To-Video Reconstruction with Large-Scale Pretrained Models
authors: Tian-Yi Zhou, Xuan-Hao Liu, Bao-Liang Lu, Wei-Long Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reconstructing human dynamic visual perception from electroencephalography (EEG) signals is of great research significance since EEG’s non-invasiveness and high temporal resolution. However, EEG-to-video reconstruction remains challenging due to: 1) Single Modality existing studies solely align EEG signals with the text modality, which ignores other modalities and are prone to suffer from overfitting problems; 2) Data Scarcity current methods often have difficulty training to converge with limited EEG-video data. To solve the above problems, we propose a novel framework MindCine to achieve high-fidelity video reconstructions on limited data. We employ a multimodal joint learning strategy to incorporate beyond-text modalities in the training stage and leverage a pre-trained large EEG model to relieve the data scarcity issue for decoding semantic information, while a Seq2Seq model with causal attention is specifically designed for decoding perceptual information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms state-of-the-art methods both qualitatively and quantitatively. Additionally, the results underscore the effectiveness of the complementary strengths of different modalities and demonstrate that leveraging a large-scale EEG model can further enhance reconstruction performance by alleviating the challenges associated with limited data. Our Code is available at https://github.com/KevinZhou6/MindCine.

title: C-Conformer: Channel-Augmented Conformer for Sound Event Localization and Detection
authors: Changjiang He, Siyao Cheng, Jiahua Bao, Jie Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Three-dimensional sound event localization and detection (SELD) jointly performs event classification, direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, and source distance estimation, enabling comprehensive acoustic scene understanding. Recently, the ResNet–Conformer architectures have shown strong performance on SELD. However, the Conformer is primarily designed for single-channel audio recognition and models only temporal dependencies, overlooking inter-channel features for multi-channel spatial perception. To address this, we propose the Channel-augmented Conformer (C-Conformer), which extends the Conformer by explicitly modeling both temporal and inter-channel relationships. C-Conformer preserves temporal self-attention (TSA) and introduces a lightweight channel self-attention (CSA) module using depthwise convolutions to maintain temporal order. The TSA and CSA outputs are fused via learnable weights. To mitigate the distribution gap between synthetic and real data, we further design a Dual-of-Experts (DOE) CSA mechanism, enhancing cross-domain robustness. Extensive experiments show that ResNet–C-Conformer (RCC) consistently outperforms most existing models, achieving up to 8.4% F-score improvement. With DOE CSA, RCC effectively leverages both synthetic and real datasets, surpassing our previous system, which ranked second in the DCASE2025 SELD Challenge.

title: Deepfake Detection Via Data-Level Multi-Stream Assessment
authors: Shijie Hou, Xinghao Jiang, Ke Xu, Qiang Xu, Zeyu Zhao, Yinuo Ge
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The proliferation of highly realistic synthetic faces poses significant challenges to information security, necessitating robust and generalizable Deepfake detection systems. Existing multi-stream networks rely on feature-level fusion to integrate complementary cues but often overlook the critical importance of data quality. In this work, we propose Iterative Consensus Learning (ICL), a novel framework that introduces data-level multi-stream assessment via collaborative sample evaluation. Instead of merging features, ICL employs multiple independent models as data quality assessors, which collectively identify reliable training samples through prediction consensus. This consensus-driven subset is used to iteratively refine the training process iteratively, enhancing generalization. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate the strong generalization capability of our method.

title: MatchGaussian: Adaptive Densification with Matching Priors for Generalizable Gaussian Splatting
authors: Zhiyan Wang, Zhanke Wang, Jiahao Wu, Guanhua Wu, Runling Liu, Ronggang Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Although novel view synthesis (NVS) has achieved remarkable progress, current methods based on Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) or 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) still require lengthy per-scene optimization. Some generalizable methods leverage large-scale dataset priors knowledge to achieve fast reconstruction, but they often struggle to recover accurate geometry, leading to quality deterioration. In this paper, we propose MatchGaussian, a feed-forward 3D reconstruction framework using matching priors to learn multi-view consistent geometry. Specifically, we utilize matching correspondences from input images as critical bridges across views to encode multi-view features and construct cost volumes. Furthermore, we design an anchor adaptive densification strategy. Leveraging matching priors, a subset of Gaussians are selected as anchor points and adaptively densified to enhance detail representation capabilities. Extensive experiments on RealEstate10K and ACID demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering good geometric consistency and high-fidelity detail quality.

title: Explainable Lightweight Deep Learning-Based Intrusion Detection for Healthcare 4.0 Using Biomedical and Network Signals
authors: Othman Saad Salman, Anas Abdulsatar Faraj, Mohanad Mahmood Abdulraheem, Shokhan M. Al-Barzinji, Abdlelah Abdlatef Abdlhamed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Healthcare 4.0 using the Internet of Things (IoT) has seen a rapid pace of development, and as a result, experts are showing an increasing level of interest in the field of network security. The use of deep learning (DL) has greatly improved the effectiveness of Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDSs). Nevertheless, due to the complex nature and the “black box” issue that arises when it comes to implementing DL-based NIDS models in real-world situations, there are several difficulties that must be addressed, such as the requirement for a lightweight design and the interpretability of the model. This study proposed the novel approach of the NIDS system for healthcare 4.0. The model consists of network and biomedical signal acquisition, automatic FL-based feature engineering, and SoftMax-based classification with its interpretation. The automatic feature engineering is performed by using lightweight DL layers followed by the Feature Selection (FS) to reduce the dimensionality and computational overhead. The SoftMax classifier is performed to detect the intrusions in the acquired signal, followed by the interpretation of the outcomes using the explainable approach. Simulation results revealed the efficiency of the proposed model compared to the existing methods using the research NIDS dataset.

title: Multi-Layer Standardized Coding Method for Power Supply Chain Materials Based on Hash Algorithm
authors: Tao Wang, Xiaozhe Hu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The more intricate and networked management of power supply chains and the expansion of digitised supply chains have made it important that distinct material data can be made both unique and traceable and also compatible with one another. This study has presented a multi-layer standardized coding method for power supply chain materials, based on the hash algorithm trying to increase the material identification, and cut down the redundancy and a view to strengthening the intelligent management of information. It combines a tree-like encoding system with cryptographic hashing to encode multiple forms of material into collision-resistant IDs in their various stages of distribution. In the first level, the attributes of materials are essentially assimilated in a standardized format. the second stratum includes the supply chain metadata including the source, batch, and quality; the final layer hashes data using a secure hash function such as secure hash algorithm (e.g., SHA-256) for data integrity and compactness. The presented method can also support fast indexing, reliable authentication and also the integration of data across platforms. In the real-world experiment, a power supply chain dataset shows that our data management and search mechanism improve data consistency, search speed, as well as the material traceability compared to traditional identification approaches. Postscript There is potential for future research to also include the application of the blockchain technology to incorporate artificial intelligence and analytics in improving the power supply chain system.

title: Syncspeech: Efficient and Low-Latency Text-to-Speech Based on Temporal Masked Transformer
authors: Zhengyan Sheng, Zhihao Du, Shiliang Zhang, Zhijie Yan, Liping Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current text-to-speech (TTS) models face a persistent limitation: autoregressive (AR) models suffer from low generation efficiency, while modern non-autoregressive (NAR) models experience high latency due to their unordered temporal nature. To bridge this divide, we introduce SyncSpeech, an efficient and low-latency TTS model based on the proposed Temporal Mask Transformer (TMT) paradigm. TMT synergistically unifies the temporally ordered generation of AR models with the parallel decoding efficiency of NAR models. TMT is realized through a meticulously designed sequence construction rule, a corresponding training objective, and a specialized hybrid attention mask. Furthermore, with the primary aim of enhancing training efficiency, a high-probability masking strategy is introduced, which also leads to a significant improvement in overall model performance. During inference, SyncSpeech achieves high efficiency by decoding all speech tokens corresponding to each newly arrived text token in a single step, and low latency by beginning to generate speech immediately upon receiving the second text token from the streaming input. Evaluations show that Sync-Speech maintains speech quality comparable to the modern AR TTS model, while achieving a 5.8-fold reduction in first-packet latency and an 8.8-fold improvement in real-time factor. Speech samples are available at https://SyncSpeech.github.io/.

title: MarkSweep: A No-Box Removal Attack on AI-Generated Image Watermarking via Noise Intensification and Frequency-Aware Denoising
authors: Jie Cao, Zelin Zhang, Qi Li, Jianbing Ni
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: AI watermarking embeds invisible signals within images to provide provenance information and identify content as AI-generated. In this paper, we introduce MarkSweep, a novel watermark removal attack that effectively erases the embedded watermarks from AI-generated images without degrading visual quality. MarkSweep first amplifies watermark noise in high-frequency regions via edge-aware Gaussian perturbations and injects it into clean images for training a denoising network. This network then integrates two modules, the learnable frequency decomposition module and the frequency-aware fusion module, to suppress amplified noise and eliminate watermark traces. Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments demonstrate that in-visible watermarks are highly vulnerable to MarkSweep, which effectively removes embedded watermarks, reducing the bit accuracy of HiDDeN and Stable Signature watermarking schemes to below 67%, while preserving perceptual quality of AI-generated images.

title: Towards Effective Negation Modeling in Joint Audio-Text Models for Music
authors: Yannis Vasilakis, Rachel Bittner, Johan Pauwels
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Joint audio–text models are widely used for music retrieval, yet they struggle with semantic phenomena such as negation. Negation is fundamental for distinguishing the absence (or presence) of musical elements (e.g., "with vocals" vs. "without vocals"), but current systems fail to represent this reliably. In this work, we investigate and mitigate this limitation by training CLAP models from scratch on the Million Song Dataset with LP-MusicCaps-MSD captions. We introduce negation through text augmentation and a dissimilarity-based contrastive loss, designed to explicitly separate original and negated captions in the joint embedding space. To evaluate progress, we propose two protocols that frame negation modeling as retrieval and binary classification tasks. Experiments demonstrate that both methods, individually and combined, improve negation handling while largely preserving retrieval performance.

title: LSAFE: Edge-Guided Lightweight Network for Remote Sensing Salient Object Detection Via Dynamic Multi-Scale Fusion
authors: Size Liu, Dongming Chen, Dongqi Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In recent years, lightweight salient object detection (SOD) in optical remote sensing images (ORSIs) has attracted considerable attention for its practical utility in resource-constrained scenarios. However, existing approaches often fail to achieve an optimal trade-off between detection accuracy and computational efficiency. To tackle this issue, we propose LSAFE—a lightweight network that incorporates multi-scale feature enhancement and dynamic edge refinement for efficient ORSI-SOD. Specifically, we introduce a Dynamic Edge Enhancement Module (DEEM) to retain fine-grained details in low-level features, and an Efficient Scale-Aware Module (ESAM) to capture multi-scale contextual information with minimal computational cost. Furthermore, a Bi-Weighted Concatenation Module (BWCM) is proposed to adaptively integrate cross-level features. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that LSAFE achieves competitive performance with only 3.18M parameters and 0.93G FLOPs. Our method effectively balances accuracy and efficiency, demonstrating strong potential for real-time applications.

title: Solving Poisson Inverse Problems with Diffusion Models via the Plug-and-Play Scheme
authors: Yang Zheng, Wen Li, Zhaoqiang Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Poisson inverse problems (IPs) commonly arise in photon-limited imaging applications, where the number of detected photons is extremely small and the noise is most accurately modeled by a Poisson distribution. In this work, we leverage diffusion models (DMs) to address Poisson IPs. We first propose a new DM-based method for Poisson IPs and formulate the corresponding optimization problem. Furthermore, we decompose the optimization problem in our proposed framework into two subproblems, which enables more efficient inference. Extensive experiments on multiple image datasets and across different Poisson noise intensities demonstrate that our methods outperform existing DM-based approaches in most cases.

title: Dual-Decoder Network with Topology Prompting and Edge Refinement for Vascular Segmentation in X-Ray Angiography
authors: Meng Cui, Nan Jiang, Tiansong Li, Zhiming Zhu, Hongbo He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vascular segmentation can accurately extract vascular structures, provide a key basis for the diagnosis and treatment of vascular diseases. However, most existing methods emphasize pixel-level accuracy at the expense of vascular topological connectivity. Maintaining the connectivity of vascular structures is crucial for reliable clinical diagnostics. To address this limitation, we propose a novel dual-decoder architecture, named TER-Net, which incorporates a dedicated topological decoder to explicitly learn the topological properties of vascular networks. The structural priors captured by this decoder are embedded as prompting cues into a semantic decoder, thereby guiding the model toward more precise pixel-wise segmentation while ensuring structural continuity. Extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed approach not only surpasses existing methods but also exhibits consistently superior performance. The code has been made publicly available on https://github.com/TiansongLi/TER-Net.

title: Evaluating Emotion Recognition in Spoken Language Models on Emotionally Incongruent Speech
authors: Pedro Corrêa, João Lima, Victor Moreno, Lucas Ueda, Paula Costa
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Advancements in spoken language processing have driven the development of spoken language models (SLMs), designed to achieve universal audio understanding by jointly learning text and audio representations for a wide range of tasks. Although promising results have been achieved, there is growing discussion regarding these models’ generalization capabilities and the extent to which they truly integrate audio and text modalities in their internal representations. In this work, we evaluate four SLMs on the task of speech emotion recognition using a dataset of emotionally incongruent speech samples, a condition under which the semantic content of the spoken utterance conveys one emotion while speech expressiveness conveys another. Our results indicate that SLMs rely predominantly on textual semantics rather than speech emotion to perform the task, indicating that text-related representations largely dominate over acoustic representations. We release both the code and the Emotionally Incongruent Synthetic Speech dataset (EMIS) to the community.

title: GMAMBAFLOW: Global-Aware Mamba Based Cost Volume Aggregation for Optical Flow
authors: Yuhao Yang, Yining Miao, Wenming Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Mamba architecture has demonstrated excellent performance in various visual tasks. It outperforms many CNN and transformer based architectures due to its long-sequence modeling capability and linear complexity. However, when we aggregate cost volume for occluded regions, Mamba module fails to properly match features of the same category due to its token-independent parameter generation mechanism. To address this issue, we propose a novel aggregation scheme called Global-aware Mamba Cost volume Aggregation, which can enhance matching ability of pixels within the same category by introducing prompts with global semantic information into the discretization step size of state space model. Additionally, we introduce an uncertainty-guided loss to enhance the network’s self-optimization ability for poorly predicted regions during iteration. Experiments show that our design achieves outstanding results on the Sintel and KITTI datasets.

title: LVC: A Lightweight Compression Framework for Enhancing VLMS in Long Video Understanding
authors: Ziyi Wang, Haoran Wu, Yiming Rong, Yunlong Zhao, Yixin Zhang, Bo Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Long video understanding is a complex task that requires both spatial detail and temporal awareness. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) obtain frame-level understanding capabilities through multi-frame input, they suffer from information loss due to the sparse sampling strategy. In contrast, Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) capture temporal relationships within visual features but are limited by the scarcity of high-quality video-text datasets. To transfer long video understanding capabilities to VLMs with minimal data and computational cost, we propose Lightweight Video Compression (LVC), a novel method featuring the Query-Attention Video Compression mechanism, which effectively tackles the sparse sampling problem in VLMs. By training only the alignment layer with 10k short video-text pairs, LVC significantly enhances the temporal reasoning abilities of VLMs. Extensive experiments show that LVC provides consistent performance improvements across various models, including the InternVL2 series and Phi-3.5-Vision. The InternVL2-40B-LVC achieves scores of 68.2 and 65.9 on the long video understanding benchmarks MLVU and Video-MME, respectively, with relative improvements of 14.6% and 7.7%.

title: Automated Dysphagia Screening Using Noninvasive Neck Acoustic Sensing
authors: Jade Chng, Rong Xing, Yunfei Luo, Kristen Linnemeyer-Risser, Tauhidur Rahman, Andrew Yousef, Philip A Weissbrod
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pharyngeal health plays a vital role in essential human functions such as breathing, swallowing, and vocalization. Early detection of swallowing abnormalities, also known as dysphagia, is crucial for timely intervention. However, current diagnostic methods often rely on radiographic imaging or invasive procedures. In this study, we propose an automated framework for detecting dysphagia using portable and noninvasive acoustic sensing coupled with applied machine learning. By capturing subtle acoustic signals from the neck during swallowing tasks, we aim to identify patterns associated with abnormal physiological conditions. Our approach achieves promising test-time abnormality detection performance, with an AUC-ROC of 0.904 under 5 independent train–test splits. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using noninvasive acoustic sensing as a practical and scalable tool for pharyngeal health monitoring.

title: Accuracy Analysis of Wear Error Compensation Through its Prediction
authors: Svetlana Koleva
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Ensuring the accuracy of diametrical dimensions in turning operations depends on a large number of disturbing factors, among which tool wear is one of the most significant. Tool wear is influenced by the cutting process, the machinability of the workpiece material, and the parameters of the cutting tool itself, which exhibit specific behavior under different operating conditions. A cause-and-effect diagram has been developed to graphically represent these influences. An experiment was conducted to obtain real production data on the tool wear process under industrial conditions. One of the possible approaches for compensating tool wear is its modelling and prediction. The accuracy of tool wear modelling is determined by the stochastic nature of the process. The total dispersion field of wear rate caused by technological factors has been determined. The accuracy of predicting cutting tool wear is a function of time and of variations in the wear rate intensity.

title: A Unified Multi-Encoder Framework for Music Lyric Intelligibility Assessment: Bridging Invasive and Non-Invasive Paradigms
authors: Jing Yang, Shenghao Liao, Shuqing Zhang, Yongyi Deng, Pan Li, Zerui Han, Gongping Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Lyric intelligibility significantly impacts music engagement and comprehension, especially for hearing-impaired individuals. Existing speech intelligibility metrics perform poorly in musical contexts due to differences in prosody, timing, and spectral dynamics. We propose a unified multi-encoder framework that supports both invasive and non-invasive modeling for robust lyric intelligibility prediction. By capturing complementary local acoustic-semantic alignments, global musical semantics, and ASR-derived textual cues, our method achieves superior performance over baselines on the ICASSP 2026 Cadenza dataset, with the best model achieving 26.61 RMSE, a 9.2% improvement over the baseline, demonstrating its flexibility and robustness.

title: Robust Kalman Filter for Additive Gaussian-Student’s T Distribution
authors: Gurui Jin, Pengcheng Hao, Zhenyu Liu, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Ercan E. Kuruoğlu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing robust Kalman filters (RKFs) fail to capture hybrid noise structures, where Gaussian background noises coexist with impulsive heavy-tailed components. In this paper, we propose RKF-AGST, a novel variational Bayesian framework formulated by the additive Gaussian-Student's t (AGST) distribution. Unlike single heavy-tailed distributions such as Gaussian or Student's t (ST) used in conventional RKFs, AGST explicitly decomposes noises into Gaussian and Student's t components, thereby enabling more accurate modeling of hybrid noise environments. A maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimator is incorporated within the variational framework to handle the non-conjugate inference for the scale parameter. Our method is validated in simulated target tracking scenarios. Experiments show that RKF-AGST achieves lower RMSE (position) compared to both the reference filters.

title: Space-Time ARC Abstraction for UAV Network Reconfiguration Under Adversarial Electro-Optical Disruption
authors: Xinyue Wang, Chenyuan Zhang, Yishan Zhang, Xiatong Hou, Shangpeng Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distributed unmanned aerial vehicle networks under electro-optical interference serve as critical infrastructure for resilient swarm coordination in adversarial environments. Existing methods primarily address radio frequency jamming and overlook the quantifiable impact of optical dazzling on link capacity, while also lacking cross-layer integration that connects physical-layer perception with reconnection decisions such as blackout exclusion and corridor gating. We propose a reconnection framework featuring verifiable space-time arc abstraction and distributed convergence. Specifically, the model integrates conditional value-at-risk-based capacity estimation, perception-driven blackout exclusion, and entropy-guided gating within optimizable arcs. Then we develop a boundary-consistent distributed alternating direction method of multipliers algorithm to decouple activation and flow optimization, while enabling multicluster coordination through consensus. Experimental results show that the proposed method improves connectivity by 12.4%, reduces exposure risk by 18.7%, and ensures reliable convergence within task deadlines under severe interference.

title: Near-Field Channel Estimation and Localization with Coprime Arrays
authors: Rafik Guellil, Karim Abed-Meraim, Adel Belouchrani
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper addresses uplink near-field, semi-blind, channel estimation and localization for Extremely Large Coprime Antenna Arrays explicitly accounting for multipath propagation. We develop a subspace-based estimation strategy that jointly exploits pilot and data symbols to recover channel responses and localization parameters. Our approach is compared to the conventional Uniform Linear Array (ULA). While ULAs provide slightly better accuracy under ideal conditions with the same aperture, Coprime Arrays (CAs) require significantly fewer elements, reducing computational complexity, and are more robust to mutual coupling (MC). Overall, CAs provide a practical and efficient solution for near-field channel estimation and localization for 6G communication systems.

title: CIFC-MFD: End-To-End Multi-Face Forgery Detection Using Cross-Image Face Contrast
authors: Xiaoyu Zhou, Jingchang Xie, Peipeng Yu, Chengfu Ou, Jianwei Fei, Zhihua Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The misuse of deepfake technology poses a serious threat to societal security. Existing detection methods are primarily designed for single-face scenarios, making them ineffective for complex multi-face scenes. Although recent end-to-end detection frameworks have emerged, they are confined to analyzing features within a single image, thus failing to leverage relational information across different images. To address this, we propose the Cross-Image Face Contrast Multi-Face Forgery Detection (CIFC-MFD) framework. This framework enhances scene-level consistency through an Intra-Image Face Relation Module (IFRM) and introduces the key innovation of a Cross-Image Face Contrast (CIFC) module, which extracts forgery-related features across different images by comparing face features with a global memory bank. Experimental results demonstrate that CIFC-MFD outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving improvements of 4.6% in detection and 4.8% in segmentation Average Precision (AP) on the test-challenge set of the OpenForensics dataset. Notably, in complex scenarios, the model improves the detection AP for small-sized faces by 14.4%, showcasing its substantial advantages.

title: Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Aggregation in Semi-Supervised Federated Learning with non-IID Data
authors: Rene Glitza, Luca Becker, Rainer Martin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables distributed training of machine learning models while preserving data privacy. However, FL struggles with heterogeneous, non-IID client data distributions, resulting in sub-optimal and biased global models. In this paper, we propose pFedMARL, a novel approach leveraging Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) to dynamically adapt aggregation strategies in FL settings. Our method employs a server-side agent adjusting client contributions to optimize global model robustness and client-side agents balancing global and local updates to personalize models effectively without pre-training. We demonstrate superior performance of pFedMARL for training a semi-supervised audio spectrogram transformer, matching or outperforming FedAvg, Ditto, and local training approaches across multiple non-IID scenarios and in the presence of adversarial clients. Our results indicate that pFedMARL actively improves accuracy, robustness, and fairness, making it suitable for real-world deployments.

title: Unlocking The Potential of Social Media Preference for Annotation-Efficient Large Language Model Alignment
authors: Wenbin Zhang, Wanxiang Che
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Preference learning is essential for aligning the generative outputs of Large Language Models (LLMs) with human values, which often relies on costly preference annotation. To mitigate this expense, annotating preference with user feedback from social media platforms presents a promising alternative. However, existing Social Media Preference (SMP) datasets show a significant performance gap compared to benchmark preference datasets. To bridge this gap, we introduce FIR, an analytical framework that dissects SMP dataset into three core components: Feedback, Instruction, and Response pairs. The FIR analysis yields a set of platform-agnostic principles that establish a systematic methodology to guide the construction of SMP datasets: 1) Using verified marks as feedback, 2) A dual-criteria instruction filtering, and 3) Optimizing response pairs. Guided by FIR, we find that carefully designed SMP dataset yields performance that rivals state-of-the-art preference datasets, successfully unlocking the potential of social media data as an annotation-efficient resource for high-quality LLM alignment.

title: KLGate: Leveraging LLM Explanations via KL-Guided Gating for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
authors: Jie Xiong, Ran Zhang, Jun Wang, Yang Zhang, Li Yu, Jigang He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) must recognize ironic intent that often arises from semantic mismatch between image and text. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can verbalize such mismatches as conflict explanations, offering useful high-level cues, but their outputs are prone to noise and hallucination. To reconcile these strengths and weaknesses, we propose KLGate. It leverages LLM-generated conflict explanations and employs a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence–based gate to align them with the original modalities, preserving reliable signals while suppressing noise. The gated features are fused with visual and textual representations to generate the final prediction. KLGate sets a new state of the art on standard benchmarks, achieving a binary F1 of 90.35 with 1.42 points gain over the best baseline.

title: Change-Aware Temporal Alignment on Heterogeneous Graph Snapshots for Insider Threat Detection
authors: Min Yang, Yingjie Zhou, Di Wu, Guansong Pang, Ce Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate insider threat detection (ITD) is crucial for uncovering potential security risks in organizations, such as data leaks and privilege abuse. However, the covert nature of insider threats poses significant challenges to detecting anomalies from massive heterogeneous log data. We propose a user-level ITD approach based on temporally-aligned heterogeneous graph snapshots, which can effectively model the evolution of users’ interaction patterns in a continuous semantic space. The method employs network logs to construct heterogeneous graph snapshots, which characterize entities’ complex interactions based on specially designed meta-paths. Compared with a single oversized graph structure, our graph construction avoids high storage and computational overhead. The temporal dynamics of entities’ interactions are also effectively captured by a change-aware smoothing regularizer through enforcing the semantic alignment of representations of similar behaviors across adjacent time steps. Experiments on two datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach under limited anomaly annotations.

title: GenLie: A Global-Enhanced Lie Detection Network under Sparsity and Semantic Interference
authors: Zongshun Zhang, Yao Liu, Qiao Liu, Xuefeng Peng, Peiyuan Jiang, Jiaye Yang, Daibing Yao, Wei Lin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video-based lie detection aims to identify deceptive behaviors from visual cues. Despite recent progress, its core challenge lies in learning sparse yet discriminative representations. Deceptive signals are typically subtle and short-lived, easily overwhelmed by redundant information, while individual and contextual variations introduce strong identity-related noise. To address this issue, we propose GenLie, a Global-Enhanced Lie Detection Network that performs local feature modeling under global supervision. Specifically, sparse and subtle deceptive cues are captured at the local level, while global supervision and optimization ensure robust and discriminative representations by suppressing identity-related noise. Experiments on three public datasets, covering both high- and low-stakes scenarios, show that GenLie consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Source code is available at https://github.com/AliasDictusZ1/GenLie.

title: Spectral or Spatial? Leveraging Both for Speaker Extraction in Challenging Data Conditions
authors: Aviad Eisenberg, Sharon Gannot, Shlomo E. Chazan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a robust multi-channel speaker extraction algorithm designed to handle inaccuracies in reference information. While existing approaches often rely solely on either spatial or spectral cues to identify the target speaker, our method integrates both sources of information to enhance robustness. A key aspect of our approach is its emphasis on stability, ensuring reliable performance even when one of the features is degraded or misleading. Given a noisy mixture and two potentially unreliable cues, a dedicated network is trained to dynamically balance their contributions—or disregard the less informative one when necessary. We evaluate the system under challenging conditions by simulating inference-time errors using a simple direction of arrival (DOA) estimator and a noisy spectral enrollment process. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model successfully extracts the desired speaker even in the presence of substantial reference inaccuracies.

title: ToS: A Team of Specialists Ensemble Framework for Stereo Sound Event Localization and Detection with Distance Estimation in Video
authors: Davide Berghi, Philip J. B. Jackson
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sound event localization and detection with distance estimation (3D SELD) in video involves identifying active sound events at each time frame while estimating their spatial coordinates. This multimodal task requires joint reasoning across semantic, spatial, and temporal dimensions, a challenge that single models often struggle to address effectively. To tackle this, we introduce the Team of Specialists (ToS) ensemble framework, which integrates three complementary sub-networks: a spatio-linguistic model, a spatio-temporal model, and a tempo-linguistic model. Each sub-network specializes in a unique pair of dimensions, contributing distinct insights to the final prediction, akin to a collaborative team with diverse expertise. ToS has been benchmarked against state-of-the-art audio-visual models for 3D SELD on the DCASE2025 Task 3 Stereo SELD development set, consistently outperforming existing methods across key metrics. Future work will extend this proof of concept by strengthening the specialists with appropriate tasks, training, and pre-training curricula.

title: AmbiDrop: Array-Agnostic Speech Enhancement Using Ambisonics Encoding and Dropout-Based Learning
authors: Michael Tatarjitzky, Boaz Rafaely
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multichannel speech enhancement leverages spatial cues to improve intelligibility and quality, but most learning-based methods rely on specific microphone array geometry, unable to account for geometry changes. To mitigate this limitation, current array-agnostic approaches employ large multi-geometry datasets but may still fail to generalize to unseen layouts. We propose AmbiDrop (Ambisonics with Dropouts), an Ambisonics-based framework that encodes arbitrary array recordings into the spherical harmonics domain using Ambisonics Signal Matching (ASM). A deep neural network is trained on simulated Ambisonics data, combined with channel dropout for robustness against array-dependent encoding errors, therefore omitting the need for a diverse microphone array database. Experiments show that while the baseline and proposed models perform similarly on the training arrays, the baseline degrades on unseen arrays. In contrast, AmbiDrop consistently improves SI-SDR, PESQ, and STOI, demonstrating strong generalization and practical potential for array-agnostic speech enhancement.

title: Empowering the Tail: Adaptive Semantic Neighborhood Enhancement for Long-Tail Reasoning in Temporal Knowledge Graphs
authors: Yi Yang, Bin Chen, Zhangtao Cheng, Kaiju Li, Jia Chen, Ting Zhong, Fan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning (TKGR) plays a vital role in modeling dynamically evolving real-world facts. However, its performance is severely hindered by the long-tail distribution of entities, which induces strong predictive bias against infrequent (tail) entities and significantly undermines the effectiveness of existing methods. To address this challenge, we propose ALSNE, a lightweight and model-agnostic plug-in for adaptive long-tail semantic neighborhood enhancement, designed to alleviate the long-tail issue in TKGR. ALSNE first retrieves semantically similar head entities from the recently interacted neighborhood of each tail entity to provide rich transferable contextual knowledge. This informative knowledge from head entities is then adaptively integrated into the tail entity, facilitating the effective transfer of rich evolutionary patterns that enhance the tail entities’ representation. Extensive experiments conducted on three real-world TKG datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ALSNE.

title: DSTCS: Dual-Student Teacher Framework with Segment Anything Model For Semi-Supervised Pubic Symphysis-Fetal Head Segmentation
authors: Yalin Luo, Shun Long, Huijin Wang, Jieyun Bai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Segmentation of pubic symphysis and fetal head (PSFH) is a critical procedure in intrapartum monitoring, essential for evaluating labor progression and identifying potential delivery complications.
