However, achieving accurate segmentation remains a significant challenge due to class imbalance, ambiguous boundaries, and noise interference in ultrasound images, compounded by a scarcity of high-quality annotated data. Current research on PSFH segmentation pre-dominantly relies on CNN and Transformer architectures, leaving the potential of more powerful models underexplored. We propose a Dual-Student and Teacher combining CNN and SAM (DSTCS) framework, which integrates the powerful Segment Anything Model (SAM) into a dual student-teacher architecture. A cooperative learning mechanism between CNN and SAM branches significantly improves segmentation accuracy. The proposed scheme also incorporates a specialized data augmentation strategy optimized for boundary processing and a novel loss function. Extensive experiments on the MICCAI 2023 and 2024 PSFH segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that our method exhibits superior robustness and significantly outperforms existing techniques, providing a reliable segmentation tool for clinical practice.

title: Leveraging Edge Impulse for Multi-Class Error-Related Potentials: Edge AI Brain-Robot Interface for Correcting Navigation Errors in Assistive Mobile Robot Wheelchairs
authors: Karameldeen Omer, Andrea Monteriù
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This study explores the integration of BrainComputer Interface (BCI) technology with microcontroller chips to be used for real-time signal processing and event classification, aiming to create a standalone and efficient system deployed as machine learning models on edge devices, optimizing classification pipelines to process Error-Related Potential (ErrP)-based EEG signals. All classification models are multi-class classifiers to differentiate among four classes: “no navigation error” and three levels of navigation error severity (small, medium, and large). The main aim is to investigate the feasibility of utilizing edge computing for real-time signal processing and classification in a BCI system. A pilot study was conducted with 10 subjects to obtain preliminary results and evaluate the system's performance. This approach allowed for testing the viability of the proposed BCI paradigm and refining the data acquisition protocol. These initial findings guide adjustments to the experimental setup, ensuring the system is optimized before scaling up to involve a larger group of participants for extensive data collection. The results underscore the potential of integrating BCI technology with edge AI platforms for real-time processing. However, the findings also reveal significant challenges, particularly the highly unbalanced datasets in multi-class classification tasks. Moreover, the study highlights the need to shorten trial lengths to improve the detection of ErrPs. Addressing these challenges is essential for refining the experimental protocol and optimizing system design before scaling to larger datasets and broader subject participation. Overall, the study demonstrates the promise of edge-based BCI systems in enabling low-latency, energy-efficient, and scalable assistive devices. This approach has the potential to significantly enhance the autonomy and safety of individuals with severe motor disabilities, providing a practical and accessible solution for assistive robotic applications.

title: Handling Heterogeneous Features: Modeling Continuous-Discrete Feature Interaction for Time Series Anomaly Detection Via Conditional Diffusion
authors: Anyi Zhang, Peng Xu, Chai Kiat Yeo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Time series anomaly detection is critical for modern systems. Recent diffusion-based models capture complex temporal distributions but usually assume homogeneous features, overlooking the heterogeneity of real data where continuous sensor values and discrete system states interact. We propose CDDiff, a Conditional Diffusion framework for heterogeneous anomaly detection, integrating two designs: a dual-path diffusion process where an autoregressive path reconstructs continuous features and a conditional path which dynamically integrates the discrete features at each reverse step. An anomaly scoring mechanism based on the reconstruction error complements CDDiff’s ability to amplify the subtle anomalies that might be missed by other methods. Experiments on multiple multivariate datasets show that CDDiff consistently surpasses State-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in modeling continuous–discrete interactions and handling real-world complexity.

title: Energy-Aware Images Via Pixel Value Reduction: The Impact of Compression on Attenuation Maps
authors: Emmanuel Sampaio, Franck Aumont, Olivier Le Meur, Thomas Maugey
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video consumption accounts for a significant share of global energy use, with end devices responsible for most of it. On end devices, display technology plays an important role in energy consumption. Interestingly, OLED technology allows power to be adapted via pixel-intensity manipulation. In this context, Pixel Value Reduction (PVR) has shown promising results for lowering display power by generating attenuation maps that adapt image luminance. However, the use of this technology in streaming services has not been fully studied. In this work, we analyze the effect of attenuation-map compression on perceptual quality, bitrate overhead, and end-device energy consumption. Using a pixel-value-reduction model, we generate attenuation maps for target power-reduction levels (10%, 20%, and 40%) and encode them with the HEVC video codec at various quantization-parameter (QP) values (i.e., codec QP). Experiments on 4K content with real OLED power measurements show that compressed attenuation maps maintain high fidelity to the originals, achieving different levels of power reduction with negligible quality loss. Moreover, the results indicate that proper alignment between content and map quantization parameters is critical for reducing bitrate overhead. These findings highlight the feasibility of transmitting compressed attenuation maps to minimize display’s energy consumption.

title: Image-Pixel Realignment for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation via Self-Training
authors: Anyi Yang, Qibo Liu, Yawen Fan, Quan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) seeks to assign pixel-level labels to arbitrary categories, including unseen ones, but faces the challenge of transferring image-level semantics from vision-language models to fine-grained dense prediction. We introduce STSeg, a novel framework that integrates pixel-level semantic alignment with adaptive self-training. A dual embedding aggregation module fuses global semantics from CLIP with spatially precise features from a frozen SAM model via cross-attention, yielding high-quality pixel representations. On top of this, a self-learning module refines SAM-guided pseudo-labels through iterative optimization, enabling the model to continuously update its knowledge. This mechanism allows STSeg not only to generalize effectively to novel categories but also to dynamically adapt during inference without additional supervision. Extensive evaluations on multiple OVSS benchmarks validate the effectiveness of STSeg, demonstrating its competitive accuracy, strong generalization, thereby highlighting its potential for real-world open-world applications.

title: Variable Metric Stochastic Line-Search For Primal-Dual Hybrid Gradient
authors: Yodai Suzuki, Kazuki Naganuma, Shunsuke Ono
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study adaptive parameter selection for PDHG through the lens of variable metrics. The primal-dual hybrid gradient (PDHG) algorithm efficiently solves a broad class of convex optimization problems without requiring operator inversions, yet its convergence can be severely slowed down due to its high sensitivity to stepsize selection. To overcome this difficulty, we first establish a new theoretical result for PDHG with variable metrics under mild assumptions, providing a basis to analyze the convergence of diverse adaptive stepsize selection schemes. Building on this result, we introduce a stochastic line-search strategy (SLS-PDHG) that explores stepsizes via stochastic sampling and adaptively selects suitable ones to achieve faster convergence. Numerical experiments on imaging tasks validate our theoretical results, showing reductions in both iterations and computational time compared to existing approaches.

title: AI-Based Offline Speech Recognition for Kazakh, Russian and English Languages
authors: Nursultan Nyssanov, Zuleikha Syzdykova, Kuandyk Niyazaliyev, Ibraheem Shayea
publish: 2025 IEEE/ACIS 29th International Conference on Software Engineering, Artificial Intelligence, Networking and Parallel/Distributed Computing (SNPD)
abstract: This paper presents a novel fully autonomous multilingual audio transcription system tailored to Kazakh, Russian, and English. The proposed solution integrates a language detection module based on SpeechBrain with a transcription engine using Vosk, and employs FFmpeg for robust audio preprocessing. The system automatically detects the language from the initial 10 seconds of an audio stream, selects the corresponding acoustic model, and produces an accurate text transcription. Experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real audio data indicate that our approach achieves competitive performance in terms of accuracy (with word error rates ranging from 5 to 10% under optimal conditions) and processing speed, while operating entirely on local resources without dependency on cloud services. These features make it particularly suitable for applications in digital forensics and other domains that require secure real-time transcription capabilities.

title: Personal Sound Zones with Flexible Bright Zone Control
authors: Wenye Zhu, Jun Tang, Xiaofei Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Personal sound zone (PSZ) reproduction system, which attempts to create distinct virtual acoustic scenes for different listeners at their respective positions within the same spatial area using one loudspeaker array, is a fundamental technology in the application of virtual reality. For practical applications, the reconstruction targets must be measured on the same fixed receiver array used to record the local room impulse responses (RIRs) from the loudspeaker array to the control points in each PSZ, which makes the system inconvenient and costly for real-world use. In this paper, a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) designed for PSZ reproduction with flexible control microphone grid and alternative reproduction target is presented, utilizing the virtual target scene as inputs and the PSZ prefilters as output. Experimental results of the proposed method are compared with the traditional method, demonstrating that the proposed method is able to handle varied reproduction targets on flexible control point grid using only one training session. Furthermore, the proposed method also demonstrates the capability to learn global spatial information from sparse sampling points distributed in PSZs.

title: FED-PISA: Federated Voice Cloning Via Personalized Identity-Style Adaptation
authors: Qi Wang, Shituo Ma, Guoxin Yu, Hanyang Peng, Yue Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Voice cloning for Text-to-Speech (TTS) aims to generate expressive and personalized speech from text using limited data from a target speaker. Federated Learning (FL) offers a collaborative and privacy-preserving framework for this task, but existing approaches suffer from high communication costs and tend to suppress stylistic heterogeneity, resulting in insufficient personalization. To address these issues, we propose Fed-PISA, which stands for Federated Personalized Identity-Style Adaptation. To minimize communication costs, Fed-PISA introduces a disentangled Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) mechanism: the speaker’s timbre is retained locally through a private ID-LoRA, while only a lightweight style-LoRA is transmitted to the server, thereby minimizing parameter exchange. To harness heterogeneity, our aggregation method, inspired by collaborative filtering, is introduced to create custom models for each client by learning from stylistically similar peers. Experiments show that Fed-PISA improves style expressivity, naturalness, and speaker similarity, outperforming standard federated baselines with minimal communication costs.

title: Joint Compression and Direction-of-Arrival Estimation in Distributed Sensor Networks
authors: Amir Weiss
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing prevalence of large-scale sensor networks calls for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation methods that operate under stringent communication constraints. In this work, we show that DOA can be reliably inferred when each remote sensor conveys only extremum indices of its observations, requiring just a few bits. Using these indices, the fusion center computes a steered-response-power-like criterion, whose maximizer is a consistent DOA estimator. Perhaps surprisingly, simulations reveal that our method approaches the accuracy of a theoretical rate-distortion-based benchmark while requiring dramatically fewer bits than conventional compression followed by classical DOA methods.

title: Disentangled Representation Learning with Cross-Factor Constraints for Medical Image Analysis
authors: Xie Huang, Linlu Xu, Yipin Zhang, Ziqi Yu, Shengjie Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate medical image diagnosis requires models that are both robust and interpretable. Existing deep learning methods often rely on end-to-end pipelines, which overlook individual-specific variations and limit clinical utility. We present a disentanglement framework that integrates contrastive learning with reconstruction regularization, decomposing latent representations into disease-specific, private, and shared components to enable individual-aware disease modeling. Experiments on two datasets show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, improving diagnostic accuracy and interpretability. These results suggest the proposed framework as a reliable tool for medical image analysis, and the code is released at https://github.com/huangxie666/MVDL.

title: Complex-Valued Phase Synchrony Reveals Directional Coupling in fMRI and Tracks Medication Effects
authors: Sir-Lord Wiafe, Najme Soleimani, Masoud Seraji, Bradley Baker, Robyn Miller, Ashkan Faghiri, Vince D. Calhoun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Understanding interactions in complex systems requires capturing the relative timing of coupling, not only its strength. Phase synchronization captures this timing, yet most methods either reduce the phase to its cosine or collapse it into scalar indices such as the phase-locking value, discarding relative timing. We propose a complex-valued phase synchrony (CVPS) framework that estimates phase with an adaptive Gabor wavelet and preserves both cosine and sine components. Simulations confirm that CVPS recovers true phase offsets and tracks non-stationary dynamics more faithfully than Hilbert-based methods. Because antipsychotics are known to modulate the timing of cortical interactions, they provide a rigorous context to evaluate whether CVPS can capture such pharmacological effects. CVPS further reveals cortical neuro-hemodynamic drivers, with occipital-to-parietal and prefrontal-to-striatal lead–lag flows consistent with known receptor targets, confirming its ability to capture pharmacological timing. CVPS, therefore, offers a robust, generalizable framework for detecting relative timing in complex systems such as the brain.

title: SODA: A Unified Framework for Joint Estimation of Speaker Orientation and Direction of Arrival
authors: Dongchan Kim, Jonguk Yoo, Kangseok Choi, Chang Woo Han, Hoon-Young Cho, Jong Hwan Ko
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Next-generation voice user interfaces must jointly infer a speaker’s orientation (direction of voice, DOV) and direction of arrival (DOA), yet practical deployment of such joint estimators has been hindered by large microphone arrays and the lack of unified datasets. We propose SODA, a unified framework for few-channel (2 or 4) settings that addresses these barriers. SODA repurposes an existing DOV dataset to generate DOA labels, employs a multi-task architecture, and, through stepwise lightweight design, selects a practical complexity–performance balance to support deployment on resource-constrained devices. Experiments show that SODA with 2 or 4 channels surpasses the 16-channel baseline on distance and orientation metrics, demonstrating the feasibility of high-precision spatial audio sensing with few microphones.

title: Evaluating Pretrained Speech Embedding Systems for Dysarthria Detection Across Heterogenous Datasets
authors: Lovisa Wihlborg, Jemima Goodall, David Wheatley, Jacob J. Webber, Johnny Tam, Christine Weaver, Suvankar Pal, Siddharthan Chandran, Sohan Seth, Oliver Watts, Cassia Valentini-Botinhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present a comprehensive evaluation of pretrained speech embedding systems for the detection of dysarthric speech using existing accessible data. Dysarthric speech datasets are often small and can suffer from recording biases as well as data imbalance. To address these we selected a range of datasets covering related conditions and adopt the use of several cross-validations runs to estimate the chance level. To certify that results are above chance, we compare the distribution of scores across these runs against the distribution of scores of a carefully crafted null hypothesis. In this manner, we evaluate 17 publicly available speech embedding systems across 6 different datasets, reporting the cross-validation performance on each. We also report cross-dataset results derived when training with one particular dataset and testing with another. We observed that within-dataset results vary considerably depending on the dataset, regardless of the embedding used, raising questions about which datasets should be used for benchmarking. We found that cross-dataset accuracy is, as expected, lower than within-dataset, highlighting challenges in the generalization of the systems. These findings have important implications for the clinical validity of systems trained and tested on the same dataset.

title: Feature Identification for Hierarchical Contrastive Learning
authors: Julius Ott, Nastassia Vysotskaya, Huawei Sun, Lorenzo Servadei, Robert Wille
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Hierarchical classification is a crucial task in many applications, where objects are organized into multiple levels of categories. However, conventional classification approaches often neglect the relationships between classes at different hierarchy levels, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose a novel hierarchical contrastive learning method that leverages a Gaussian Mixture Model and an attention mechanism to capture hierarchy-specific features. Our approach explicitly models the interclass relationships and imbalanced class distribution at higher hierarchy levels, enabling fine-grained clustering across all hierarchy levels. On the competitive CIFAR 100 and ModelNet40 datasets, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in linear evaluation, outperforming existing hierarchical contrastive learning methods by 2%. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in learning hierarchical representations and highlight its potential for applications in computer vision and beyond.

title: IBMCT: Breaking The Cost Barrier in Industrial Internet of Things Via High-Fidelity Virtual Sensing
authors: Qingzheng Hu, Chong Zhang, Qiuyan He, Wenyang Xiao, Hangyu Xiong, Ningrong Lai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is essential for developing smart cities, yet its large-scale deployment is constrained by the high cost of sensor hardware. To overcome this limitation, we propose IBMCT, a deep learning model designed to generate high-fidelity virtual sensor data from sparse physical sensor networks through high-precision, time-series-based spatial interpolation. To achieve this goal, we propose Inception Bidirectional Mamba (IB-Mamba) block, which integrates the Mamba with a multi-scale convolutional module, effectively capturing spatiotemporal dependencies at various scales and complex temporal patterns. Additionally, we propose the Trend-Infused Coordinate Attention (TICA) mechanism, which reallocates spatiotemporal weights and emphasizes temporal trends, thereby improving spatial interpolation accuracy and enhancing robustness against noise. Evaluated using real-world data from public datasets, our model offers a cost-effective pathway to advance IIoT development and its scalable application in urban infrastructure.

title: ECSA: Dual-Branch Emotion Compensation for Emotion-Consistent Speaker Anonymization
authors: Chenghan Lin, Junjie Li, Tingting Wang, Meng Ge, Longbiao Wang, Kong Aik Lee, Jianwu Dang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker anonymization techniques have achieved remarkable success in preserving linguistic content while protecting speaker identity. However, these methods often degrade the emotional information in speech, limiting their reliability in emotion-sensitive scenarios. To mitigate this issue, we propose an emotion-preserving speaker anonymization framework. Our approach eliminates the external emotion encoder used in prior methods to avoid speaker information leakage, and instead introduces a novel dual-branch emotion compensation module. This module consists of a static compensator that captures dataset-level emotion distributions via soft labels and non-linear deep learning models, and a dynamic compensator that enhances emotional expression by exploiting utterance-level residuals. Furthermore, we incorporate an emotion consistency loss into the HiFi-GAN training process to ensure effective use of emotional information during anonymization. Experiments on the VPC 2024 benchmark show that our method significantly improves emotion preservation without compromising anonymization performance. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component, and additional evaluations on IEMOCAP demonstrate superior Unweighted Average Recall (UAR) and class-wise recall compared to baseline systems.

title: Joint Calibration and Direction-of-Arrival Estimation for Sparse Linear Arrays: Identifiability and Array Design
authors: Weichao Zheng, Zai Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation is a fundamental problem in array signal processing. In practice, gain and phase errors of sensors are inevitable and manual calibration is often costly, highlighting the importance of joint calibration and DOA estimation. In this paper, we investigate the parameter identifiability problem for sparse linear arrays (SLAs) under certain stochastic assumptions. We provide conditions under which the parameters can be uniquely identified up to a trivial ambiguity. Based on the classical nested arrays, we design a specific type of SLA that satisfies these conditions, referred to as self-calibratable nested arrays (SCNAs). Numerical experiments are conducted to verify the identifiability results and demonstrate the advantages of the SCNAs.

title: MEDCOREF: Co-Training with Pseudo-Label Refinement for Robust Medical Image Classification
authors: Shiming Guo, Yining Zhao, Haodong Chen, Chenghao Yang, Yang Wang, Ali Anaissi, Ali Braytee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning holds great promise for medical image classification; however, its success hinges on large-scale, high-quality annotations that are costly and time-consuming to obtain from expert clinicians, leading to a persistent scarcity of labeled data. While semi-supervised approaches aim to leverage unlabeled images to alleviate this challenge, they frequently suffer from error accumulation in pseudo-labels, which biases model training and degrades generalization. To address these limitations, we propose MedCoRef, a semi-supervised classification framework that improves accuracy under limited annotation and mitigates error propagation by integrating a weak–strong augmentation co-training mechanism to enrich data diversity, contrastive-guided loss integration to steer model updates toward reliable representations, and validation-guided parameter optimization to stabilize training against residual label noise. We evaluate MedCoRef on multiple public medical image datasets and demonstrate that with either 1% or 10% of the dataset labeled it achieves a roughly 4% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods. These results highlight MedCoRef’s effectiveness and robustness in resource constrained medical imaging scenarios.

title: Impact of Employer Branding Determinants on Employee Retention: A PLS-SEM Approach
authors: Jayalakshmi K, Preetha Subrahmanyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The retention of employees has emerged as a dilemma to the organizations in the urban areas like Chennai where talent mobility is heightened by intense competition in the workplace. The paper address how relationship exists between the major employer-branding predictors that include Organizational Leadership (OL), Corporate Reputation (CR), and Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) and employee retention intentions. The study based on the data of 255 employees who are employed in various organizations in Chennai adopts Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) and Structural Equation Modelling Partial Least Squares (PLS-SEM) to test the measurement model, and to test the structural relationships. Findings indicate that there is high reliability, convergent, and discriminant validity between all the constructs with leadership being the most predictive of employee retention, followed by corporate reputation and CSR. The model identifies 35.5% variance in the retention intention, which proves that employer branding is a strong strategic tool to stabilize the workforce. The results not only provide theoretical contributions to the Social Exchange Theory and Signaling Theory but also offer practical implications of organizational retention practices in Chennai where leadership development, CSR integration, and reputation-building activities can be suggested.

title: Wavelet Coherence Analysis of Sensor Location Effects on OLTC Vibrations in Power Transformers
authors: Srđan Jokić, Petar Matić, Budimir Kovačević, Bojana Čolić
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Vibration measurement of on-load tap changers (OLTCs) offers a non-invasive means of assessing their mechanical condition, but the diagnostic value of these measurements is often limited by structural resonances, oil-borne attenuation, and the complex dynamics of the OLTC mechanism. Because vibration responses are highly sensitive to sensor placement, understanding how measurement location influences signal characteristics is essential for reliable interpretation. This study investigates vibration signals recorded at multiple positions on an OLTC and analyses their relationships using wavelet coherence, enabling time-frequency correlation and phase relationship assessment. The results reveal clear location-dependent coherence patterns, demonstrate the influence of resonant structural modes and burst events, and show that mechanical interactions within the OLTC drive system are reflected in the motor stator current. The results provide a basis for improved sensor placement and more reliable interpretation of OLTC vibration diagnostics.

title: IoT-Assisted Multi-Robot System: Impact of Information Sharing on Control
authors: Satwik Mondal, Manish H. Kausik, Sudipta Saha
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: In a decentralized multi-robot system, inter-robot communication plays a crucial role in achieving coordination among the robots. However, the expected precision in the coordination to achieve the goal widely varies from application to application. In this work, we demonstrate the effect of nondeterministic communication delays on a multi-robot system through theoretical and empirical studies. The study reveals that uneven and uncertain communication delays may cause a multi-robot system to experience significant jitter, potentially leading to unexpected state transitions, which can be detrimental in several applications. We demonstrate that recent advances in Synchronous-Transmission (ST)-based protocols, which have proven to be a highly efficient tool for information sharing in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), can mitigate this problem quite effectively. Furthermore, to demonstrate the concept through simulation, we modify an existing network simulation tool, called Cooja, to include support for a multirobot system along with ST-based inter-robot data sharing.

title: Generalization in One-Step Contextual Bandit Based DoA Estimation with Passive Backscatter Tags
authors: Omikumar B. Makadia, Georgios Andreadis, Anastasios Kleniatis, Ivan Seskar, Narayan Mandayam, Aggelos Bletsas
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Spectrum situational awareness demands accurate, low-power and scalable direction of arrival (DoA) estimation of multiple RF signals. We introduce a data-driven DoA solution employing a novel, one-step contextual bandit-based approach with passive backscatter tags and a single-antenna collector. Test parameters (e.g., SNR, Ricean fading K-factor, tag synchronization mismatch, reflection coefficients) are vastly different than those used during training. Interestingly, the proposed approach surpasses MUSIC with linear tag topologies or MUSIC with diverse tag topologies and low number of tags.

title: EMS-Mixer: Extreme Multi-Scale Mixing for Time Series Forecasting
authors: Jiali Deng, Bolun Cai, Yuqing Chen, Ying Hao, Kunkun Liu, Zhanpeng Jin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As a fundamental task, time series forecasting remains highly challenging due to the inherent multi-scale characteristics of temporal data. Existing methods often model spatial and frequency domains separately, limiting their ability to capture both fine- and coarse-grained patterns as well as slow- and fast-varying dynamics. To address this issue, we propose EMS-Mixer, an extreme multi-scale framework that unifies spatial and frequency perspectives for comprehensive temporal modeling. EMS-Mixer leverages multi-scale sampling to disentangle low- and high-frequency components, a dual-path architecture to capture local and global structures, and a cross-scale mixing mechanism that maximizes complementary interactions across resolutions. Extensive experiments on 18 real-world benchmarks show that EMS-Mixer consistently surpasses 13 state-of-the-art baselines in both long- and short-term forecasting, achieving significant gains in accuracy and robustness. Code: https://github.com/jelly1130/EMS-Mixer.

title: Emilia-NV: A Non-Verbal Speech Dataset with Word-Level Annotation for Human-Like Speech Modeling
authors: Huan Liao, Qinke Ni, Yuancheng Wang, Yiheng Lu, Haoyue Zhan, Pengyuan Xie, Qiang Zhang, Zhizheng Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Paralinguistic vocalizations—including non-verbal sounds like laughter, breathing, and lexicalized interjections such as "uhm"—are integral to spoken communication. However, existing corpora rarely provide large-scale, word-level annotations of such cues, leaving them overlooked in conventional ASR and TTS research. Therefore, we present Emilia-NV, the first large-scale Mandarin corpus with word-level annotations for both lexical content and 18 paralinguistic vocalizations. It comprises 48k human-annotated utterances and 174k automatically labeled utterances (573h) The key contributions are: (1) a high-quality, fine-grained dataset of paralinguistic vocalizations that establishes a new benchmark for expressive speech modeling; (2) a paralinguistic-aware ASR (NVASR) that scales manual annotations to large corpora, enabling joint transcription of lexical and non-verbal content; (3) controllable zero-shot TTS(CV2@Emilia-NV) with explicit token-level vocalization control, enabling natural and expressive synthesis. Dataset and audio demos are available at https://nvspeech170k.github.io/.

title: Triad: Tri-Head with Auxiliary Duplicating Permutation Invariant Training for Multi-Task Sound Event Localization and Detection
authors: Bingnan Duan, Yinhuan Dong, Tughrul Arslan, John Thompson
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sound event localization and detection (SELD) requires simultaneous recognition of events and estimation of their spatial positions. Existing output representations have notable limitations. Single-branch heads couple detection and localization too tightly, so improving one task inevitably affects others. Conversely, conventional multi-branch heads fail to represent overlapping events of the same class, reducing their effectiveness. To overcome these limitations, we propose TriAD, a tri-head output representation for SELD. It combines track-wise localization with Auxiliary Duplicating Permutation Invariant Training (ADPIT), enabling the capture of overlapping events. Experiments on DCASE2025 stereo dataset show that TriAD achieves state-of-the-art F1 score performance. We further conduct the first systematic study of gradient-aware multi-task optimization for SELD and demonstrate that, among all tested strategies, Projected Conflicting Gradient (PCGrad) delivers the largest F1 score gains. These findings highlight the importance of robust representations and effective optimization strategies for advancing SELD systems.

title: Multi-Physics: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Multimodal LLMs Reasoning on Chinese Multi-Subject Physics Problems
authors: Zhongze Luo, Zhenshuai Yin, Yongxin Guo, Zhichao Wang, Jionghao Zhu, Xiaoying Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: While multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) demonstrate remarkable reasoning progress, their application in specialized scientific domains like physics reveals significant gaps in current evaluation benchmarks. Specifically, existing benchmarks often lack fine-grained subject coverage, neglect the step-by-step reasoning process, and are predominantly English-centric, failing to systematically evaluate the role of visual information. Therefore, we introduce Multi-Physics for Chinese physics reasoning, a comprehensive benchmark that includes 5 difficulty levels, featuring 1,412 image-associated, multiple-choice questions spanning 11 high-school physics subjects. We employ a dual evaluation framework to evaluate 20 different MLLMs, analyzing both final answer accuracy and the step-by-step integrity of their chain-of-thought. Furthermore, we systematically study the impact of difficulty level and visual information by comparing the model performance before and after changing the input mode. Our work provides not only a fine-grained resource for the community but also offers a robust methodology for dissecting the multimodal reasoning process of state-of-the-art MLLMs, and our dataset and code have been open-sourced <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: VoroGeomNet: A Graph Neural Network Based on Voronoi Tessellation for Property Prediction of Porous Material
authors: Hengda Gao, Linghua Xiao, Ke Duan, Xiao-Wei Guo, Chao Li, Jianwei Zhang, Yonglyu He, Juan Chen, Canqun Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Porous materials have attracted extensive attention across multiple fields due to their remarkable mechanical properties. Nevertheless, predicting these properties often depends on labor-intensive experiments or computationally expensive simulations, which limit the efficiency of materials design. Recent advances in pixel-based neural networks provide a data-driven alternative. Still, they face a dilemma: high-resolution inputs impose prohibitive computational costs, whereas low-resolution inputs lead to critical information loss. To tackle this issue, we propose a resolution-agnostic graph representation based on Voronoi tessellation and introduce the Voronoi Geometric Graph Neural Network (VoroGeomNet) model with the VoroGeom Attention Convolutional Operator (VACO), specifically designed for efficient and effective message passing. Comprehensive experiments across two real-world tasks demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline in prediction accuracy, achieving a maximum improvement of 51.86%, which highlights its potential for efficient and reliable screening of porous materials.

title: DADAGAN: An Image Super-Resolution Network with Pixel-Wise Estimation of Degradation Degrees
authors: Xinyue Zhan, Jiaxiong Liu, Man Zhao, Jiaran Qiu, Jun Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Blind super-resolution methods have shown remarkable progress in handling image degradation. Recent approaches rely on complex degradation models with broad yet imprecise parameter distributions, which enhance generalization but often compromise fine detail restoration due to mismatches with real images. To address this issue, we propose DADAGAN (Degradation-Aware and Degradation-Adaptive GAN), which explicitly adapts to pixel-wise, spatially variant degradation. Our framework integrates (i) a degradation-aware network that predicts a degradation map from the input, and (ii) a degradation-adaptive network that dynamically modulates its blocks with this map. This design enables fine-grained adaptation to varying degradation degrees, effectively balancing degradation removal and detail preservation without retraining. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that DADAGAN achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality and robustness across diverse degradation conditions.

title: Hybrid Power Supply of Alarm Stations in the Surveillance, Alert and Warning Systems
authors: Željko V.Despotović, Marko.R. Tajdić
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Surveillance, alert and warning systems (SAWS) have the task of carrying out all necessary measures in accordance with legal regulations and rules in order to monitor, notify, and if necessary, partially or generally alert the population in the endangered area. The basic element of the SAWS is the alarm station, which is based on alarm sirens and the associated electronic and telecommunication system. The power supply of the alarm station is very important for the reliable functioning of the entire system. The power supply in these cases is usually obtained from a permanent mains connection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$230 ~\mathrm{V}, 50 ~\text{Hz}$</tex>. In the event that the connection is not permanent but occasional (as is very often the case in practice), it is necessary to base the power supply system on a hybrid power supply, which is a combination of mains power supply and power supply from renewable energy sources (RES). The most common combination of this type of power supply is mains and photovoltaic (PV) power. This paper presents the implementation of PV power, which was realized as part of the hybrid power supply of the alarm station in the flood wave protection system at the “Bovan” dam in the municipality of Aleksinac.

title: An Efficient Channel Estimation Structure for 6G IRS-Assisted Mimo Via DFT-Noma with Enhanced Uplink Performance
authors: S. Seethalakshmi, Mahendran R, M Soniya, Surendran R, K S. Balamurugan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: This article develops a novel channel estimation scheme for the IRS-aided 6G MIMO uplink communications. Different from the current IRS-aided NOMA architectures, this model includes an enhanced downlink DFT-based multiuser approach, in addition to the optimised IRS beamforming and the reducedcorrelation channel modelling. For the sake of fair comparison, the system parameters are typical and updated as follows: 12 W transmit power, 128 IRS elements, 120 GHz carrier frequency, 8 dB antenna gain, reflection coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.95$</tex>, and optimised RRC pulse with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta=0.3$</tex> in order to have strong signal reception performance improvements. Simulation results show significant performance improvement over the baseline method, i.e., received power gain exceeding 50 % for any user pair, SINR increase of 17 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2 ~ d B}$</tex> and correlation reduction by more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 5 \%}$</tex>. It confirms that the introduced enhanced-DFT IRSMIMO uplink model substantially enhances channel estimation accuracy, spectral efficiency and multiuser robustness, thereby making it a promising candidate for 6 G communication systems.

title: TLD-PGD: Two-Stage Low Frequency Degradation Adversarial Attack in Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Ziyu You, Yaqi Liu, Song Xiao, Weize Li, Yifan Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Enhancing the transferability of adversarial attacks is a critical challenge in Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification. This is difficult for HSI, as its complex localized features cause models to learn distinct representations, limiting attack generalization. Traditional frequency-domain attacks, often global, fail to disrupt these crucial local features. This paper proposes the Two-Stage Low-Frequency Degradation Projected Gradient Descent (TLD-PGD) framework to address this. TLD-PGD employs a novel Low-Frequency Degradation (LFD) loss to disrupt localized low-frequency patterns that are more likely to be model-invariant. It also integrates a Wavelet-based Low-Frequency Gradient Smoothing (WLGS) technique to refine the perturbation direction and suppress gradient noise, thereby improving transferability. Experiments on two HSI datasets validate that our method generates resilient adversarial examples with superior transferability across diverse target architectures.

title: A Lightweight and Accurate Defect-Detection Framework for Battery Cells Based on EfficientNet-YOLOv9
authors: Yixiang Liu, Jinlong Wu, Bing Leng, Haihua Guo, Yibin Li
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: In this study, a lightweight and high-accuracy visual inspection framework is developed for distinguishing defective and normal prismatic lithium-ion battery cells under non-standardized recycling conditions. An EfficientNet-YOLOv9 variant is constructed by replacing the baseline CSP backbone with a compound-scaled feature extractor. Experimental results show that the proposed EfficientNet-YOLOv9 achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text {1. 1 1 M}$</tex> parameters and 6.4 GFLOPs, representing a 46.6% reduction in parameters and a 24.7% reduction in computation compared with YOLOv9-t(2.08M, 8.5 GFLOPs). And the EfficientNet-YOLOv9 model achieves strong detection results, with Precision reaching 0.946, Recall reaching 0.960, mAP50 reaching 0.986, and mAP50-95 reaching 0.915. These results indicate that the proposed framework maintains high discriminative capability while substantially reducing model size and computational cost. Overall, EfficientNet-YOLOv9 provides an effective, lightweight, and computation-efficient solution for large-scale battery-cell inspection and offers strong potential for deployment in automated recycling systems.

title: Background Disambiguation Contrastive Loss for Robust Binary Segmentation
authors: Pantelis Mentesidis, Vasileios Mygdalis, Ioannis Pitas
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Binary region segmentation, critical for applications such as natural disaster response (e.g., flood mapping) and industrial inspection (e.g., pipe defect detection), remains challenging due to the high variance and ambiguity within background regions. Standard Deep Neural Network (DNN) training often produces distorted feature spaces where diverse background patterns collapse into indistinct clusters. To address this, we propose a Background Disambiguation Contrastive Loss (BDCL), introduced as an auxiliary objective alongside the task loss. BDCL encourages compact intra-class clustering while simultaneously enforcing stronger inter-class separation, effectively disambiguating heterogeneous background features and leading to more discriminative representations. When applied to a state-of-the-art (SOTA) model, our approach achieves absolute performance gains of +2.4% on flood segmentation and +4.8% on pipe segmentation over baseline training. Beyond quantitative improvements, BDCL consistently yields sharper and semantically meaningful segmentations in cluttered scenes, outperforming conventional training and existing contrastive learning strategies.

title: APSFormer: Enhancing Transformer in Time Series Forecasting with Adaptive Multi-Scale Patch and Sparse Attention
authors: Xuanchi Guo, Zhiwei Zhang, Weihao Wang, Wenjuan Han
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting is a fundamental task in many real-world applications and has benefited from recent advances in Transformer-based models, which improve the modeling of long-term dependencies and sequence dynamics. However, these models often suffer from redundancy and limited adaptability under distribution shifts. In this paper, we propose APSFormer, a novel Transformer architecture that employs adaptive multi-scale patch embeddings to dynamically capture heterogeneous temporal patterns at different granularities and emphasize more informative patches, coupled with a sparse attention mechanism to suppress redundant or irrelevant correlations effectively. Extensive experiments on seven widely used real-world datasets demonstrate that APSFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across various forecasting horizons, achieving superior performance.

title: ParaEdit: Unifying Parallel Transport and Generative Flows for High-Fidelity Image Editing
authors: Yuan Zhou, Jinyoung Wen, Yuyang Hong, Kun Ding, Yan Zhang, Guangwen Yang, Shiming Xiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing inversion-based image editing methods often rely on a two-stage process of inverting an image to a noisy latent and then regenerating the image, a detour which introduces semantic artifacts and degrades fine-grained details. We present ParaEdit, a training-free, model-agnostic framework that reimagines editing through differential geometry. ParaEdit avoids explicit inversion by constructing a direct editing trajectory on the latent manifold between source and target states, guided by parallel transport. The trajectory follows a minimal-energy principle, analogous to a Linear Quadratic Regulator, via incremental corrections to the underlying vector fields. Experiments show ParaEdit preserves fine details, and demonstrates its superior sharpness and semantic consistency over state-of-the-art methods, underscoring the efficacy of a geometric approach for high-fidelity editing.

title: Physics Informed Generative Models for Magnetic Field Images
authors: A.P.P. Aung, Lucas Lum, Zhansen Shi, Wen Qiu, Bernice Zee, JM Chin, Yeow Kheng Lim, J. Senthilnath
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In semiconductor manufacturing, defect detection and localization are critical to ensuring product quality and yield. While X-ray imaging is a reliable non-destructive testing method, it is memory-intensive and time-consuming for large-scale scanning, Magnetic Field Imaging (MFI) offers a more efficient means to localize regions of interest (ROI) for targeted X-ray scanning. However, the limited availability of MFI datasets due to proprietary concerns presents a significant bottleneck for training machine learning (ML) models using MFI. To address this challenge, we propose Physics Informed Generative Models for Magnetic Field Images (PI-GenMFI) to generate synthetic MFI samples by integrating specific physical information. We generate MFI images for the most common defect types: power shorts. These synthetic images will serve as training data for ML algorithms designed to localize defect areas efficiently. We present domain experts, qualitative and quantitative evaluation using various metrics used for image generation and signal processing, showing promising results to optimize the defect localization process.

title: Cactus-inspired ionic liquid functionalized MOF-based humidity sensor for high response ultra-low humidity detection_supp1-3684636.pdf
authors: Ke Wu
publish: N/A
abstract: Accurate monitoring of low humidity exhibits critical application value in semiconductor manufacturing, precision agriculture, and related fields. However, most reported low humidity sensors commonly face the limitations of low response value and linearity within the ultralow humidity range (<10% RH). Herein, we develop a cactus-inspired ionic liquid (IL)-modified metal organic frameworks (MOFs), in which the UiO-66-2SH was prepared through the solvothermal method first. Then, the IL with a specific length was introduced via click reaction, retaining the pore structure and enhancing the hydrophilicity and ionic conductivity of UiO-66. The optimized sensor exhibits excellent sensing performance in the ultralow humidity range of 0.02% RH–10.3% RH, showing a high response of 23.2, a narrow humidity hysteresis of 0.6% RH, a good linearity (R<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{2}= 0.994$ </tex-math></inline-formula>), and a fast response time of 23.0 s. The sensing mechanism of the sensor is discussed, combined with the water molecule adsorption process and proton conduction. Furthermore, the potential application of the sensor in a water source location is demonstrated. This work provides a promising sensitive material for high-performance ultralow humidity detection.

title: Timely Trajectory Reconstruction in Finite Buffer Remote Tracking Systems
authors: Sunjung Kang, Vishrant Tripathi, Christopher G. Brinton
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT, monitoring, surveillance and healthcare. In such systems, maintaining both real-time state awareness (for online decision making) and accurate reconstruction of historical trajectories (for offline post-processing) are essential. While the Age of Information (AoI) metric has been extensively studied as a measure of freshness, it does not capture the accuracy with which past trajectories can be reconstructed. In this work, we investigate reconstruction error as a complementary metric to AoI, addressing the trade-off between timely updates and historical accuracy. Specifically, we we consider a remote tracking system where the source evolves as a Wiener process and examine three policies, each prioritizing different aspects of information management: Keep-Old, Keep-Fresh, and our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy. We compare these policies in terms of impact on both AoI and reconstruction error in a remote tracking system with a finite buffer. Through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations of queueing behavior, we demonstrate that while the Keep-Fresh policy minimizes AoI, it does not necessarily minimize reconstruction error. In contrast, our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy dynamically adjusts packet retention decisions based on generation times, achieving a balance between AoI and reconstruction error. Our results provide key insights into the design of efficient buffer management policies for resource-constrained IoT networks.

title: Design of Polar Subcodes for Permutation Decoding
authors: Mikhail Chernikov, Peter Trifonov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: novel construction of randomized polar subcodes and subcodes of extended BCH codes is presented. The proposed approach stems from a new heuristic for building polar code frozen set which minimizes the error probability of successive cancellation algorithm applied to permuted noisy codewords. The frame error rate (FER) of the proposed codes under permutationbased decoding is comparable to FER of existing polar subcodes under successive cancellation list or sequential algorithm.

title: RadioVAR: Fast Sampling-Free Radio Map Construction via Coarse-to-Fine Token Prediction
authors: Han Zhang, Yu Han, Hanhui Chen, Mengchen Yao, Yun Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Accurate spatial priors of received power spectral density (PSD) are important for communication-aware urban sensing and learning-based resource management. Sampling-free radio map (RM) construction avoids costly in-area measurements by directly constructing a full PSD-based radio map from environmental priors. However, mainstream diffusion-based methods incur high inference latency and lack an explicit coarse-to-fine generation path. To address these issues, we propose RadioVAR, a visual autoregressive (VAR) progressive framework for sampling-free RM construction. RadioVAR replaces iterative denoising with a feed-forward next-scale token prediction process, which substantially reduces inference computation and latency. RadioVAR first represents radio maps in a discrete multi-scale latent space using a frozen tokenizer, and performs coarse-to-fine next-scale token prediction conditioned on multi-channel priors (e.g., building layout, Tx location, and vehicle occupancy maps). We further introduce dynamic residual-token supervision to construct state-dependent token targets from the residual-to-go, improving training–inference alignment. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that RadioVAR improves reconstruction quality over diffusion-based baselines while significantly reducing inference latency, thereby offering a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off for edge deployments.

title: Forgettable Federated Linear Learning With Certified Data Unlearning
authors: Ruinan Jin, Minghui Chen, Qiong Zhang, Xiaoxiao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The advent of federated learning (FL) has revolutionized the way distributed systems handle collaborative model training while preserving user privacy. Recently, federated unlearning (FU) has emerged to address demands for the “right to be forgotten” and unlearning of the impact of poisoned clients without requiring retraining in FL. Most FU algorithms require the cooperation of retained or target clients (clients to be unlearned), introducing additional communication overhead and potential security risks. In addition, some FU methods need to store historical models to execute the unlearning process. These challenges hinder the efficiency and memory constraints of the current FU methods. Moreover, due to the complexity of nonlinear models and their training strategies, most existing FU methods for deep neural networks (DNNs) lack theoretical certification. In this work, we introduce a novel FL training and unlearning strategy in DNN, termed forgettable federated linear learning (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> considers a common practice of using pretrained models to approximate DNN linearly, allowing them to achieve similar performance as the original networks via federated linear training (<monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FLT</monospace>). We then present <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FedRemoval</monospace>, a certified, efficient, and secure unlearning strategy that enables the server to unlearn a target client without requiring client communication or adding additional storage. We have conducted extensive empirical validation on small- to large-scale datasets, using both convolutional neural networks and modern foundation models (FMs). These experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in balancing model accuracy with the successful unlearning of target clients. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> represents a promising pipeline for efficient and trustworthy FU. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://anonymous.4open.science/r/2F2L-Federated-Unlearning-D57D/README.md</uri>

title: IEEE GRSS Schools: Advancing Career Development and Promoting Impactful Educational Initiatives
authors: Nouha Mezned, Carlos López Martínez, Ryo Natsuaki, Qiang Zhan
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: An 18.3- μ W 108.3-dB DR Discrete-Time Delta-Sigma Modulator Using a Loop Filter Auto-Shift Technique
authors: Cong Wei, Ziqiang Peng, Lijie Huang, Jinze Lai, Rongshan Wei, Xiaoqiang Lu, Zhichao Tan
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This article presents a discrete-time, third-order, single-loop, 17-level delta-sigma modulator (DSM) for high-dynamic-range sensor measurement applications, which employs a loop filter auto-shift (LFAS) technique. It uses an on-chip 1.5-bit Schmitt monitor to determine whether the input amplitude is large or small. The DSM automatically shifts to loop filter A for large input amplitudes to achieve a larger maximum stable amplitude (MSA) and shifts to loop filter B for small input amplitudes to reduce equivalent kT/C noise and in-band quantization noise (IBQN). The 1.5-bit successive-approximation-register (SAR) monitor, 17-level SAR quantizer, false trigger protection (FTP) module, capacitor compensation array, and pre-charge cascoded floating inverter amplifier (PFIA) support real-time shifting of the proposed DSM while ensuring good performance. The prototype DSM is designed and fabricated in a 180-nm CMOS process, achieving a 101.1-dB SNDR and a 108.3-dB dynamic range (DR) within a 3-kHz bandwidth, while consuming <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$18.3~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>W. With the help of the LFAS technique, the SNDR is improved by 5.65 dB at small input amplitudes. As a result, the Schreier figure of merit (FoM) for SNDR and DR is 183.2 and 190.4 dB, respectively.

title:
S
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    Mamba-Pan: Spectral-Spatial-Scale Mamba with Frequency-Decoupled Dual-Stream for Pansharpening
authors: Zishun Song, Yao Zhang, Haoyu Li, Yanlin He, Jiawei Zhao, Yi Yang, Wei Zhang, Dezhen Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Pansharpening plays a critical role in remote sensing image processing by generating high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images through the fusion of low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) and high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) images. In recent years, deep learning–based approaches have significantly advanced fusion performance via end-to-end modeling. However, existing methods still face notable limitations, including insufficient modeling of long-range dependencies, tight coupling between spectral and spatial information that may introduce spectral distortion and high computational complexity that restricts practical deployment in high-resolution scenarios. To address these issues, we propose Spectral-Spatial-Scale Mamba for Pansharpening (S3Mamba-Pan), a frequency-aware state space framework that jointly models spectral structure, spatial texture and multi-scale context in a coordinated yet decoupled manner. The method first extracts a global spectral anchor from the LRMS input to guide fusion, then performs wavelet-based frequency decoupling to separate low-frequency spectral components from high-frequency spatial details. A dual-stream Mamba architecture conducts parallel state space modeling for spectral correlation learning and PAN-guided texture enhancement and an adaptive distribution recalibration module aligns channel-wise statistics before reconstruction. Experiments on WV3, QuickBird and GF2 demonstrate consistent improvements over representative CNN, Transformer and SSM-based baselines under reduced-resolution evaluation. Under full-resolution no-reference assessment on WV3, S3Mamba-Pan achieves Dλ 0.0144, Ds 0.0303 and HQNR 0.960, indicating improved spectral fidelity and spatial consistency. Ablation and visualization analyses further corroborate the contribution of each component and provide interpretable evidence for the effectiveness of frequency decoupling and spectral anchoring. Code is available at https://github.com/FreeZS-a/S3Mamba.

title: Scalable neural pushbroom architectures for real-time denoising of hyperspectral images onboard satellites
authors: Ziyao Yi, Davide Piccinini, Diego Valsesia, Tiziano Bianchi, Enrico Magli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The next generation of Earth observation satellites will seek to deploy intelligent models directly onboard the payload in order to minimize the latency incurred by the transmission and processing chain of the ground segment, for time-critical applications. Designing neural architectures for onboard execution, particularly for satellite-based hyperspectral imagers, poses novel challenges due to the unique constraints of this environment and imaging system that are largely unexplored by the traditional computer vision literature. In this paper, we show that this setting requires addressing three competing objectives, namely high-quality inference with low complexity, dynamic power scalability and fault tolerance. We focus on the problem of hyperspectral image denoising, which is a critical task to enable effective downstream inference, and highlights the constraints of the onboard processing scenario. We propose a neural network design that addresses the three aforementioned objectives with several novel contributions. In particular, we propose a mixture of denoisers that can be resilient to radiation-induced faults as well as allowing for time-varying power scaling. Moreover, each denoiser employs an innovative architecture where an image is processed line-by-line in a causal way, with a memory of past lines, in order to match the acquisition process of pushbroom hyperspectral sensors and greatly limit memory requirements. We show that the proposed architecture can run in real-time, i.e., process one line in the time it takes to acquire the next one, on low-power hardware and provide competitive denoising quality with respect to significantly more complex state-of-the-art models. We also show that the power scalability and fault tolerance objectives provide a design space with multiple tradeoffs between those properties and denoising quality.

title: O-Band 8x8x8λ Wavelength Selective Optical Cross-Connect Switch for Data Center Networks
authors: Boyang Zheng, Marijn Rombouts, Robert Palmer, Giuseppe Talli, Nicola Calabretta
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Driven by the rapid expansion of AI and cloud-based workloads, intra-data center connections demand scalable, flexible, and energy-efficient all-optical switching technologies. Considering the diverse internal traffic patterns in data centers, wavelength-selective optical cross-connect (WSOXC) switch enables flexible reconfiguration in wavelength, space, time, bandwidth, and routing, providing an efficient solution for dynamic traffic adaptation. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a fast (sub-microseconds) low loss and crosstalk and polarization independent 8 channels O-band 4×4×8λ and 8×8×8λ WSOXC switch. The modular WSOXC switch is based on ultra-low crosstalk, flat-top and polarization independent AWGs and SOAs wavelength switches with 400 GHz wavelength spacing. The proposed WSOXC exhibits low loss both for the 4×4 and 8×8 configurations, with crosstalk below –40 dB. In 800 Gb/s PAM4 transmission experiments, power penalties of 0.7 dB and 1.2 dB are achieved under the evaluation standard of KP4 FEC. Moreover, the contributions to the overall power penalty in the proposed WSOXC were individually analyzed, confirming that the measured penalties in the system verification originated entirely from SOA-related noise. In addition, the impacts of crosstalk, OSNR, and gain-saturation effects were clearly identified. The crosstalk analysis reveals that the demonstrated AWG provides sufficient margin for hybrid photonic integration and can be further scaled to support more wavelength channels. Meanwhile, the OSNR investigation establishes clear design guidelines for the required OSNR and SOA characteristics to enable larger port-count implementations, such as 16×16 and 32×32. These results validate the feasibility and scalability of O-band modular WSOXC for next-generation optical switched datacenter networks.

title: Counterfactual Risk Minimization for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
authors: Yanhua Yang, Muli Yang, Jiahua Li, Henan Wang, Cheng Deng, Hongyuan Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: The out-of-distribution (OOD) property in data is deemed as one main challenge hindering the generalization ability of machine learning algorithms. However, the underlying reasons for this property remain an intriguing and open question that has yet to be fully understood. In this paper, we seek to enhance our understanding of the OOD phenomenon by framing it as a problem of distribution shift and addressing it through two complementary causal perspectives. The first is a generative causal view that elucidates the data generation process. We introduce a novel three-dimensional coordinate system to represent three fundamental distribution shifts, illustrating their role in various OOD generalization problems. The second is an anti-causal view that focuses on the model learning process. We develop an effective approach dubbed Counterfactual Risk Minimization (CRM) to address arbitrary distribution shifts in a unified framework. Additionally, we introduce a new multidomain visual recognition dataset called CONA to facilitate further exploration of OOD generalization. We conduct evaluations of CRM alongside several state-of-the-art competitors on four benchmark datasets under the three distribution shifts. The results not only affirm CRM’s superiority but also shed light on potential future directions.

title: Capacitance-to-Digital Converters: Recent Advances and Evolving Trends from Circuit-Level Performances to System Requirements
authors: Donghyun Youn, Gichan Yun, Yoontae Jung, Hoyong Seong, Sohmyung Ha, Minkyu Je
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of capacitance-to-digital converters (CDCs) for capacitive sensor interfaces, combining a historically thorough CDC survey with detailed architectural analysis. Covering over a decade of IEEE-published designs, it introduces key performance metrics of CDC design—energy efficiency, resolution, and input range—across successive-approximation-register (SAR), delta-sigma-modulation (DSM), period-modulation (PM), and hybrid architectures, supported by survey plots that reveal existing trade-offs and design trends. Each topology is examined quantitatively for recent advances that mitigate the trade-offs between intrinsic energy consumption and noise performance, as well as its remaining circuit-level limitations. Hybrid architectures, which alleviate these limitations while effectively combining the strengths of each topology, are also introduced. In addition, this review provides a detailed comparison of numerical metrics for recent CDC designs, analyzing how each topology improves its characteristic performance and aligns with specific application requirements. Moreover, beyond numerical metrics, it addresses critical system-level considerations, such as stand-alone operation from unregulated supplies, temperature insensitivity, parasitic robustness, and realistic sensor configurations, which are essential for practical CDC deployment. The paper concludes by emphasizing the importance of balancing circuit-level performances and system-level requirements in CDC designs to accommodate the increasing significance of capacitive sensor interfaces.

title: Target Speaker Isolation for Mobile Health Assessment from Speech with Crosstalk
authors: Apiwat Ditthapron, Adam C. Lammert, Emmanuel O. Agu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Speech assessments are crucial for monitoring medical conditions such as Parkinson’s disease, depression, and Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI). However, crosstalk—speech from non-target speakers—presents a critical challenge in real-world mobile health assessments, often leading to false diagnoses. Continuous, real-world speech assessments using mobile devices are realistic but require additional pre-processing steps to ensure speech quality. Isolating the target speaker’s speech is necessary before health assessments. Previous research has focused on removing background noise but paid limited attention to crosstalk in passive health assessments. Some prior work addressed crosstalk using speech separation, which is found to create artifacts on the extracted speech. Instead, we propose Target Speaker Isolation with Normal Distribution (TSI-N) to isolate speech samples of the target speaker. Unlike standard embeddings, TSI-N utilizes an N-vector speaker representation trained to fit an unbounded normal distribution for each speaker cluster. This probabilistic property enables robust null-hypothesis testing and rapid online learning on resource-constrained mobile devices. In rigorous evaluations on various levels of crosstalk, TSI-N significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of up to 36% for TBI detection and 18% for depression detection in passive speech assessments.

title: Mobility-Aware Joint Task Offloading and Resource Allocation in SDN-Enabled MEC Networks via Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Hengli Jin, Mark A. Gregory, Shuo Li
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The exponential growth of computation-intensive mobile applications calls for intelligent resource management in multi-access edge computing (MEC) networks under realistic user mobility. However, mobility-induced dynamics couple wireless quality, queue evolution, and offloading decisions, which can degrade performance when using mobility-agnostic designs. This paper proposes a unified framework for joint task offloading and resource allocation in SDN-enabled MEC systems. We formulate the problem as a Markov decision process with explicit velocity-based mobility modeling and develop a Mobility-aware Hierarchical Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MH-DDPG) algorithm. The hierarchical policy decomposes the hybrid discrete–continuous action space: a high-level module selects edge servers, while a low-level module allocates continuous resources, and a coordination-enhanced hierarchical attention mechanism promotes coherent decisions across the two levels. We further introduce mobility-adaptive prioritized experience replay to account for mobility-driven distribution shifts during training. Theoretical analysis establishes convergence guarantees under the two-timescale stochastic approximation framework, while adaptive weight adjustment facilitates trade-off navigation across operating regions of the multi-objective space. Extensive evaluations on two real-world urban mobility traces (MDT-NJUST and T-Drive) show consistent improvements in latency, energy consumption, and task success rate over seven representative baselines, with stable learning dynamics under dynamic network conditions.

title: Multirate Finite-Control-Set Motor Parameter-Free Predictive Control for SPMSM Drives
authors: Xuesong Wu, Bowei Li, Cheng Xue, Yunwei Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Compared to conventional single-rate schemes, the multirate finite-control-set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) allows higher switching frequencies at relatively low sampling rates. However, multirate schemes exhibit increased sensitivity to parameter variations, particularly as the number of subcycles increases. Although motor parameter-free prediction techniques can partially mitigate this sensitivity, their effectiveness within a multirate framework remains constrained by control parameters and nonlinear factors. To address these challenges, this work proposes an improved multirate FCS motor parameter-free predictive control (FCS-MPFPC) scheme. First, an adaptive control-parameter-tuning approach based on Kalman filters is introduced, which utilizes current variation information to recursively estimate the impact of input voltage. Second, corrected voltage vectors accounting for dead-time effects are incorporated into both the prediction process and observers. Furthermore, rotor movement effects are compensated using second-order generalized integrators (SOGI) and Lagrange interpolation. Experimental results validate the proposed method, demonstrating improved performance under both nominal and mismatched parameter conditions.

title: Integrated Sensing and Covert Communications: A Robust Design Against Mobile Surveillance
authors: Jiaqi Wang, Lu Lv, Yilin Wang, Bingtao He, Long Yang
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This paper investigates a two-stage covert enhancement beamforming design based on the extended Kalman filter (EKF) to combat the mobile eavesdropper (Willie) in the integrated sensing and covert communications. The proposed scheme employs artificial noise (AN) to interfere with and track Willie, while the beamwidth of AN is controlled by the number of antennas. In the initial stage, due to the lack of prior information, the EKF suffers from large prediction errors, preventing the jamming beam from fully aligning with Willie. Thus, a wide AN beam is adopted to guarantee the jamming effect on Willie. In the second stage, as the prediction error of the EKF converges after iterative updates, the scheme switches to a narrow AN beam to track Willie effectively. Considering the imperfect channel state information of the covert user, we jointly optimize the time allocation and transmit beamforming for the two stages, aiming to minimize transmission energy, and propose the joint wide and narrow beam optimization algorithm to address the non-convex problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme reduces the transmission energy by approximately 23% compared with the benchmark schemes.

title: A Minimal Hardware System For Impedance Measurement
authors: Paolo Carbone, Alessio De Angelis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article describes the realization of a low-complexity impedance measurement system. Besides the analog part designed to realize a voltage-controlled current source, data acquisition is based on one-bit quantization. The acquisition framework is fully described. It extends a known technique for estimating sine wave parameters from one-bit quantized data by including the available a priori information on the properties of the dither signal. The realized instrument is applied to the two-wire measurement of the dynamic impedance of a ${\bf 18650}$ rechargeable battery. Experimental results are compared with impedance measurements based on a full-resolution quantizer and an LCR meter on a reference impedance, showing good agreement while achieving significantly lower complexity.

title: Analog Implementation of a Fractional-Order PID Controller for the T200 Thruster Using a Novel Lattice Type Structure
authors: Yunus Emre Yokuş, Aslihan Kartci, Umut Engin Ayten, Roman Sotner, Jaroslav Koton
publish: IEEE Access
