abstract: The T200 thruster is a widely used propulsion system for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs); however, achieving high-performance control with low energy consumption remains a challenge due to inherent system nonlinearities and uncertainties. Fractional-order PID (FOPID) controllers offer enhanced flexibility and robustness compared to classical PID structures, making them well-suited for such complex dynamic systems; however, their practical analog realization and algorithm-based parameter optimization remain limited in the existing literature. This paper presents a unified control–hardware co-design framework for the T200 thruster by combining metaheuristic optimization, FOPID control theory, and low-power analog circuit design. Within this framework, a transfer function of the T200 thruster is obtained from experimental input–output data, and the parameters of the FOPID controller are optimized using multiple metaheuristic algorithms to enhance dynamic performance. A novel lattice-type circuit structure is then introduced for the analog implementation of the optimized FOPID controller. Comparative performance evaluation using transient response criteria and a series of statistical analysis methods demonstrates that the Salp Swarm Algorithm provides the most consistent and effective tuning results, yielding a well-damped closed-loop response with zero overshoot, improved transient behavior, and strong robustness against disturbances and parameter variations. SPICE simulations of the proposed lattice-type analog implementation show close agreement with MATLAB-based control results while achieving low power consumption of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$22.902~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W and reliable operation under process, voltage, temperature, and noise variations. These results confirm the feasibility and advantages of algorithm-optimized FOPID controllers for next-generation marine robotic systems in which high-precision control and low power consumption are crucial.

title: Symptom-Aware Adaptation of CLIP for Few-Shot Medical Image Classification_supp2-3685174.pdf
authors: Wenxu Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Vision-language pre-training model yields powerful general-purpose representations, yet its direct application to medical images is hindered by two key gaps: (i) low sensitivity to fine-grained pathological cues and (ii) semantic misalignment with structured clinical terminology. We propose SITSA, a framework that adapts a frozen CLIP backbone for data-scarce medical tasks without full fine-tuning. SITSA introduces three synergistic components: (1) MedVisExtractor, a depthwise-separable convolutional adapter that recalibrates patch tokens to emphasize subtle lesions; (2) SymSemLoRA, which injects low-rank updates into the text encoder conditioned on large-language-model-generated prompts that encode texture, location, and shape attributes; and (3) Prototype-Guided Alignment, a cross-modal module that binds image patches and prompt tokens via shared learnable prototypes. Extensive experiments on COVID-QU-Ex, OCTMNIST, and DERMAMNIST demonstrate that SITSA surpasses thirteen state-of-the-art baselines in both few-shot and base-to-novel generalization, achieving the highest harmonic mean accuracy while keeping the backbone frozen. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/enfantsRichesDeprimes/sitsa</uri>.

title: CNIE: Content-Aware Non-Transferable Information Extraction for Fine-Grained Visual Categorization
authors: Qiaoqi Li, Shuo Ye, Yunfeng Diao, Dan Guo, Yantao Wei, Shutao Xia, Zitong Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Non-transferable learning (NTL) aims to restrict a model's generalization ability to a target domain. Existing methods primarily focus on coarse-grained tasks. However, the problem of NTL at the fine-grained level remains largely unexplored, mainly due to the difficulty in distinguishing subtle inter-class variations within the source domain while simultaneously suppressing transferable patterns that may generalize to the target domain. To this end, this paper proposes CNIE for this challenge, and the framework consists of three key components. The Fine-grained Prompt Representation (FPR) module effectively activates the rich semantic knowledge embedded in the pre-trained model by introducing mixed-granularity prompts and transfers it to downstream tasks. The Content-Aware Decoupling Model (CDM) disentangles visual features into two orthogonal components: content and context. The context captures latent domain-specific information to guide domain-level suppression and enhance non-transferability. The Non-Transferable Information Extraction (NIE) module encodes subclass-specific content, which is constrained by an information bottleneck to learn a minimal sufficient representation that preserves the intrinsic causal semantics of fine-grained categories. Both qualitative results and quantitative visualizations on three FGVC-related NTL datasets consistently demonstrate that the learned fine-grained feature representations achieve optimal non-transferability to the target domain with minimal degradation in the source domain, while also establishing new state-of-the-art results in non-transferable fine-grained representation learning.

title: LoD-GS: Robust and Lightweight Gaussian Splatting SLAM for Real-Time Volumetric Scene Reconstruction
authors: Jiachen Wang, Seung-Hyun Kong
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Real-time 3D reconstruction is becoming a key enabler for robotics, mixed reality, and autonomous vehicles. Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled high-fidelity volumetric modeling, and their integration with SLAM shows strong potential for real-time deployment. However, the substantial size of 3DGS models limits deployment on heterogeneous devices, while their rendering quality remains highly sensitive to tracking accuracy under motion blur and abrupt texture variations. In this work, we propose LoD-GS, a lightweight and robust 3DGS-SLAM framework that produces compact yet high-fidelity Gaussian scene representations for flexible deployment. LoD-GS integrates entropy-driven scene-complete volumetric mapping to improve pose quality and Gaussian initialization, a geometry-aware rendering quality optimizer that emphasizes near-field and structure-rich regions under limited optimization budgets, and a deployment-aware level-of-detail 3DGS compression module that enables adaptive resource-quality tradeoffs. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world office sequences demonstrate its effectiveness, reducing model size by up to 53.8%, increasing rendering FPS by up to 43.72%, and improving PSNR by up to 2.471 dB.

title: Non-Intrusive Contactless Gesture Recognition for Human-Robot Interaction
authors: Dimitrios Tsiakmakis, Massimiliano Iosi, Gastone Ciuti
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: As robots increasingly integrate into collaborative and assistive environments, natural gesture interpretation becomes crucial for effective human-robot interaction. While vision-based gesture recognition systems face privacy concerns and performance limitations under occlusion and varying lighting conditions, wearable sensor approaches often compromise user comfort and interaction naturalness. These challenges necessitate alternative approaches that balance recognition accuracy with user acceptance in real-world deployment scenarios. This work proposes a lightweight transformer-based feature extractor where test-time augmentation with majority voting is employed during inference to enhance classification performance. With approximately 2M trainable parameters, the network maintains reasonable efficiency on modern acceleration hardware. Its modular architecture also indicates an easy adaptation to a variety of sensor configurations. It reached an overall accuracy of 97.21%, with similarly high scores for f1-score, precision, and recall. The pipeline's inference duration of 16 ms on GPU and 40 ms on CPU demonstrate its suitability for real-time applications in collaborative robotics.

title: Feature-Guided Reversible Network for Point Cloud Denoising
authors: Huaibo Wang, Chengzhuan Yang, Lincong Fang, Hui Wei
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Point clouds inevitably introduce noise during the acquisition process, which severely impacts subsequent applications and downstream tasks. It is crucial for efficient and accurate point cloud denoising. However, existing methods struggle to simultaneously maintain strong expressive power and high fidelity to geometric details under complex noise interference. This paper proposes a feature-guided reversible neural network method for point cloud denoising. This method significantly improves the performance of point cloud denoising by extracting effective point cloud feature representations. First, we design a feature extraction and sharpening module capable of progressively accumulating features and capturing rich geometric information. Specifically, we apply a global bilinear sharpening method to aggregate global descriptors at both the channel and point levels, and simultaneously propose a bilinear interaction mechanism to achieve feature sharpening and noise suppression. Second, we present a reversible neural network designed to perform noise decoupling processing, further enhancing the discrimination of features. Moreover, we use an adaptive convolution module to improve the dimensionality of the input, thereby avoiding the low-dimensional bottleneck that cannot provide practical, rich geometric structure information. Finally, we separate noisy points from clean points in the constructed prior latent space through the inverse transformation of the reversible network, thereby obtaining a noise-free point cloud. We evaluate the denoising ability of our method on benchmark synthesized and real-world datasets, and the experimental results show that our method achieves a state-of-the-art performance in point cloud denoising tasks, surpassing previous point cloud denoising methods.

title: Natural Language Interfaces for Spatial and Temporal Databases: A Comprehensive Overview of Methods, Taxonomy, and Future Directions
authors: Samya Acharja, Kanchan Chowdhury
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The task of building a natural language interface to a database, known as NLIDB, has recently gained significant attention from both the database and Natural Language Processing (NLP) communities. There have been many research works that study the problem of automatically converting natural language questions into database queries. With the proliferation of geospatial datasets driven by the rapid emergence of location-aware sensors, geospatial databases play a vital role in supporting geospatial applications. However, querying geospatial and temporal databases differs substantially from querying traditional relational databases due to the presence of geospatial topological operators and temporal operators. To bridge the gap between geospatial query languages and non-expert users, the geospatial research community has increasingly focused on developing NLIDBs for geospatial databases. Yet, existing research remains fragmented across systems, datasets, and methodological choices, making it difficult to clearly understand the landscape of existing methods, their strengths and weaknesses, available benchmarks, and opportunities for future research. Existing surveys on NLIDBs focus on general-purpose database systems and do not treat geospatial and temporal databases as primary focus for analysis. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive survey of studies on NLIDBs for geospatial and temporal databases. Specifically, we first provide a detailed overview of datasets and evaluation metrics used to train and evaluate geospatial and temporal NLIDBs. We then study the taxonomy of the methods for geospatial and temporal NLIDBs and provide a comprehensive summary and comparative analysis of the existing methods. Our survey reveals recurring trends in existing methods, substantial variation in datasets and evaluation practices, and several open challenges that continue to hinder progress in this area. Based on these findings, we identify promising directions for future research to advance natural language interfaces to geospatial and temporal databases.

title: A U-Net based LSGAN Framework for 12-Lead ECG Synthesis in Wearable IoMT Systems
authors: Min Gu Kim, Jaehyo Jung
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Wearable devices, such as smartwatches, offer a convenient way to monitor cardiac conditions through single- or few-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) recordings. However, these devices fail to provide the comprehensive diagnostic information available from the standard 12-lead ECG used in clinical settings. To address this technological gap, this paper proposes a novel framework for generating a complete 12-lead ECG from only two leads (Lead I and II). We designed a deep learning model based on a Least Squares Generative Adversarial Network (LSGAN) that employs a U-Net architecture as its generator. The U-Net effectively extracts multi-scale features and morphological details of the ECG signal, while the LSGAN framework ensures a stable training process and generates high-fidelity waveforms. Furthermore, an L1 loss was incorporated to enforce pixel-wise accuracy in the generated signals. Experimental results on the large-scale PTB-XL dataset, using a patient-wise split for evaluation, show that our proposed model achieves a high average Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) of 0.905 across all ten generated leads, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. These findings confirm that our model can reliably generate clinically significant 12-lead ECGs, presenting a practical solution for enhancing remote cardiac monitoring in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environment.

title: A Global-Local Data Clustering Framework With Adaptive Feature and Sample Weighting and Balanced Constraint
authors: Yong Peng, Mimi Jin, Yiyan Wang, Yuxin Chen, Feiwei Qin, Wanzeng Kong, Andrzej Cichocki
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Clustering remains a hot research topic in machine learning and data mining communities. The underlying data similarity serves as the foundation of clustering data into respective groups. However, since different features have different discriminative capabilities and different samples might correspond to different importance levels, it is inaccurate to capture the data similarity if we ignore any of them in real applications. By taking both into consideration, we propose an adaptive feature and sample weighting (AFSW) scheme to quantitatively measure the importance in the least square regression sense, serving as a global clustering. As a local complementary, a graph is utilized to facilitate cluster indicator matrix learning, leading to a global-local (GL) clustering framework. As a secondary contribution, an entropy-based balanced constraint is introduced to enforce the cluster sizes to be close, which is a reasonable assumption if we are clueless to a certain given data set. In concrete model formulation, two strategies respectively based on self-paced learning and exponential probability coefficient are designed to determine the sample importance. The formulated model, termed GLAFSW, is optimized efficiently within an alternating direction framework. Experiments are performed on both synthetic and benchmark data sets from diverse aspects. By comparing the proposed framework against the state-of-the-arts, the results verified that it achieved competitive performance in clustering.

title: Non-Orthogonal Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing for Spectrally Efficient High-Mobility Communications
authors: Qin Yi, Zilong Liu, Leila Musavian, Zeping Sui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: This paper proposes a novel non-orthogonal affine frequency division multiplexing (nAFDM) waveform for reliable high-mobility communications with enhanced spectral efficiency (SE). The key idea is to introduce a bandwidth compression factor into the AFDM modulator to enable controllable subcarrier overlapping. We first detail the proposed nAFDM transceiver and derive the corresponding input-output signal relationship. Then, an efficient nAFDM signal generation method based on the inverse discrete Fourier transform (IDFT) is proposed, enabling practical implementation using existing inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) modules without additional hardware complexity. Next, to characterize the impact of non-orthogonal modulation, we derive a closed-form expression of inter-carrier interference (ICI), showing its dependence on the bandwidth compression factor. To mitigate the resulting interference, we propose a soft iterative detection algorithm and a low-complexity implementation approach that leverages the distribution characteristics of ICI. Simulation results demonstrate that 1) in terms of bit error rate (BER), the proposed nAFDM can achieve nearly identical BER compared to conventional AFDM, while outperforms other waveform counterparts; 2) nAFDM is capable of striking higher SE compared to other existing waveforms; and 3) the proposed nAFDM achieves an attractive BER vs. SE trade-off, and the proposed soft iterative detection (ID) scheme can attain a trade-off between BER and complexity.

title: Application of a Cylindrical Halbach Array for HTS Tapes Characterization
authors: Krzysztof Habelok, Paweł Lasek, Kamil Gruszczyk, Mariusz Stępień
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: For precise angular characterization of high temperature superconducting (HTS) tapes, creating a stable and uniform magnetic field that allowsfull 360° sample rotation poses specific design and implementationchallenges. This work introduces a compact cylindrical Halbach array composed of permanent magnets, designetto generate a uniform magnetic flux density up to 350 mT. The system is dedicated to angular dependent measurements of the critical current l<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> in HTS tapes. The Halbach configuration was optimized using finite element method (FEM) simulations to maximize field uniformity in the measurement region. The experimental setup was fabricated usingadditive manufacturing and integrates the Halbach array with a four probe measurement system operating at 77 K. The mechanical design ensures precise, continuous rotation of the superconducting sample within a cryogenic environment without degrading field symmetry. Angular dependence of l<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> was experimentally characterized and compared with results obtained using conventional solenoidal magnets and established analytical models. The results confirm that the Halbach array provides sufficient magnetic field uniformityfor reliable l<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> (θ) determination while significantly reducing system cost, power demand, and cooling complexity. The maximum field of approximately 0.35 T limits the applicability of the device to low- and moderate-field measurements, making it particularly suitable for laboratory scale testing and industrial quality control of HTS conductors

title: Efficient Distributed Randomized Iterative Detection for Decentralized XL-MIMO Systems
authors: Zheng Wang, Chunguo Li, Yongming Huang, Shi Jin, Giuseppe Caire
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: With the rapid scaling up of system dimensions, distributed signal detection based on decentralized baseband architectures has become an important problem in extremely large-scale MIMO (XL-MIMO). In this paper, to reduce the complexity cost of distributed detections for XL-MIMO, the efficient distributed randomized iterative detection (EDRID) algorithm is proposed. First of all, by removing the computationally expensive calculation of matrix pseudoinverse, the randomized iteration in EDRID is still able to exponentially converge into a close range around the target least squares (LS) solution but with significant complexity reduction. Then, to overcome the convergence bias of EDRID, we adopt a dynamic step-size strategy into EDRID to ensure its exact convergence to the LS solution. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed EDRID with dynamic step-size not only returns the LS solution with arbitrary accuracy for sufficient iterations, but also features the global convergence for different practical scenarios. Finally, to obtain further improvements in both convergence and efficiency, the technique of conditional sampling is also introduced to EDRID, making it a low-complexity, flexible, scalable detection choice for XL-MIMO systems.

title: Soft labelling for deep ordinal classification: an experimental review_supp1-3681678.pdf
authors: David Guijo-Rubio
publish: N/A

title: Heuristic-Supervised-DRL: A Unified Optimization Framework With Convergence Analysis_supp1-3681665.pdf
authors: Zhi Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Many real-world problems involve hierarchical multi-objective optimization over coupled sequential decisions. A common strategy is to combine heuristic planning with deep reinforcement learning (DRL). While existing hybrid methods often show strong empirical performance, the coupled closed-loop learning dynamics between upper-tier planning and lower-tier control are typically analyzed only empirically, and explicit convergence guarantees for the integrated scheme remain limited. To address this gap, we propose a heuristic-supervised-DRL (HSD) framework that tightly couples (i) a heuristic planner for upper-tier decision-making, (ii) a DRL agent for lower-tier execution, and (iii) an online supervised predictor that serves as an adaptive bridge between planning and execution. The key novelty of HSD lies in this closed-loop architecture and its accompanying theoretical treatment. By formulating the coupled updates as a two-timescale stochastic approximation process, we show that, under standard conditions, the supervised predictor tracks a quasi-stationary regression target and the overall joint process converges almost surely to an asymptotically stable equilibrium. We further analyze robustness under approximate planning errors. As a case study, we instantiate HSD in a multi-UAV-assisted mobile edge computing system. Experimental results show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms representative baselines in key performance metrics, demonstrating both its practical effectiveness and its value as a principled framework for hierarchical optimization in dynamic environments.

authors: N/A
publish: 2025 Conference on AI x Software Engineering (AIxSE)

title: A SAC-GHULSTM Algorithm for Consumer Robot Navigation in Complex Indoor Environments with Progressive Training_supp1-3685311.pdf
authors: Yuemin Ding
publish: N/A
abstract: To improve the robustness and efficiency of consumer mobile robot navigation in complex indoor environments, this paper presents a deep reinforcement learning method named SAC-GHULSTM. The method integrates a gated highway unit enhanced long short-term memory (GHULSTM) into the soft actor-critic (SAC) framework, combined with a progressive training strategy. Standard LSTM networks tend to exhibit limited effectiveness in preserving informative temporal features when processing long-sequence navigation data, which can lead to slow convergence and suboptimal stability performance. To address this issue, a GHULSTM network structure is proposed. By integrating GHU into each hidden layer of the LSTM, the hidden state can directly retain and transmit key historical sequence information while fusing with current inputs. This design enhances long-term information retention and improves the effectiveness of temporal dependency modeling in long-horizon navigation scenarios. Additionally, a curriculum-based progressive training mechanism is designed during the training phase. This approach ensures the algorithm quickly learns effective strategies in initial simple scenarios and gradually adapts to complex environments, thereby improving its generalization ability. Experimental results in scenarios with output noise demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly accelerates training convergence and enhances stable-phase performance. In particular, the robot’s average collision rate is substantially reduced, the target arrival rate is notably improved, and the GHULSTM-based strategy combined with progressive training achieves superior target arrival efficiency in identical test scenarios, enabling reliable target reaching.

authors: N/A

title: Breaking the Curse of Dimensionality in Gaussian Process Training with zeroth-order Adaptive Perturbation
authors: Richard Cornelius Suwandi, Feng Yin, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Hyperparameter optimization remains a core challenge in training Gaussian processes (GPs), primarily due to the high computational cost and numerical instabilities associated with gradient-based optimizers. These issues are exacerbated in high-dimensional spaces, where the optimization landscapes become increasingly complex and difficult to navigate efficiently. We propose zeroth-order adaptive perturbation (ZAP), a scalable gradient-free algorithm that leverages simultaneous perturbations sampled from a Bernoulli distribution to obtain accurate gradient estimates with only two function evaluations per iteration, regardless of the hyperparameter dimensionality. Our theoretical analysis establishes that the gradient estimator is asymptotically unbiased and that ZAP converges to a stationary point under standard assumptions. Empirically, ZAP outperforms state-of-the-art gradient-based and gradient-free baselines on real-world datasets, achieving an average of 20× reduction in mean-squared-error and a 118× speedup in optimization time. Code is available at https://github.com/richardcsuwandi/zap.

title: Can Synthetic Images Serve as Effective and Efficient Class Prototypes?
authors: Dianxing Shi, Dingjie Fu, Yuqiao Liu, Jun Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong performance in zero-shot image classification tasks. However, existing methods, including Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), all rely on annotated text-to-image pairs for aligning visual and textual modalities. This dependency introduces substantial cost and accuracy requirement in preparing high-quality datasets. At the same time, processing data from two modes also requires dual-tower encoders for most models, which also hinders their lightweight. To address these limitations, we introduce a "Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training via Large-Language-Model-based Generation (LGCLIP)" framework. LGCLIP leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate class-specific prompts that guide a diffusion model in synthesizing reference images. Afterwards these generated images serve as visual prototypes, and the visual features of real images are extracted and compared with the visual features of these prototypes to achieve comparative prediction. By optimizing prompt generation through the LLM and employing only a visual encoder, LGCLIP remains lightweight and efficient. Crucially, our framework requires only class labels as input during whole experimental procedure, eliminating the need for manually annotated image-text pairs and extra pre-processing. Experimental results validate the feasibility and efficiency of LGCLIP, demonstrating great performance in zero-shot classification tasks and establishing a novel paradigm for classification. Codes at Project Link.

title: Online Cursive Handwriting Generation using Trace Transformation and Symbol-Independent Point Classification Model
authors: Karina Korovai, Nataliya Sakhnenko, Oleg Yakovchuk, Dmytro Zhelezniakov, Andrii Kroitor, Vadym Honcharenko, Olga Radyvonenko
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generating handwritten text that mimics a user’s style has improved significantly due to the application of RNNs, GANs, transformers, and diffusion models. However, most of the methods focus on individual characters, which poses limitations for languages based on Latin and Cyrillic scripts, where writing is often cursive and connected. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for generating cursive handwritten text as digital ink traces, complementing existing single-character generation methods. Our method focuses on seamless and smooth linking of symbols while preserving the original handwriting style. It consists of head/tail detection to predict potential connection points and transformation of the adjacent trace segments to ensure a continuous and natural flow of the generated text. We also prioritize efficiency to enable a responsive experience in mobile applications. While evaluated on Latin-based languages, this method is adaptable to other scripts that employ cursive handwriting with connected letters or strokes.

title: A Support Vector Approach in Segmented Regression for Map-Assisted Non-Cooperative Source Localization
authors: Hao Sun, Weiming Huang, Xianghao Yu, Junting Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a non-cooperative source localization approach based on received signal strength (RSS) and 2D environment map, considering both line-of-sight (LOS) and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) conditions. Conventional localization methods, e.g., weighted centroid localization (WCL), may perform bad. This paper proposes a segmented regression approach using 2D maps to estimate source location and propagation environment jointly. By leveraging topological information from the 2D maps, a support vector-assisted algorithm is developed to solve the segmented regression problem, separate the LOS and NLOS measurements, and estimate the location of source. The proposed method demonstrates a good localization performance with an improvement of over 30% in localization rooted mean squared error (RMSE) compared to the baseline methods.

title: OGRA-YOLOv8: Overlapping Gridded and Rhombus Attention for Underwater Object Detection
authors: Huanhuan Lv, Yuanjing Luo, Songru Jiang, Yuhao Bai, Haiyang Bai, Lijun Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Underwater object detection (UOD) is vital for marine exploration, ecosystem monitoring, and aquaculture management. However, reliable UOD remains challenging due to optical degradation, low illumination, and the prevalence of small or densely distributed targets. Existing detectors often struggle to balance accuracy with real-time performance, limiting their robustness in practice. To address these issues, we propose OGRA-YOLOv8, an enhanced YOLOv8 framework tailored for UOD. We design two novel attention modules: Overlapping Gridded Attention (OGA) that strengthens local feature extraction while reducing the cost of self-attention, and Rhombus Attention (RA) to capture long-range dependencies and complement OGA. In addition, a small-object detection head (YOLOv8-P2) and WIoU v2 loss are integrated to improve bounding box regression under uneven and multi-scale target distributions. Extensive experiments on the DUO and URPC2020 datasets show that OGRA-YOLOv8 consistently surpasses state-of-the-art YOLO variants and other competitive detectors, achieving significant gains in accuracy while preserving real-time efficiency. We make the code public at: https://github.com/LYJ0126/OGRA-YOLOV8.

title: A Game-Theoretic Approach for Distributed MEC-Enabled Collaborative Inference in AIGC Networks
authors: Licheng Ye, Zehui Xiong, Lin Gao, Dusit Niyato
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: AI-Generated Content (AIGC) based on Generative Diffusion Models (GDMs) has garnered significant attention for its transformative role in content creation and broad application potential. Leveraging Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) and model compression, GDMs can be deployed on edge servers (ESs) and mobile user (MU) devices, enabling collaborative inference between MUs and the ES for high-quality content generation and low-latency services. Unlike most existing studies that focus on centralized offloading and resource allocation for system efficiency, this work examines the distributed offloading decisions of rational MUs. Specifically, we explore a distributed MEC-enabled AIGC network with heterogeneous GDMs, where multiple MUs each hold a local GDM and an ES hosts multiple GDMs. To address the challenge of rational offloading, we formulate a joint GDM selection and computing offloading (JGSCO) game, where each MU acts as a game player to select the optimal GDM and inference steps offloaded to the ES to minimize its cost. We prove that the JGSCO game is a potential game and design a best-response (BR)-based iterative algorithm that converges to its Nash equilibrium (NE). Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed collaborative inference scheme, achieving at least a 11.32% cost reduction compared to other schemes.

title: FADIoT: Federated Anomaly Detection Framework for DDoS Attacks in IoT
authors: Tushar Rana, Sai Akshita Dimpu Pagoti, Neha Agrawal, Amulya Namburi
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks pose a severe threat to resource-constrained and heterogeneous Internet of Things (IoT) networks. This paper presents a three-layer IoT-Fog-Cloud architecture for collaborative, scalable, and lowlatency anomaly detection. The framework combines Continuous Ranked Probability Score with Exponential Smoothing (CRPS-ES) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for feature extraction, with a federated threshold aggregation mechanism to preserve privacy. Localized fog processing enables real-time analysis, while a CNN-BiLSTM model with federated learning enhances detection accuracy without sharing raw data. Experiments on heterogeneous IoT environments achieved up to 99% detection accuracy, outperforming existing CRPS-based methods, offering a robust, privacy-preserving defense against evolving DDoS threats.

title: ReCoM: Realistic Co-Speech Motion Generation with Recurrent Embedded Transformer
authors: Yong Xie, Yunlian Sun, Hongwen Zhang, Yebin Liu, Jinhui Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present ReCoM, an efficient framework for generating high-fidelity and generalizable human body motions synchronized with speech. The core innovation lies in the Recurrent Embedded Transformer (RET), which integrates Dynamic Embedding Regularization (DER) into a Vision Transformer (ViT) core architecture to explicitly model co-speech motion dynamics, enabling joint spatial-temporal dependency modeling. We enhance the model’s robustness, noise resistance and cross-domain generalization. To mitigate inherent limitations of autoregressive inference, including error accumulation and limited self-correction, we propose an iterative reconstruction inference (IRI) strategy, which refines motion sequences via cyclic pose reconstruction. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets validate ReCoM’s effectiveness, achieving state-of-the-art performance across metrics. Notably, it reduces the Fréchet Gesture Distance (FGD) from 18.70 to 2.48, demonstrating an 86.7% improvement in motion realism.

title: Bayesian Channel Estimation with Diffusion Probabilistic Priors
authors: Cheng Jin, Qitan Shi, Yuantao Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose a generalized maximum a posteriori (MAP) channel estimation framework that exploits diffusion probabilistic priors for downlink massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. The channel estimation problem is reformulated as a generalized MAP inference task, where we derive the probability density of the implicit channel prior encoded by the diffusion model and obtain a simplified gradient expression of its log-prior. This leads to an efficient stochastic gradient ascent algorithm, termed Diffusion Prior Bayesian Stochastic Optimization (DP-BSO). Numerical experiments show that DP-BSO consistently outperforms classical baselines, with particularly significant gains under limited computational resources.

title: Supervised Makeup Transfer with a Curated Dataset: Decoupling Identity and Makeup Features for Enhanced Transformation
authors: Qihe Pan, Yiming Wu, Xing Zhao, Liang Xie, Guodao Sun, Ronghua Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models have recently shown strong progress in generative tasks, offering a more stable alternative to GAN-based approaches for makeup transfer. Existing methods often suffer from limited datasets, poor disentanglement between identity and makeup features, and weak controllability. To address these issues, we make three contributions. First, we construct a curated high-quality dataset using a train–generate–filter–retrain strategy that combines synthetic, realistic, and filtered samples to improve diversity and fidelity. Second, we design a diffusion-based framework that disentangles identity and makeup features, ensuring facial structure and skin tone are preserved while applying accurate and diverse cosmetic styles. Third, we propose a text-guided mechanism that allows fine-grained and region-specific control, enabling users to modify eyes, lips, or face makeup with natural language prompts. Experiments on benchmarks and real-world scenarios demonstrate improvements in fidelity, identity preservation, and flexibility. Examples of our dataset can be found at: https://makeup-adapter.github.io/.

title: LexTra: Folded Prompt and Split-Role Attention for Target Speaker Extraction
authors: Pengjie Shen, Shulin He, Xueliang Zhang, Zhong-Qiu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Target speaker extraction (TSE) aims at isolating a desired target speaker from a mixture of multiple speakers, based on a short enrollment utterance of the target speaker. In LExt, a recent onset-prompted TSE method obtaining state-of-the-art performance, it is observed that longer prompts often help but the encoder and separator have to process longer sequences, which increases computation, and the performance often drops when the enrollment is clean while the mixture is noisy-reverberant. To address these issues, we propose LExTra, an extension of LExt which combines folded prompt conditioning with split-role multi-head attention. The folded prompt divides an enrollment into equal-length sub-segments, prepends each sub-segment to a copy of the mixture, and stacks the prepended mixture along a pseudo channel axis. This way, the backbone of the TSE model sees an enrollment utterance with shorter time span while still capable of exploiting the full enrollment. Split-role attention assigns some attention heads to the cross-attention from mixture to enrollment to extract speaker cues, and assigns the remaining ones to mixture self-attention to preserve stream coherence. We find that this mechanism can improve the robustness of TSE when there are acoustic mismatches between the enrollment and mixture. Evaluation results on the WSJ0-2mix and WHAMR! datasets show the effectiveness of LExTra.

title: Online Register For Dual-Mode Self-Supervised Speech Models: Mitigating the Lack of Future Context
authors: Keita Goto, Takashi Maekaku, Jin Sakuma, Jinchuan Tian, Yusuke Shinohara, Shinji Watanabe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised speech models (S3Ms) have become a key foundation for modern speech processing. However, models pre-trained under offline conditions often suffer from degraded performance in online scenarios due to the absence of future context. To address this challenge, we propose online registers, learnable tokens appended to each chunk in online mode. These tokens act as virtual place-holders for unseen future frames, enabling the model to compensate for missing context without introducing additional latency. Furthermore, we introduce a future prediction loss that explicitly guides the registers to capture predictive cues, thereby enriching their ability to retain future information. Experiments on LibriSpeech, and out-of-domain benchmarks demonstrate that online registers consistently reduce the performance gap between offline and online modes, achieving a 3.4% relative improvement on LibriSpeech with 160 ms chunks, especially in low-latency settings.

title: Equivariant Hamiltonian Graph Neural Networks for Generalizing Dynamics of Magnetic Pendulum System
authors: Zitong Zhang, Hao Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Learning predictive models that generalize to unseen physical configurations remains a major challenge, especially when systems involve varying numbers of interacting components. Traditional neural networks often fail at such extrapolation tasks because they rely on pattern recognition rather than physical principles. This paper tackles this problem using the magnetic pendulum system, where conventional neural networks fail to extrapolate from learned, simpler configurations. We propose the Equivariant Hamiltonian Graph Neural Network (EHGNN), an architecture that embeds two critical physical priors: geometric symmetry and energy conservation. The model leverages a graph representation to handle the variable number of magnets. Its E(n)-equivariance learns a coordinate-based potential energy function, ensuring inherent rotational and translational equivariance. We validate our approach by training the EHGNN solely on trajectories generated by magnetic pendulum systems with a symmetric fixed number of magnets. The results demonstrate successful zero-shot generalization, as the model accurately predicts stable and physically plausible long-term dynamics for systems with a greater number of magnets. Our approach establishes a new paradigm for learning generalizable physical dynamics through the strategic integration of symmetry and conservation principles.

title: Spatially-Coupled OTFS Systems via Block Markov Superposition Transmission
authors: Jiayi Yang, Shuangyang Li, Qianfan Wang, Linqi Song, Bo Ai, Xiao Ma, Giuseppe Caire
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper investigates a novel spatially coupled orthogonal time frequency space (SC-OTFS) transmission scheme by integrating block Markov superposition transmission (BMST) with OTFS modulation. We first statistically analyze the distribution of detector mutual information across coded blocks and establish a decoding-threshold-based lower bound to characterize the asymptotic performance of conventional LDPC-coded OTFS systems. The analysis reveals that increasing the code length yields only marginal coding gains in typical doubly-selective channels with limited resolvable paths. Motivated by this observation, we propose an SC-OTFS system in which a portion of each previously coded block is superimposed onto the current block and subsequently modulated using OTFS, thereby exploiting both coding gain and inter-block diversity gain in high-mobility channels. Furthermore, an extrinsic information transfer (EXIT) chart analysis is developed for the SC-OTFS system, which enables optimization of the critical superposition fraction based on decoding thresholds. Simulation results demonstrate that this optimization leads to a significant performance improvement, achieving more than 3 dB gain over conventional 5G LDPC-coded OTFS systems.

title: HFDFormer: Monocular 3D Human Reconstruction Via Layer-Wise Hierarchical Feature Decoupling Transformer
authors: Ruizhi Liu, Paolo Remagnino
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Monocular 3D human reconstruction remains challenging due to incomplete 2D features extracted from single-view images and the inherent inconsistency among them, often leading to overly smooth surfaces and geometrically implausible shapes. We observe that such inconsistencies in the 2D feature space are a primary cause of degraded surface fidelity and irregular geometry. To address these issues, we propose HFDFormer, a transformer-based framework designed to enhance feature capacity while preserving cross-view and intra-view consistency. Rather than performing a one-time feature decoupling like the previous methods, HFDFormer applies layer-wise tri-plane decoupling, allowing progressive refinement of low- and high-frequency components throughout the network. This design both expands feature capacity and enforces cross-plane consistency, leading to sharper surface details and more coherent geometry. This structured decoupling alleviates the smoothness artifact by providing fine-grained, detail-preserving features, while the consistency constraint ensures coherent geometry across the reconstructed mesh. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that HFDFormer produces sharper surface details and more accurate geometry than existing state-of-the-art methods, without sacrificing robustness or generalization.

title: A Low-Rank Angular Domain Sampling SVD Approximation for Massive Mimo Signal Processing
authors: Kaixuan Bao, Guosheng Ling, Wei Xu, Zheng Wang, Yongming Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The computational complexity of signal processing algorithms has become increasingly significant, emerging as a major contributor to the overall end-to-end system latency. Low-rank matrix approximation is an effective approach for reducing the computational burden of matrix operations, which constitute the dominant component of the overall signal processing complexity. In this paper, we propose a low-rank approximation method for singular value decomposition (SVD), referred to as angular domain sampling SVD (ADS-SVD). ADS-SVD exploits the angular energy distribution of the channel matrix and employs a low-dimensional projection technique, thereby achieving an effective low-rank approximation. In addition, we also design a subspace iteration method to further improve the approximation accuracy. Numerical simulations demonstrate that ADS-SVD offers stabler and more accurate performance under the same level of computational complexity.

title: Synergy Map–Guided Spectral–Domain Enhanced Network for Camouflaged Object Detection
authors: Fuhua Zhang, Anqi Lao, Kongtao Wu, Ru Yi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Camouflaged object detection (COD) aims to automatically localize and segment concealed objects from backgrounds that are nearly indistinguishable. Owing to strong texture similarity and ambiguous boundaries, existing methods often miss subtle structures or produce coarse masks. To this end, we propose S2Net, a Synergy-Map–Guided Spectral-Domain Enhancement network that strengthens boundary fidelity and micro-texture representation. Specifically, we first design a dual-prior synergy module that fuses boundary and region priors into a synergy map for pixel-wise guidance. Second, we propose a spectral-domain enhancement module that performs single value decomposition on pooled features to estimate a compact background subspace and extract the complementary high-frequency residual, thereby sharpening boundaries while preserving semantics. Finally, context aggregation module of the refined features yields more complete and accurate predictions. On three challenging public datasets, S2Net achieves competitive performance against 21 state-of-the-art baselines. The code will be available at https://github.com/zhangz-cyber/S2Net.

title: Enhancing Speaker Verification with Layer-Wise Mixture-of-Experts on Pre-Trained Models
authors: Yishuang Li, Yi Yu, Yongfeng Tu, Shuhao Deng, Weihao Gan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel speaker verification (SV) method that integrates speech pre-trained models (PTMs) with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism. The method first extracts multilayer representations from the PTMs and inserts an MoE module after each Transformer layer. Through dynamic routing, the MoE module performs input-adaptive feature transformations, which in turn enables implicit feature selection under varying domain conditions. We then design a cascaded residual gating mechanism to propagate key shallow-layer acoustic cues and prevent their attenuation in deeper representations. Furthermore, we introduce a cross-attention fusion module that dynamically integrates multilayer features along the layer dimension, thereby jointly maintaining the robustness of shallow features and the discriminability of deep features. The fused representations are finally fed into a downstream SV network to generate highly discriminative speaker embedding. Experiments on the VoxCeleb1-O test set show that, with the Base and Large versions of PTMs, our method achieves equal error rates of 0.628% and 0.487%, respectively, which demonstrates the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method.

title: Timbre-Aware Audio Difference Captioning for Anomalous Machine Sounds without Paired Training Data via Synthetic Perturbations
authors: Tomoya Nishida, Harsh Purohit, Kota Dohi, Takashi Endo, Yohei Kawaguchi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We introduce a difference captioning framework for explaining how anomalous machine sounds differ from normal ones without paired training data or human-annotated difference captions. While several difference captioning methods exists in the literature, they did not meet the requirements for explaining anomalous machine sounds; real paired data are infeasible for training due to lack of anomalous samples and the differences are often subtle. To meet the above requirements, we simulate small acoustic changes in terms of timbre via simple augmentations on audio captioning data, auto-generate difference captions with a large language model, and train a difference captioning model that integrates timbre-attribute modules to capture subtle differences. This overcomes prior methods focusing on rather large changes, while preserving the pipeline to be extensible to other audio differences in the future. Subjective tests on DCASE Task 2 normal–anomalous pairs show our captions generalize to real machine anomalies and effectively describe subtle differences compared to existing audio difference captioning models.

title: A Design of Cost-Effective Wireless Body Sensor Network with Electrocardiogram Acquisition System Using Hybrid Deep Learning Approaches
authors: Supriya C M, Ahmad Al-Qerem, Chrispin Jiji, Srinivasan Gayathri, Chinthakindi Babaiah, Rajini Parimala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: To create a cost-efficient Wireless Body Sensor Network (WBSN), essential components include low-energy, affordable wearable sensors that monitor basic physiological parameters such as blood pressure heart rate, and temperaturecontinuously. The CWBEHD model is a cost-saving health-monitoring system designed for continuous vital signs monitoring using WBSN. Heart rate, blood pressure is monitored by small wearable sensors linked to an ESP32 microcontroller, sending data through RF signals in a star network. Data after a local preprocessing step are transmitted in a secure way to the ThingsBoard IoT platform, which is the main place for classification, rule-based processing, and dynamic visualization. The model embraces deep learning architectures a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for rapid classification and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for ECG feature extraction by both synthetic and in vivo data verified by professional ECG monitors. The parameter evaluation is accuracy, heart rate, blood pressure, body temperature, comparative accuracy calculation.

title: Contextual Relationship Feature-Enhanced Steganalysis for Social Texts
authors: Kaibo Huang, Xiaohan Zhang, Wansheng Wu, Yukun Wei, Zhongliang Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Steganalysis techniques for social media are used primarily to detect potential steganographic content within social networks, contributing to cybersecurity efforts. Most existing research focuses on analyzing individual media features, neglecting the social connectivity aspects that are crucial for identifying steganographic texts in fragmented social media environments. To address this gap, we propose a feature-enhanced linguistic steganalysis model that integrates improved node features, optimized message passing, and robust feature fusion. This model enables more accurate detection of steganographic texts even in complex social media scenarios. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art steganalysis techniques in both accuracy and efficiency.

title: FANSR: Frequency Adaptive Network for Efficient Image Super-Resolution
authors: Qinhong Yang, Dongdong Chen, Qi Chu, Tao Gong, Qiankun Liu, Zhentao Tan, Huamin Feng, Nenghai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transformer models have advanced single image super-resolution (SISR), but most emphasize enlarging the receptive field while underusing high-frequency cues across spatial and channel dimensions. To mitigate this self-attention(SA)’s low-pass bias, We propose FANSR (Frequency Adaptive Network for Efficient Image Super Resolution), a lightweight framework that considering receptive-field expansion with explicit high-frequency modeling. FANSR introduces two frequency-based token mixers that transform features into frequency representations along spatial and channel axes, efficiently isolating high-frequency components and injecting them as sparse priors into the subsequent Self-Attention block. We further introduce a Frequency Enhancement Loss (FE-Loss) that adaptively prioritizes high-frequency regions, enabled by the model’s explicit distinction of low- and high-frequency content. Extensive experiments show that FANSR achieves a superior trade-off between image quality and latency compared to state-of-the-art lightweight methods. Code and pretrained models will be released.

title: Do You Hear What I Mean? Quantifying the Instruction-Perception GAP in Instruction-Guided Expressive Text-to-Speech Systems
authors: Yi-Cheng Lin, Huang-Cheng Chou, Tzu-Chieh Wei, Kuan-Yu Chen, Hung-Yi Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Instruction-guided text-to-speech (ITTS) enables users to control speech generation through natural language prompts, offering a more intuitive interface than traditional TTS. However, the alignment between user style instructions and listener perception remains largely unexplored. This work first presents a perceptual analysis of ITTS controllability across two expressive dimensions (adverbs of degree and graded emotion intensity) and collects human ratings on speaker age and word-level emphasis attributes. To comprehensively reveal the instruction-perception gap, we provide a data collection with large-scale human evaluations, named Expressive VOice Control (E-VOC) corpus. Furthermore, we reveal that (1) gpt-4o-mini-tts is the most reliable ITTS model with great alignment between instruction and generated utterances across acoustic dimensions. (2) The 5 analyzed ITTS systems tend to generate Adult voices even when the instructions ask to use child or elderly voices. (3) Fine-grained control remains a major challenge, indicating that most ITTS systems have substantial room for improvement in interpreting slightly different attribute instructions.

title: Do we really need self-attention for streaming automatic speech recognition?
authors: Youness Dkhissi, Valentin Vielzeuf, Elys Allesiardo, Anthony Larcher
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transformer-based architectures are the most used architectures in many deep learning fields like Natural Language Processing, Computer Vision or Speech processing. It may encourage the direct use of Transformers in the constrained tasks, without questioning whether it will yield the same benefits as in standard tasks.Given specific constraints, it is essential to evaluate the relevance of transformer models. This work questions the suitability of transformers for specific domains. We argue that the high computational requirements and latency issues associated with these models do not align well with streaming applications. Our study promotes the search for alternative strategies to improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.In light of this observation, our paper critically examines the usefulness of transformer architecture in such constrained environments. As a first attempt, we show that the computational cost for Streaming Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) can be reduced using deformable convolution instead of Self-Attention. Furthermore, we show that Self-Attention mechanisms can be entirely removed and not replaced, without observing significant degradation in the Word Error Rate.

title: Sparse Bayesian Learning with Simple and Interpretable DNNs Exploiting Data-Driven Priors
authors: Hitesh D. Khunti, Bhaskar D. Rao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper, we present a low complexity deep neural network, built using algorithmic unrolling, for hyper-parameter estimation in the sparse Bayesian learning methodology. The resulting sparse recovery algorithm is shown to be better or comparable to existing algorithms with many additional virtues, notable of which are the ability to learn priors from the data and the trained network being interpretable. The architecture is motivated by looking at the structure in the maximum a posteriori estimation framework and abstracting the mapping in the Expectation-Maximization algorithm. A key feature of the proposed deep neural network architecture is its simplicity in that for each hyper-parameter, for each iteration, the network has a single input and a single output. The network configuration is flexible: it can share weights across iterations and hyper-parameters, or be tailored per iteration and variable, depending on the prior assumptions. Numerical results are presented that show the benefits of the data driven approach over conventional modeling in terms of their superior performance in support recovery as well as learning priors without any user assistance.

title: Crowdsourced Digital Twins for Beamforming Optimization In XR Communications
authors: Alperen Duru, Todd Humphreys, Jeffrey Andrews
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper explores how coarse physical-layer (PHY) digital twins (DTs) can improve beamforming accuracy and reduce latency for outdoor extended reality (XR) communications in Frequency Range 3 (FR3, 7–24GHz). We account for geometric uncertainty in reflectors to restrict the beam search space, and introduce a particle filter framework that calibrates reflector location and orientation using crowdsourced XR feedback. We also define the path false alarm (PFA) probability as the chance that a DT-predicted path does not exist in reality. Numerical results show that the proposed framework calibrates reflector pose to within 25cm in location and 0.25° in yaw, achieving beam prediction accuracy comparable to known DT geometry. Moreover, even without calibration, modeling reflector-pose uncertainty increases the beam prediction success rate of coarse DT by up to 7%.

title: STCFormer: Robust Malicious Traffic Detection Via Short-Term Traffic Profiling and A Hybrid Transformer
authors: Junjie Yang, Junrong Liu, Song Liu, Xueying Han, Yan Zhu, Bo Jiang, Zhigang Lu, Huamin Feng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The widespread deployment of encryption has made it increasingly difficult for Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) to identify malicious activities hidden in encrypted channels. To mitigate the limitations of prior approaches in global context modeling, noise robustness, and efficiency on long traffic sequences, we propose STCFormer, a robust malicious traffic detection model. The key idea is a Short-Term Traffic Grid Matrix (TGM) that encodes packet-byte content and short-term temporal ordering into an image-like representation, preserving both semantic and spatiotemporal cues. Built on this representation, STCFormer adopts a hybrid Transformer that combines the global modeling capability of the Swin Transformer with the local feature refinement efficiency of inverted residual blocks. Extensive Experiments on three public benchmarks show that STCFormer consistently outperforms strong baselines. Moreover, under random noise injection up to 30%, STCFormer maintains high detection accuracy, indicating strong robustness and generalization.

title: An Experimental Methodology to Predict Cyber Attacks Using Learning-Assisted Generative AI Technology
authors: Deepa G, Geetha K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Cyber attacks have taken a new form in the dynamic digital environment and are extremely threatening to the critical infrastructure, state systems, and organizational networks. The current intrusion detection systems tend to be based on the traditional machine learning models that fail to deal with the class imbalance, sparse data, and limited ability to generalize towards unknown attacks or zero-day attacks. This paper is a new hybrid learning-supported model that combines a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with Bi-LSTM and Transformer structure to improve predictive accuracy and resilience. The methodology starts by preprocessing NSL-KDD dataset and then extracting features and rebalancing the classes using SMOTE and synthetic samples generated by VAE. The main novelty is that it combines the generative augmentation approach with ensemble deep learning models, which provide the ability to be both temporally and contextually aware of more elaborate attack behavior. The proposed model did attain the total accuracy of 98.79, which exceeded the traditional classifiers such as Random Forest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(91.12 \%)$</tex>, SVM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(88.34 \%)$</tex>, and even the independent deep learners, such as Bi-LSTM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(94.88 \%)$</tex>. The level of detection in classes was also better and the minority attacks such as U2R attacks and R2L attacks were also well detected, unlike traditional systems. The discriminative power of the model was also ensured with the help of ROC-AUC analysis (0.994) and class-wise measures of precision. This experimental design proves that learning-assisted generative AI can become a promising approach to high-fidelity cyber threat detection, which allows one to intervene in the early stage. Adversarial training and sequence modeling is also presented in the framework to provide flexibility in adapting to dynamic cyber environments. Future directions will involve implementation in real-time settings and extrapolate the model to multimodal cybersecurity data to achieve higher levels of generalization and applicability to a real-life setting.

title: Towards Data Drift Monitoring for Speech Deepfake Detection in the Context of MLOps
authors: Xin Wang, Wanying Ge, Junichi Yamagishi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: When being delivered in applications or services on the cloud, static speech deepfake detectors that are not updated will become vulnerable to newly created speech deepfake attacks. From the perspective of machine learning operations (MLOps), this paper tries to answer whether we can monitor new and unseen speech deepfake data that drifts away from a seen reference data set. We further ask, if drift is detected, whether we can fine-tune the detector using similarly drifted data, reduce the drift, and improve the detection performance. On a toy dataset and the large-scale MLAAD dataset, we show that the drift caused by new text-to-speech (TTS) attacks can be monitored using distances between the distributions of the new data and reference data. Furthermore, we demonstrate that fine-tuning the detector using data generated by the new TTS deepfakes can reduce the drift and the detection error rates.

title: Towards Emergent AGI: Self-Preservation and Reproduction as Foundational Drives
authors: Djallel Bouneffouf
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The quest for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) has traditionally focused on designing explicit architectures for cognition, reasoning, and planning. However, we propose an alternative perspective: AGI may emerge naturally from two biologically inspired goals—self-preservation and reproduction. These goals provide the foundation for selection pressures that drive the evolution of increasingly sophisticated behaviors, culminating in general intelligence as an instrumental capability. Rather than treating intelligence as a predefined goal or function to be engineered, we suggest that intelligence is better understood as a strategy that arises organically from the interaction between agents and their environments. This shift in perspective has profound implications for AGI design, as it suggests that intelligence may emerge through the pursuit of basic survival and replication drives in open-ended, dynamic environments. Moreover, this approach to AGI aligns with the principles of open-ended learning, where agents continually adapt and evolve in response to novel challenges and opportunities. In addition to its implications for the design of intelligent systems, this framework also raises important safety considerations. As agents evolve autonomously, ensuring alignment with human values and preventing harmful emergent behaviors will be critical. Our work suggests a new paradigm for AGI research that emphasizes the importance of long-term evolution and adaptive learning in dynamic, resource-constrained environments. By positioning intelligence as a strategy rather than a goal, we hope to inspire new approaches to AGI that prioritize flexibility, autonomy, and safety in the development of artificial agents.

title: Plug-in-and-Play Dual-Domain Mixup Augmentation Network for PET Denoising at Multiple Dose Levels
authors: Pinxian Zeng, Bob Zhang, Haozhuo Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: High-quality Standard-dose Positron Emission Tomography (SPET) scans are preferred for their rich evidence in clinical diagnosis, but their radiation exposure poses potential harm to patients. Numerous methods have been developed to denoise low-dose PET (LPET) images and estimate their corresponding SPET images. However, most of them focus on the LPET images with a single dose level, overlooking the different dose reduction protocols and the varying dose requirements for patients in clinical practice. Some multi-dose methods can address this issue but require scans from multiple doses as training samples, which is impractical and resource-intensive. To address these limitations, we propose a Dual-Domain Mixup Augmentation Network (D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net) for robust multi-dose PET denoising using only training samples of a single dose level. D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net applies two learnable mixup networks, i.e., Spatial-domain Mixup Augmentation (SMA) and Frequency-domain Mixup Augmentation (FMA), in the spatial and frequency domains to generate new scans with varied noise distributions for further training. Additionally, the mixup frameworks are updated adversarially, being forced to provide more challenging samples. Experimental results show that D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net outperforms existing methods and demonstrates strong generalization ability across different denoising backbones. Code will be available on https://github.com/Aru321/D2MA-Net.

title: Transponder-Assisted Direct Tracking of Time-Varying Emitters under Extreme Blockage
authors: Shuozhen Li, Chuang Han, Yuexian Wang, Mingliang Tao, Wei Liu, Shahid Mumtaz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: A direct tracking method using distributed passive arrays is proposed for time-varying emitters under extreme blockage conditions, where only one observation station is available. A transponder-assisted observation model is constructed first, from which a novel single-station direct tracking scheme is designed. In this scheme, the rank reduction estimator is first used to separate the unknown channel fading from the nominal array manifold, and a subspace-based likelihood function is then formulated for initial emitter tracking, followed by channel fading estimation and compensation to refine the results. Simulation results are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, showing that its performance approaches that of a particle filter with prior time-varying information at relatively high signal-to-noise ratios.

title: Plug-and-Play Emotion Graphs for Compositional Prompting in Zero-Shot Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Jiacheng Shi, Hongfei Du, Y. Alicia Hong, Ye Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large audio-language models (LALMs) exhibit strong zero-shot performance across speech tasks but struggle with speech emotion recognition (SER) due to weak paralinguistic modeling and limited cross-modal reasoning. We propose Compositional Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Emotion Reasoning (CCoT-Emo), a framework that introduces structured Emotion Graphs (EGs) to guide LALMs in emotion inference without fine-tuning. Each EG encodes seven acoustic features (e.g., pitch, speech rate, jitter, shimmer), textual sentiment, keywords, and cross-modal associations. Embedded into prompts, EGs provide interpretable and compositional representations that enhance LALM reasoning. Experiments across SER benchmarks show that CCoT-Emo outperforms prior SOTA and improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines.

title: Generative Spatiotemporal Modeling for Uncertainty Quantification in High-Dimensional Physical Systems
authors: Fangzhou Liu, Chang Liu, Yuqi Li, Zhijiang Wang, Chuanguang Yang, Libo Huang, Zhulin An
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Forecasting high-dimensional chaotic systems, such as weather and turbulence, is fundamentally limited by uncertainty. Traditional deterministic models fail to capture this, collapsing to blurry, physically implausible mean-state predictions and offering no measure of confidence. We introduce Prism, a generative spatiotemporal framework that directly addresses this by learning the probability distribution of future states. Instead of regressing a single outcome, Prism, built on a conditional diffusion model, generates a diverse ensemble of high-fidelity, physically consistent future trajectories. This ensemble enables principled uncertainty quantification and improves the capture of low-probability, high-impact events. On standard benchmarks like WeatherBench and 2D turbulence, Prism significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on probabilistic metrics such as the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Our work establishes generative modeling as a powerful paradigm for building trustworthy AI tools for scientific discovery.

title: Focus Before Reasoning: A Bidirectional Selection Framework for Noise-Mitigation in Knowledge-Based Vision Question Answering
authors: Huan Zhao, Zexin Zhou, Guanghui Ye
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: While MLLM-based knowledge acquisition has become prevalent in Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA), such methods inevitably introduce noise due to inadequate instruction following or insufficient prompt engineering. We propose a "focus before reasoning" framework that pre-filters question-irrelevant visual objects through a bidirectional selection mechanism. Unlike existing approaches, we deploy both a Selector agent to identify relevant objects and an Excluder agent to explicitly filter out irrelevant ones, with a Judger agent resolving conflicts to produce the final focal context with interpretable rationales. These rationales then guide the Reasoner agent to produce knowledge-enhanced reasoning chains. We further leverage these reasoning chains as auxiliary supervision losses and design a focal-aware module to integrate focal context as knowledge into LLMs, enhancing reasoning performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with 70.5% on OK-VQA and 74.3% on A-OKVQA, which are standard benchmarks in the KBVQA field, surpassing previous best methods by 2.3% and 1.9% respectively.

title: CellExch: A Multimodal Feature-Based Framework for End-to-End Prediction of LRI-Mediated Cellcell Communication
authors: Hong Xia, Boya Ji, Shaoliang Peng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The cell-cell communications (CCCs) have a significant role in the development of invasion and metastasis in tumor tissues. However, exploring CCCs using traditional experimental methods is time-consuming and costly. To assist with CCC analysis, a computational framework named CellExch is proposed. CellExch includes two main steps: construction of ligand-receptor interactions (LRIs) database and measurement of the strength of CCCs mediated by LRIs. Specifically, CellExch first predicts LRIs using multimodal features of ligands and receptors via graph convolutional networks and selects high-confident LRIs from these predictions. Then, CellExch quantifies the strength of cell–cell communication (CCC) by integrating the product result, thresholding result, and cell result obtained from high-confidence lig-and–receptor interactions (LRIs) and imputed gene expression data. A five-fold cross-validation was performed on each of the four benchmark datasets to evaluate the performance of CellExch, demonstrating its robustness in the LRI prediction task. Furthermore, we analyzed CCC in combination with high-confident LRIs in mouse kidney tissue. In summary, CellExch provides an effective LRI prediction model as well as an evaluation method for the strength of CCC, which allows researchers to perform CCC analysis in a more efficient manner.

title: High Quality Underwater Image Compression with Adaptive Color Correction
authors: Yimin Zhou, Yichong Xia, Sicheng Pan, Bin Chen, Yaowei Li, Jiawei Li, Mingyao Hong, Zhi Wang, Yaowei Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the increasing exploration and exploitation of the underwater world, underwater images have become a critical medium for human interaction with marine environments, driving extensive research into their efficient transmission and storage. However, contemporary underwater image compression algorithms fail to adequately address the impact of water refraction and scattering on light waves, which not only elevate training complexity but also result in suboptimal compression performance. To tackle this limitation, we propose High Quality Underwater Image Compression (HQUIC), a novel framework designed to handle the unique illumination conditions and color shifts inherent in underwater images, thereby achieving superior compression performance. HQUIC first incorporates an Adaptive Lighting and Tone Correction (ALTC) module to adaptively predict the attenuation coefficients and global light information of images, effectively alleviating issues stemming from variations in illumination and tone across underwater images. Secondly, it dynamically weights multi-scale frequency components, prioritizing information critical to distortion quality while discarding redundant details. Furthermore, we introduce a tone adjustment loss to enable the model to better balance discrepancies among different color channels. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse underwater datasets validate that HQUIC outperforms state-of-the-art compression methods, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Encoder-Decoder Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Tri-Factorization for Graph Clustering
authors: Amjad Seyedi, Nicolas Gillis
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Symmetric nonnegative matrix tri-factorization (SNMTF) provides an interpretable, parts-based representation for graph data, where node memberships and cluster interactions jointly expose the community structure.
