Yet many SNMTF variants are decoder-only, reconstructing the graph without guaranteeing the latent variables are recoverable, which can yield drifting embeddings and unstable memberships. We introduce an encoder–decoder SNMTF (ED-SNMTF) that couples graph reconstruction with an encoder-consistency principle: the interaction matrix must be inferable by projecting the graph onto the learned membership space. This coupling induces implicit orthogonality: membership columns repel through the consistency term, so crisp clusters emerge without explicit orthogonality penalties or post hoc k-means. The optimization of the parameters of our model relies on GPU-accelerated computing with CUDA and is trained using the Adam optimizer, with a nonnegative projection step to guarantee feasibility and promote stable convergence. We evaluate ED-SNMTF on real-world networks and show that it outperforms both traditional baselines and recent deep methods.

title: Emo-TTA: Improving Test-Time Adaptation of Audio-Language Models for Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Jiacheng Shi, Hongfei Du, Y Alicia Hong, Ye Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech emotion recognition (SER) with audio-language models (ALMs) remains vulnerable to distribution shifts at test time, leading to performance degradation in out-of-domain scenarios. Test-time adaptation (TTA) provides a promising solution but often relies on gradient-based updates or prompt tuning, limiting flexibility and practicality. We propose Emo-TTA, a lightweight, training-free adaptation framework that incrementally updates class-conditional statistics via an Expectation-Maximization procedure for explicit test-time distribution estimation, using ALM predictions as priors. Emo-TTA operates on individual test samples without modifying model weights. Experiments on six out-of-domain SER benchmarks show consistent accuracy improvements over prior TTA baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of statistical adaptation in aligning model predictions with evolving test distributions.

title: FGAL-DM: Privacy-Preserving Semantic Communication via Federated Generative Adversarial Latent Diffusion Models
authors: Xinfeng Deng, Li Zhou, Haitao Chen, Nan Li, Yichi Zhang, Haitao Zhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Training generative models for semantic communication requires vast, distributed datasets, but this process faces critical challenges in distributed settings, including strict user privacy, limited client resources, and complex channel heterogeneity. Most existing works typically deploy the whole models on clients, leading to substantial overhead and limited adaptability to channel variations. To this end, we propose a training framework for the federated generative adversarial latent diffusion models (FGAL-DM). This framework employs an asymmetric architecture with a lightweight encoder on each client and a powerful signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)-aware generative model on the server. To overcome inherent privacy problem, we introduce a two-stage training strategy: supervised pre-training on public data for robust initialization, followed by adversarial fine-tuning in the latent space using a public data reference to avoid accessing private data. Additionally, a layer-wise contribution-aware aggregation mechanism is employed to further enhance training efficiency. Experimental results show that our framework achieves up to a 0.8 dB PSNR gain and a 1.4% LPIPS reduction, which facilitates the construction of a high-fidelity federated semantic communication model in practical scenarios.

title: SSCM: A Spatial-Semantic Consistent Model for Multi-Contrast MRI Super-Resolution
authors: Xiaoman Wu, Lubin Gan, Siying Wu, Jing Zhang, Yunwei Ou, Xiaoyan Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-contrast Magnetic Resonance Imaging super-resolution (MC-MRI SR) aims to enhance low-resolution (LR) contrasts leveraging high-resolution (HR) references, shortening acquisition time and improving imaging efficiency while preserving anatomical details. The main challenge lies in maintaining spatial-semantic consistency, ensuring anatomical structures remain well-aligned and coherent despite structural discrepancies and motion between the target and reference images. Conventional methods insufficiently model spatial–semantic consistency and underuse frequency-domain information, which leads to poor fine-grained alignment and inadequate recovery of high-frequency details. In this paper, we propose the Spatial-Semantic Consistent Model (SSCM), which integrates a Dynamic Spatial Warping Module for inter-contrast spatial alignment, a Semantic-Aware Token Aggregation Block for long-range semantic consistency, and a Spatial-Frequency Fusion Block for fine structure restoration. Experiments on public and private datasets show that SSCM achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters while ensuring spatially and semantically consistent reconstructions.

title: A Non-Overlapping Hawkes Modeling for Testing Granger Causality
authors: Xinhui Rong, Girish N. Nair
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Granger causality (GC) is a standard criterion for causal inference. In Hawkes networks (of random temporal event points), the usual GC test is nested. The Granger noncausality (GNC) model is obtained by forcing the causality coefficients to zero, and a likelihood ratio statistic is computed against the full model. This is unfair because the GNC model has fewer parameters and, more critically, its null lies on the boundary, so the χ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> limit fails and likelihood ratio thresholds are miscalibrated. We propose a non-overlapping, fair formulation of GC for Hawkes processes based on a marginal-intensity representation, producing models with the same parameter dimension and matched first-order dynamics, thereby avoiding boundary issues. This yields a simple two-sided test for GC.

title: Robust Deep Cross-Modal Hashing Via Dual Consensus Learning
authors: Yu Liu, Yang Wang, Muqiu Xie
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Cross-modal hashing (CMH) maps heterogeneous data such as images and text into a shared Hamming space for efficient retrieval. While supervised CMH achieves strong retrieval performance by leveraging label information, it remains highly vulnerable to noisy annotations. Existing noise-handling methods alleviate this issue but generally lack mechanisms to validate the reliability of processed samples, thereby limiting robustness. To address this gap, we propose Robust Deep Cross-modal Hashing via Dual Consensus Learning (RCHDC), which integrates two complementary modules. Label Refinement via Dual Consensus Learning (LRDCL) refines labels through dual-pathway consensus, while the Robust Contrastive Loss Function (RCL) enhances cross-modal discrimination and facilitates robust prototype training. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that RCHDC consistently improves retrieval accuracy and robustness under different noisy conditions, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

title: IRIS: Low-Complexity High-Efficiency Neural Network Codec for Real-Time Audio Transmission
authors: Ziqian Wu, Jiawei Jiang, Kunpeng Lin, He Wang, Qingbo Huang, Dejun Zhang, Andong Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The 2025 Low-Resource Audio Codec (LRAC) Challenge aims to advance the development of low-resource neural audio codec. It requires participating systems to achieve high-quality audio compression under strict resource constraints. LRAC Challenge Track 1 focuses on transparent audio transmission under real-world noise and reverberation, while Track 2 integrates speech enhancement and transmission. This paper introduces IRIS (Internet Real-time Intelligent Streaming Codec): an end-to-end, low-complexity, low-latency neural audio codec. IRIS enables encoding and decoding at bitrates of 1 kbps and 6 kbps, with inherent robustness to noise and reverberation. We designed a lightweight codec architecture, incorporating multiple discriminators and loss functions to achieve high-quality reconstruction at extremely low complexity and bitrates. In the LRAC Challenge, IRIS ranked first in Track 1, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed codec.

title: Graph-Based Emotion Consensus Perception Learning for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversation
authors: Huan Zhao, Gong Chen, Zhijie Yu, Yingxue Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition in conversations (MERC) combines speech, text, and visual cues to identify emotions. Existing methods often overlook the emotional consensus across modalities for the same emotion. For example, anger may manifest through a raised voice, harsh words, or a scowl, but its underlying emotion is consistent. Without modeling this consensus, conflicting signals from different modalities hinder recognition accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a Graph-based Emotion Consensus Perception (GECP), a novel framework designed to capture and leverage emotional consensus across modalities. The core of GECP is the consensus-aware learning module, which operates in two stages. First, multimodal propagation graphs are constructed to capture both shared emotional signals and modality-specific discrepancies. Second, an emotion consensus learning unit aligns the signals from each modality with a common emotional prototype, distilling the essential emotional features for each category. By integrating propagation-based learning with consensus-driven alignment, GECP effectively captures the core emotional essence. Experiments on two benchmark datasets, IEMOCAP and MELD, demonstrate that GECP consistently outperforms existing MERC methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/Clancyy/ConGraNet.

title: Lamut: Lighting-Aware Multi-Material Appearance Transfer from a Single Image
authors: Songlin Xiao, Peng Li, Kunbin Yao, Zhe Kong, Guanying Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Material appearance transfer from a single image is vital for virtual content creation and design. Recent diffusion-based methods have shown promising results in transferring materials onto objects in an image without requiring explicit 3D information. However, they struggle to preserve the original scene’s illumination and cannot handle the simultaneous transfer of multiple materials. To address these limitations, we propose LaMuT, a lighting-aware multi-material transfer approach. Our method introduces a Lighting-invariant Material Encoding module to disentangle lighting effects, and a Region-aware Cross-attention Reweighting mechanism for simultaneous multi-material transfer. Extensive evaluations on both real and synthetic datasets demonstrate that LaMuT achieves photo-realistic, lighting-consistent, and semantically aware multi-material transfer, significantly outperforming existing approaches.

title: Accelerating Vehicular Federated Learning via Convergence-Aware Hierarchical Scheduling
authors: Jiasheng Zhu, Xinrui Gao, Yaolin Wang, Haoran Yang, Shangpeng Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficient client scheduling is vital for federated learning in integrated space-air-ground vehicular networks. Existing methods often neglect dynamic communication links and learning convergence metrics. We propose a Hierarchical Decoupling Scheduling (HDS) framework that aligns network decisions with long-term learning objectives. This approach splits the complex joint scheduling problem into a stable matching game for client association and a convex subproblem for resource allocation. Our convergence-aware method manages data heterogeneity and prevents client starvation. Simulations show our framework outperforms baselines in convergence speed and final model accuracy, achieving over 80% accuracy on highly non-IID data while maintaining near-linear scalability.

title: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Video Colorization and Color Propagation Methods
authors: Sergey Muravlev, Sergey Lavrushkin, David Chikovani, Dmitriy Vatolin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video colorization and color propagation transform grayscale footage into a realistic color version. The field lacks a standardized evaluation framework to compare algorithms under diverse conditions. In this paper, we present the first comprehensive benchmark for video colorization, providing a rigorous comparison of 13 exemplar-based and fully automatic colorization methods. We introduce new datasets consisting of 153 diverse real-world scenes with annotated subjective quality scores. We also propose a novel evaluation metric, the Video Colorization Quality Index (VCQI), which is trained on human ratings and outperforms existing objective metrics in predicting perceived video colorization quality. Extensive experiments on this benchmark highlight the relative strengths and weaknesses of current colorization methods.

title: Three Seconds is Sufficient: A Multi-Pronged Framework for Model-Based Speaker Adaptation in ASR Under Data-Scarce Conditions
authors: Jiajun Deng, Guinan Li, Chunyat Wu, Tristan Tsoi, Huimeng Wang, Tao Zhong, Zhaoqing Li, Chengxi Deng, Youjun Chen, Shujie Hu, Xunying Liu, Simon Lui
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The practical application of model-based adaptation for end-to-end ASR is severely hindered by speaker-level data sparsity. To address this critical limitation, this paper presents a holistic, multi-pronged framework that simultaneously targets three key areas: 1) model efficiency using a low-rank subspace factorization with probabilistic adaptive training to reduce data requirements; 2) data augmentation through novel speaker retrieval and guided synthesis methods; and 3) parameter stability by adopting a Bayesian estimation and gradient stabilization for uncertainty modeling. Experiments on the 300-hour Switchboard and a 5,000-hour in-house dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently delivers robust performance gains across all data conditions, achieving absolute WER reductions of up to 1.6% (Switchboard) and 4.3% (in-house) over the standard model adaptation with only three seconds of speech from the target speaker.

title: Prediction of Diabetic Nephropathy Using Machine Learning Algorithms Based on Crucial Eight Clinical Features
authors: Seeta Devi, Manisha Mistry
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Objective It is still challenging to precisely differentiate diabetic nephropathy from non-diabetic renal disease. In order to promote the differential diagnosis between diabetic nephropathy and non-diabetic renal conditions, the current study aims to develop and validate a machine learning-based model that utilizes the utilizes clinical parameters. Methods: We included 1,077 patients from a Maharashtra hospital between January 2024 and April 2025. Clinical features including numbness, sensitivity to touch, nocturia, proteinuria, swelling, and more are present in the dataset. The presence or absence of diabetic nephropathy was determined by a binary target variable. Several machine learning algorithms, including NB, AB, SGD, Gradient Boosting, CN2 Rule Inducer, NN, SVM, and kNN, were used to pre-process and analyze the dataset. Ten-fold cross-validation was employed to assess the model's performance, and each model's accuracy and AUC were examined. Results: AdaBoost outperformed Gradient Boosting and CN2 Rule Inducer (AUC 0.805, accuracy 0.737), with the highest AUC of 0.807 and accuracy of 0.738. With an AUC of 0.500, the SGD model wasn't performing well. Strong correlations between clinical characteristics, including touch sensitivity and urine protein, and diabetic nephropathy were found by the feature analysis <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\chi^{2}= 62.32, \mathrm{p}=0.000)$</tex>. When it attempted predicting the risk of diabetic nephropathy, AdaBoost performed exceptionally well overall. Conclusion: AdaBoost and other ML algorithms are particularly effective at differentiating between nondiabetic renal disease and diabetic nephropathy. The creation of a decision-support system to help with the early detection and treatment of diabetic nephropathy is supported by these results.

title: DSPC: Dual-Stage Progressive Compression Framework for Efficient Long-Context Reasoning
authors: Yaxin Gao, Yao Lu, Zongfei Zhang, Jiaqi Nie, Shanqing Yu, Qi Xuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in many natural language processing (NLP) tasks. To achieve more accurate output, the prompts used to drive LLMs have become increasingly longer, which incurs higher computational costs. To address this prompt inflation problem, prompt compression has been proposed. However, most existing methods require training a small auxiliary model for compression, incurring a significant amount of additional computation. To avoid this, we propose a two-stage, training-free approach, called Dual-Stage Progressive Compression (DSPC). In the coarse-grained stage, semantic-related sentence filtering removes sentences with low semantic value based on TF-IDF. In the fine-grained stage, token importance is assessed using attention contribution, cross-model loss difference, and positional importance, enabling the pruning of low-utility tokens while preserving semantics. We validate DSPC on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct and GPT-3.5-Turbo under a constrained token budget and observe consistent improvements. For instance, in the FewShot task of the Longbench dataset, DSPC achieves a performance of 49.17 by using only 3× fewer tokens, outperforming the best state-of-the-art baseline LongLLMLingua by 7.76.

title: Low-Bandwidth High-Fidelity Speech Transmission with Generative Latent Joint Source-Channel Coding
authors: Guangkuan Li, Shengshi Yao, Sixian Wang, Zhenyu Liu, Kai Niu, Jincheng Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing speech joint source-channel coding (JSCC) methods show advantages in robustness, however, when it goes to extreme-low bandwidth, its perceptual quality drops sharply. This poses severe challenges for high-fidelity speech transmission over bandwidth-limited weak networks. To tackle this, we propose a new Generative Latent JSCC (GL-JSCC) framework. It performs JSCC in the generative latent space learned by a residual vector quantization generative adversarial network (RVQ-GAN), instead of in the source space. Our generative latent code exhibits higher sparsity and better alignment with human perception, and generative modeling in the latent space provides an additional prior for better quality in low bandwidth. Moreover, a three-stage progressive training strategy is given to ensure effective model alignment and training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GL-JSCC achieves robust, high-quality speech transmission across a range of low SNR and bandwidth settings. Our scheme achieves the same perceptual quality while saving up to 60% of the bandwidth compared to Opus + 5G LDPC transmission.

title: Optimal QAM Constellation for Over-the-air Computation in the Presence of Heavy-Tailed Channel Noise
authors: Saeed Razavikia, Deniz Gündüz, Carlo Fischione
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Over-the-air computation (OAC) enables low-latency aggregation over multiple-access channels (MACs) by exploiting the superposition property of the wireless medium to compute functions efficiently in distributed networks. A critical but often overlooked challenge is that electromagnetic interference in practical radio channels frequently exhibits heavy-tailed behavior, causing strong impulsive noise that severely degrades computation performance. This work studies digital OAC with QAM-based signaling under heavy-tailed interference modeled by a Cauchy distribution (lacking a finite second moment). We seek QAM-like constellations that minimize the mean-squared error (MSE) of sum aggregation subject to an average-power constraint. The problem is formulated as a constrained optimization, whose solution yields unique optimality conditions. Numerical results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed design. Notably, the framework extends naturally to nomographic functions, broader constellation families, and alternative noise models.

title: RIS-Assisted SWIPT Under Bounded CSI Uncertainty: A Worst-Case Max-Min Approach
authors: Dev Shah, Kunwar Pritiraj Rajput, Manish Kumar
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: This work studies a multiuser simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) network assisted by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS). A realistic scenario with imperfect channel state information (CSI) is considered and modeled using a bounded uncertainty framework. With a user fairness objective, we maximize the minimum worst-case signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) by jointly optimizing the base station (BS) beamformers, RIS reflection coefficients, and user power-splitting ratios subject to BS transmit power and minimum harvested energy constraints. The resulting nonconvex problem is solved through an alternating optimization method that divides the design into three tractable parts. Robustness against CSI errors is achieved by reformulating the worst-case constraints into linear matrix inequalities via S-procedure and sign definiteness-based tools. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed robust design and demonstrate clear SINR gains over uncertainty-agnostic schemes, highlighting the benefit of the proposed robust design.

title: AI-aided Consensus Kalman Tracking in Partially-Known State-Space Models
authors: Aviv Golan, Nir Shlezinger
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distributed Kalman filters (KFs) implement efficient decentralized tracking by combining local filtering with message passing among neighboring agents. However, their performance degrades when the dynamics are only partially known and when noise statistics are unknown or mismatched. In this work, we introduce Distributed KalmanNet, which augments the distributed KF with a carefully designed graph neural network to operate reliably under such imperfect modeling conditions. This augmentation enables each agent to learn, from data, how to compute the equivalent of the local filter and aggregate neighbor information without requiring knowledge of the underlying noise distributions, while leveraging the available (possibly approximate) system dynamics. We numerically show that Distributed KalmanNet achieves notable performance gains over conventional distributed KFs in nonlinear and mismatched dynamical environments.

title: Explainable Comparative Analysis of Tree-Based Ensemble Learning Models for MASLD Screening
authors: Kokhulash MS, Varalakshmi P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is a growing global health concern, yet its asymptomatic progression makes early, non-invasive screening critical for intervention. This study utilized a dataset of 9,023 participants from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) 2009-2016. We systematically compared four tree-based ensemble methodsRandom Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost, and LightGBM-using model-specific feature selection and robust cross-validation. Performance was evaluated on a held-out test set using ROC-AUC, F1score, precision, recall, and specificity. All gradient boosting models demonstrated exceptional discriminatory power (ROC-AUC > 0.995). The optimized LightGBM model yielded the best and most balanced performance, achieving the highest F1-score of 0.8841 and precision of 0.8306, with a ROC-AUC of 0.9970 and 0.9450 recall. Model interpretability via SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) confirmed the LightGBM model's predictions were driven by clinically- relevant factors, identifying Ethnicity, GGT, and HOMA IR as the top predictors. This work demonstrates that an optimized LightGBM model, trained on a parsimonious set of non-invasive features, can serve as a highly accurate, robust, and interpretable tool for large-scale MASLD screening.

title: Experimental Mitigation of Radiated Electromagnetic Interference in Variable-Frequency Drive Systems
authors: Hasen Zejćirović, Majda Tešanović, Sanela Užičanin, Lejla Mehanović
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Variable-frequency drives (VFDs) have enabled the use of robust induction motors in industrial, regulated electric drive systems. Their application has led to increased reliability, energy efficiency, and flexibility of industrial processes. However, their operation, based on pulse-width modulation (PWM), results in the occurrence of electromagnetic interference (EMI) and degradation of power quality in the supply network. This paper presents an experimental analysis of electromagnetic interference in a low-power VFD system under laboratory conditions. The investigation was conducted on an Altivar 320 VFD supplying a 250 W induction motor operating in no-load mode. The influence of the switching frequency of the VFD on radiated electromagnetic interference was analyzed. In addition, the effectiveness of different EMI mitigation methods was experimentally examined. The obtained results indicate a correlation between the operating mode of the drive and the level of radiated electromagnetic interference.

title: Expressive Voice Conversion with Controllable Emotional Intensity
authors: Nannan Teng, Ying Hu, Zhijian Ou, Sheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Expressive voice conversion (VC) aims to transfer the speaker identity and emotion from the target speech to the source speech. In this paper, we propose an expressive voice conversion model with controllable emotional intensity named CEIVC, which includes a specific attribute augmentation (SAA) training strategy for perturbing the speaker and emotional attributes of source speech, and an emotional disentanglement and intensity control (EDIC) module to control the emotional intensity of converted speech. Moreover, we leverage perturbation adaptive instance normalization (PbAdaIN) to further boost the synthesis quality. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed CEI-VC outperforms five compared models and is capable of controlling the emotional intensity of converted speech. Demos and codes are available at https://tengnn.github.io/ExpressiveVC/.

title: Enhanced Video Compression with Context-Aware Dynamic Neural Adapter
authors: Shaofan Sun, Shuangming Ma, Han Chen, Ling-Yu Duan, Jiaying Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing demand for video data to simultaneously serve human and machine visual analysis brings significant challenges to compression design. Traditional codecs are primarily tailored for pixel-level reconstruction, offering high compression efficiency but limited adaptability to diverse downstream tasks. With the emergence of end-to-end learned compression, it becomes possible to tailor representations for different purposes. However, such methods usually suffer from high complexity and incompatibility with existing compression standards, which limits their deployment. To address these issues and meet the requirements of diverse application scenarios, we propose for the first time a Context-Aware dynamic neural adapter for enhanced Video Compression (CAVC) that incorporates a context mode injection mechanism, allowing a single model to dynamically adjust to different compression requirements via numerical context modes. This method adds only a small number of learnable parameters to enable diverse adaptation, while maintaining compatibility with standard codecs. Experimental results on UVG and TUMTraf datasets demonstrate that our approach enhances compression performance for visual perception, pixel-level reconstruction, and machine recognition.

title: Large-Scale EEG Models For Meditation State Recognition
authors: Ying-Jie Zhang, Mingyu Gou, Zuxin Song, Tianzhen Chen, Fei Cheng, Bao-Liang Lu, Jiang Du, Wei-Long Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Meditation has received widespread attention for its positive effects on mental health. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers a promising objective approach for identifying meditation states; however, traditional EEG analysis models suffer from limited generalizability and dependence on task-specific data. Recently, large-scale EEG models (LEMs) based on self-supervised learning have opened new avenues for EEG analysis by using large-scale pretraining and cross-task transferability. This study provides the first systematic review of LEM architectures and empirically evaluates their effectiveness in the classification of meditation and resting states. Comparative experiments on five representative LEMs (EEGPT, BENDR, BIOT, LaBraM, and Gram) revealed that: (1) LEMs exhibit varying performance across different setups, highlighting the importance of model-task alignment; (2) continued pretraining on the SEED dataset may degrade performance due to negative transfer. In our evaluation, LaBraM-Large achieved the best cross-subject F1 score of 70.49%, while LaBraM-Base reached the highest subject-dependent F1 score of 67.33%, demonstrating the potential of LEMs for practical meditation monitoring, laying an important foundation for objective assessment and intervention technologies in future mental health applications.

title: Boosting Anomaly Detection in Industrial 3D Data: An Entropy-Guided Denoising Autoencoder
authors: Zihe Chen, Yuan Zhang, Bin Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Point cloud anomaly detection is crucial for quality assurance in manufacturing. Reconstruction-based methods excel in efficient training and direct error-based scoring; however, they face limitations like suboptimal masking strategies, inadequate semantic recovery, and poor performance in high-precision scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce an Entropy-Guided Denoising Autoencoder (Eng-DAE), a novel reconstruction-based framework that integrates masked and denoising autoencoders for efficient and robust anomaly detection. The core innovation lies in an entropy-guided encoder that dynamically masks patches based on structural complexity, prioritizing reconstruction of information-rich local features to enhance model focus and efficiency. A coarse-fine decoder reconstructs multiscale representations, effectively capturing fine-grained local details alongside global shapes for precise anomaly localization. Feature-level Gaussian noise injection further boosts generalizability and few-shot capabilities without relying on domain-specific anomaly simulations. Evaluated on the Real3D-AD dataset, Eng-DAE achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating superior anomaly detection in point clouds.

title: Retrieval Augmented Pretrained Transformer for Competing Risks Survival in Statistical Signal Processing
authors: Yingzi Wang, Jiaxin Li, Xiaoming Wang, Chaobo Zhang, Yiming Li, Xiaojun Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: High frequency limit order books (LOBs) stream is execution cancellation event sequences that form a structured time series signal critical to algorithmic execution, transaction cost analysis, and risk control. This work contributes to the exploration of pre-trained transformer-based model in algorithmic trading and execution risk management. However, building a pre-trained model for transaction data is non-trivial due to the censoring structure, regime dependent order flows, and severe class imbalance under mutually exclusive outcomes. In this paper, we propose TABR-CSH, a retrieval enhanced pre-trained model for prediction of mutually exclusive execution and cancellation probabilities in LOBs. We integrate the historical retrieval block to provide context from similar past events and the cause specific hazards heads to estimate time varying execution and cancellation hazards. We evaluate TABR with a real word tick-level order execution and cancellation dataset and demonstrated state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance AUROC over 0.713 in prediction accuracy.

title: Ara-BEST-RQ: Multi Dialectal Arabic SSL
authors: Haroun Elleuch, Ryan Whetten, Salima Mdhaffar, Yannick Estève, Fethi Bougares
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present Ara-BEST-RQ, a family of self-supervised learning (SSL) models specifically designed for multi-dialectal Arabic speech processing. Leveraging 5,640 hours of crawled Creative Commons speech and combining it with publicly available datasets, we pre-train conformer-based BEST-RQ models up to 600M parameters. Our models are evaluated on dialect identification (DID) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the former while using fewer parameters than competing models. We demonstrate that family-targeted pre-training on Arabic dialects significantly improves downstream performance compared to multilingual or monolingual models trained on non-Arabic data. All models, code, and pre-processed datasets will be publicly released to support reproducibility and further research in Arabic speech technologies.

title: Training-Free Framework For Defending Unsafe Image Synthesis Attack
authors: Junha Park, Jaehui Hwang, Ian Ryu, Hyungkeun Park, Jiyoon Kim, Jong-Seok Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With advances in generative models, image generation has shown significant improvements. This raises concerns about potential abuse of generation models, such as creation of explicit or violent images, commonly referred to as Not Safe For Work (NSFW) content. To address this, the Stable Diffusion model includes safety checkers to censor initial text prompts and output images from the model. However, these safety checkers have vulnerabilities against adversarial attacks, allowing them to generate NSFW images. In this paper, we find that these adversarial attacks are not robust against small changes in embeddings of text prompts. Based on this, we propose a training-free defense method using randomized probing of prompt embeddings for text-to-image generation (T2I) to detect adversarial attacks generating NSFW images. We demonstrate the superiority of our method in terms of performance and applicability.

title: Learning What to Hear: Boosting Sound-Source Association for Robust Audiovisual Instance Segmentation
authors: Jinbae Seo, Hyeongjun Kwon, Kwonyoung Kim, Jiyoung Lee, Kwanghoon Sohn
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audiovisual instance segmentation (AVIS) aims to accurately localize and track sounding objects throughout video sequences. Existing methods suffer from visual bias stemming from two fundamental issues: uniform additive fusion prevents queries from specializing to different sound sources, while visual-only training objectives limit queries from converging to arbitrary salient objects. We propose Audio-Centric Query Generation (ACQG) using cross-attention mechanism, enabling each query to selectively attend to distinct sound sources and carry sound-specific priors into visual decoding. Additionally, we introduce Sound-Aware Ordinal Counting (SAOC) loss that explicitly supervises sounding object numbers through ordinal regression with monotonic consistency constraints, preventing visual-only convergence during training. Experiments on AVISeg benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements: +1.64 mAP, +0.6 HOTA, and +2.06 FSLA, validating that query specialization and explicit counting supervision are crucial for accurate audiovisual instance segmentation. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/jinbae-s/ACVIS

title: Semantic-Aware 3D Scene Decomposition Using Superquadrics
authors: Siqi Liu, Cewu Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-view scene reconstruction has achieved remarkable performance with the advancement of neural networks and novel 3D representations such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting. However, these approaches fail to capture the semantic geometric structures of scene components and lack support for intuitive scene manipulation. We propose a novel method for decomposing multi-view images into superquadric primitives that addresses these limitations. Our approach operates directly on uncalibrated multi-view images, eliminating the need for camera calibration or neural network training, and enabling efficient scene decomposition through geometric primitive fitting. We evaluate our method on the DTU and BlendedMVS datasets, demonstrating comparable reconstruction quality to state-of-the-art neural network-based approaches while requiring significantly fewer computational resources and training time. The resulting superquadric decomposition provides semantically meaningful geometric representations that facilitate downstream applications such as scene editing and object manipulation.

title: A Novel Underwater Integrated Communication and Positioning Algorithm Based on OAM-OFDM
authors: Kaiyuan Liu, Wanlong Zhao, Deyue Zou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Aiming at the underwater positioning needs of multi-Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) operations, this paper proposes an Underwater Integrated Communication and Positioning (UICP) technology combining Orbital Angular Momentum (OAM) and Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM). It utilizes OAM’s multi-mode characteristics and OFDM’s high spectral efficiency to achieve multi-dimensional multiplexing, constructs an OAM-OFDM integrated waveform model, and estimates the Angle of Arrival (AOA) and Time of Arrival (TOA) of signals through a Uniform Circular Array (UCA). Simulations show that this algorithm has higher positioning accuracy than traditional methods, providing a new direction for the development of underwater integrated communication and positioning.

title: E2E-AEC: Implementing An End-To-End Neural Network Learning Approach for Acoustic Echo Cancellation
authors: Yiheng Jiang, Biao Tian, Haoxu Wang, Shengkui Zhao, Bin Ma, Daren Chen, Xiangang Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose a novel neural network-based end-to-end acoustic echo cancellation (E2E-AEC) method capable of streaming inference, which operates effectively without reliance on traditional linear AEC (LAEC) techniques and time delay estimation. Our approach includes several key strategies: First, we introduce and refine progressive learning to gradually enhance echo suppression. Second, our model employs knowledge transfer by initializing with a pre-trained LAEC-based model, harnessing the insights gained from LAEC training. Third, we optimize the attention mechanism with a loss function applied on attention weights to achieve precise time alignment between the reference and microphone signals. Lastly, we incorporate voice activity detection to enhance speech quality and improve echo removal by masking the network output when near-end speech is absent. The effectiveness of our approach is validated through experiments conducted on public datasets.

title: DocLayout: Elevating the Role of Complex Layout Understanding in Document Visual Question Answering
authors: Jinxu Zhang, Yongqi Yu, Qiyuan Fan, Yuqi Wang, Haoxuan Zhu, Haofeng Wang, Yu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in document visual question answering (DVQA). However, complex document layouts still present significant challenges for these models. This work proposes DocLayout, a comprehensive multimodal benchmark comprising 1,305 question–answer pairs derived from 328 complex, multi-source document images. An empirical evaluation of 12 LVLMs shows that these challenges persist in complex layout understanding. To address this limitation, we introduce the Layout-aware Reasoning and Document Understanding (LRDU) framework, which combines semantic document segmentation and multi-agent collaboration. Experimental results demonstrate that LRDU significantly improves performance and highlight the critical need for enhanced visual reasoning capabilities in LVLMs for complex document understanding tasks.

title: Orthotvnet: A Self-Supervised Denoising Method on Raw Mesh for Complex Dental Geometry
authors: Yutong Hu, Haisheng Li, Li Chen, Huanpu Yin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In recent years, artificial intelligence (AI) has propelled digital innovation in dentistry. Reconstructed crowns restore tooth geometry but often introduce reconstruction noise, which increases the demand for mesh denoising. Current methods are not designed to handle dental mesh with large curvature variation and complex occlusal topology. Moreover, these methods convert mesh into graph and thus lose geometric information, and the high cost of paired high-quality labeled datasets makes supervised approaches impractical. To address these challenges, we propose a self-supervised framework that directly analyzes and denoises raw mesh without graph conversion or paired labels. Our method integrates orthogonal-channel attention, inspired by signal processing principles of orthogonality, to sharpen features and let the network separate anatomical details from noise, analogous to signal separation. Experimental results confirm that our method reduces noise and restores curvature more accurately than existing graph-based methods, demonstrating its effectiveness for dental mesh denoising.

title: Physics-Informed Video Diffusion for Shallow Water Equations
authors: Yang Bai, George Eskandar, Ziyuan Liu, Gitta Kutyniok
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Traditional fluid dynamics simulation pipelines combine numerical solvers with rendering, producing highly realistic results but at considerable computational cost. Diffusion-based generative video models offer a faster alternative, yet often ignore physical laws and thus fail to capture consistent dynamics. We propose a physics-informed video diffusion framework that jointly generates visual outputs and physical states. Unlike prior two-stage approaches that first simulate the physical variables and then render, we directly integrate physics constraints into the generative process, enabling simultaneous prediction of physical states and realistic videos without a separate rendering step. Built on the two-dimensional shallow water equations with terrain topography, our method produces temporally coherent water flow while maintaining physical plausibility. Experiments show that it outperforms purely data-driven video diffusion baselines in both realism and physical fidelity, while generating videos significantly faster than traditional simulation-plus-rendering pipelines.

title: AR-LIF: Adaptive Reset Leaky Integrate-And-Fire Neuron for Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Zeyu Huang, Wei Meng, Quan Liu, Kun Chen, Li Ma
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Spiking neural networks offer low energy consumption due to their event-driven nature. Beyond binary spike outputs, their intrinsic floating-point dynamics merit greater attention. Neuronal threshold levels and reset modes critically determine spike count and timing. Hard reset cause information loss, while soft reset apply uniform treatment to neurons. To address these issues, we design an adaptive reset neuron that establishes relationships between inputs, outputs, and reset, while integrating a simple yet effective threshold adjustment strategy. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves excellent performance while maintaining lower energy consumption. In particular, it attains state-of-the-art accuracy on Tiny-ImageNet and CIFAR10-DVS. Codes are available at https://github.com/2ephyrus/AR-LIF.

title: A Fusion-Based Random Forest-LSTM Model for Continuous Digital Health Monitoring via Smartwatch Wearing Prediction
authors: Emine Nur Nacar, Melda Kevser Akgün, Nurullah Güleç
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Smartwatches enable continuous acquisition of physiological and behavioral signals for digital health; however, non-wearing periods caused by user non-adherence interrupts data streams and degrades downstream analytics. This paper proposes a fusion-based model that predicts unworn intervals to preserve continuity in long-term monitoring. The approach couples an ensemble learner and a temporal model: a Random Forest (RF) is trained on engineered behavioral/contextual features (battery dynamics, charging status, motion statistics, and time-of-day encodings), while a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network processes short sequences of physiological signals to capture temporal precursors of detachment. We adopt late fusion with a tunable coefficient to blend calibrated probabilities from both branches. Experiments on smartwatch-like data streams demonstrate that the fusion approach matches or exceeds the RF baseline in discrimination while improving stability across operating points (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{ROC}-\text{AUC}=0.738; \text{PR}-\text{AUC}=0.816$</tex>). Feature attribution highlights variability measures, acceleration, skin temperature, and heart rate standard deviations, as dominant predictors of non-wear, with circadian encodings and battery trends providing complementary context. The proposed framework supports uninterrupted digital health analytics by flagging likely gaps before they propagate into imputation and inference pipelines, and it is compatible with edge or cloud deployment. These results suggest a practical path to more reliable, personalized e-health monitoring through behavior-temporal fusion.

title: Artificial Intelligence—Driven Agricultural Intelligence Systems for Enhancing Crop Productivity and Sustainable Farm Management
authors: Sangeetha T, Mahendran R, M Soniya, Surendran R, Gedela Kalyani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Fast population increase, climate change and depletion of resources are some reasons due to which there is an acceleration in demand for technology adoption for managing agricultural activities. AI (including IoT, ML, and robots) brings predictive analysis, autonomous decision-making and precision resource management to agriculture. The proposed AIAF (AI-driven Agricultural Intelligence Framework) provides a better framework to integrate multisource data (soil, climate, crop imagery, market data) with ML techniques for predicting the yield of crops, identifying the diseases and optimizing irrigation. Results from the field scale validation showed that the developed system increases water use efficiency by 27%, increases fertilizer use efficiency by 21%, and overall crop productivity is enhanced up to 18% in comparison to traditional practices. The platform also cuts the operational manpower needs by 34% with robotic automation. Results illustrate the importance of AI technologies in moving towards future sustainable agriculture and food security. Besides, this work discusses adoption challenges and provides a roadmap to large-scale deployment.

title: Forecasting Customer Attrition in Digital Banking with Real-Time Deep Learning
authors: Rajat Gera, Sandhya Antony M, Nisarga H A, Chirag V, Gangothri V, Chaturya Poornashree B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: In digital banking, the challenge of customer attrition is vital since the threat of service choice and resistance to switch increases due to an increase in service options and low switching costs. The nonlinear, dynamic trends in customer behavior are difficult to model using conventional statistical and rule-of-thumb models, leading to subsequent delayed or inaccurate forecasts. To propose one real-time deep learning model that predicts attrition based on behavioral telemetry, transactional sequence, frequency of interaction, and engagement. The system achieves a hybrid temporal structure, which is a combination of sequence modelling based on LSTM, feature weighting based on attention, and highdimensional learning with a multilayer predictive fusion block. Experimental analysis shows that the proposed framework has the highest prediction accuracy, 95.8%, F 1 -score, 94.6%, AUC 96.2%, and a decrease in false positives of 21.3%, whereas the time to detect early warnings increases by 31.7%. The model further performs better than the baseline machine learning systems and deep learners, with an overall performance improvement of 12 to 18% on core measures. The results support the fact that realtime deep learning-based churn forecasting has the potential to improve retention approaches, risk reduction, and data-driven decision-making in contemporary digital banking settings.

title: A Speech-Driven Paradigm for Physics-Informed Modeling of Coupled Micro-Speakers
authors: Chen Huang, Chen Gong, Lei Zhou, Guoliang Wu, Hongqing Liu, Lu Gan, Liming Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Modeling of co-cavity micro-speakers is challenging due to the pronounced nonlinearities and coupling behavior, which are poorly captured by traditional identification methods (e.g., sine sweep). We propose and validate an alternative paradigm: system identification using realistic speech stimuli to excite the full range of nonlinear behaviors. To this end, a compact, physics-informed neural system model is developed to jointly model the entire speaker array, rather than individual drivers. The proposed framework achieves superior prediction accuracy compared to traditional methods, demonstrating the potential of leveraging realistic signals and gray-box modeling for the efficient characterization of complex nonlinear systems.

title: Towards Self-Evaluation of Sycophantic Hallucinations in Mathematical Reasoning
authors: Zixuan Shangguan, Yanjie Dong, Xiaoyi Fan, Victor C. M. Leung, Xiping Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sycophantic hallucinations occur when large language models (LLMs) produce misleading outputs that align with user biases or incorrect opinions. Sycophantic hallucinations undermine the reliability and trustworthiness of LLMs and become particularly pronounced in mathematical reasoning tasks. Therefore, we first propose the SycoMath framework that introduces three progressively structured prompting paradigms to explicitly reproduce sycophantic hallucinations during mathematical reasoning tasks. Based on the induced sycophantic hallucinations from the SycoMath framework, we then propose a self-evaluation method, which is named as ISF-Eval. Our ISF-Eval exploits the hidden states of LLMs during inference to differentiate hallucinated outputs from de-facto ones without additional training and external supervision. Extensive experiments illustrate that the ISF-Eval exhibits strong generalizability across diverse models and reasoning complexity levels and can effectively address both hallucination-induced and intrinsic challenges.

title: Cardiobridge-DM: Bridging Cross-Cohort Heart Sound Synthesis via Rhythm-Aware Semi-Supervised Diffusion
authors: Chenyang Xu, Siming Li, Hao Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Robust AI for cardiac auscultation is hampered by data fragmentation, creating a trade-off between small, richly-annotated datasets and large, weakly-labeled ones. We introduce CardioBridge-DM, a semi-supervised diffusion framework that bridges this gap by synthesizing high-fidelity, controllable phonocardiograms (PCGs). Its core innovation is a rhythm-aware semi-supervised Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) strategy, empowering the model to infer physiological rhythm from textual diagnostics alone, even without ECG data. This breakthrough achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD) of 4.3 and an unprecedented Cross-Cohort Transferability (CCT) of 0.82. Crucially, a perceptual study confirms our synthetic audio is difficult for trained listeners to distinguish, validating Cardio-Bridge-DM as a scalable data augmentation solution that significantly enhances the generalizability and clinical utility of medical AI.

title: F5E-TTS: Enhancing Speech Synthesis by Aligning Text with Rich Semantic Representations
authors: Yihang Chen, Hualei Wang, Na Li, Zhifeng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Mainstream Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems still struggle with semantic alignment between speech and text, resulting in unnatural or incorrect speech for some hard cases, a limitation we attribute to the semantic sparsity of text. To address this, we introduce F5E-TTS, a novel flow-matching framework that enriches the synthesis process by conditioning on both text and semantically dense PPG embeddings, the bottleneck features from a Phonetic PosteriorGrams (PPG) model. The core of our framework is a training strategy where a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone learns to implicitly align text and PPGs, further encouraged by an explicit cross-modal regularization technique using a shared vector-quantized (VQ) codebook. This enriched content representation generates more natural-sounding speech and significantly improves content accuracy. Our model achieves a state-of-the-art 16.4% relative Word Error Rate (WER) reduction over the strong F5-TTS baseline. All codes and dependencies are released at https://github.com/kaleo996/F5E-TTS.

title: Semantic-Guided Modal Alignment for Multimodal Cardiovascular Disease Detection
authors: Guodao Zhang, Dongyang Liu, Yanjie Lu, Hong Sun, Bin Lin, Zhongping Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is major global health concern. Their complex etiology makes accurate detection particularly challenging. Multimodal data can offer complementary insights from different perspectives, thereby enhancing detection accuracy. However, existing multimodal detection methods overlook the inherent differences between modalities, leading to suboptimal fusion effects. Inspired by visual-language model (VLM), we propose a fusion network based on Semantic-guided Modal Alignment (SMA), utilizing disease diagnosis labels as prompts to align electrocardiogram features with electronic health record features. This approach enables consistent high-level semantic representation across the two modalities, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of multimodal fusion. The experimental results on MIMIC-IV-ECG myocardial infarction (MI) dataset indicate that our model outperforms baseline models with an accuracy of 89.81% and an AUC of 91.24%.

title: E3MMDTA: Predicting Drug Target Affinity by Combining E3FP Fingerprints and Multimodal Fusion
authors: Zhensong Wang, Weiliang Han, Ge Kong, Juan Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Predicting drug–target affinity (DTA) is vital for drug discovery. Traditional computational approaches are limited by structural requirements and scalability, while many deep learning methods neglect stereochemistry and underexploit multimodal data. We present E3MMDTA, a framework that integrates Extended 3-Dimensional FingerPrints (E3FP) with molecular graphs, sequence embeddings, and pre-trained language features. An attention-based fusion module adaptively balances these heterogeneous inputs. Evaluated on Davis, KIBA, and Metz datasets, E3MMDTA achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing DeepDTA, GraphDTA, and recent large-model approaches. Ablation studies confirm that both stereochemical descriptors and multimodal fusion provide significant contributions. By effectively unifying complementary 2D, 3D, and sequence-level information, E3MMDTA advances DTA prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/xwtxbzz/E3MMDTA.

title: Semantic Pilot Design for Data-Aided Channel Estimation Using A Large Language Model
authors: Sojeong Park, Hyun Jong Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper proposes a semantic pilot design for data-aided channel estimation in text-inclusive data transmission, using a large language model (LLM). In this scenario, channel impairments often appear as typographical errors in the decoded text, which can be corrected using an LLM. The proposed method compares the initially decoded text with the LLM-corrected version to identify reliable decoded symbols. A set of selected symbols, referred to as a semantic pilot, is used as an additional pilot for data-aided channel estimation. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to leverage semantic information for reliable symbol selection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme outperforms conventional pilot-only estimation, achieving lower normalized mean squared error and phase error of the estimated channel, as well as reduced bit error rate.

title: Seaf: Semantic-Aware Frame Selection for Long-form Video Understanding
authors: Mengyu Yang, Ye Tian, Lanshan Zhang, Deli Yu, å Peizhuang Cong, Wendong Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: While recent advances in multimodal large language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in image-language reasoning tasks, their performance is often hampered when applied to long videos. Traditional frame selection methods mainly rely on uniform sampling, which is inherently inflexible. In this work, we propose Seaf, a semantic-aware frame sampling framework designed specifically for long-form video understanding via MLLMs. Unlike traditional uniform sampling methods, Seaf adaptively selects key frames that are relevant to the question from the entire video sequence. Notably, our method is fully training-free and parameter-free, making it highly compatible with any existing MLLM architecture as a plug-and-play module. Extensive experiments on multiple MLLMs demonstrate the effectiveness of our Seaf in achieving effective and scalable comprehension of long-form videos.

title: Active Jammer Localization via Acquisition-Aware Path Planning
authors: Luis González-Gudiño, Mariona Jaramillo-Civill, Pau Closas, Tales Imbiriba
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose an active jammer localization framework that combines Bayesian optimization with acquisition-aware path planning. Unlike passive crowdsourced methods, our approach adaptively guides a mobile agent to collect high-utility Received Signal Strength measurements while accounting for urban obstacles and mobility constraints. For this, we modified the A* algorithm, A-UCB*, by incorporating acquisition values into trajectory costs, leading to high-acquisition planned paths. Simulations on realistic urban scenarios show that the proposed method achieves accurate localization with fewer measurements compared to uninformed baselines, demonstrating consistent performance under different environments.

title: CFA: Lightweight Defense Against Membership Inference Attacks Through Class-Wise Feature Aggregation
authors: Ao Dong, Zhihao Li, Yifei Tian, Xiaobai Chen, Jieming Yin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As membership inference attacks increasingly threaten the privacy of machine learning models, existing defenses either lack formal guarantees or incur significant utility and computational costs. We propose Class-wise Feature Aggregation (CFA), a novel and lightweight privacy-preserving mechanism that shifts the defense perspective from gradient space to feature space. CFA performs per-sample normalization, class-wise aggregation, and Gaussian noise injection, offering strong privacy guarantees under Rényi Differential Privacy while maintaining high utility. Unlike prior certified approaches such as DP-SGD, CFA avoids expensive per-example gradient computations and integrates seamlessly into standard training pipelines without architectural modifications. CFA achieves accuracy within 1-5% of non-private models (versus DP-SGD’s 15-50% drops) without increasing training time. By outperforming empirical defenses and establishing feature-space perturbation as a deployable solution, CFA bridges the gap between theoretical privacy guarantees and practical deployment requirements.

title: Following the TRACE: A Structured Path to Empathetic Response Generation with Multi-Agent Models
authors: Ziqi Liu, Ziyang Zhou, Yilin Li, Haiyang Zhang, Yangbin Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Empathetic response generation is a crucial task for creating more human-like and supportive conversational agents. However, existing methods face a core trade-off between the analytical depth of specialized models and the generative fluency of Large Language Models (LLMs). To address this, we propose TRACE, Task-decomposed Reasoning for Affective Communication and Empathy, a novel framework that models empathy as a structured cognitive process by decomposing the task into a pipeline for analysis and synthesis. By building a comprehensive understanding before generation, TRACE unites deep analysis with expressive generation. Experimental results show that our framework significantly outperforms strong baselines in both automatic and LLM-based evaluations, confirming that our structured decomposition is a promising paradigm for creating more capable and interpretable empathetic agents. Our code is available at https://github.com/sunbus100/TRACE.

title: A Dynamic Gated Cross-Attention Framework for Audio-Text Apparent Personality Analysis
authors: Yunan Li, Zixiang Lu, Yang Ma, Haozhe Bu, Zhuoqi Ma, Qiguang Miao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Apparent Personality Analysis (APA) seeks to infer intrinsic traits from external behaviors, but aligning heterogeneous modalities such as audio and text remains a major challenge. Existing methods often rely on simple concatenation or weighted fusion, which overlooks deep semantic relations and fails to dynamically adapt to modality-specific contributions. To address these limitations, we propose a novel audio-text data-based personality analysis framework based on Dynamic Gated Cross-Attention (DGCA). We first encode the audio and text data with a residual network enhanced with attention and RoBERTa, respectively. Then, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism models fine-grained interactions between audio and text, while a dynamic gating module adaptively regulates modality contributions, suppressing noise and preserving complementary signals. Finally, a regression head predicts Big-Five personality traits from the fused representation. Experimental evaluations on the ChaLearn First Impressions V2 dataset demonstrate that our framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its potential as a robust and interpretable solution for multimodal personality analysis.

title: Knowledge Distillation via Generative Reconstruction Pathways for End-to-End Automatic Speech Recognition
authors: Sang-Heon Jeong, Dong-Hyun Kim, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: End-to-end automatic speech recognition (ASR) has achieved remarkable progress, with encoder-only models based on the connectionist temporal classification (CTC) objective being widely adopted for their efficiency and simplicity. However, the large model size of state-of-the-art systems remains a major obstacle for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge distillation (KD) provides a promising solution to compress ASR models while preserving accuracy, but most existing approaches rely on point-wise feature alignment losses, which bias the student toward reproducing local correspondences of teacher features. In this work, we propose a generative reconstruction pathway knowledge distillation (GRP-KD) framework that leverages flow matching and diffusion processes to achieve richer knowledge transfer. By treating teacher features as empirical distributions, GRP-KD formulates distillation as a generative reconstruction task that drives the student to approximate the distributional structure of teacher representations. Experiments on the LibriSpeech and GigaSpeech datasets demonstrate that GRP-KD consistently outperforms feature alignment–based KD while retaining a compact parameter footprint.

title: PFE-Net: Proxy-Guided Frequency Enhancement for Camouflaged Object Detection
authors: Tianzhen Dong, YouKang Tu, Chenyang Xu, Junjie Wang, Wei He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: To address the challenge of detecting camouflaged objects due to their complexity and diversity, we propose a camouflaged object detection method based on enhanced image generation, named PFE-Net. We first employ a novel conditional diffusion sub-network termed ReVisionNet(RVN), designed to fuse depth and RGB information for generating proxy images that reveal underlying structures. Next, we introduce the FreqBoost Module(FBM), which fuses the structural information of the original image with the texture and color features from the proxy image using an adaptive frequency mask in the frequency domain, to produce enhanced images. Finally, PixelFuse Net(PFN) integrates RGB-D fused features from Conditional Fusion Net and multi-scale features from enhanced images, and effective feature aggregation is performed using our proposed Pixel Guided Attention Fusion (PGAF) module. Extensive experiments demonstrate PFE-Net’s superior performance across multiple benchmark datasets, validating its effectiveness and superiority.

title: Interpretable Churn Prediction in Motor Liability Insurance: A Comparative Analysis of Ensemble Methods and SHAP Explanations
authors: Ajla Ćerimagić Hasibović, Muhamed Hasić, Anel Tanović
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Although motor third-party liability insurance is mandatory in most countries, it is very difficult to retain customers with a single insurance company. Retaining clients is crucial, as acquiring new ones is very expensive and doing business with new clients is always risky, whereas retaining existing clients allows insurance companies to focus their resources on other objectives. To retain customers, we use machine learning (ML) algorithms. Although advanced ML algorithms have demonstrated superior predictive performance compared to traditional actuarial methods, their lack of transparency (“black box”) hinders their use in regulatory environments that require explainability. This paper proposes a hybrid churn prediction framework that balances high accuracy with interpretability. We use ensemble learning techniques, specifically CatBoost, trained on a real dataset of automobile liability policies. To address the problem of class imbalance, typical in insurance data, we use the synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE). The main contribution of this work is the integration of the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method for deconstructing model predictions. This approach provides global insight into key churn drivers – such as premium fluctuations and vehicle age – and local explanations at the individual policy level. Experimental results show that the proposed model achieves a higher AUC-ROC score than basic logistic regression models. Furthermore, the use of XAI (Explainable AI) enables insurers to develop targeted retention strategies while adhering to transparency standards.

title: A Comparative Study of Environmental Sensing for Ambient Intelligence with Distributed Sensor Networks and Mobile Robots
authors: Miloš Radović, Ivana Šenk
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Ambient Intelligence (AmI) systems depend on reliable environmental sensing to support comfort, safety, and energy efficiency in indoor spaces. This paper reviews three sensing paradigms for monitoring indoor environmental conditions: distributed wireless sensor networks (WSN), mobile robots employed as sensing platforms, and hybrid architectures that integrate static and mobile sensing. Typical system architectures and operational characteristics are examined, with emphasis on the trade-offs between continuous temporal monitoring at fixed locations and adaptive spatial mapping enabled by mobility. Based on this analysis, a decision framework is introduced that defines practical selection criteria including spatial and temporal requirements, cost (CAPEX/OPEX), reliability, safety, privacy, scalability, and infrastructure constraints, and illustrates how these criteria guide the choice between WSN-based, robot-based, and hybrid solutions. Finally, directions for future research are outlined, focusing on quantitative validation and software-supported methods for automated sensing architecture selection in AmI systems.

title: A Dataset of Robot-Patient and Doctor-Patient Medical Dialogues for Spoken Language Processing Tasks
authors: Heriberto Cuayáhuitl, Grace Jang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have brought huge improvements to Artificial Intelligence (AI), which can be applied to general-purpose tasks. However, their application to textual or spoken medical consultations is still an open research problem. This paper proposes MeDial-Speech, a novel speech dataset for training and evaluating Med-AIs that can carry out consultations with patients. It was collected in realistic environments from robot-patient and doctor-patient dialogues, contains 111+ hours of speech data (without data augmentation), and covers four health conditions: Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, and angina. In addition, we propose a dialogue benchmark via sentence selection (with 20 options) to evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, and Claude Sonnet 4. Experimental results reveal that Claude Sonnet 4 is the best in sentence selection, with 71.1% accuracy using manual transcriptions and 74.7% using automatic transcriptions, and that all LLMs are highly overconfident in their probabilistic predictions, regardless of selecting correct or incorrect sentences in medical dialogues. This dataset is free of charge for non-commercial purposes.

title: Diffusion Bridges with Dual Conditioning and Trajectory Consistency for Multi-Contrast MRI Translation
authors: Xiaobin Pei, Rui Ding, Zhenhuan Zeng, Xianhong Wen, Kehua Guo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion bridges have recently shown promise for multi-contrast MRI translation by explicitly modeling transformations between source and target modalities. However, existing methods independently predict each timestep without effectively utilizing intermediate predictions, causing temporal inconsistency and reduced structural fidelity. To address this, we propose dual conditioning and trajectory consistency strategies. Dual conditioning jointly leverages external conditioning from the source modality and self-conditioning from intermediate predictions via a two-pass forward scheme, while trajectory consistency further improves temporal coherence across adjacent timesteps via an on-policy replay mechanism. Experiments on IXI and BraTS datasets demonstrate that our method achieves consistent improvements in structural fidelity and effectively reduces inference drift.

title: Split Learning Over NOMA-Enabled HetNets for Scalable IoT-Based Precision Agriculture
authors: Mohammad Arif Hossain, Nazmus Sadat, Nirwan Ansari, Fathi Amsaad
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: The convergence of artificial intelligence (AI) and precision agriculture is driving the rapid deployment of machine learning (ML) models within Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems. However, traditional centralized ML frameworks pose significant challenges in energy efficiency, communication overhead, and real-time responsiveness, especially in remote agricultural environments. To address these limitations, we propose a novel hierarchical split learning framework enabled by non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and deployed over a heterogeneous network (HetNet) topology. In the proposed system, each geographically distributed low-power edge node equipped with a nano-GPU executes a segment of a common deep neural network (DNN) on locally collected agricultural data and transmits intermediate activations to a central aggregator edge node (AEN), which completes the remaining layers and coordinates the backpropagation process. NOMA enables simultaneous data uploads from multiple sensors over shared frequency resources, improving spectral efficiency and supporting scalability. HetNet-based zone segmentation further enhances resource reuse and system coverage. We formulate a comprehensive optimization problem aimed at minimizing total system energy consumption, accounting for both local computation and wireless communication. To solve it, we develop an efficient distributed solution with proven convergence. Extensive simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in reducing energy consumption and transmission delay while maintaining high model accuracy, establishing it as a scalable and sustainable solution for intelligent agricultural systems.

title: Sparse Gradient Compression for Fine-Tuning Large Language Models
authors: David H. Yang, Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri, Tejaswini Pedapati, Subhajit Chaudhury, Pin-Yu Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for downstream tasks has become increasingly crucial due to their widespread use and the growing availability of open-source models. However, the high memory costs associated with fine-tuning remain a significant challenge, especially as models increase in size. To address this, parameter efficient fine-tuning methods have been proposed to minimize the number of parameters required for fine-tuning LLMs. However, these approaches often tie the number of optimizer states to dimensions of model parameters, limiting flexibility and control during fine-tuning. In this paper, we propose sparse gradient compression (SGC), a training regime that leverages inherent sparsity in gradients to compress optimizer states by projecting them onto a low-dimensonal subspace, with dimensionality independent of the original model’s parameters. By enabling optimizer state updates in an arbitrary low-dimensional subspace, SGC offers a flexible tradeoff between memory efficiency and performance. By fine-tuning LLMs on downstream tasks, we show that SGC can deliver superior performance while substantially lowering optimizer state memory requirements, particularly in both data-limited and memory-limited settings.

title: Chunkwise Aligners for Streaming Speech Recognition
authors: Wen Shen Teo, Takafumi Moriya, Masato Mimura
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose the Chunkwise Aligner, a novel architecture for streaming automatic speech recognition (ASR). While the Transducer is the standard model for streaming ASR, its training is costly due to the need to compute all possible audio-label alignments. The recently introduced Aligner reduces this cost by discarding explicit alignments, but this modification makes it unsuitable for streaming. Our approach overcomes this limitation by dividing the audio into chunks and aligning each label to the leftmost frames of its chunk, whereas transitions between chunks are managed by a learned end-of-chunk probability. Experiments show that the Chunkwise Aligner not only matches the Transducer’s accuracy in both offline and streaming scenarios, but also offers superior training and decoding efficiencies.
