Experiments on two advanced neural speech codecs, Encodec and WavTokenizer, demonstrate that the proposed strategy substantially improves robustness under noisy conditions—for example, boosting UTMOS from 3.475 to 3.586 at 15 dB SNR on Encodec—while also enhancing coding quality for clean speech.

title: Super-Resolution Diffusion Model for Explainable Microscopic Peripheral Blood Cell Image Synthesis
authors: Kar-Ee Hoh, Hwa Hui Tew, Junn-Yong Loo, Raphaël C.-W. Phan, Chee-Ming Ting
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Microscopic imaging of diseased cells is essential for medical diagnosis and the study of hematological conditions. Generative models, including Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and diffusion models, have achieved impressive performance in the medical image synthesis. However, assessing the quality and explainability of images generated by these models remains challenging due to the complexity and opacity of deep learning (DL) architectures. Despite their high accuracy, these models are often regarded as black-boxes, limiting their clinical trustworthiness and adoption. In this work, we propose a super-resolution diffusion model that generates highly realistic synthetic blood cell images on the BloodMNIST dataset, achieving a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 3.57 and a Kernel Inception Distance (KID) of 0.01. Moreover, using synthetic images generated by our model for data augmentation improves blood cell classification accuracy compared to training on real data alone. To enhance explainability, we incorporate explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques such as Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), Integrated Gradients (IG), and GradientSHAP (GS) to analyze classification decisions on real and synthetic images. This integration enhances the transparency and practical applicability of generative models in digital hematology.

title: Are Modern Speech Enhancement Systems Vulnerable to Adversarial Attacks?
authors: Rostislav Makarov, Lea Schönherr, Timo Gerkmann
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Machine learning approaches for speech enhancement are becoming increasingly expressive, enabling ever more powerful modifications of input signals. In this paper, we demonstrate that this expressiveness introduces a vulnerability: advanced speech enhancement models can be susceptible to adversarial attacks. Specifically, we show that adversarial noise, carefully crafted and psychoacoustically masked by the original input, can be injected such that the enhanced speech output conveys an entirely different semantic meaning. We experimentally verify that contemporary predictive speech enhancement models can indeed be manipulated in this way. Furthermore, we highlight that diffusion models with stochastic samplers exhibit inherent robustness to such adversarial attacks by design.

title: PHomo: Patch Homogeneity for No-Reference Inpainting Assessment and Verification
authors: Jianfu Zhang, Yan Hong, Jiahui Zhan, Jun Lan, Liqing Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generative models advance creativity but also raise security and privacy risks, particularly from regional forgeries created via inpainting when reference images are unavailable. We introduce PHomo, an efficient no-reference method that evaluates inpainting image quality by measuring local patch homogeneity without relying on ground truth or mask information. Our approach samples patches, extracts feature representations with pretrained encoders, and computes similarity-based internal homogeneity. High homogeneity reflects seamless blending between masked and unmasked regions, while low values reveal structural or semantic inconsistencies. By quantifying local feature coherence, PHomo enables robust detection of anomalous inpainted areas and provides a practical metric for assessing inpainting image/model quality in real-world scenarios.

title: Low-Channel EMG Gesture Recognition via Sparse-To-Dense Representation Learning
authors: Kyun Kyu Kim, Zhenan Bao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electromyography (EMG) has recently emerged as a promising modality for wearable gesture interfaces. However, wearable EMG devices face an inherent trade-off between user comfort and the need for large-area sensing for high-resolution data collection. We propose a self-supervised representation learning framework, trained on a 32-channel dataset and enabling lower channel device to leverage the pre-trained muscle activity correlations. In addition, we incorporate a meta-learning strategy with channel permutation, which improves robustness to electrode placement shifts and allows rapid adaptation with only a few fine-tuning cycles. Connected with downstream classifiers, the model achieved comparable performance on American Sign Language Translation with reduced sensor count.

title: Adaptive Distillation For LM-GNN Alignment in Semi-Supervised Text-Attributed Graph Node Classification
authors: Xingyu Wu, Jintao Chen, Xuanye Zheng, Chenkai Pan, Yuxiang Cai, Sun Bing, Ge Su, Shengye Pang, Xuhong Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Node classification on Text-Attributed Graphs is a critical task with a wide range of applications, especially in semi-supervised settings where labeled data is often scarce. Inspired by the recent success of Language Models (LMs), integrating Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and LMs has become a leading paradigm, especially the mutual knowledge distillation alignment strategy. However, we identify a key limitation early alignment degradation: where GNN’s accuracy unexpectedly drops during alignment training. We attribute this issue to a premature, forceful alignment with an LM that has not yet fully adapted to the graph context. To address this challenge, we introduce ADAPT-GLEM, a novel framework featuring two key components: (1) a macro-level warm-up weighting strategy that gradually phases in the distillation for a smoother knowledge transfer. (2) a node-level distillation controller that dynamically directs alignment using a novel class separability metric to leverage the most reliable model for each node. Extensive experiments show that ADAPT-GLEM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Graph-LM/LLM methods.

title: AI-Enhanced Stock Screening System for Multi-Bagger Identification Using Financial and Market Indicators
authors: Muhamed Rafi, G T Sasetharan, Al Thasni C, Soumya. B, Althaf V T
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The paper will outline a new AI-enhanced stock screening tool to find possible multi-bagger stocks using the combination of financial and market-indicator-driven approach. The offered system utilizes an integrated machine-learning pipeline that combines a set of standard measures, including revenue growth, profitability ratios, cash-flow stability, and debt efficiency, and technical features, such as trend strength, volatility changes, and momentum signals. A feature-engineering layer is added that converts the raw financial statements and market data into predictive variables that are best suited to detect long-term values. The model also has an adaptive weighting mechanism that dynamically changes the weighting of indicators depending on the changing market conditions. The system predicts better and reduces false positives by training gradient-boosting algorithms and ensemble learning on multi-bagger forecasting. Multi-sector historical back-testing shows that the discovery of stocks at their early-stage is significantly enhanced using the machine learning approach as compared to the conventional screening heuristics. The findings confirm the fact that AI-based hybrid analysis has a significant positive impact on the reliability, speed, and accuracy of stocks with high long-term compounding potential.

title: Scale-Aware Self-Supervised Learning for Segmentation of Small and Sparse Structures
authors: Jorge Quesada, Ghassan AlRegib
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful strategy for representation learning under limited annotation regimes, yet its effectiveness remains highly sensitive to many factors, especially the nature of the target task. In segmentation, existing pipelines are typically tuned to large, homogeneous regions, but their performance drops when objects are small, sparse, or locally irregular. In this work, we propose a scale-aware SSL adaptation that integrates small-window cropping into the augmentation pipeline, zooming in on fine-scale structures during pretraining. We evaluate this approach across two domains with markedly different data modalities: seismic imaging, where the goal is to segment sparse faults, and neuroimaging, where the task is to delineate small cellular structures. In both settings, our method yields consistent improvements over standard and state-of-the-art baselines under label constraints, improving accuracy by up to 13% for fault segmentation and 5% for cell delineation. In contrast, large-scale features such as seismic facies or tissue regions see little benefit, underscoring that the value of SSL depends critically on the scale of the target objects. Our findings highlight the need to align SSL design with object size and sparsity, offering a general principle for buil ding more effective representation learning pipelines across scientific imaging domains.

title: Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks Via Subsampling Stability
authors: Yexin Zhang, Zhongtian Ma, Qiaosheng Zhang, Zhen Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study differential privacy (DP) in Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) through the framework of subsampling stability. We derive upper bounds on the misclassification rate that depend explicitly on the subsampling probability p<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">s</inf>. Furthermore, we characterize the privacy–utility trade-off by identifying feasible ranges of p<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">s</inf>; if p<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">s</inf> is too small, the stability-based privacy condition becomes difficult to satisfy , yielding vacuous guarantees, whereas if it is too large, accuracy deteriorates. Our results provide the first rigorous theoretical framework for understanding subsampling stability in GCNs under DP.

title: Uncertainty-Based Ensemble Learning in CMR Semantic Segmentation
authors: Yiwei Liu, Liang Zhong, Lingyi Wen, Yuankai Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing methods derive clinical functional metrics from ventricular semantic segmentation in cardiac cine sequences. While performing well on overall segmentation, they struggle with the end slices. To address this, we extract global uncertainty from segmentation variance and use it in our ensemble learning method, Streaming, for classifier weighting, balancing overall and end-slice performance. We introduce the End Coefficient (EC) to quantify end-slice accuracy. Experiments on ACDC and M&Ms datasets show that our framework achieves near state-of-the-art Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and outperforms all models on end-slice performance, improving patient-specific segmentation accuracy. We open-sourced our code on https://github.com/LEw1sin/Uncertainty-Ensemble.

title: Design and Experimental Verification of a Four-Bar Separation Mechanism
authors: Licheng Liu, Shaohua Wang, Kang Gong, Tiejun Wang, Jinkui Gu, Haoran Jia, Gang Wang
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This paper addresses the requirement for precise payload deployment during deorbiting missions by designing a reusable four-bar linkage separation mechanism. It first outlines the system configuration, including core components such as the mounting structure, linkage mechanism, unlocking mechanism, and locking mechanism. Subsequently, it details the operational principles across three phases: initial state, payload loading, and payload deployment. Key parameters were determined through structural design. ADAMS kinematic analysis verifies that the mechanism can withstand maximum flight loads except for isolated components. Manual functional tests and payload release tests confirm normal locking/unlocking operations with execution times <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 10$</tex> milliseconds and no damage. This mechanism provides a reference design for similar separation devices.

title: Thinking In a Crowd: How Auxiliary Information Shapes LLM Reasoning
authors: Haodong Zhao, Chenyan Zhao, Yansi Li, Zhuosheng Zhang, Gongshen Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason is fundamental to their application in complex, knowledge-intensive domains. In real-world scenarios, LLMs are often augmented with external information that can be helpful, irrelevant, or even misleading. This paper investigates the causal impact of this auxiliary information on the reasoning process of LLMs with explicit step-by-step thinking capabilities. We introduce SciAux, a new dataset derived from ScienceQA, to systematically test the robustness of the model against these types of information. Our findings reveal a critical vulnerability: the model’s deliberative "thinking mode" is a double-edged sword. While helpful context improves accuracy, misleading information causes a catastrophic drop in performance, which is amplified by the thinking process. Instead of conferring robustness, thinking reinforces the degree of error when provided with misinformation. This highlights that the challenge is not merely to make models "think", but to endow them with the critical faculty to evaluate the information upon which their reasoning is based. The SciAux dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/billhdzhao/SciAux.

title: [ACVLAB] Solution for ICASSP 2026 Hyper-Object Challenge: Track 1
authors: Jen-Wei Lee, Bang-Kang Chen, Ting-Yao Chen, Chao-Lin Chang, Chia-Ming Lee, Chih-Chung Hsu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recovering hyperspectral images (HSI) from mosaic sensors is a challenging ill-posed problem. We present our 3rd place solution for the ICASSP 2026 Hyper-Object Challenge (Track 1)[1]. We reconstruct 1024 × 1024 × 61 HSIs from 1024 × 1024 × 1 RGGB mosaic inputs. Our pipeline decomposes the mosaic into a 512 × 512 × 4 tensor, processes it with an adapted MST++ backbone, and restores spatial resolution via PixelShuffle. Evaluated by the Spectral-Spatial-Color (SSC) score (incorporating SAM, SID, ERGAS, PSNR, SSIM, and ∆E00), team ACVLAB achieved a final score of 0.61226.

title: A Hybrid Heuristic Spectral Efficient Chunk Allocation, Relay Selection, and Deep Learning-Empowered Energy-Efficient Power Control in Multi-user OFDMA-PNC
authors: Ehsan Atefat Doost, Jonathan Rodriguez, Mário Antunes, Pablo Fondo-Ferreiro, Antonio Navarro
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this paper, we study joint spectral- and energy-efficient resource allocation in a multi-user, multi-relay orthogonal frequency-division multiple access physical-layer network coding (OFDMA-PNC) two-way relay network, where paired user equipments communicate via decode-and-forward relay nodes under global energy efficiency (GEE) maximization. The underlying problem couples chunk-level assignment, relay selection, and subcarrier-level power control into a non-convex mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) formulation with binary allocation variables and continuous power variables, which is computationally prohibitive to solve optimally at practical network scales. To address this challenge, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs a heuristic joint chunk allocation and relay selection based on a three-dimensional tensor of quality-of-service (QoS)-filtered active subcarriers and then applies a supervised deep neural network (DNN) to approximate the optimal power allocation generated by a fractional programming–based solver. The heuristic algorithm operates in a reduced two-dimensional search space with collision-avoidance rules that ensure feasibility with respect to chunk and relay constraints, while the DNN-based power control module is trained on labeled samples and uses a projection step to preserve all node-wise power and QoS constraints at inference time. Simulation results for a single-cell scenario with 8 user pairs and 8 relays show that the proposed joint heuristic scheme achieves spectral efficiency close to an mixed-integer linear programming (MILP)-based alternating-optimization (AO) benchmark and significantly higher than a purely greedy heuristic baseline, while the DNN-based power control attains near-optimal energy efficiency with an acceleration rate of about 15× compared to iterative optimization. Overall, the combined heuristic–DNN framework reduces computational complexity by approximately 9.94×10⁴ relative to an exact AO-based method and about 3× relative to the heuristic AO scheme, providing a scalable and real-time-capable solution for energy-efficient resource allocation in large-scale OFDMA-based decode-and-forward (DF)-PNC systems.

title: FairCLIP: Bridging Visual Diversity and Linguistic Representation for Equitable Image Synthesis
authors: Xinxing Li, Guangyong Zheng, Jiacheng Liu, Wenli Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Ensuring fairness in text-to-image generation remains a challenging problem. Existing solutions often rely on costly retraining, large attribute-balanced datasets, or natural-language prompt interventions that remain vulnerable to linguistic ambiguity. To address these limitations, we propose FairCLIP, a plug-and-play, visually guided prompt-learning framework that uses reference images to learn lightweight attribute-aware prompt embeddings, termed FairTokens, in a pretrained multimodal embedding space. FairTokens are optimized with three complementary objectives—direction alignment, semantic consistency, and a generation-time fairness regularizer—to provide visually grounded attribute control while preserving the semantics of the original prompt. Experiments on CelebA and FairFace indicate that FairCLIP improves attribute balance in many evaluated single- and multi-attribute settings relative to text-only baselines while maintaining competitive image quality under standard metrics such as FID. We further analyze residual failure cases and discuss the limitations of perceived-attribute benchmarks and the KL-divergence proxy used for distributional fairness assessment. Overall, FairCLIP provides a lightweight representation-level approach for mitigating certain distributional skews in generated samples, while highlighting the need for broader evaluation and deployment-aware fairness analysis.

title: Assessment of modelling fidelity for predicting transmission error in dynamically coupled spur gear systems
authors: Guruparan Pitchai, Lakshmi Pathi Jakkamputi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In the present study, the transmission error (TE) behaviour of an identical involute spur gear pair dynamically coupled with an attached subsystem is investigated through numerical modelling. The surrounding mechanical system is represented by an equivalent spring–mass subsystem, and the transmission error response is examined over a range of operating conditions associated with different resonance regimes. Two finite element–based modelling approaches are considered to describe the local elastic deformations occurring at the gear mesh. In the fully coupled finite element formulation, the interaction between meshing gear teeth is described using nonlinear contact conditions implemented through an augmented Lagrangian approach, in which tooth contact behaviour, elastic deformation, and subsystem dynamics are treated simultaneously. In the reduced hybrid finite element formulation, elastic deformations arising from tooth bending and contact compliance are evaluated separately under quasi-static equilibrium and subsequently combined to estimate the overall mesh deformation and transmission error. Quasi-static analyses are conducted to assess the accuracy of both modelling approaches through comparison with published benchmark results at multiple load levels. Dynamic simulations based on the fully coupled formulation are then used to investigate transmission error behaviour in quasi-static, near-resonant, resonant, and post-resonant operating regimes of the attached subsystem. The effect of the subsystem resonance on the amplitude and harmonic content of transmission error is examined. The results indicate that subsystem dynamics have a pronounced influence on transmission error behaviour, with peak-to-peak values increasing by up to approximately 70% at the fundamental resonance of the attached subsystem and with associated changes in harmonic content that persist beyond the resonance region. The modelling approaches are compared in terms of predictive capability across resonance regimes, computational effort, and relevance to automotive gear transmission applications.

title: A Survey on Spiking Neural Network Foundation And Recent Progress
authors: Nhan Trong Luu, Duong Trung Luu, Pham Ngoc Nam, Truong Cong Thang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Over the past decade, deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable success across numerous fields. Despite this progress, DNNs remain highly demanding in terms of computational power, energy consumption, and data requirements. As the demand for autonomous systems such as self-driving cars, drones, and collaborative robots, etc. continues to rise, the deployment of DNNs in these real-world scenarios has gained significant attention. However, these applications require energy-efficient and computationally lightweight models to ensure real-time performance under limited power resources. A promising alternative has emerged in the form of biologically inspired spiking neural networks (SNNs), which offer a more energy-efficient and temporally dynamic computing paradigm. By mimicking the behavior of biological neurons, SNNs provide a bridge between neuroscience and machine learning, leveraging neural sparsity and temporal coding mechanisms inherent to biological systems. In this work, we make the following contributions: provide a comprehensive overview of biological neuron theories; summarize existing spike-based neuron models from neuroscience; describe synaptic modeling approaches; review conventional artificial neural networks; outline several methodologies for preprocessing, training and conversion approaches for spike-based models; explore SNN applications in various fields; discuss major frameworks supporting SNN implementation.

title: Medium-Frequency Transformer for SST Applications: Design and Optimization
authors: Reza Mirzadarani, Leonardo Bolzonella, Zhengzhao Li, Zian Qin, Peter Vaessen, Pavol Bauer, Mohamad Ghaffarian Niasar
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: This work presents a down-scaled validation of a medium-voltage, medium-frequency transformer (MFT) concept designed for high-current operation on the secondary side using multiple parallel paths. The design is based on a modular winding approach, which simplifies the construction process and conductor placement on the bobbin. A systematic design and optimization procedure is developed, combining analytical calculations and finite-element simulations to explore the mass-efficiency trade-off and to select a candidate design that meets specified leakage inductance and loss targets. The developed prototype serves as a proof of concept, demonstrating that the electrical, magnetic, and insulation requirements of the full-scale MFT can be effectively verified at reduced power levels. The fabricated prototype is tested under short-circuit and partial discharge conditions. The impedance measurements confirmed the expected resonance behavior, and the partial discharge test results verified sufficient insulation performance under high-voltage stress. The results provide experimental evidence for the scalability and feasibility of the proposed transformer design and offer guidelines for the use of 3D-printed supports, grain-oriented electrical steel cores, and windings in medium-voltage, medium-frequency transformer systems for hydrogen production applications.

title: LearningFlow: Automated Policy Learning Workflow for Urban Driving with Large Language Models
authors: Zengqi Peng, Yubin Wang, Xu Han, Lei Zheng, Jun Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Recent advancements in reinforcement learning (RL) demonstrate the significant potential in autonomous driving. Despite this promise, challenges such as the manual design of reward functions and low sample efficiency in complex environments continue to impede the development of safe and effective driving policies. To tackle these issues, we introduce LearningFlow, an innovative automated policy learning workflow tailored to urban driving. This framework leverages the collaboration of multiple large language model (LLM) agents throughout the RL training process. LearningFlow includes a curriculum sequence generation process and a reward generation process, which work in tandem to guide the RL policy by generating tailored training curricula and reward functions. Particularly, each process is supported by an analysis agent that evaluates training progress and provides critical insights to the generation agent. Through the collaborative efforts of these LLM agents, LearningFlow automates policy learning across a series of complex driving tasks, and it significantly reduces the reliance on manual reward function design while enhancing sample efficiency. Comprehensive experiments are conducted in the high-fidelity CARLA simulator, along with comparisons with other existing methods, to demonstrate the efficacy of our proposed approach. The results demonstrate that LearningFlow excels in generating rewards and curricula. It also achieves superior performance and robust generalization across various driving tasks, as well as commendable adaptation to different RL algorithms.

title: Physics-Informed Deep Gated Recurrent Unit Network for Lithium-Ion Battery State of Health Estimation Under Field Conditions
authors: Laijin Luo, Yu Wang, Yang Li, Qiushi Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Accurate estimation of lithium-ion battery state of health (SOH) in electric vehicles (EVs) under real-world conditions is much more challenging than using well-designed laboratory cycling data due to unreliable SOH labeling and segmented charging behavior, and the results often lack interpretability. To address these issues, this paper proposes a physics-informed deep gated recurrent unit (PIDGRU) architecture for robust and interpretable SOH estimation without requiring explicit physical modeling. First, a modified inverse ampere-hour integral method is combined with the Bayesian estimator of abrupt, seasonality, and trend (BEAST) algorithm to estimate battery capacity and characterize SOH uncertainty. A universal feature extraction and selection framework is then developed to handle segmented EV charging data, utilizing a hybrid linear-nonlinear redundancy analysis to ensure an optimal input feature set. The PIDGRU integrates empirical degradation modeling and nonlinear dynamic degradation learning through a deep gated recurrent unit network, utilizing a deep hidden physics model (DeepHPM). A Bayesian inference uncertainty-constrained (BIUC) strategy is introduced to enhance training reliability and uncertainty quantification. Extensive evaluations on both in-vehicle and cross-vehicle datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high accuracy, robustness, and generalizability, with the mean absolute error and root mean squared error consistently below 1.10% and 1.31%, respectively.

title: Pruned and Quantized Hybrid Models for Edge-Based Automatic Modulation Recognition
authors: Yerin Byeon, Dohyun Kim, Yue Cao, Wansu Lim
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Learning-based automatic modulation recognition on edge devices is envisioned as a critical enabler of real-time spectrum management in future 6G Internet of Things networks. In addition to the requirement of accurate modulation detection, edge devices pose the challenge of reducing the size of the automatic modulation recognition model. This paper proposes a lightweight hybrid model of multi-channel convolutional neural network and the mobile vision transformer. The proposed model addresses both accuracy improvement and model size reduction by employing weight-based pruning and post-training dynamic range quantization. Performance results in edge computing environments such as the Raspberry Pi and Jetson platforms using the RadioML 2016.10a dataset show that the proposed model achieved up to a 91% reduction in memory usage compared to its original version prior to pruning and quantization and demonstrated up to an 8% improvement in average accuracy compared to the baseline convolutional neural network model.

title: Decoding kinematic parameters of the whole arm using spikes from area 2 of primary somatosensory cortex
authors: Parinaz Mohammadi, Matin Asgharpour, Mohammad Reza Daliri
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The development of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) has primarily focused on decoding motor commands from the primary motor cortex (M1) to restore mobility in individuals with severe neuromuscular disabilities. As neuroprosthetic control technologies advance, integrating afferent feedback mechanisms to provide proprioceptive and tactile awareness has become increasingly important. The primary somatosensory cortex (S1), particularly Area 2, serves as a specialized hub for proprioception by integrating multimodal muscle and cutaneous inputs. Although previous studies have demonstrated that Area 2 encodes whole-arm kinematics more effectively than hand-only models, the potential for high-accuracy directional classification to serve as BCI command triggers has not been fully investigated. This study evaluated the efficacy of decoding whole-arm movement directions using spiking activity recorded from 100-electrode arrays implanted in Area 2 of two rhesus macaques (Monkeys C and H) during a center-out reaching task. The experimental paradigm comprised two conditions: active (voluntary) reaches and passive (involuntary) movements induced by a 2N external force. Mean Firing Rates were used as primary features, with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied for dimensionality reduction and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) employed to classify movement directions across four and eight principal axes. Our results demonstrate near-perfect classification accuracies, averaging 99.84% ± 0.53 for active trials and 94.28% ± 6.24 for passive trials across both subjects. Crucially, voluntary movements were decoded significantly more accurately than involuntary movements (p<0.001), supporting the hypothesis that S1 integrates efference-copy signals from motor areas to enhance the estimation of limb state. These findings validate the robust representation of whole-arm kinematics in S1 and establish Area 2 as a vital source of discriminative signals for future closed-loop, afferent neural interfaces.

title: Delayed Backdoor: Let the Trigger Fly for a While in Backdoor Attack on Internet of Things
authors: Bo Liu, Peiwen Zhu, Shaofeng Zhao, Xiang Chen, Haojie Huang, Liling Shi, Xiaokang Wang, Zhigao Zheng, Laurence T. Yang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Since Large Language Models (LLMs) have gained wide attention as the core of AI agents in IoT for conversation and generation tasks, their security issues have become more prominent, especially regarding backdoor attacks. Traditional backdoor attacks often rely on fixed triggers and static outputs, failing to fully exploit the conversational characteristics and generativity of LLMs, which limits their stealth and attack effectiveness in complex human-agent interactions. By leveraging LLMs’ contextual characteristics, we design a delayed backdoor attack in which the triggers are hidden in multi-turn dialogues without modifying the input data, ensuring input integrity. This delayed attack makes the trigger dissociate from poisoned data to enhance stealth and generalization, posing a significant threat to autonomous systems. Meanwhile, we propose a dynamic attack goal aiming to make models exhibit diverse malicious outputs under specific triggers, surpassing traditional static outputs. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms traditional methods, achieving a 20% to 80% performance improvement with a 5% poisoning rate and demonstrating stronger robustness under defense mechanisms, highlighting a critical vulnerability in the security of AI agents for IoT.

title: Analyzing and Characterizing the Split-Transformer LLC Converters
authors: Jinheng Dong, Hongjun Wang, Cheng Cheng, Shaojun Xie, Haichun Liu, Wenming Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: An LLC converter with non-equal-turns-ratios split-transformer (NET-ST) has been proposed to address the large rectified current pulsation in the conventional LLC converters. However, due to the complex resonant processes, the conventional fundamental harmonic approximation is not applicable for analyzing this converter and there is a lack of guidance on designing the NET-ST LLC converter. A data fitting analysis method based on simulation data is proposed to investigate the influence of the parameter KN (defined as the ratio of two transformers turns ratios, i.e., KN = n1/n2) on characterizing the NET-ST LLC converter. This method establishes the quantitative relationship between the parameter KN and both the current pulsation and total losses, and reveals the underlying design principles along with the optimal range for KN. The analysis and experimental results of a 1600W, 400V-input/400V-output prototype demonstrate that the NET-ST design reduces the current pulsation by approximately 40% and losses by about 10% compared to the conventional design. The proposed methodology for analyzing and designing the NET-ST LLC converters has also been promoted to design a split-transformer (ST) LLC named NEI-ST with equal-turns-ratios but non-equal magnetizing inductance. The demonstrated designs validate the effectiveness of presented method and show the appliable of the ST LLC converters.

title: Optimal Fractional-Order Terminal Synergetic Controller for Grid-Connected DFIG-based Wind-PV Hybrid Energy Conversion Systems
authors: Cheyma Aribi, Messaouda Azzouzi, Motab Turki Almousa, Hegazy Rezk
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Hybrid renewable energy systems (HRESs) require advanced control strategies to ensure grid stability and reliable integration under variable operating conditions. Although Conventional Synergetic Control (SC) is an effective nonlinear controller, it often lacks robustness and adaptability. This limitation arises from fixed, manually tuned parameters, limited dynamic flexibility, and suboptimal performance under parametric uncertainties and external disturbances. This paper proposes a Fractional-Order Terminal Synergetic Controller (FOTSC) for both the rotor-side and grid-side converters of a doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG) in a hybrid wind-photovoltaic (PV) system, where the PV system acts as a supplementary source. The controller parameters are optimally tuned using an Enzyme Activation Optimizer (EAO), which minimizes a formulated objective function based on the Integral of Time multiplied by the Absolute Error (ITAE) criterion to systematically balance the transient response and steady-state accuracy. The FOTSC introduces fractional-order dynamics and terminal attractors to ensure finite-time convergence, whereas EAO optimization enhances the robustness and consistency across operating conditions. MATLAB/Simulink simulation results highlight that the optimized FOTSC (OFOTSC) outperforms both the manually-tuned FOTSC and Conventional SC, reducing the overshoot in active power, reactive power, rotor currents, DC-link voltage, and grid currents by up to 58%, and shortening the settling times by up to 43%. In terms of power quality, the OFOTSC achieves a Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of 1.37% for the grid-injected currents, which is well below the IEEE 519-2014 standard limit of 5%. This represents a significant reduction of 84% compared to the Conventional SC (THD = 8.75%) and 67% compared to the manually-tuned FOTSC (THD = 4.14%). These findings confirm that the EAO-tuned FOTSC significantly enhances the stability, power quality, and robustness of DFIG-based HRES.

title: A Keypoint-Based Approach to Estimating Spinal Curvature from 2D Images
authors: Rafał Wysocki, Aleksandra Świetlicka
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Pose estimation is one of the most frequently discussed topics in the field of machine vision. Nevertheless, there are not enough models that effectively solve problems related to spinal detection and assessment. The subject of this project is the development and evaluation of a system for the automatic detection of spinal curvature, followed by the use of the obtained model to detect abnormalities. The system is dedicated to strength training; nonetheless, it could serve as a supportive tool for individuals engaged in other physical exercises. The assessment is based on a keypoint estimation model, with points selected to provide the most informative representation while maintaining a minimal model structure. In this research, several model variants are presented, ranging from lightweight networks to advanced architectures. The results obtained allow us to conclude that the models created for the project meet the requirements set for them in terms of both stability and effectiveness. The models presented in this publication are suitable for further development and wider use in areas such as rehabilitation and motion analysis.

title: GOSPA and T-GOSPA quasi-metrics for evaluation of multi-object tracking algorithms
authors: Ángel F. Garcá-Fernández, Jinhao Gu, Lennart Svensson, Yuxuan Xia, Jan Krejčí, Oliver Kost, Ondřej Straka
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper introduces two quasi-metrics for performance assessment of multi-object tracking (MOT) algorithms. One quasi-metric is an extension of the generalised optimal subpattern assignment (GOSPA) metric and measures the discrepancy between sets of objects. The other quasi-metric is an extension of the trajectory GOSPA (T-GOSPA) metric and measures the discrepancy between sets of trajectories. Similar to the GOSPA-based metrics, these quasi-metrics include costs for localisation error for properly detected objects, the number of false objects and the number of missed objects. The T-GOSPA quasi-metric also includes a track switching cost. Differently from the GOSPA and T-GOSPA metrics, the proposed quasi-metrics have the flexibility of penalising missed and false objects with different costs, and the localisation costs are not required to be symmetric. We also explain how to obtain similarity score functions based on these quasi-metrics. The performance of several Bayesian MOT algorithms is assessed with the T-GOSPA quasi-metric via simulations.

title: Enhancing Target Recognition Performance in SSVEP-Based Brain–Computer Interfaces via Deep Neural Networks with Pyramid Squeeze Attention
authors: Xiao Wu, Ian Daly, Andrew Ty Lau, Weijie Chen, Chongfeng Wang, Andrzej Cichocki, Jing Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Steady state visual evoked potential (SSVEP)-based brain-computer interfaces have been widely studied for their fast response speeds and high information transfer rates. However, how to fully utilize the potential information of existing subjects to realize the mining of common information among different subjects and then realize the information migration in a small amount of data scenarios is a difficult problem faced by current research. In order to solve the above problems, this study proposes a deep neural network based on the pyramid squeeze attention (PSA-DNN) mechanism to enhance the performance of SSVEP-BCI through common information migration. Specifically, the band-pass filtered EEG signals were first Fourier transformed to obtain the frequency domain information; subsequently, the frequency domain information is input into a deep neural network, followed by a spatial convolution step to extract spatial domain information. In order to further enhance the quality of information extraction, a pyramid attention module is introduced into the network to realize the enhancement of frequency domain and spatial domain information. Time domain information from the EEG signals is then mined using temporal convolution. Finally, the full connectivity layer is used to output the recognition results. The model is trained in a three-stage stepped approach for SSVEP target recognition. The first stage uses data from all participants in the training set for common information learning and transfers the model parameters trained in the first stage to the network model in the second stage. In the second stage, some of the information from participants in the test set is used for fine-tuning and to mine personalized information from these new participants. The third stage uses the remaining data from participants in the test set to produce classification results. The proposed method is systematically evaluated using the Benchmark and BETA datasets, where it demonstrates favorable performance compared to established baselines. These findings contribute theoretical insights and methodological references for the application of SSVEP-based brain–computer interfaces in real-world scenarios.

title: GASegNet: Global Self-Attention Mechanism Meets Structural Feature Fusion for Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation
authors: Xu Lu, Haijun Liu, Guang'an Luo, Zhike Chen, Cheng Zhou, Xinyu Wu, Jun Liu
publish: IEEE Latin America Transactions
abstract: With the rapid development of autonomous driving technology, semantic segmentation, as one of the key technologies contributing to the environment perception of autonomous driving systems, still suffers from a lack of connections between local features, as well as high computational consumption and an inability to meet real-time requirements. To address the above problems, this paper proposes a lightweight and efficient point cloud semantic segmentation network based on spherical projection with encoder-decoder structure. The encoder consists of a global self-attention mechanism that captures global information, as well as multi-scale convolution. This module achieves the unification of local feature extraction and global characteristic information for high-dimensional semantic information. In order to alleviate the high computational cost, a feature fusion module is introduced to enhance the compactness of the range image structure obtained from point cloud projection. The decoder utilizes bilinear interpolation to upsample multi-resolution feature maps, and introduces multiple auxiliary segmentation heads to further enhance the network's accuracy. Experiments conducted on the SemanticKITTI and SemanticPOSS datasets reveal that, in comparison to the CENet architecture, the proposed approach attains enhancements in mIoU of 4.3% and 2.6% on the respective datasets, thereby substantiating its efficacy. The code is available at GitHub: https://github.com/haifeng925/GASegNet.

title: A Grounded Coplanar Waveguide to Empty Substrate Integrated Waveguide Transition With Near-Octave Bandwidth
authors: David Herraiz-Zanón, Héctor Esteban, Darío Herraiz, Angel Belenguer, Vicente E. Boria
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents an in-line grounded coplanar waveguide (GCPW) to empty the substrate integrated waveguide (ESIW) transition that enables the low-loss, high-performance microwave systems based on the ESIW technology. The proposed structure uses several matching elements including an ESIW widening, triangular and transverse slots, and an iris—without complex manufacturing dielectric tapers or bulky multilayer structures. A fabricated prototype validates the structure, achieving a return loss level better than 18dB and an insertion loss of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathrm {0.45~}}~\pm ~{\mathrm {0.1~\text {dB}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> over 10–17.3GHz, demonstrating a near-octave bandwidth and low-loss performance.

title: Optimizing Incoming Quality Control with a Dynamic Set Covering Strategy for Zero-Defect Inspection
authors: Wenqing Zhou, Yufeng Chen, Rui Zhou, Mohamed Sharaf
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper develops an adaptive incoming quality control framework that integrates a dynamic set-covering strategy to support zero-defect inspection in large-scale production, ensuring complete identification of non-conforming parts while reducing inspection time and cost. Conventional set-covering approaches typically guarantee full coverage at limited batch sizes, and often fail to maintain both detection completeness and operational efficiency in large-scale production. To address this limitation, we reformulate the inspection process using a two-phase dynamic set-covering mechanism. In the first phase, a baseline set is established by hierarchically prioritizing inspection items. Items detecting more non-conforming parts are assigned a higher priority. The remaining items that do not contribute to current coverage are subsequently ranked according to their inherent defect probabilities. In the second phase, a reduced inspection item subset is selected from the prioritized set to cover all observed non-conforming parts in the current batch. The proposed strategy operates through two interconnected mechanisms to ensure practical defect coverage. It establishes explicit coverage constraints and employs an adaptive update rule that refines item priorities across batches based on residual inspection information. Finally, we present an example to demonstrate the proposed approach.

title: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid Information for Authors
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid

title: HemoSC-P: A Hemodynamic Semantic Channel Paradigm for Cardiovascular Parameter Estimation
authors: Xi Qiu, Zhifei Zhang, Hailin Cao, Li Yang, Hao Tang, Lianyang Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: As a leading cause of death worldwide, cardiovascular disease demands more precise monitoring and early warning systems, posing significant challenges to modern healthcare. However, cardiovascular early warning systems often face two major dilemmas: the “black box dilemma” leads to unreliable estimation results due to limited physiological interpretability, and limited robustness across subjects and blood pressure states arises from physiological heterogeneity. This study innovatively maps the methods of semantic extraction and channel modeling to the cardiovascular system, inspired by the 6G network concept of transmitting meaning rather than data under the semantic communication paradigm. It proposes a novel hemodynamic channel-guided cardiovascular parameter estimation paradigm (HemoSC-P). This paradigm adopts a dual-pillar modeling framework: the semantic pillar utilizes a multi-scale convolutional and phase-aware attention architecture to model the non-stationary dynamics of cardiovascular signals, elevating feature alignment from the temporal domain to the physiological domain. The cardiovascular channel, parameterized by the Windkessel circuit model, serves dual roles as a semantic pathway from cardiac contractions to observable physiological signals and as a repository of physiological knowledge. It guides channel-level physiologically deformable attention to achieve inverse estimation of cardiovascular parameters. To validate this paradigm, this study employs non-invasive blood pressure estimation as a representative case study. Validation was conducted on three representative public datasets (UCI-BP, MIMIC-III, PPG-BP). On MIMIC-III, the mean absolute error $\pm$ standard deviation for systolic and diastolic blood pressure reached 3.04 $\pm$ 3.24 mmHg and 2.57 $\pm$ 2.70 mmHg, respectively. Multi-dataset validation indicates that this paradigm surpasses benchmark methods in accuracy and stability while maintaining scalability.

title: Reparameterization-Driven Depthwise Separable Large-Kernel Network for Lightweight Salient Object Detection of Strip Steel Surface Defects
authors: Xiaofei Zhou, Zhenkun Mo, Gongyang Li, Liuxin Bao, Xiaobin Xu, Jiyong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: With the rapid development of neural networks, strip steel surface defect detection, as an important task in computer vision, has achieved remarkable progress. However, state-of-the-art methods still face a tradeoff between accuracy and efficiency. High-performing models are usually large and computationally expensive, whereas lightweight models often suffer from limited detection accuracy. To address this issue, we first propose a spatial channel enhancement (SCE) module, which consists of a reparameterizable depthwise large-kernel convolution and a reparameterizable pointwise (RepPw) convolution. The proposed SCE module enlarges the receptive field and strengthens long-range spatial and channel interactions while preserving computational efficiency. Based on the SCE module, we propose a novel lightweight saliency model for strip steel surface defects, namely, reparameterization-driven depthwise separable large-kernel network (RepDSLKNet). RepDSLKNet employs SCE modules to build an encoder and a decoder, and utilizes cascaded channel attention (CCA) modules for the feature fusion. The lightweight architecture can effectively extract and fuse the semantic information and detailed features of strip steel surface defects, thereby improving the accuracy and speed of detection with a small model size. With an input size of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$224\times 224$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, our RepDSLKNet has only 0.47 M parameters and 0.42 G FLOPs during inference. Compared to the current state-of-the-art methods, our approach achieves a 19-fold improvement in throughput and a twofold reduction in latency. Experiments on two public strip steel defect datasets demonstrate that RepDSLKNet delivers competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods.

title: Impact of TSV Quantity Scaling on TDDB Reliability and Failure Mechanisms in TSV Interposers
authors: Fa Wu, Yahui Mao, Shuwen Zeng, Juan Tang, Sijie Tian, Kai Yuan, Lun Zhang, Huang Wenjun, Jun Shen, Zhaohuan Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: This article investigates the impact of through-silicon via (TSV) quantity on time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) lifetime and the associated failure mechanisms. Experimental results demonstrate a pronounced reduction in TDDB lifetime with increasing TSV quantity, and the corresponding quantity-dependent scaling factors are systematically extracted. TDDB failure locations are precisely localized using a combination of low-light InGaAs infrared (IR) microscopy and focused ion beam (FIB) analysis. Finite element analysis (FEAs) reveals severe electric field crowding at the TSV bottom corners, mainly induced by copper sharp-edge geometries, which dominate the dielectric breakdown behavior. These results elucidate the physical origin of quantity-dependent TDDB degradation and provide device-level design guidelines for improving the TDDB reliability of TSV interposers.

title: LEO Satellite-Enabled ISAC: Bistatic Framework and Beamforming Design
authors: Yuexia Zhang, Yuanshuo Gang, Tianqi Mao, Ruiqi Liu, Meng Hua, Qingqing Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: With advantages such as wide coverage, high data rates, and precise target localization, low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites show great potential in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) and are expected to play a pivotal role in 6G networks. However, LEO satellite ISAC systems face several challenges such as highly dynamic channel environments, limited onboard payloads, and significant attenuation of long-link sensing echoes, which hinder their effectiveness in high-precision sensing and high-quality communication tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a LEO satellite-enabled bistatic ISAC framework, wherein the LEO satellite performs both communication and sensing tasks, while a terrestrial gateway is responsible for receiving the echo signals. This design reduces path loss and enhances airborne target detection accuracy. The paper derives the achievable rate expression based on statistical channel state information and the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for two-dimensional angle of arrival estimation. Furthermore, an optimization problem is formulated to minimize the CRB while satisfying communication constraints: 1) For the hybrid beamformer (HBF), a penalty dual decomposition method is employed, and a two-layer iterative optimization algorithm is proposed to optimize beamforming while satisfying the unit-modulus constraint. 2) For the fully digital beamformer (FDBF), a globally optimal solution is derived using the semidefinite relaxation technique, and a closed-form solution is obtained to reduce computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that: 1) Compared with the monostatic framework, the bistatic framework significantly improves CRB performance, enabling higher-precision target sensing in LEO satellite ISAC systems. 2) The proposed algorithms achieve excellent target sensing accuracy while effectively ensuring communication quality for ground users. 3) The proposed HBF design approaches the performance of the FDBF design while requiring fewer radio frequency chains.

title: Modulation-Agnostic Interference Cancellation Using Approximate Message Passing Driven by Empirical Bayes
authors: Haochuan Zhang, Zhiyi Liang, Huimin Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: Interference cancellation (IC) is crucial for sustaining high-performance massive MIMO downlink receivers at cell edges, where capacity is severely limited by strong co-channel interference. Achieving Bayes-optimal recovery of this interference is fundamentally challenging, as it typically requires explicit prior knowledge of the interfering modulation formats. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel, modulation-agnostic interference estimation approach that unfolds the Approximate Message Passing (AMP) framework, replacing its standard static denoiser with a dynamic Empirical Bayes (EB) component. In this synergistic architecture, AMP systematically decouples the spatial MIMO channel to provide the scalar environment required by EB, while EB adaptively learns the prior distribution and infers the signal without any pre-existing modulation knowledge. This approach completely circumvents the need for brute-force exhaustive searches, rendering it significantly more efficient than conventional model selection methods, while exhibiting superior computational scalability compared to Expectation-Maximization (EM) based AMP. We integrate this EB-AMP estimator into a comprehensive joint detection framework that iteratively alternates among data detection, signal subtraction, interference recovery, and interference cancellation. Numerical simulations demonstrate that under the assumptions of perfect channel state information (CSI), i.i.d. Rayleigh fading, and a single interfering cell, the proposed IC receiver effectively suppresses unknown interference at power levels comparable to the desired data signal, achieving an estimation accuracy that closely approaches the theoretical interference-free bound.

title: Spectroscopic Characterization of Plasmoid Properties During Pellet Fueling in W7-X
authors: Edgardo Villalobos, Jürgen Baldzuhn, Ameer I. Mohammed, Larry Baylor, Nerea Panadero
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: This study utilizes a spectroscopic approach to investigate the properties of plasmoids that are formed during the process of cryogenic hydrogen pellet fueling in the Wendelstein 7-X (W7-X) stellarator. An analysis of the Balmer series emissions was conducted using a diagnostic that was installed during the 2024 operational campaign. Electron temperature, density, and plasma beta (b) values can be inferred from the emissions of radiation from the ablation plasmoid. These values are essential for validating pellet ablation models and, in the future, optimizing fueling strategies in steady-state fusion devices.

title: Power Diagram Enhanced Adaptive Isosurface Extraction from Signed Distance Fields
authors: Pengfei Wang, Ziyang Zhang, Wensong Wang, Shuangmin Chen, Lin Lu, Shiqing Xin, Changhe Tu, Wenping Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Extracting high-fidelity mesh surfaces from Signed Distance Fields (SDFs) has become a fundamental operation in geometry processing. Despite significant progress over the past decades, key challenges remain—namely, how to automatically capture the intricate geometric and topological structures en coded in the zero level set of SDFs. In this paper, we present a novel isosurface extraction algorithm that introduces two key innovations: 1) An incrementally constructed power diagram through the addition of sample points, which enables repeated updates to the extracted surface via its dual—regular Delaunay tetrahedralization; and 2) An adaptive point insertion strategy that identifies regions exhibiting the greatest discrepancy between the current mesh and the underlying continuous surface. As the teaser figure shows, our framework progressively refines the extracted mesh with minimal computational cost until it sufficiently approximates the underlying surface. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the art methods, particularly for models with intricate geometric variations and complex topologies

title: Multi-Beholder: Biomarker Prediction for Low-Grade Glioma with Multiple Instance Learning and One-Class Classification
authors: Zijie Fang, Yihan Liu, Yifeng Wang, Xiangyang Zhang, Yang Chen, Changjing Cai, Yiyang Lin, Ying Han, Zhi Wang, Shan Zeng, Jun Tan, Yongbing Zhang, Hong Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Biomarker detection is an indispensable part of the diagnosis and treatment of low-grade glioma (LGG). However, current LGG biomarker detection methods rely on expensive and complex molecular genetic testing, for which professionals are required to analyze the results, and intra-rater variability is often reported. To overcome these challenges, we propose an interpretable deep learning pipeline, named Multi-Biomarker Histomorphology Discoverer (Multi-Beholder), to predict the status of five biomarkers in LGG using only hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images. Specifically, Multi-Beholder incorporates one-class classification into the multiple instance learning framework to achieve accurate instance-level pseudo-labeling, thereby complementing slide-level labels and improving prediction performance. Multi-Beholder demonstrates high performance on two LGG cohorts with diverse races and scanning protocols, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve up to 0.973 on the internal-validated TCGA-LGG dataset and 0.820 on the external-validated Xiangya cohort. Moreover, the interpretability of Multi-Beholder allows for discovering quantitative and qualitative correlations between biomarker status and histomorphology characteristics. Our pipeline not only provides a novel approach for biomarker prediction, enhancing the applicability of molecular treatments for LGG patients but also facilitates the discovery of new mechanisms in molecular functionality and LGG progression. Code can be accessed at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Vison307/Multi-Beholder</uri>.

title: Compressed Sensing for Efficient Near-Field Scanning of Embedded Systems
authors: Vishnuvardhan V. Iyer, Jacob D. Rezac, James C. Booth
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electromagnetic Compatibility
abstract: We propose a novel compressed sensing (CS) approach for high-resolution near-field electromagnetic (EM) cartography for embedded system blocks. Assuming near-fields can be represented using a truncated plane-wave expansion, the CS approach estimates optimal solutions of coefficients in this expansion using considerably fewer samples than the theoretically defined Nyquist rate. The method is demonstrated in two phases: First, the simulation phase enables us to build and demonstrate the CS framework on a source model that mimics near fields from computing chips. Several simulation experiments are implemented to verify the compressibility of the dataset and the limits within which CS can be applied. Second, the measurement phase validates the methodology by implementing CS-based scans on a commercial test board and reconstructing the field maps expected from high-resolution scans. Experiments in the measurement phase also demonstrate the utility of the CS approach for anomaly detection using near-field measurements. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method by showing at least <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> and as much as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> reduction in sample requirements, in order to reach less than 10<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> relative error using the CS-based method compared to Nyquist-style sampling in simulations and measurements.

title: Shielding Federated Learning Services From a Novel Stealthy Label-Flipping Attack Exposing Membership
authors: Geetanjli Sharma, M.A.P. Chamikara, Mohan Baruwal Chhetri, Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Label-flipping attacks pose a significant threat to the security and privacy of machine learning models, particularly in federated learning (FL). While existing defenses, such as FoolsGold and Krum, aim to mitigate these attacks, they often fail to account for more subtle adversarial behaviors, such as intermittent class-targeted flips. In this work, we introduce the Stealthy Label-Flipping Attack (SLFA), which exposes this overlooked vulnerability in FL services. SLFA is an adaptive attack that intermittently flips a fixed subset of class labels across multiple communication rounds before reverting them to their original state. We show that this transient manipulation (i) evades state-of-the-art poisoning and anomaly detectors, (ii) preserves overall model utility, and (iii) introduces gradient instability during the attacking rounds, thereby increasing the risk of privacy leakage. To counter this hybrid threat that not only evades state-of-the-art defenses but also amplifies privacy leakage, we propose FLStealthShield, a server-side defense mechanism that detects and neutralizes such adaptive attacks by analyzing temporal patterns in client updates. Extensive experiments under both IID and non-IID data distributions demonstrate that FLStealthShield achieves Byzantine robustness and maintains high model utility, outperforming existing defenses even when over 40% of clients are malicious.

title: LightFire-Net: Efficient Cross-Modal Spectral–Spatial Information Fusion for Real-Time Wildfire Detection in Remote Sensing
authors: Muhammad John Abbas, Muhammad Attique Khan, Jiaxin Li, Irfan Haider, Latifah Almuqren, Mohammad Alhefdi, Jungpil Shin, Yunyoung Nam
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Wildfires are among the most devastating natural disasters, causing significant damage to the environment, the economy, wildlife, and human lives, and requiring an early detection system. This paper presents a novel LightFire-Net deep cross model fusion architecture for Wildfire classification and interpretation from remote sensing images. The proposed model is computationally efficient and employs Efficient Flame Pattern blocks with depthwise separable convolutional layers, a Lightweight spectral spatial module, and Efficient Feature Fusion for real-time wildfire classification and detection. The model's multi-stage architecture enables hierarchical feature extraction, resulting in more accurate and efficient classification. Bayesian Optimization (BO) is employed to select hyperparameters, thereby improving the training of the proposed model on selected remote sensing datasets. A Softmax classification layer is employed for the final prediction. The proposed model achieves exceptional performance with above 98% prediction accuracy while maintaining only 1.37 million parameters and an inference speed of 7.74 ms. A comprehensive evaluation across multiple dataset splits (90:05:05-40:30:30) demonstrates robust generalization, with accuracy ranging from 98.02% to 99.25%. The five-fold mean accuracy further confirms the model's stability, at 98.62%±0.15%. The proposed model outperforms existing CNN and transformer-based architectures while being significantly more efficient, making it highly suitable and reliable for real-time monitoring in resource-constrained environments.

title: A Synthetic Data Generation Approach for Time Series Datasets of the Hydrological Context
authors: Ricardo Dos Santos, Jose Aguilar, Rodrigo García
publish: 2025 44th International Conference of the Chilean Computer Science Society (SCCC)
abstract: Machine learning (ML) plays a crucial role in hydrological modeling, offering significant advantages in the prediction and forecasting of phenomena such as water flow. Its effectiveness is intrinsically linked to the quality and quantity of the data used. This paper proposes a synthetic data generation approach that focuses on the reconstruction of time series datasets with long sequences of missing values, using LSTM (Long ShortTerm Memory) models. The reconstructed datasets are then used to build prediction models. Our missing data imputation approach employs a hybrid method that integrates the simplicity of univariate techniques for short segments of missing data with the advanced predictive capability of LSTM models based on temporal windows for longer sequences. The process is performed iteratively, where LSTM models are trained with the present data to progressively predict and generate missing values, thus completing the original dataset. The experimentation carried out evidences the effectiveness of this method in the field of hydrology, through the use of data from flow sensors in Colombia and the evaluation of the performance of the predictive models built with the reconstructed datasets. The analysis based on the MAE, MAPE, MSE, R2 and RMSE metrics yields very good values, such as R2 close to one and values close to zero in the other metrics, which is interpreted as satisfactory performance. These results demonstrate the robustness of the synthetic data generation approach for the reconstruction of missing information, achieving high performance in the evaluation metrics and overcoming the limitations of traditional methods by allowing the construction of functional predictive models where classical imputation could not be used.

title: A Vision-Driven End-User Robot Programming Method Based on the Alignment Between Body Language and Motion Commands_supp1-3679132.pdf
authors: David Navarro-Alarcon
publish: N/A
abstract: This work is motivated by the complexity of conventional robot programming methods, which require a deep understanding of both robotics and software development. This situation presents significant challenges for nontechnical end-users who need to program a new robotic task. To address this issue, we propose a new robot programming method that enables humans to specify robot motion tasks using simple and intuitive body-language commands. To this end, we propose a triadic task decomposition framework to decompose complex robot motion tasks into three basic task units, i.e., location, operation, and motion. These units are paired with a set of body-language cues that enable end-users to specify a variety of actions and motions, providing an intuitive alternative to traditional robot programming methods. To program these units, we develop a multimodal fusion-based instruction-parsing algorithm with a dual-phase visual architecture, enabling temporally aware body-language recognition and high-accuracy cross-modal object localization. Furthermore, a semantics-adaptive task execution system is proposed, which incorporates an intent reasoning module that dynamically handles end-users' nonstandard programming sequences and incomplete task logic, enhancing programming success rates and system usability. We evaluate the proposed method on a dual-arm robotic platform and a humanoid robot through various pick-and-place tasks, and compare its performance with that of several baselines from the literature.

title: DTopAgent: A Multi-Agent Framework for Dynamic Top-K Chunk Retrieval in Rag Pipeline
authors: Sijia Wang, Chunming Liu, JianYong Duan, ZhongYuan Zhao, Zibo Yi, Xin Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In QA tasks, the effectiveness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is not only determined by large language models (LLMs), but also the accuracy of retrieval. A key point of retrieval is to determine the number of retrieved document chunks. Fixed top-K strategies struggles to balance the requirements of different queries for context coverage and noise suppression: too few chunks may miss crucial information, while too many introduce noise. They also lack a fallback mechanism when retrieval fails and are limited by a static corpus, thereby diminishing the accuracy of generated results. To address these challenges, we propose DTopAgent, a novel multi-agent RAG framework tailored for dynamic K prediction. Our framework employs three agents: a predictor agent, an answer agent and a decision agent. They collaborate to perform dynamic K prediction, combining their individual insights to achieve a coarse-to-fine refinement of the K. This approach adaptively balances information coverage and noise suppression, thereby improving QA accuracy. Experiments on five datasets covering both short- and long-form generation tasks show that DTopAgent significantly improves performance and reduces inference costs. Notably, DTopAgent outperforms existing methods by over 8% on the QA benchmarks, while reducing token count by nearly 50%.

title: Time-Aware Multi-Exponential Analysis to Optimize Analyte Identification Using Zif-8-90
authors: Laura Rami Matas, Margot F. K. Verstreken, Rob Ameloot, Agustin Gutierrez-Galvez
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper studies a chemical sensor response dataset based on experimental kinetic information of adsorption processes measured by a quartz crystal microbalance covered with a metal-organic framework, namely ZIF-8-90. It is first noted that the general behavior of the experiments in the dataset diverges from the expected signal responses based on the most widely-used ideal model. Thus, a new approach based on time translation is proposed and compared to the traditional model using a simple classifier. We are able to establish experimentally that the new model performs significantly better. However, other aspects regarding a simplification of the model and the potential generalization of the results require further study.

title: UniLoRA: A Unified Framework for Efficient and Secure LoRA Management in Multi-Tenant LLM Inference
authors: Zechao Lin, Xingbin Wang, Dan Meng, Rui Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in multi-tenant services via the Adapter-as-a-Service paradigm, where LoRA enables lightweight task customization. However, existing LoRA management systems suffer from dynamic-loading latency, weak isolation, GPU underutilization, and cross-framework incompatibilities, limiting scalability and reproducibility. We present UniLoRA, a unified framework for efficient and secure LoRA management in multi-tenant LLM inference. It combines three key modules: (1) SLA-Aware Scheduler (SAS), which employs a DQN-based policy, asynchronous loading, and sandbox isolation to cut tail latency and enforce access control; (2) Adaptive Memory Management (AMM), which combines predictive caching, LoRA-aware CUDA kernels, and adaptive batching to boost GPU utilization and throughput; and (3) Standardized Adapter Protocol (SAP), which defines a JSON-based metadata schema and conversion toolchain for seamless cross-framework migration. UniLoRA delivers 1.8× faster P99 latency with SLA compliance above 92%, blocks 90% of unauthorized adapters, accelerates adapter conversion by 1.6× , and simultaneously improves throughput and GPU memory efficiency.

title: Individualize the HRTF Neural Field Using Anthropometric Parameters Weighted by Direction-Attention
authors: Yuhang Xiao, Xiaochen Wang, Chenhao Hu, XueYang Lv, Miaomiao Li, Yulin Wu, Jiajun Yuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Neural field (NeRF) has recently been used in head-related transfer function(HRTF) spatial interpolation tasks due to its excellent ability to reconstruct three-dimensional space. By some conditional strategies, NeRF may be able to solve individual HRTF tasks, which has not received much attention in past research. This paper focus on achieving individualization of HRTF NeRF. The main work of this paper is to design and implement a anthropometric parameters encoding network with direction-attention weighting and an individual feature mapping network, as well as a two-stage network training strategy. Moreover, experiments were conducted to compare with different encoding and mapping modes to determine the optimal combination. The proposed structure endows the HRTF NeRF network with the performance of generating HRTFs of unknown individuals, extending the NeRF technology from HRTF spatial interpolation tasks to individual tasks. In an objective evaluation comparison of HRTF individualization algorithms based on different network architecture, our method performs better on both the HUTUBS database and the CIPIC database.

title: ECHO: Frequency-Aware Hierarchical Encoding for Variable-Length Signals
authors: Yucong Zhang, Juan Liu, Ming Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pre-trained foundation models have demonstrated remarkable success in audio, vision and language, yet their potential for general machine signal modeling with arbitrary sampling rates—covering acoustic, vibration, and other industrial sensor data—remains under-explored. In this work, we propose a novel foundation model ECHO that integrates an advanced band-split architecture with frequency positional embeddings, enabling spectral localization across arbitrary sampling configurations. Moreover, the model incorporates sliding patches to support inputs of variable length without padding or cropping, producing a concise embedding that retains both temporal and spectral fidelity and naturally extends to streaming scenarios. We evaluate our method on various kinds of machine signal datasets, including previous DCASE task 2 challenges (2020–2025), and widely-used industrial signal corpora. Experimental results demonstrate consistent state-of-the-art performance in machine signal anomaly detection and fault classification, confirming the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed model. We open-sourced ECHO on https://github.com/yucongzh/ECHO.

title: Unsupervised TBD-MIG Detectors in Nonhomogeneous Clutter
authors: Xiaoqiang Hua, Jialun Zhou, Hao Wu, Zheng Yang, Yongqiang Cheng, Hongqiang Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper develops an unsupervised manifold projection method for enhancing total Bregman divergence (TBD)based matrix information geometry (MIG) detectors in nonhomogeneous clutter environments. Specifically, we design a variance-maximizing manifold projection that maps Hermitian positive-definite matrices onto a lower-dimensional manifold while preserving their intrinsic geometric structure. The projection matrix is learned in an unsupervised manner by formulating variance maximization as an optimization problem with orthonormal constraints on the Stiefel manifold, solved via the Riemannian conjugate gradient algorithm. The proposed projection is integrated into three existing TBDMIG detectors, yielding a new class of projected TBD-MIG detectors. Simulation results demonstrate that the manifold projection significantly improves detection performance compared with both non-projected TBD-MIG detectors and conventional methods under various nonhomogeneous clutter conditions.

title: Overcoming Binning Dilemma: Cumulative Calibration for Doubly Robust Learning in Debiased Recommendation
authors: Xiwen Jiang, Wenli Wang, Xiaofeng Meng, Tianyu Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
