abstract: Recommender systems often suffer from selection bias: users are more likely to interact with preferred items, so observed data are Missing Not At Random (MNAR), and training directly on such datasets can yield biased predictions. Doubly robust (DR) learning mitigates this challenge by combining propensity reweighting and error imputation, but previous studies found that the learned propensities are always overconfident, which hinders their effectiveness. To avoid this issue, existing methods typically adopt the expected calibration error as a penalty, and empirically compute this term using a binning-based strategy, which inevitably introduces a trade-off tied to the number of bins and per-bin sample sizes. In this paper, we first point out this trade-off, and then propose two cumulative calibration metrics (CCMs), which evaluate mis-calibration over the full cumulative curve of predicted propensities and observations. In addition, we show that these two CCMs converge to the maximum absolute value / the range of the standard Brownian motion, respectively, propose a hypothesis testing to evaluate miscalibration, and use the p-value as the constraint term. Experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that eliminating the binning dilemma yields better debiasing performance.

title: An Anomaly-Aware and Audio-Enhanced Dual-Pathway Framework for Alzheimer’s Disease Progression Classification
authors: Zirui Lin, Ling Bai, Pengcheng Xi, Zheng Liu, Gaozhi George Xiao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) offer significant advantages for Alzheimer’s disease detection through their interactivity, interpretability, and capacity to analyze patient interview transcripts and other textual data. However, when analyzing text, LLMs tend to normalize linguistic errors and irregularities in their internal representations, treating these features as noise rather than recognizing them as diagnostic markers. This representational bias reduces the model’s ability to detect the subtle language impairments characteristic of Alzheimer’s disease. To address these issues, we propose an Anomaly-Aware and Audio-Enhanced Dual-Pathway framework for Alzheimer’s Disease classification (AUDP-AD), which enhances large language models through a dual-path architecture that simultaneously captures linguistic and paralinguistic information in patient speech. This proposed framework innovatively integrates language anomaly features at the input layer, while acoustic features are incorporated at intermediate layers through a cross-attention mechanism. This multimodal feature integration strategy achieves significant improvements in detection performance compared to the baseline model. Moreover, ablation experiments indicate that combining paralinguistic pathways with anomaly-aware linguistic processing provides clear performance advantages. In summary, this dual-pathway architecture demonstrates substantial value in overcoming the inherent limitations of standard language models and holds strong potential for effective early Alzheimer’s disease detection.

title: Quantum SVM vs Classical SVM: Why Do QSVM Underperform SVM?
authors: Justina Michael J, Praveen Kumar G, F. Sangeetha Francelin Vinnarasi, Thenmozhi M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) provides a pathway for analysing nonlinear relationships through quantum- enhanced feature spaces. Although quantum models are theoreti- cally well suited for high-complexity data, current hardware and simulator limitations restrict their use to small datasets. For this reason, the original large Web Phishing Detection Dataset was reduced to 200 representative samples through dimensionality reduction, allowing controlled experimentation and enabling evaluation of quantum behaviour with manageable computational cost. QML research commonly follows this approach, testing algorithms on small datasets first, because quantum feature maps can generate exponentially expressive Hilbert-space embeddings even from limited inputs. In this study, multiple Quantum Support Vector Machine (QSVM) models were implemented using fidelity-based quantum kernels and Pauli feature maps. Models with 2 to 10 qubits were evaluated to observe how circuit width affects performance. The two-qubit QSVM achieved the best quantum result with 72 % accuracy, whereas deeper circuits showed declining performance, indicating overfitting and reduced generalization on limited data. In contrast, the classical SVM attained 97 % accuracy with significantly lower computational effort. These findings highlight the challenges and potential of QML for cybersecurity tasks. Future work will explore circuit- expressivity tuning, optimized quantum kernels, hybrid quantum- classical approaches, and scalability to larger phishing datasets as quantum hardware evolves.

title: CrashSplat: 2D to 3D Vehicle Damage Segmentation in Gaussian Splatting
authors: Dragoş-Andrei Chileban, Andrei-Ştefan Bulzan, Cosmin Cernǎzanu-Glǎvan
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: Automatic car damage detection has been a topic of significant interest for the auto insurance industry as it promises faster, accurate, and cost-effective damage assessments. However, few works have gone beyond 2D image analysis to leverage 3D reconstruction methods, which have the potential to provide a more comprehensive and geometrically accurate representation of the damage. Moreover, recent methods employing 3D representations for novel view synthesis, particularly 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), have demonstrated the ability to generate accurate and coherent 3D reconstructions from a limited number of views. In this work we introduce an automatic car damage detection pipeline that performs 3D damage segmentation by up-lifting 2D masks. Additionally, we propose a simple yet effective learningfree approach for single-view 3D-GS segmentation. Specifically, Gaussians are projected onto the image plane using camera parameters obtained via Structure from Motion (SfM). They are then filtered through an algorithm that utilizes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Z}$</tex>-buffering along with a normal distribution model of depth and opacities. Through experiments we found that this method is particularly effective for challenging scenarios like car damage detection, where target objects (e.g., scratches, small dents) may only be clearly visible in a single view, making multi-view consistency approaches impractical or impossible. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/DragosChileban/CrashSplat.

title: Keeping Models Listening: Segment- and time-aware attention rescaling at decoding time
authors: Hangyu Du, Jingxing Zhong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audio Large Language Models (ALLMs) are increasingly used for perception and reasoning, yet we find their audio grounding degrades over time: as decoding proceeds, attention drifts from audio evidence to language priors and early "sink" tokens. We first analyze attention routing across layers and timesteps, revealing systematic decay of attention to audio keys during incremental decoding. To counter this, we introduce AttnAdapter, a training-free, plug-and-play module that rescales attention logits in a segment-and time-aware manner. AttnAdapter combines (i) sink suppression for the system token, (ii) a time-aware ramp that sustains attention to audio keys as more tokens are generated, and (iii) a local output-to-output window that stabilizes fluency without over-whelming audio evidence. The method leaves model architecture, RoPE, masking, KV cache, and complexity unchanged, and can be enabled at inference for existing decoder-only ALLMs. On MMAU-mini and AIR-Bench, AttnAdapter improves audio grounding and response quality on both open- and closed-source baselines with minimal overhead. Our results suggest that simple, decoding-time logit rescaling can reliably "re-listen" to audio when models are most prone to drift toward textual priors.

title: MemoryPrompt: Memory-Augmented Multi-Layer Prompting for Vision-Language Models
authors: Zhongchen Ma, Jiahao Song, Xingchen Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient prompt learning for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often relies on a single global image feature, neglecting rich, patch-level semantics. These local features are inherently noisy and weakly aligned with text, making them ineffective for direct prompting. To overcome this, we propose MemoryPrompt, a novel framework that refines local features using an internal, dynamic memory. This memory aggregates salient patch exemplars from multiple layers of a frozen encoder. For each input image, a sparse cross-attention module retrieves relevant memory prototypes to create robust, memory-augmented local representations. We then learn two complementary sets of prompts: a global prompt conditioned on the standard image feature, and a local prompt conditioned on our memory-augmented patch features. MemoryPrompt sets a new state-of-the-art across eleven benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements in fine-grained classification, few-shot learning, and domain generalization.

title: Stress Prediction from Temporal Emotion Trajectories in Clinical Patient-Physician Conversations
authors: Tobias Pertlwieser, Hiuching Hung, Tomás Arias-Vergara, Paula Andrea Pérez-Toro, Carolin Müller, Meike Schmitt, Hanna Huebner, Philipp Kreis, Irem Karaman, Miriam Saatze, Annika Krückel, Chloë Goossens, Katharina Seitz, Jonathan Singer, Armine Garibyan, Peter Uhrig, Peter A. Fasching, Andreas Maier, Manuel Hörner
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Evaluating psychological stress during oncology consultations is important for treatment adherence, understanding, and well-being, yet is typically measured only retrospectively with questionnaires. We propose a framework to estimate stress levels of 30 breast cancer patients directly from German speech. An emotion-based audio encoder first produces temporal emotion trajectories, which are then used by a regressor to predict PSQ-20 stress scores. The model achieves RMSE = 0.136 and r = 0.784, with Bland–Altman analysis showing negligible bias. Acoustic SVR baselines on paralinguistic features perform lower than trajectory-based temporal models.

title: SemantiCache: Efficient KV Cache Compression Via Semantic Chunking and Clustered Merging
authors: Shunlong Wu, Hai Lin, Shaoshen Chen, Tingwei Lu, Yongqin Zeng, Shaoxiong Zhan, Hai-Tao Zheng, Hong-Gee Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing KV cache compression methods generally operate on discrete tokens or non-semantic chunks. However, such approaches often lead to semantic fragmentation, where linguistically coherent units are disrupted, causing irreversible information loss and degradation in model performance. To address this, we introduce SemantiCache, a novel compression framework that preserves semantic integrity by aligning the compression process with the semantic hierarchical nature of language. Specifically, we first partition the cache into semantically coherent chunks by delimiters, which are natural semantic boundaries. Within each chunk, we introduce a computationally efficient Greedy Seed-Based Clustering (GSC) algorithm to group tokens into semantic clusters. These clusters are further merged into semantic cores, enhanced by a Proportional Attention mechanism that re-balances the reduced attention contributions of the merged tokens. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks and models demonstrate that SemantiCache accelerates the decoding stage of inference by up to 2.61× and substantially reduces memory footprint, while maintaining performance comparable to the original model.

title: ATTN-Defense: Attention-Guided Detection, Location and Removal for Indirect Prompt Injection
authors: Ziyang Zhang, Yuechen Lin, Xuanxuan An, Mingyang Wan, Caigui Jiang, Ning Ding
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been severely affected by prompt injection attacks due to their strong instruction-following capabilities. Among these, indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks pose broader security threats, as their injection occurs outside the model’s system boundary. Recent research efforts to defend against such attacks primarily focus on detection and prevention strategies. However, existing detection methods often degrade user experience by directly rejecting inputs after attack identification, while prevention approaches face inherent security and utility trade-off. To address this issue, this paper proposes Attn-Defense, an IPI removal method based on the LLM attention weight. Our approach extracts attention sequence features generated during input processing to achieve both detection and location of IPI attacks, followed by precise sentence-level removal operations. This enables accurate defense implementation without compromising the model’s ability to fulfill legitimate user instructions. Our method demonstrates consistent superiority across various models, jailbreak attacks, and datasets, achieving a 10.1% lower Attack Success Rate and 14.8% higher F1 Difference compared to existing approaches on two extended and augmented datasets. Furthermore, we validate the robustness of our framework and the effectiveness of its individual components through comprehensive experiments.

title: CoreAnchor-QA: Center-Anchored and Self-Improving for Question-Answer Generation
authors: Mengting Huang, Qiming Fu, Jianping Chen, Hongjie Wu, You Lu, Yunzhe Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Long document question-answering (QA) data is crucial for deploying domain models, yet high-quality corpora are scarce and cross-chunk evidence is hard to align, which makes generated questions and answers drift from the main theme. To address this, we propose CoreAnchor-QA, a framework that builds semantic anchors at document and chunk levels, asks from multiple perspectives around a fixed answer, and applies a two-stage gate based on semantic consistency and judgment scores together with a failure memory to form a self-improving loop. On the People’s Daily 2024 long-news benchmark, CoreAnchor-QA outperforms the best baseline by 12.26% on AlignScore chunk-level consistency (AS-Chk) and achieves improvements of 9.9%, 20.46%, and 2.88% on UniEval naturalness, understandability, and coherence. These results indicate that the proposed method keeps themes stable and makes evidence traceable while preserving diversity, improving the usability of synthetic QA data and the reliability of downstream applications.

title: OSG: Training-Free Objectness, Semantics, and Geometry Fusion for Zero-Shot Referring Expression Comprehension
authors: Lin Zhu, Junbo Wang, Yuke Li, Yining Zhu, Binyi Bai, Hongsong Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Referring expression comprehension aims to localize target objects in images based on natural language descriptions. In the zero-shot setting where no task-specific training is allowed, existing approaches often suffer from critical limitations: open-vocabulary detectors tend to generate visually salient but semantically irrelevant proposals, while vision-language models align well with semantics yet fail to resolve spatial ambiguities. To address the issues, we propose a training-free objectness, semantics, and geometry fusion strategy, namely OSG, for zero-shot REC that leverages pretrained vision-language models without any task-specific fine-tuning. Our method first employs an open-vocabulary detector to generate candidate regions, then evaluates the relevance of each region to the query expression using three complementary measure metrics: (1) detection confidence indicating objectness, (2) image-text matching scores from vision-language models, and (3) spatial plausibility from a lightweight geometry-aware reasoning model. In addition, we also propose a plug-and-play score fusion strategy that estimates the reliability of the scoring model through two statistics: discrimination deviation, indicating the confidence of the model in distinguishing candidate scores, and model independence, indicating its informational complementarity. Extensive experiments on RefCOCO/+/g datasets show that our method outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods by a large margin.

title: ASWE: Adaptive Small-World Encoder for Efficient Channel Coding
authors: Shubham Dwivedi, Om Jee Pandey, Rajesh M. Hegde
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Neural channel encoders must efficiently spread information across message dimensions to ensure robustness under noise, especially for large symbol alphabets. We propose an encoder architecture inspired by small-world networks, combining local and global mixing within a compact residual design. Each message is mapped to a power-constrained codeword using residual blocks composed of (i) adaptive multi-dilation circular depthwise convolutions and (ii) sparse long-range links grouped by hop distance with shared parameters. The resulting Adaptive Small-World Encoder (ASWE) maintains low parameter and compute cost while enabling efficient information propagation. We evaluate several ASWE variants, including ones with annealed rewiring and increased link density. Experiments across symbol sizes M = 64,256,1024 show that ASWE consistently outperforms low-rank and fixed-kernel baselines, and rivals dense encoders despite using significantly fewer parameters and lower or matched compute. Gains are most pronounced at moderate and large M, where structured long-range links enhance mixing. These results highlight the utility of small-world connectivity in designing efficient neural encoders.

title: HFMCA: Orthonormal Feature Learning for EEG-Based Brain Decoding
authors: Yinghao Wang, Lintao Xu, Shujian Yu, Enzo Tartaglione, Van-Tam Nguyen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) analysis is critical for brain-computer interfaces and neuroscience, but the intrinsic noise and high dimensionality of EEG signals hinder effective feature learning. We propose a self-supervised framework based on the Hierarchical Functional Maximal Correlation Algorithm (HFMCA), which learns orthonormal EEG representations by enforcing feature decorrelation and reducing redundancy. This design enables robust capture of essential brain dynamics for various EEG recognition tasks. We validate HFMCA on two benchmark datasets, SEED and BCIC-2A, where pretraining with HFMCA consistently outperforms competitive self-supervised baselines, achieving notable gains in classification accuracy. Across diverse EEG tasks, our method demonstrates superior cross-subject generalization under leave-one-subject-out validation, advancing state-of-the-art by 2.71% on SEED emotion recognition and 2.57% on BCIC-2A motor imagery classification. Our code and supplementary material are available at: https://github.com/W-Yinghao/HFMCA_EEG.

title: EchoFake: A Replay-Aware Dataset For Practical Speech Deepfake Detection
authors: Tong Zhang, Yihuan Huang, Yanzhen Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing prevalence of speech deepfakes has raised serious concerns, particularly in real-world scenarios such as telephone fraud and identity theft. While many anti-spoofing systems have demonstrated promising performance on lab-generated synthetic speech, they often fail when confronted with physical replay attacks—a common and low-cost form of attack used in practical settings. Our experiments show that models trained on existing datasets exhibit severe performance degradation, with average accuracy dropping to 59.6% when evaluated on replayed audio. To bridge this gap, we present EchoFake, a comprehensive dataset comprising more than 120 hours of audio from over 13,000 speakers, featuring both cutting-edge zero-shot text-to-speech TTS speech and physical replay recordings collected under varied devices and real-world environmental settings. Additionally, we evaluate three baseline detection models and show that models trained on EchoFake achieve lower average EERs across datasets, indicating better generalization. By introducing more practical challenges relevant to real-world deployment, EchoFake offers a more realistic foundation for advancing spoofing detection methods.

title: FreqMIL: Frequency-Aware Mamba Multi-Instance Learning for Acute Leukemia Typing
authors: Zhaoyi Ye, Liye Mei, Jionghong Li, Yueyun Weng, Hui Shen, Fuling Zhou, Cheng Lei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Acute leukemia (AL) is a heterogeneous hematologic malignancy, where accurate diagnosis requires large-scale data, and the weak labeling problem makes AL typing particularly challenging. To address the above issues, we propose a novel framework integrating optical time-stretch imaging flow cytometry (OTS-IFC) with frequency-aware Mamba multiple instance learning (FreqMIL). We recruit 33 clinical patients, including 7 acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), 18 acute myelogenous leukemia (AML), and 8 healthy donors, and acquire 229,835 high-throughput cell images in a label-free manner. To capture heterogeneous features from low-abundance cell populations, we propose FreqMIL, which enhances feature representation in the frequency domain and models sequential dependencies, enabling robust sample-level AL typing. Experimental results demonstrate that FreqMIL outperforms six representative MIL methods and achieves 92.08% accuracy in AL typing, highlighting its potential for reliable and efficient clinical diagnosis.

title: Multimodal Symptoms-Assisted Depression Detection Using EEG and Eye Movements
authors: Hao-Long Yin, Jian-Ming Zhan, Wei-Long Zheng, Qinyu Lv, Zhenghui Yi, Bao-Liang Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Depression, a widespread mental health disorder, is characterized by persistent sadness and diminished interest in daily activities. Early detection facilitates timely intervention, thereby reducing its negative impact. Electroencephalography (EEG) and eye movements, owing to their non-invasive and cost-effective nature, have gained recognition as reliable biomarkers for detecting depression. Depression symptoms, as prominent external manifestations of the disorder, play a crucial role in its diagnosis. However, limited research has focused on incorporating these symptoms into depression detection tasks. In this study, we collected EEG data and eye movement recordings from 100 participants. In addition to depression diagnoses, we compiled a list of depression-related symptoms, based on assessments made by experienced psychiatrist and responses to clinical scales. To fully explore the potential of symptoms-assisted detection of depression, we propose the Multimodal Symptoms-Assisted Depression Detection (MSADD) model. This model utilizes EEG and eye movements not only to identify symptoms but also to enhance the performance of depression detection through the integration of symptom information.

title: MAGE: A Coarse-to-Fine Speech Enhancer with Masked Generative Model
authors: The Hieu Pham, Tan Dat Nguyen, Phuong Thanh Tran, Joon Son Chung, Duc Dung Nguyen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech enhancement remains challenging due to the trade-off between efficiency and perceptual quality. In this paper, we introduce MAGE, a Masked Audio Generative Enhancer that advances generative speech enhancement through a compact and robust design. Unlike prior masked generative models with random masking, MAGE employs a scarcity-aware coarse-to-fine masking strategy that prioritizes frequent tokens in early steps and rare tokens in later refinements, improving efficiency and generalization. We also propose a lightweight corrector module that further stabilizes inference by detecting low-confidence predictions and re-masking them for refinement. Built on BigCodec and finetuned from Qwen2.5-0.5B, MAGE is reduced to 200M parameters through selective layer retention. Experiments on DNS Challenge and noisy LibriSpeech show that MAGE achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality and significantly reduces word error rate for downstream recognition, outperforming larger baselines. Audio examples are available at https://hieugiaosu.github.io/MAGE.

title: Multi Level Patch-Wise Contrastive Self-Supervised Learning with Dynamic Scale-Aware Attention for Airport Object Detection
authors: Caihua Liu, Wenjing Xue, Lu Yu, Xia Feng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Directly transferring existing object detectors to airport scenes faces challenges such as extreme scale variations and frequent occlusions, which significantly impair the representation of critical objects. To address these issues, we propose a two-stage detection framework specifically designed for airport scenes. In the first stage, a multi-level patch-wise contrastive self-supervised learning is adopted to learn discriminative and semantically rich features at both instance and part levels, greatly enhancing robustness to scale changes and effectively compensating for information loss under occlusion. In the second stage, the pre-trained backbone is adapted to the detection task and augmented with a scale-aware deformable attention module embedded within the encoder. This module dynamically adjusts sampling offsets and adaptively optimizes receptive field sizes across different feature levels, enabling precise alignment and efficient fusion of multi-scale features. Extensive experiments on AGVS-R airport dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing mainstream detectors and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: Case2Knowledge: Structured Knowledge-Infused Prompting for Faithful Radiology Report Generation
authors: Qingqing Gao, Tengfei Liu, Zhongfan Sun, Boyue Wang, Baocai Yin, Zhaohui Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Radiology report generation (RRG) aims to automatically describe imaging studies, reducing radiologists’ workload and improving diagnostic reliability. Despite recent advances, current methods still suffer from hallucinations and semantic inconsistencies, limiting clinical adoption. To address these issues, we present Case2Knowledge (C2K), a retrieval-augmented and knowledge-infused prompting framework for RRG. Specifically, C2K first performs coarse-to-fine retrieval to identify exemplar reports that are both visually and semantically aligned with the query image. The reports are then converted into structured RadGraph-style triplets, filtered by a medical large multimodal model for clinical relevance, correctness, and utility. Finally, these triplets are used to guide the report generation process, producing accurate and faithful descriptions. Experiments on the MIMIC-CXR dataset demonstrate that C2K achieves superior factual accuracy and semantic consistency, highlighting its potential for reliable automated radiology reporting.

title: Hybrid Intelligence Technique for Brain Tumor Detection and Segmentation Using Deep Learning and AI Techniques
authors: D.E.Gnana Shiney, Anil Kumar Muthevi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The accurate detection and segmentation of brain tumor in MRI is important for early diagnosis and planning of treatment, however traditional deep learning approaches are based on separate classifiers or typical encoder-decoder networks tend to exhibit poor contextual reasoning, boundary accuracy and least clinical interpretability. Noise, class imbalance, and heterogeneous tumor morphology make such systems sensitive and result in unstable performance during practical use. To address these issues, this paper proposes a hybrid intelligence system that combines attention transfer learning for tumor detection and a recurrent-inception segmentation network with the help from uncertainty modeling and explainability. The method integrates strong preprocessing, multi-scale feature learning and confidence-aware refinement to enhance reliability under various imaging conditions. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms existing deep learning methods with 99.4 % of detection accuracy, a Dice coefficient of 0.878, sensitivity of 0.903 and specificity of 0.994. The results indicate enhanced robustness, segmentation accuracy, and clinically interpretable decision support.

title: Multimodal Self-Attention Network with Temporal Alignment for Audio-Visual Emotion Recognition
authors: Inyong Koo, Yeeun Seong, Minseok Son, Jaehyuk Jang, Changick Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audio-visual emotion recognition (AVER) methods typically fuse utterance-level features, and even frame-level attention models seldom address the frame-rate mismatch across modalities. In this paper, we propose a Transformer-based framework focusing on the temporal alignment of multimodal features. Our design employs a multimodal self-attention encoder that simultaneously captures intra- and inter-modal dependencies within a shared feature space. To address heterogeneous sampling rates, we incorporate Temporally-aligned Rotary Position Embeddings (TaRoPE), which implicitly synchronize audio and video tokens. Furthermore, we introduce a Cross-Temporal Matching (CTM) loss that enforces consistency among temporally proximate pairs, guiding the encoder toward better alignment. Experiments on CREMA-D and RAVDESS datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over recent baselines, suggesting that explicitly addressing frame-rate mismatch helps preserve temporal cues and enhances cross-modal fusion.

title: Quality Enhancement for Anomaly Detection via Injective Linear Attention
authors: Zhenchao Ma, Hamid Reza Tohidypour, Panos Nasiopoulos, Victor C. M. Leung
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Anomaly detection in computer vision aims to identify rare abnormal patterns among normal samples. While deep learning methods, including CNNs and Transformers, have achieved strong performance in this area, their effectiveness degrades on compressed inputs commonly encountered in real-world scenarios due to bandwidth and storage constraints. To address this, we propose an injective linear attention-based quality enhancement framework for anomaly detection. In the first stage, a quality enhancement module restores compressed images by efficiently capturing global dependencies. In the second stage, a frozen anomaly detection model guides the optimization of the enhancement module, tailoring restoration specifically to improve detection performance rather than visual fidelity. Experiments on the MVTecAD dataset under varying JPEG compression levels demonstrate substantial improvements: at QF10, image-level AUROC increases from 0.723 to 0.884 (+22.3%) and pixel-level AUROC from 0.831 to 0.932 (+12.2%), showing that our framework effectively mitigates compression-induced degradation while enhancing both detection accuracy and localization.

title: D3PIA: A Discrete Denoising Diffusion Model for Piano Accompaniment Generation from Lead Sheet
authors: Eunjin Choi, Hounsu Kim, Hayeon Bang, Taegyun Kwon, Juhan Nam
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generating piano accompaniments in the symbolic music domain is a challenging task that requires producing a complete piece of piano music from given melody and chord constraints, such as those provided by a lead sheet. In this paper, we propose a discrete diffusion-based piano accompaniment generation model, D3PIA, leveraging local alignment between lead sheet and accompaniment in piano-roll representation. D3PIA incorporates Neighborhood Attention (NA) to both encode the lead sheet and condition it for predicting note states in the piano accompaniment. This design enhances local contextual modeling by efficiently attending to nearby melody and chord conditions. We evaluate our model using the POP909 dataset, a widely used benchmark for piano accompaniment generation. Objective evaluation results demonstrate that D3PIA preserves chord conditions more faithfully compared to continuous diffusion–based and Transformer-based baselines. Furthermore, a subjective listening test indicates that D3PIA generates more musically coherent accompaniments than the comparison models.

title: Resolving Low-Rank Update Limitations for Memory-Efficient Visual Neural Network Training
authors: YuChuan Wang, Jingyu Wang, Qi Qi, Zirui Zhuang, Yiyin Xie, Haifeng Sun, Bo He, Yuhao Li, Jianxin Liao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Training large visual neural networks is highly memory-intensive, with optimizer states often acting as the main bottleneck. While memory-efficient subspace optimizers (MeSO) alleviate this by projecting gradients into low-dimensional subspaces, they severely limit updates to restricted subspaces—this degrades optimization expressivity and traps pretraining in suboptimal regions, a critical issue for visual tasks requiring broad parameter exploration. To address this long-standing limitation, we propose ViLaX(Visual Low-rank to High-rank Update eXtension), a novel dual-factor reparameterization framework that achieves low-rank gradient compression while decoupling the optimizer update rank. By factorizing each weight matrix into two low-rank components and applying independent MeSO updates to each, ViLaX enables high-rank effective updates to the original weights, a key innovation that preserves memory efficiency without sacrificing optimization capability. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny ImageNet demonstrate ViLaX’s superiority: it outperforms state-of-the-art low-rank MeSO methods in pretraining accuracy, solving the critical memory-expressivity tradeoff that has challenged visual neural network training.

title: DAM: Dual Active Learning with Multimodal Foundation Model for Source-free Domain Adaptation
authors: Xi Chen, Hongxun Yao, Zhaopan Xu, Kui Jiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Source-free active domain adaptation (SFADA) enhances knowledge transfer from a source model to an unlabeled target domain using limited manual labels selected via active learning. While recent domain adaptation studies have introduced Vision-and-Language (ViL) models to improve pseudo-label quality or feature alignment, they often treat ViL-based and data supervision as separate sources, lacking effective fusion. To overcome this limitation, we propose Dual Active learning with Multimodal (DAM) foundation model, a novel framework that integrates multimodal supervision from a ViL model to complement sparse human annotations, thereby forming a dual supervisory signal. DAM initializes stable ViL-guided targets and employs a bidirectional distillation mechanism to foster mutual knowledge exchange between the target model and the dual supervisions during iterative adaptation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DAM consistently outperforms existing methods and sets a new state-of-the-art across multiple SFADA benchmarks and active learning strategies.

title: Spiking Neural Networks for Ordinal Regression
authors: Wenwu Ma, Ana Filipa Sequeira, Jaime S. Cardoso
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In numerous predictive tasks, categories inherently possess ordinal relationships that, when properly leveraged during model training, can substantially enhance model performance. However, conventional classification loss functions, such as cross-entropy (CE), disregard these ordinal relationships, resulting in suboptimal performance. While recently proposed loss functions, such as Wasserstein Regularization, can exploit the relative ordering information embedded in class labels, they introduce additional computational overhead and consequently increase energy consumption. This paper addresses this challenge by replacing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with spiking neural networks (SNNs) to reduce the energy requirements during model training and inference. To the best of our knowledge, this work represents the first application of SNNs to ordinal regression tasks. Our primary contribution is the successful deployment of SNNs for ordinal regression tasks, demonstrating competitive performance comparable to traditional deep learning approaches while achieving substantial reductions in energy consumption. Specifically, our SNN-based models outperform architecturally equivalent CNNs on several benchmark datasets.

title: Dust Storm Anomaly Detection on Mars With Event Camera
authors: Haosheng Chen, Chentong Xu, Ji Gan, Jiaxu Leng, Xinbo Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Martian dust storms pose severe challenges to rover exploration by causing dust occlusion that often impairs conventional cameras. To address this, we present the EVent Mars Dataset (EVMars), the first multimodal dataset that synchronizes color event camera recordings with air particle sensor measurements under simulated dust storm conditions. Based on EVMars, we propose a method termed EVent Anomaly Detection (EVAD), which incorporates color event signal processing to construct robust event representation and employs feature fusion enhanced by Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) and Efficient Multi-scale Attention (EMA) mechanisms for particulate matter estimation and anomaly detection. Experiments and ablation studies demonstrate that color-aware signal processing combined with SE and EMA attention mechanisms substantially improves prediction accuracy over single-channel baselines. Our method provides an efficient and reliable solution for perception in Martian dust storms, advancing event-based multimodality fusion for autonomous planetary exploration. Our dataset has been publicly released at https://github.com/xuchentong/EVMars-Anomaly.

title: CodEOE: A Benchmark for Jointly Extracting Cross-Document Events and Opinions From Social Media
authors: Zixuan Wang, Yun Hu, Mengying Yuan, Kang He, Fei Li, Huisheng Ma, Donghong Ji, Chong Teng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Event extraction and opinion/sentiment analysis have been extensively studied within recent decades, but their joint research remains an under-explored area. To bridge this gap, we introduce a challenge Cross-Document Event-Opinion Extraction (CodEOE) task, which requires a model extracting event triggers and arguments as well as their associated opinions or sentiments by understanding cross-document long contexts. We manually construct a high-quality bilingual CodEOE dataset in both Chinese and English with 6,000+ event trigger-argument pairs and 4,000+ trigger-opinion-sentiment triplets. We also develop an end-to-end grid-tagging model to benchmark the task, which jointly extracts event and opinion elements. The results of our model are comparable to those of strong baselines such as large language models. This new benchmark will advance research on both information extraction and sentiment analysis, which are very important domains of NLP. Our data, code and supplementary materials are available at https://github.com/osakana373/CodEOE.

title: PTSWIN-Net: Parallel Temporal Swin-Net for Multi-Scale EEG Emotion Recognition
authors: Ruijie He, Xin Wen, Yan Niu, Yanrong Hao, Mengni Zhou, Rui Cao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Emotion recognition is crucial in healthcare. Existing EEG-based studies often emphasize spatiotemporal features but neglect dynamic variations that carry key emotional cues. To address this, we propose PTSwin-Net, a multi-scale framework with three components: the Infinity Mask Alignment Method (IMAM), the Parallel Temporal Swin Module, and the Time-Adaptive Linear (T-A Linear) classifier. IMAM enhances dataset consistency, the Parallel Temporal Swin Module extracts spatial–temporal features via dual Swin Transformer branches and 3D convolution fusion, and T-A Linear adapts classification to temporal dynamics. On SEED and SEED-IV, PTSwin-Net achieves 93.92% and 86.76% accuracy, demonstrating its robustness and effectiveness in modeling emotional variations.

title: AI-Driven Industrial Robotic Sorting with Real-Time Object Detection and PLC Control for Smart Manufacturing
authors: Phahuthon Sonpim, Thiraphat Thepsutham, Pakpoom Susansuk, Thanatsorn Chaisirithungnaklang, Kontorn Chamniprasart, Anawin Pechbooranin
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This paper presents the development of an AI-driven robotic sorting system optimized for real-time industrial automation. The system integrates a lightweight deep learning model, YOLOv11n, for high-speed object detection on a conveyor, enabling accurate classification and localization of workpieces. Detected coordinates are transformed into real-world positions through homography mapping and sent to a Dobot CR5 collaborative robot, which performs class-based pick-and-place operations via a Python control interface. A Mitsubishi FX5U PLC manages conveyor logic, I/O signaling, and system synchronization using a hybrid communication scheme combining Modbus TCP/IP and GPIO triggers to ensure low-latency, closed-loop execution. Experimental trials across varying lighting and object conditions achieved a pick-and-place success rate of 96.7% and demonstrated real-time responsiveness, confirming the system's reliability and scalability. The proposed architecture is well suited for smart manufacturing, offering a modular and adaptive framework aligned with Industry 4.0 standards.

title: Fractional Programming Approach to Radial Distribution Reconfiguration via Dinkelbach's Algorithm
authors: T. D. Sudhakar, Jayaram M, Manoj Kumar V, Prabakaran Paranthaman, V. Senthil Kumaran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Distribution reconfiguration involves altering the status of switches to optimize power flow and reduce losses while ensuring the network remains radial and voltage levels stay within allowed limits. This study applies Dinkelbach's Algorithm, a fractional programming optimization technique, to identify the best switch configuration for minimizing losses. The process is divided into three stages: first, Dinkelbach's Algorithm selects potential tie switches; second, the selected configuration is tested for radiality and iterated if it fails this constraint; finally, Forward Sweep load flow analysis evaluates the system losses, and the procedure repeats until losses are minimized. This approach ensures that the optimization adheres to operational constraints, making it practical for real-world Distribution Networks (DN). The method has been implemented in MATLAB and tested on a 33-bus single-feeder DN, chosen because it simplifies the model by excluding power balance considerations across multiple feeders. The results confirm the algorithm's effectiveness in systematically determining an improved switch status that reduces power losses while maintaining network integrity, demonstrating its applicability for distribution system operation and planning. This approach combines the mathematical rigor of Dinkelbach's fractional optimization with the computational efficiency of the Forward Sweep method, offering a robust solution for loss minimization inDN reconfiguration.

title: Discrete-Phase Metasurfaces-Aided Index Modulation for Practical RIS Deployments
authors: Bibin Baby John, Yash Vasavada
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel discrete-phase metasurfaces (MTS)-aided index modulation (DP-MIM) scheme that exploits practical <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$B$</tex>-bit phase shifters for information transmission. Unlike conventional MTS-assisted systems that optimize phase configurations for channel enhancement, our scheme directly encodes information bits into the selection of MTS phase patterns across sectioned elements. The proposed approach eliminates the need for complex MTS phase optimization and individual channel estimation, as only the joint cascade channel needs to be estimated at the receiver. We extend the basic DP-MIM scheme by integrating space shift keying (SSK) to form a hybrid DP-MIM-SSK architecture that enhances spectral efficiency (SE) while operating under limited system resources and design constraints. A theoretical analysis of the pairwise squared Euclidean distance (PSED) and a union bound on the bit error rate (BER) is presented. Simulation results validate our analytical framework and show that the proposed scheme achieves high spectral efficiency with practical hardware constraints and manageable detection complexity. The simplicity and practicality of the DP-MIM scheme make it a viable candidate for real-world MTS deployments.

title: Dual Channel Squeeze-Excitation Based Res-Net Architecture for Acute Ischemic Stroke Lesion Segmentation Using Diffusion-Weighted Imaging
authors: Manukonda Avinash, Tripti Goel, Rabul H Laskar
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: Ischemic stroke has the highest mortality rate, mostly due to the lack of fast and accurate quantification of stroke volume. Many deep learning models are good at segmentation, but when it comes to the stroke lesions, they often struggle with the lesion boundaries. They overlook smaller areas and frequently confuse surrounding healthy brain matter due to the low soft tissue contrast. To overcome these drawbacks, the ResUnet model with Dual Squeeze-and-Excitation (DSE) as a bottleneck has been proposed. The model is integrated with a hybrid loss function of Dice loss, Binary cross-entropy, and Boundary loss. By using this, this model has accomplished a Dice similarity coefficient of 89% by experimenting on the ISLES 2022 dataset, which has an improvement of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> over other state-of-the-art models. This proposed architecture demonstrates an improvement in the segmentation stroke lesion along with a sharp boundary. This improvement ensures precise segmentation of infarcted regions, helping clinicians with diagnosis.

title: Conflict-Aware Client Selection for Multi-Server Federated Learning
authors: Mingwei Hong, Zheng Lin, Zehang Lin, Lin Li, Miao Yang, Xia Du, Zihan Fang, Zhaolu Kang, Dianxin Luan, Shunzhi Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has emerged as a promising distributed machine learning (ML) that enables collaborative model training across clients without exposing raw data, thereby preserving user privacy and reducing communication costs. Despite these benefits, traditional single-server FL suffers from high communication latency due to the aggregation of models from a large number of clients. While multi-server FL distributes workloads across edge servers, overlapping client coverage and uncoordinated selection often lead to resource contention, causing bandwidth conflicts and training failures. To address these limitations, we propose a decentralized reinforcement learning with conflict risk prediction, named RL-CRP, to optimize client selection in multi-server FL systems. Specifically, each server estimates the likelihood of client selection conflicts using a categorical hidden Markov model based on its sparse historical client selection sequence. Then, a fairness-aware reward mechanism is incorporated to promote long-term client participation for minimizing training latency and resource contention. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed RL-CRP framework effectively reduces inter-server conflicts and significantly improves training efficiency in terms of convergence speed and communication cost.

title: Balancing Accuracy and Diversity: Evolving Anchor Matching for Video Temporal Grounding
authors: Xiaolong Sun, Bo Xu, Ziyi Liu, Cheng Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video temporal grounding is a challenging task that aims to localize the moment spans relevant to a language description. Recent DETR-based methods typically adopt prediction-based matching to assign predictions to groundtruth moments. However, this often causes the prediction clustering phenomenon, which reduces output diversity and increases classification difficulty. Motivated by previous work on predefined anchors, we investigate an alternative anchor-based matching strategy. This strategy preserves prediction diversity, but the excessive sparsity of anchors can compromise prediction accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel matching strategy, Evolving Anchor Matching (EAM), to balance prediction accuracy and diversity for video temporal grounding. Our method allows predefined anchors to evolve and redistribute themselves under specific scenes, thereby enhancing prediction accuracy compared to anchor-based matching. To further mitigate the prediction clustering issue that emerges in the anchor evolution process, we select distinct anchors before matching them with the groundtruth, resulting in a notable scoring performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of EAM, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on three public benchmarks.

title: OIRS-Assisted Hybrid THz/VLC Communication Under the Impact of Human Blockages
authors: Hanshita Prabhakar, Govind Kumar Jha, Vivek Ashok Bohara
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: The terahertz (THz) and visible light communication (VLC) technologies are expected to play a pivotal role in sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks, owing to their ability to support ultra-high data rates and wide spectrum availability. Integrating THz/VLC with optical intelligent reflecting surfaces (OIRS) has emerged as a promising approach for next-generation. In this work, we investigate an OIRS-assisted hybrid terahertz/visible light communicationaccess point (THz/VLC-AP) system under the impact of human blockages. While THz/VLC-AP links provide line-of-sight (LoS) transmission, the OIRS enhances non-line-of-sight (NLoS) communication. Consequently, the overall signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is derived by jointly accounting for both the direct THz/VLC-AP link and the OIRS-assisted signal components. Comprehensive performance evaluations are carried out to analyze key metrics, including spectral efficiency (SE), outage probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{o}$</tex>), blockage density (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda_{B}$</tex>), and the number of OIRS elements.

title: SUSTAIN-VLM: Coordinated Data and Compute for Low-Carbon Vision Language Model Fine-Tuning
authors: Hassan Khan, Sunbal Iftikhar, Steven Davy, John G. Breslin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Training and fine-tuning vision–language models (VLMs) consume substantial energy, while grid carbon intensity varies by time and region. We present SUSTAIN-VLM, a carbon-elastic framework that co-adapts (i) data selection via a carbon-elastic curriculum (CEC) and (ii) model compute via emission-adaptive knobs (EACK: resolution, precision, MoE top-k, LoRA rank, checkpointing), orchestrated by a constrained RL controller under real-time carbon signals. SUSTAIN-VLM achieves near-iso-accuracy (–0.7 pts vs. Vanilla) while cutting emissions by ∼52% relative to Vanilla and ∼31% vs. static MoE; it also yields the best emission efficiency (kgCO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>e per %-accuracy). Results indicate that coordinating what to train and how hard to train across carbon conditions outperforms single-axis strategies such as fixed mixed precision, static MoE, or time-of-day scheduling.

title: Dynamic-AF: A Photoplethysmography-Based Atrial Fibrillation Burden Evolution Model in A Post-Ablation Cohort
authors: Xuenan Liu, Yuanyuan Wu, Jingwen Wang, Chen Cao, Yan Wei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transitioning from static binary classification to dynamic monitoring, this study addresses the critical need for continuous atrial fibrillation (AF) burden evolution mapping in a post-ablation cohort. We propose Dynamic-AF, a hierarchical photoplethysmography (PPG) framework operating through three integrated tiers: (1) Daily Snapshot employing multi-layer perceptron with Monte Carlo dropout for probabilistic AF classification; (2) Weekly Burden computed via Bayesian fusion of daily probabilities; (3) Trajectory Prediction utilizing dilated temporal convolutional networks for progression modeling. Prospective validation on 400 subjects (200 persistent AF) demonstrated 0.963 sensitivity and 0.958 specificity in daily detection with 5.8% ambiguous samples excluded; longitudinal analysis of 40 post-ablation patients showed 0.903 Area Under Receiver Operating Curve (AUC) for 12-week deterioration prediction, significantly outperforming conventional methods (∆AUC=0.114 vs. frequency-based transformer, p<0.001).

title: WEEP: A Differentiable Nonconvex Sparse Regularizer via Weakly-Convex Envelope
authors: Takanobu Furuhashi, Hidekata Hontani, Qibin Zhao, Tatsuya Yokota
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse regularization is fundamental in signal processing and feature extraction but often relies on non-differentiable penalties, conflicting with gradient-based optimizers. We propose WEEP (Weakly-convex Envelope of Piecewise Penalty), a novel differentiable regularizer derived from the weakly-convex envelope framework. WEEP provides tunable, unbiased sparsity and a simple closed-form proximal operator, while maintaining full differentiability and L-smoothness, ensuring compatibility with both gradient-based and proximal algorithms. This resolves the tradeoff between statistical performance and computational tractability. We demonstrate superior performance compared to established convex and non-convex sparse regularizers on challenging compressive sensing and image denoising tasks.

title: GaRA: Gated Low-Rank Adaptation for Fine-Tuning Time-Series Foundation Models
authors: Leike An, Zhen Li, Shuai Chen, Chaoqun Rao, Linlin Zhang, Tao Tao, Wei Zhu, Shaozhe Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Fine-tuning large pre-trained language models for downstream tasks has emerged as a crucial paradigm in time-series analysis. Traditional methods involve fine-tuning all parameters of a pretrained model, which is impractical. As a representative parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) approach, the low-rank adaptation (LoRA) method incorporates trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, reducing the number of trainable parameters required for downstream tasks. To enhance adaptation performance, recent variants of LoRA focus on rank allocation through pruning, regularization constraints, and importance assessment. However, these approaches often overlook the potential for reactivating the suppressed matrix and evaluate rank allocation solely on the training set, risking overfitting. To tackle these challenges, we propose GaRA, a novel method that introduces a reactivation mechanism, dynamically adjusting the rank of LoRA matrices throughout training and using validation set importance scores. We conduct extensive experiments with several pre-trained models on time-series forecasting tasks to validate GaRA’s effectiveness. Results indicate that GaRA significantly outperforms baseline methods, particularly LoRA and its variants. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/shaozheliu/GaRA.

title: Decoupling Orthogonal Lip Features Against Generative Imposters
authors: Bofan Chen, Hongyu Zhu, Sichu Liang, Shi-Lin Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The proliferation of Talking Face Generation (TFG) techniques presents a formidable challenge to Visual Speaker Authentication (VSA) systems by enabling instant creation of highly realistic imposter videos. Conventional supervised liveness detectors, trained on finite sets of known forgeries, inevitably overfit to generator-specific artifacts and struggle with unforeseen TFG imposters. In this paper, we introduce a paradigm shift away from simple discrimination, seeking instead to model the fundamental deviations between authentic and synthetic lip movements through a rigorous orthogonal decomposition in the latent space. Specifically, we disentangle lip representations into three mutually exclusive subspaces: a shared semantic space, a real-exclusive space capturing genuine biometric patterns, and a forgery-exclusive space encapsulating tell-tale synthesis artifacts. By design, the basis vectors of the real and forgery subspaces are orthogonal complements, preventing cross-domain confounding and promoting generalizable signatures of authenticity and forgery. To further bolster generalization, we equip the forgeries with video-level mixtures, serving as hard negatives that enrich the forgery subspace and encourage the discovery of universal imposter clues. Extensive experiments validate that our detector, trained with only 10 forgery video segments, achieves remarkable seen-to-unseen generalization and surpasses seven state-of-the-art (SOTA) detectors by an average margin of 10% in AUC.

title: MP-CXR: A Modular Pipeline with Multi-Label Disease-State Classification and Image-Label Alignment for Chest X-Ray Report Generation
authors: Haifeng Zhao, Yufei Zhang, Leilei Ma, Shuo Xu, Dengdi Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Automating chest X-ray report generation is crucial, yet current LLM-based approaches remain limited due to complex disease states in images and unaligned, low-quality inputs. In this paper, to capture the refined disease information from medical images, we develop a multi-label disease-state classification approach with disease-state expansion and organ-grouped MLP to characterize clinical nuances. Moreover, for modality alignment, we introduce Optimal Transport (OT) with the improved Sinkhorn algorithm to align image patches with disease-state labels, thereby enhancing LLM input quality. Building on these components, we propose MP-CXR, a novel three-stage modular pipeline: 1) disease-state classification, 2) OT-aligned LLM fine-tuning, and 3) integrated report generation. Unlike monolithic end-to-end training, this MP-CXR pipeline boosts report accuracy, supports independent component upgrades, and is highly scalable. Extensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR and IU X-Ray datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of MP-CXR in both natural language generation (NLG) and clinical efficacy (CE) metrics, delivering reports that are not only linguistically coherent but also clinically reliable.

title: Accommodation of Severe Faults During Multi-Robot Transportation
authors: Chen Wang, Daniela Constantinescu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This paper proposes a planning and control framework that aims to ensure that a multi-robot system supporting an object from below carries it to a target destination despite several robots severely malfunctioning during task execution. The framework proposes a measure of balance stability for the object which serves to identify optimal formations for the healthy robots initially and when some robots suffer severe faults. It then assigns the healthy robots to positions in the new formation based on the Hungarian algorithm. Controls derived from an Improved Artificial Potential Field (IAPF) drive the robots to their positions in the new formation and a strategy for selecting temporary targets helps them escape the local minima of the IAPF during repositioning. Treating the entire system as a rigid body whose steering wheels are the healthy robots lets the IAPF and the temporary target selection strategies steer the system towards the target destination while avoiding collisions with the severely faulty robots. A MATLAB simulation illustrates the feasibility of the proposed framework.

title: Why Temporal Modeling Modules Fall Short in Temporally Sensitive Video-Text Retrieval Tasks
authors: Chen He, Bowen Yang, Yuqi Pang, Yun Cao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in CLIP-based methods have significantly enhanced performance in video-text retrieval tasks, but their efficacy remains unsatisfactory for temporally sensitive scenarios. We identify two key conditions for effective temporal modeling enhancement: 1) Shortcuts for optimizing models based on spatial features need to be suppressed. 2) The CLIP feature space should be preserved during initialization. We propose the CLIP-Preserving Decoupled Temporal Branch (CP-DTB), which uses self-attention and convolution to construct temporal modeling modules, leverages video self-supervised learning to reduce temporal modeling difficulty, and decouples the temporal branch from CLIP’s visual branch to preserve CLIP’s initial feature space. Extensive experiments on the RTime dataset demonstrate the superiority of our method: on RTime-Binary, it achieves 8.6% (T2V) and 3.3% (V2T) R@1 gains over the second-ranked method.

title: Harmonic Summation-Based Robust Pitch Estimation in Noisy and Reverberant Environments
authors: Anup Singh, Kris Demuynck
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: Accurate pitch estimation is essential for numerous speech processing applications, yet it remains challenging in high-distortion environments. This paper proposes a robust pitch estimation method that delivers robust pitch estimates in challenging noise environments. Our approach computes the Normalized Average Magnitude Difference Function (NAMDF), transforms it into a likelihood function, and generates probabilistic pitch states for frames at each sample shift. To enhance noise robustness, we aggregate likelihood values across integer multiples of the pitch period and neighboring frames. Furthermore, we introduce a simple yet effective continuity constraint in the Viterbi algorithm to refine pitch selection among multiple candidates. Experimental results show that our method consistently achieves lower Gross Pitch Error (GPE) and Voicing Decision Error (VDE) across various SNR levels, outperforming existing methods in both noisy and reverberant conditions.

title: ARCTIMESDE: Aligning Compute with Information Via ARC Length Time in Neural SDES
authors: Zongyao Yin, Xianchuan Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Irregularly sampled time series (ISTS) arise in ICU monitoring, finance, and astronomy, where observation density varies sharply across time and channels. Integrating models in physical time ties the step size to sampling gaps rather than local variability, causing overfitting in dense segments and underfitting in sparse ones. We propose ArcTimeSDE, which reparameterizes time by arc-length so that computational density aligns with information density. We build control features via exponential-decay imputation, align them to a uniform arc-length time grid through kernel interpolation, and evolve latent states using a control-driven neural SDE with diagonal or low-rank diffusion. This design reduces resampling bias, and stabilizes long-horizon extrapolation. Across classification and forecasting tasks on irregular and asynchronous data, ArcTimeSDE matches or surpasses strong baselines under sampling sparsity and time-distribution shift while remaining computationally efficient.

title: Equipping Large Language Model with Directional Speech Understanding Capabilities
authors: Ju Lin, Jing Pan, Ruizhi Li, Ming Sun, Yuzong Liu, Alaa Hassan, Jing Zheng, Florian Metze
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that prompting large language models (LLM) with audio encodings enables effective speech understanding capabilities. However, most speech LLMs are trained on single-channel, single-talker data, which makes it challenging to directly apply them to multi-talker and multi-channel speech understanding task. In this work, we present a comprehensive investigation on how to enable directional multi-talker speech understanding capabilities for LLMs, specifically in smart glasses usecase. We propose two novel approaches to integrate directivity into LLMs: (1) a cascaded system that leverages a source separation frontend module, and (2) an end-to-end system that utilizes serialized output training. All of the approaches utilize a multi-microphone array embedded in smart glasses to optimize directivity interpretation and processing in a streaming manner. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of our proposed methods in endowing LLMs with directional speech understanding capabilities, achieving strong performance in both speech recognition and speech translation tasks.

title: Structured Pruning Via Multi-Observation Iterative Hard Thresholding
authors: Huoxiang Yang, Bohuai Xiao, Shuangyan Yi, Binqiang Liu, Fanyang Meng, Wei Liu, Yongsheng Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Most existing structured pruning methods rely on layer-wise heuristics, which often lead to suboptimal pruning ratio allocations and considerable computational overhead during inference. Furthermore, these methods typically exhibit limited architectural flexibility, restricting their applicability beyond convolutional neural networks (CNNs). To address these limitations, we propose a unified structured pruning framework based on Multi-Observation Iterative Hard Thresholding (MO-IHT), which enables consistent and architecture-agnostic pruning across both CNNs and Vision Transformers (ViTs). Specifically, we introduce a loss-aware pruning ratio allocation strategy that estimates pruning sensitivity via per-weight, gradient-based loss variation. In addition, we design a unified feature extraction strategy that generalizes structured pruning across diverse network architectures. Building upon these two strategies, we formulate the pruning task as a sparse feature selection problem and solve it using the proposed MO-IHT algorithm, which jointly optimizes a shared support set across multiple observations. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet confirm the superior accuracy and efficiency of our method under diverse compression ratios.

title: A Statistically Grounded Exploratory Analysis of IoT Network Traffic Using the ACI-IoT-2023 Dataset: Student paper
authors: Kenan Čengić
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: The heterogeneity and scale of Internet of Things (IoT) networks pose significant challenges for intrusion detection systems (IDS), particularly when statistical assumptions underlying common preprocessing and modeling techniques are not validated. This study presents a statistically grounded exploratory analysis of IoT network traffic using the ACI-IoT-2023 dataset of approximately 1.23 million flow records across multiple attack categories. Instead of proposing a detection model, the analysis examines distributional behavior, quantifies feature-target associations using non-parametric effect sizes, and assesses robustness and interpretive limits prior to model design. Results show pervasive non-Gaussian and heavy-tailed behavior across continuous traffic features, challenging parametric assumptions. Effect-size analysis identifies packet-structure and flow-level byte aggregation features as the strongest discriminators between benign and malicious traffic, while temporal dispersion and protocol indicators provide complementary but secondary signal. Robustness analysis confirms the stability of dominant effects, whereas secondary contrasts reveal limited generalization to fine-grained attack differentiation. These findings highlight the importance of statistically rigorous exploratory data analysis (EDA) as a foundation for IDS research in IoT environments. Grounding feature relevance in robust effect sizes and uncertainty quantification provides a statistically defensible foundation for IDS feature engineering, threshold calibration, and downstream model development.

title: GMS-CAVP: Improving Audio-Video Correspondence with Multi-Scale Constrative and Generative Pretraining
authors: Shentong Mo, Zehua Chen, Jun Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in video-audio (V-A) understanding and generation have increasingly relied on joint V-A embeddings, which serve as the foundation for tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and generation. While prior methods like CAVP effectively model semantic and temporal correspondences between modalities using contrastive objectives, their performance remains suboptimal. A key limitation is the insufficient modeling of the dense, multi-scale nature of both video and audio signals, correspondences often span fine- to coarse-grained spatial-temporal structures, which are underutilized in existing frameworks. To this end, we propose GMS-CAVP, a novel framework that combines Multi-Scale Video-Audio Alignment and Multi-Scale Spatial-Temporal Diffusion-based pretraining objectives to enhance V-A correspondence modeling. First, GMS-CAVP introduces a multi-scale contrastive learning strategy that captures semantic and temporal relations across varying granularities. Second, we go beyond traditional contrastive learning by incorporating a diffusion-based generative objective, enabling modality translation and synthesis between video and audio. This unified discriminative-generative formulation facilitates deeper cross-modal understanding and paves the way for high-fidelity generation. Extensive experiments on VGGSound, AudioSet, and Panda70M demonstrate that GMS-CAVP outperforms previous methods in generation and retrieval.

title: Learning to Cascade: A Pomdp Approach to Sequential Model Selection
authors: Ali Salehi Darjani, Daphney-Stavroula Zois
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficient inference is increasingly important as modern models grow in complexity and computational cost. We formulate cascade model evaluation as a sequential decision–making task and, to our knowledge, present the first approach that leverages a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) to route easy samples to simpler models and invoke expensive models only when necessary. Our method achieves strong accuracy–cost trade–offs across homogeneous and heterogeneous model settings. On MNIST, it reduces inference time by over 10×, while on CIFAR-10, it saves 7.9G floating–point operations, achieving 3× faster inference. Compared with a greedy baseline, our approach consistently yields lower run-time at the same accuracy, and with heterogeneous models, it achieves up to 7.7× speedup. These results demonstrate that our POMDP–based cascades provide a principled, flexible, and highly efficient framework for sequential model evaluation under varying computational budgets.

title: Still Thinking or Stopped Talking? Dialogue Silence Intention Classification Using Multimodal Large Language Model
authors: Muyun Wu, Zi Haur Pang, Koji Inoue, Tatsuya Kawahara
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Silence during conversation is a crucial element. Silence may indicate thinking, memory retrieval, or the decision-making process, not just the end of the utterance. Deep understanding of silence is a key to developing natural Spoken Dialogue Systems (SDSs). We frame silence understanding as a multimodal classification task, aiming to distinguish whether a user’s long pause indicates they are Thinking or they have Stopped the utterance. To support this research, we construct a new multimodal dataset collected from 63 speakers interacting with an "attentive listening" system designed to elicit natural pauses. Each silent interval over two seconds is labeled based on preceding Japanese linguistic cues, non-verbal behaviors such as gaze during the pause, and subsequent user actions. Using this dataset, we develop a Multi-modal Large Language Model (MLLM) named SilenceLLM for this audiovisual classification task. Experiments on our dataset show that our model achieves 0.857 marco F1 score after comparing several different Audio Encoders and Visual Encoders, accompanied by a Q-former as a fusion model.

title: Enhance, Then Separate: A Two-Stage Framework for Music Source Restoration
authors: Linfeng Feng, Jiajun Li, Cheng Gong, Shansong Liu, Chi Zhang, Xiao-Lei Zhang, Xuelong Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Conventional music source separation systems typically assume mixtures are linear combinations of dry stems, failing to address the non-linear mastering effects and acoustic degradations inherent in real-world scenarios. To address the ICASSP 2026 music source restoration (MSR) challenge, we propose a two-stage "Enhance-then-Separate" framework. We employ a unified BSRoformer backbone for both stages. The first stage restores the high-fidelity mixture via spectral bandwidth extension, while the second stage isolates the target dry sources from the enhanced mixture. This cascaded approach effectively decouples restoration from separation, enabling robust performance under complex distortions. Our system achieved third place in subjective ranking on the official blind test set.

title: AROMMA: Unifying Olfactory Embeddings for Single Molecules and Mixtures
authors: Dayoung Kang, JongWon Kim, Jiho Park, Keonseock Lee, Ji-Woong Choi, Jinhyun So
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Public olfaction datasets are small and fragmented across single molecules and mixtures, limiting learning of generalizable odor representations. Recent works either learn single-molecule embeddings or address mixtures via similarity or pairwise label prediction, leaving representations separate and unaligned. In this work, we propose AROMMA, a framework that learns a unified embedding space for single molecules and two-molecule mixtures. Each molecule is encoded by a chemical foundation model and the mixtures are composed by an attention-based aggregator, ensuring both permutation invariance and asymmetric molecular interactions. We further align odor descriptor sets using knowledge distillation and class-aware pseudo-labeling to enrich missing mixture annotations. AROMMA achieves state-of-the-art performance in both single-molecule and molecule-pair datasets, with up to 19.1% AUROC improvement, demonstrating a robust generalization in two domains.

title: Multi-Band Frequency Prompt Tuning for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
authors: Rui Wu, Shengwu Xiong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SF-CDFSL) suffers from the absence of source-domain data and the scarcity of target-domain samples, which makes it challenging to transfer domain knowledge and to learn discriminative representations for novel classes in the target domain. To address these issues, We propose Multi-Band Frequency Prompt Tuning (MB-FPT), a prompt-based framework that simultaneously aligns domain information and enhances class discrimination. Specifically, we introduces a Multi-Band Frequency Prompt(MB-FP) module by decomposing images into multiple frequency bands and learning independent prompts for each. Through adaptive fusion, MB-FP leverages complementary frequency-specific representations, thereby strengthening category relevance while mitigating domain misalignment. In addition,, to ensure reliable prompt optimization, we design a Mutual Neighbor Consistency Alignment (MNCA) module, which leverages reciprocal neighbor relationships to discover reliable local structures in the target domain. By refining high-confidence samples and enforcing neighbor-level consistency, MNCA reduces label noise, enforces distributional compactness, and ultimately enhances cross-domain generalization. Experiments on multiple cross-domain few-shot classification benchmarks demonstrate that MB-FPT shows consistent improvements over existing methods.

title: VR Mirror Therapy with Hand Tracking for Stroke Rehabilitation: A Prototype Study
