authors: Kantawit Pitiphongphiphat, Gridsada Phanomchoeng, Kittikul Kovitanggoon, Pravee Kruachottikul
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Stroke is a leading cause of long-term disability, often resulting in impaired upper-limb motor function. Mirror therapy has been widely applied in stroke rehabilitation, but conventional approaches using physical mirrors are limited by fixed viewing angles, task constraints, and lack of quantitative assessment. To address these limitations, this study presents a prototype virtual reality (VR) mirror therapy system implemented on the Oculus Quest 3 with integrated hand tracking. The system captures the motion of the unaffected arm at joint level, mirrors the data across the sagittal plane, and visualizes a virtual arm aligned with the impaired side in real time. Simultaneously, the system records three-dimensional wrist trajectories (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x, y, z$</tex>) and exports quantitative motion data for subsequent analysis. Experimental evaluation using a basic rehabilitation task of repetitive arm lifting demonstrated that the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex>-axis trajectory reliably reflected vertical motion cycles, while the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex> - and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$z$</tex>-axes provided complementary information on stability. The VR environment also reproduced pronation and supination postures, confirming its ability to render complex joint rotations. These results highlight the feasibility of VR-based hand tracking as a tool for both immersive rehabilitation feedback and quantitative motion analysis. Future work will extend the system with more realistic limb visualization, complex tasks, and clinical validation with stroke patients.

title: CAST-ACF: Robust Generation and Evaluation for Multi-Granularity Timeline Summarization
authors: Yuming Ai, Fang Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-Granularity TimeLine Summarization (MGTLS) is a new task in Natural Language Processing (NLP). It aims to create clear timelines with different detail levels from numerous articles. However, two main issues hinder progress. First, there is a lack of specialized methods for generating these timelines. Second, current evaluation metrics have a bias that gives overly optimistic scores. To solve these problems, we introduce a new framework consisting of: (i) Chronology-windowed Abstractive Summarization for multi-granularity Timelines (CAST), an end-to-end generation method using a chronology-windowed approach; and (ii) ACF (Alignment/Coverage/Factuality), an improved evaluation method that assesses timelines without relying on the Hungarian algorithm to ensure fairness. The ACF metrics can better distinguish different systems. At the same time, CAST provides the first specialized generation method for MGTLS. It performs better than baselines overall on three different granularity settings (e.g., improving scores by +27.9%/+60.8%/+46.8% at N/10/5 granularity levels). <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Fine-Grained Hashing Via Center Similarity Guided Quantization
authors: Chao He, Hongxi Wei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Fine-grained image retrieval is a challenging task characterized by small inter-class variation and large intra-class variation. The recent fine-grained hashing methods achieve state-of-the-art performance by constructing proxy vectors (also known as "hash centers") and combining the classification task, which encourages hash codes of similar images to approach their common hash center and distance themselves from other hash centers. Nevertheless, the minimum Hamming distance between hash centers constructed by these methods fails to guarantee the worst-case, i.e., the hash centers can be arbitrarily close to each other. To this end, we propose a Fine-Grained Hashing method via Center Similarity Guided Quantization (FGH). Specifically, a hash center generation method is introduced to constrain the minimum Hamming distance between any pair of hash centers. Moreover, a feature enhancement module is designed to enhance the feature representation capability of the network. We perform comprehensive experiments on four widely-studied fine-grained datasets. Experimental results indicate that our proposed method has superior performance compared to state-of-the-art fine-grained hashing methods. Code is available: https://github.com/shuaichaochao/FGH

title: Positive-and-Multi-Negative Learning with Adaptive Reweighting for Noisy Labels
authors: Chen-Chen Zong, Yu-Qi Chi, Tong Jin, Sheng-Jun Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Loss correction, a typical approach for learning with noisy labels, often designs robust loss functions or reweights example losses to mitigate noise. However, robust losses may underfit clean examples, while reweighting typically requires extra information (e.g., clean validation data) to set weights. To address this, we propose Positive and Multi-Negative Learning with Adaptive Reweighting (PMNAR). PMNAR adapts loss weights using per-example confidence and the overall prediction distribution. By inverting gradient-based weights, it alleviates noise-induced overfitting. Moreover, our positive and multi-negative loss curbs overconfidence and enables accurate per-example weighting. Together, these components exploit clean examples and suppress mislabeled ones. Experiments on benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance.

title: A Training-Free Framework for High-Fidelity Appearance Transfer via Diffusion Transformers
authors: Shengrong Gu, Ye Wang, Song Wu, Rui Ma, Qian Wang, Lanjun Wang, Zili Yi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) excel at generation, but their global self-attention makes controllable, reference-image-based editing a distinct challenge. Unlike U-Nets, naively injecting local appearance into a DiT can disrupt its holistic scene structure. We address this by proposing the first training-free framework specifically designed to tame DiTs for high-fidelity appearance transfer. Our core is a synergistic system that disentangles structure and appearance. We leverage high-fidelity inversion to establish a rich content prior for the source image, capturing its lighting and micro-textures. A novel attention-sharing mechanism then dynamically fuses purified appearance features from a reference, guided by geometric priors. Our unified approach operates at 1024px and outperforms specialized methods on tasks ranging from semantic attribute transfer to fine-grained material application. Extensive experiments confirm our state-of-the-art performance in both structural preservation and appearance fidelity.

title: Deconfusion CLIP Towards Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection
authors: Ziliang Xun, Zhaohui Hu, Long Lan, Wenjing Yang, Guijian Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection aims to identify test samples that fall outside the training distribution, which is crucial for deploying reliable vision-language models in open-world applications. Recent advances in CLIP-based OOD detection have shown promising results via mining OOD cues from in-distribution (ID) data. However, CLIP suffers from inter-class confusion in fine-grained classification tasks, which further undermines the reliability of OOD detection. In this work, we propose a novel framework that employs a few-shot approach to first deconfuse CLIP and then learn prompts that can better distinguish between ID and OOD data. Specifically, we first extract ID-irrelevant regions from ID data as pseudo-OOD samples and use them as negative samples in contrastive learning to deconfuse CLIP, then further leverage these extracted OOD data to learn prompts. Extensive experiments and analyses have been conducted to characterize and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

title: An In-Class Activity Exploring Hash Function Quality
authors: Ellen Spertus, Rasika Bhalerao
publish: N/A
abstract: None

title: Implementation of Low-Power Ultrasonic Water Level Measurement with LoRaWAN
authors: Luka Vidić, Željko Ivanović
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: This paper introduces an implementation of a low-power ultrasonic water level measurement device capable of transferring the data to the remote servers using LoRaWAN network. The device is implemented using PIC24FJ64GA702 microcontroller, RHF76-052 LoRaWAN module and GY-US42v2 ultrasonic sensor module on a custom printed circuit board. Average current consumption of the implemented device is as low as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$39 \mu \mathrm{A}$</tex>. Powering the device with two alkaline C -type batteries results in battery life that spans over several years with the worst-case scenario battery life of almost a year and a half. The device is also capable of measuring water level from distances up to 7.2 meters making it suitable for water level measurement of rivers, lakes and other natural water surfaces.

title: Action-Aware Query Selection and Ambiguous Snippet Disambiguation for Weakly-Supervised Temporal Action Localization
authors: Jiakang Sun, Yong Zhong, Cheng Peng, Xu Liu, Ke Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Weakly-supervised temporal action localization (WTAL) is limited by coarse-grained video-level annotations and still lags behind fully-supervised methods (FTAL). To this end, we propose a novel WTAL framework that integrates generative and regression models within DETR, bridging the gap between WTAL and FTAL. In addition, we introduce the Ambiguous Snippet Disambiguation technique, which improves the quality of pseudo proposals by reducing snippet ambiguity. Finally, we validate our approach on the THUMOS14 and ActivityNet1.3 datasets, and the experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance. Code is available at https://github.com/sjkucas/QSSD.

title: QPnet: Quaternion Physics-Driven Neural Network for Underwater Polarized Image Recovery
authors: Mengyuan Liao, Yuan Fang, Shengxi Li, Xiaolin Huang, Ce Zhu, Yipeng Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep-learning methods have achieved state-of-the-art visual performance for polarized underwater image reconstruction, yet they still face three major challenges: (1) heavy reliance on large-scale labeled datasets, (2) limited physical interpretability, and (3) lack of publicly available real-world polarized underwater datasets. To address these issues, we propose an unsupervised quaternion physics-driven network (QPnet) to alleviate these problems. Our QPnet estimates imaging parameters from a degradation–reconstruction physical model and captures inter-channel couplings of data via quaternion convolutions. By combining imaging physics with quaternion algebra, QPnet reduces the parameter count while improving color consistency, detail preservation, and interpretability compared with existing real-valued networks. In addition, we collect a real-world underwater polarized image dataset for quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QPnet produces clear, artifact-free reconstructions and outperforms existing methods in both perceptual quality and reconstruction fidelity.

title: Enhanced Generative Machine Listener
authors: Vishnu Raj, Gouthaman K V, Shiv Gehlot, Lars Villemoes, Arijit Biswas
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present GMLv2, a reference-based model designed for the prediction of subjective audio quality as measured by MUSHRA scores. GMLv2 introduces a Beta distribution-based loss to model the listener ratings and incorporates additional neural audio coding (NAC) subjective datasets to extend its generalization and applicability. Extensive evaluations on diverse testset demonstrate that proposed GMLv2 consistently outperforms widely used metrics, such as PEAQ and ViSQOL, both in terms of correlation with subjective scores and in reliably predicting these scores across diverse content types and codec configurations. Consequently, GMLv2 offers a scalable and automated framework for perceptual audio quality evaluation, poised to accelerate research and development in modern audio coding technologies.

title: Revisiting the Seasonal Trend Decomposition for Enhanced Time Series Forecasting
authors: Sanjeev Panta, Xu Yuan, Li Chen, Nian-Feng Tzeng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Time series forecasting presents significant challenges in real-world applications across various domains. Building upon the decomposition of the time series, we enhance the architecture of machine learning models for better multivariate time series forecasting. To achieve this, we focus on the trend and seasonal components individually and investigate solutions to predict them with less errors. Recognizing that reversible instance normalization is effective only for the trend component, we take a different approach with the seasonal component by directly applying backbone models without any normalization or scaling procedures. Through these strategies, we successfully reduce error values of the existing state-of-the-art models and finally introduce dualMLP models as more computationally efficient solutions. Furthermore, our approach consistently yields positive results with around 10% MSE average reduction across four state-of-the-art baselines on the benchmark datasets. We also evaluate our approach on a hydrological dataset extracted from the United States Geological Survey (USGS) river stations, where our models achieve significant improvements while maintaining linear time complexity, demonstrating real-world effectiveness. The source code is available at https://github.com/Sanjeev97/Time-Series-Decomposition

title: Exploring the Existence of Over-Squashing In Directed Networks
authors: Kejia Zhu, Minglai Shao, Wenjun Wang, Yueheng Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We identify topological imbalance in semi-supervised node classification, where varying neighborhood structures of labeled nodes cause biased learning. Directionality exacerbates this imbalance, leading to severe over-squashing—yet this issue is largely unaddressed in directed graph learning. This paper is the first to formalize topological imbalance in directed graphs and propose Directed Imbalance Learning (DIL), a novel structure learning framework. DIL incorporates directed graph encoding into deep learning to capture node global positions and edge directionality. By learning an optimized graph structure, DIL alleviates over-squashing and improves information flow. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that DIL effectively models directional dependencies and yields superior node representations, outperforming existing methods in node classification.

title: Symbolic Computation, Standards, & the EU AI Act
authors: James Harold Davenport
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: The EU AI Act is one of the most significant pieces of AI legislation in the world. This is accepted, even if reluctantly, by all in AI. Like other product safety legislation, it is written in fairly general terms, using words such as “unbiased”, leaving it to standards to describe what this actually means in detail. Again, this is unfamiliar to many in AI. We will therefore first look at the Act, and the European standardisation process. In particular, we will look at the various actors in this process. Then we will ask what this has to do with Symbolic Computation: it is the general view that “Artificial Intelligence” as commonly understood, i.e. Machine Learning, has nothing to do with symbolic computation.

title: AnimateScene: Camera-Controllable Animation in Any Scene
authors: Qingyang Liu, Bingjie Gao, Weiheng Huang, Jun Zhang, Zhongqian Sun, Yang Wei, Fengrui Liu, Zelin Peng, Qianli Ma, Shuai Yang, Zhaohe Liao, Haonan Zhao, Li Niu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in 3D scene reconstruction and 4D human animation have broadened adoption, but integrating the two remains difficult. Key challenges include placing humans at plausible locations and scales without interpenetration, aligning lighting and style between humans and background, and handling dynamic camera trajectories. We introduce AnimateScene, a unified framework addressing these issues. First, an accurate placement module automatically determines realistic 3D positions and prevents collisions during motion. Second, we propose a training-free style alignment method that adapts the 4D human to match background illumination and appearance, achieving coherent composites. Finally, we design a joint post-reconstruction approach that incorporates camera trajectories for smooth, visually engaging motion videos. Experiments across diverse scenes and actions demonstrate that AnimateScene generates dynamic results with high geometric detail and strong spatiotemporal coherence. The project page is available at this https URL.

title: RaFD: Flow-Guided Radar Detection for Robust Autonomous Driving
authors: Shuocheng Yang, Zikun Xu, Jiahao Wang, Shahid Nawaz, Jianqiang Wang, Shaobing Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Radar has shown strong potential for robust perception in autonomous driving; however, raw radar images are frequently degraded by noise and "ghost" artifacts, making object detection based solely on semantic features highly challenging. To address this limitation, we introduce RaFD, a radar-based object detection framework that estimates inter-frame bird’s-eye-view (BEV) flow and leverages the resulting geometric cues to enhance detection accuracy. Specifically, we design a supervised flow estimation auxiliary task that is jointly trained with the detection network. The estimated flow is further utilized to guide feature propagation from the previous frame to the current one. Our flow-guided, radar-only detector achieves achieves state-of-the-art performance on the RADIATE dataset, underscoring the importance of incorporating geometric information to effectively interpret radar signals, which are inherently ambiguous in semantics.

title: HyperDeform: A Cross-Level Semantic and Spatial Adaptive Module for Robust Scene Text Detection
authors: Ke Chang, Xiangdong Su, Xingxiang Zhou, Zhi Chen, Minzhi Zhang, Changyan Li, Guanglai Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate scene text detection requires the ability to model complex semantic structures across hierarchical levels and adaptively capture spatial irregularities. In this work, we propose HyperDeform, a novel feature enhancement module that addresses these challenges by unifying high-order semantic modeling and dynamic spatial adaptation. HyperDeform integrates two complementary components: Hypergraph Convolution, which encodes cross-level semantic relationships through high-order connectivity, and Alterable Convolution, which enables flexible sampling to better capture fine-grained spatial cues. This synergy allows the module to simultaneously preserve global semantic coherence and enhance local detail sensitivity. In addition, we identify and address the underexplored issue of boundary discontinuity in scene text detection. We introduce an enhanced angle encoder that incorporates angular continuity priors to produce smoother and more coherent text boundaries. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed framework significantly improves detection accuracy and robustness in and challenging scenarios.

title: MathHalu: A Benchmark for Mathematical Reasoning Process Hallucination Detection in Large Reasoning Models
authors: Bo Zhang, Cong Gao, Bingxu Han, Minghao Hu, Zhunchen Luo, Jun Zhang, Wen Yao, Xiaoying Bai, Guotong Geng, Zhong Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have advanced in information retrieval and complex reasoning, but they remain prone to reasoning hallucinations that weaken logical consistency. Current LLM-as-a-Judge methods detect answer-level hallucinations but overlook hallucinations in LRM's reasoning processes. To address this gap, we present MathHalu, a benchmark for detecting hallucinations in mathematical reasoning. MathHalu distinguishes two categories: cognitive hallucinations, caused by misinterpreting the problem, and logical hallucinations, arising from faulty reasoning steps. We generate hallucinated traces from eight open-source LRMs on GSM8K and MathQA, filter hallucination data using semantic similarity and natural language inference (NLI), and validate labels with LLM voting and human verification. Experiments reveal that LLMs frequently misclassify cognitive hallucinations as logical ones. The benchmark is available at https://github.com/bodoa7/MathHalu

title: Grey-Box Prompt Tuning With Graph Alignment for Speech-Language Models
authors: Yuhang Lu, Li-e Wang, Xianxian Li, Feng Yu, Linghui Meng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and Speech-Language Models (SLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance on tasks such as speech understanding, emotion recognition, and multi-turn dialogue. However, adapting these models to downstream tasks remains costly; full-parameter fine-tuning is often prohibitive, and even parameter-efficient methods such as LoRA still demand substantial computational resources. In contrast to expensive parameter tuning, we adopt a lightweight prompt-tuning approach in a grey-box setting. In this configuration, while the model parameters remain frozen, limited ‘slot’ interfaces enable the injection of external prompts or modules, thereby facilitating cross-modal alignment and task adaptation. Nevertheless, two challenges persist within this framework: (i) gradient-free prompt tuning is often inefficient and unstable, and (ii) insufficient speech–text alignment limits semantic transfer and conditional control. We propose a lightweight adaptation framework operating under grey-box constraints. The framework employs a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to construct a cross-modal adjacency graph in a joint representation space, dynamically aggregating acoustic and textual nodes to achieve robust alignment and fusion. Furthermore, we develop a progressive, gradient-free optimization strategy that improves prompt quality by gradually narrowing the search space via distributional comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that, while adhering to grey-box constraints, our method improves adaptability and robustness on downstream speech-language tasks.

title: Latent Temporal Discrepancy as Motion Prior: A Loss-Weighting Strategy for Dynamic Fidelity in T2V
authors: Meiqi Wu, Bingze Song, Ruimin Lin, Chen Zhu, Xiaokun Feng, Jiahong Wu, Xiangxiang Chu, Kaiqi Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video generation models have achieved notable progress in static scenarios, yet their performance in motion video generation remains limited, with quality degrading under drastic dynamic changes. This is due to noise disrupting temporal coherence and increasing the difficulty of learning dynamic regions. Unfortunately, existing diffusion models rely on static loss for all scenarios, constraining their ability to capture complex dynamics. To address this issue, we introduce Latent Temporal Discrepancy (LTD) as a motion prior to guide loss weighting. LTD measures frame-to-frame variation in the latent space, assigning larger penalties to regions with higher discrepancy while maintaining regular optimization for stable regions. This motion-aware strategy stabilizes training and enables the model to better reconstruct high-frequency dynamics. Extensive experiments on the general benchmark VBench and the motion-focused VMBench show consistent gains, with our method outperforming strong baselines by 3.31% on VBench and 3.58% on VMBench, achieving significant improvements in motion quality.

title: Short-Segment Speaker Verification with Pre-Trained Models and Multi-Resolution Encoder
authors: Jisoo Myoung, Sangwook Han, Kihyuk Kim, Jong Won Shin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker verification (SV) utilizing features obtained from models pre-trained via self-supervised learning has recently demonstrated impressive performances. However, these pre-trained models (PTMs) usually have a temporal resolution of 20 ms, which is lower than typical filterbank features. It may be problematic especially for short-segment SV with an input segment shorter than 2 s, in which we need to extract as much information as possible from the input with a limited length. Although there have been approaches to utilize multi-resolution features from the HuBERT models, the window shifts were 20, 40, and 100 ms when the sampling rate was 16 kHz and thus only lower resolution features were considered. In this study, we propose an SV system which utilizes PTM features along with filterbank features and those from the multi-resolution time domain encoder with window shifts of 1.56, 3.13, 6.25, and 12.5 ms. Experimental results on the VoxCeleb dataset with various input lengths showed consistent improvements over systems with various combinations of input features.

title: Reg3D: Reconstructive Geometry Instruction Tuning for 3D Scene Understanding
authors: Hongpei Zheng, Lintao Xiang, Lin Qian, Zhenghao Li, Qijun Yang, Hujun Yin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The rapid development of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) has led to notable advances in 2D visual understanding, however, extending these capabilities to 3D scene comprehension remains a significant challenge. Existing methods primarily rely on text-only supervision, which lacks the geometric constraints essential for learning accurate and robust 3D spatial representations. To address this limitation, we introduce Reg3D, a novel Reconstructive Geometry Instruction Tuning framework that integrates geometry-aware supervision directly into model training. Unlike previous approaches that inject 3D information solely at the input level, Reg3D adopts a dual-supervision paradigm, utilizing 3D geometry both as input and as explicit training targets. We design complementary object-level and frame-level reconstruction tasks within a dual-encoder architecture to enforce geometric consistency and enhance spatial reasoning. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets: ScanQA, Scan2Cap, ScanRefer, and SQA3D demonstrate that with only RGB input, Reg3D outperforms existing methods, establishing a new paradigm for spatially grounded multimodal learning.

title: Sliding-Cache VLA: Training-Free Acceleration of Vision Language Action Models via Foreground-Background Decoupling
authors: Yunqing Jiang, Xiangyuan Chen, Huijia Zhao, Tianlin Liang, Yanyan Liang, Chio-In Ieong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vision Language Action (VLA) models have demonstrated remarkable potential in robotic applications, yet they encounter substantial latency issues when deployed in real-time scenarios. While current acceleration approaches prove effective, they typically demand additional training procedures that constrain deployment flexibility. To tackle this challenge, we propose Sliding-Cache VLA, an efficient acceleration framework that exploits input redundancy through intelligent resource allocation, eliminating the need for supplementary training. Specifically, our approach implements foreground-background decoupling to concentrate computational resources on dynamic foreground elements while efficiently caching activations for static background regions. Furthermore, we present a sliding-window technique incorporating an exponential decay mechanism, which effectively filters out variations irrelevant to the task, thereby enhancing model performance. Extensive experimental assessments conducted on both simulated and real-world robotic platforms that our acceleration framework significantly reduces inference latency while maintaining model performance.

title: Hypergraph-Based Asymmetric Embedding Framework for Attribute-Missing Graph Clustering
authors: Ze Yang, Changjian Wang, Yuanpu Cai, Tiange Jiao, Weiguo Chen, Shijun Li, Tianpeng Yang, Zhengsheng Peng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In deep graph clustering, attribute missing problem severely hinders the effectiveness of clustering methods in real-world applications. Therefore, various methods based on generative and contrastive learning have been proposed. However, existing methods primarily rely on adding random noise to graph structures to construct views for contrastive training. This strategy suffers from two limitations: 1) model performance is sensitive to its perturbation extent, 2) the randomness of noise often deviates from real-world patterns. To address this problem, we propose a Hypergraph-Based Asymmetric Embedding (HyperAE) framework, which incorporates hyper-graph to form a heterogeneous view alongside the original adjacency graph. In this framework, we propose an Asymmetric Propagation Alignment (APA) mechanism to align effective components, and a Dual Embedding Generation (DEG) mechanism for joint attribute structure reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that HyperAE consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across various clustering evaluation metrics. Corresponding code is available at: https://github.com/accrusher/HyperAE.

title: Closed-Form Ziv-Zakai Bound for Compressive Time Delay Estimation
authors: Sihan Wen, Zongyu Zhang, Chengwei Zhou, Zhiguo Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Compressed sensing (CS) techniques have been adopted to time delay estimation problems, allowing the utilization of wider band signals for improved performance. However, the incorporation of CS compromises system robustness to noise. In such cases, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) threshold, below which the estimation performance rapidly degrades, becomes an important factor for system design. To study this, Ziv-Zakai bound (ZZB) has been adopted due to its well-known global tightness, which accurately identifies this threshold. Nevertheless, the existing ZZB relies on numerical integration. The SNR threshold is not analytically available and can only be determined graphically from the ZZB curve. This paper overcomes this limitation by deriving a closed-form expression for the ZZB in this context. Simulation results demonstrate that the derived ZZB is consistent with its numerical counterpart, and reveal that the impact of CS on the SNR threshold can be described by a factor termed k<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Φ</inf>.

title: Understanding Frechet Speech Distance for Synthetic Speech Quality Evaluation
authors: June-Woo Kim, Dhruv Agarwal, Federica Cerina
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Objective evaluation of synthetic speech quality remains a critical challenge. Human listening tests are the gold standard, but costly and impractical at scale. Fréchet Distance has emerged as a promising alternative, yet its reliability depends heavily on the choice of embeddings and experimental settings. In this work, we comprehensively evaluate Fréchet Speech Distance (FSD) and its variant Speech Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SMMD) under varied embeddings and conditions. We further incorporate human listening evaluations alongside TTS intelligibility and synthetic-trained ASR WER to validate the perceptual relevance of these metrics. Our findings show that WavLM Base+ features yield the most stable alignment with human ratings. While FSD and SMMD cannot fully replace subjective evaluation, we show that they can serve as complementary, cost-efficient, and reproducible measures, particularly useful when large-scale or direct listening assessments are infeasible. Code is available at https://github.com/kaen2891/FrechetSpeechDistance.

title: Exploiting Scatterers for Sensing Security in ISAC Systems
authors: Jiangong Chen, Xia Lei, Christos Masouros
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper explores sensing security in Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) scenarios by exploiting known scatterers in the environment. Specifically, we propose an Angle-of-Arrival (AoA) deception scheme in which scatterers are deliberately illuminated with higher probing power than the targets of interest, aiming to deceive potential Eavesdroppers (Eves) with sensing capability into misidentifying scatterers as targets. The legitimate sensing receiver, with prior information about the known scatterers, can suppress clutter interference. To strike a flexible three-way tradeoff among communication, sensing, and sensing-security performance, we optimize communication sum-rate, power allocated to scatterers, and legitimate radar Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) jointly by Fractional Programming (FP) and Semidefinite Relaxation (SDR) techniques. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed AoA deception scheme flexibly adapts beamforming strategy to meet performance demands, achieving the desired three-way tradeoff. Notably, it strengthens clutter interference at Eve’s side, resulting in confusion or even missed detection of the actual target.

title: Robust DOA Estimation with Unknown Source Number Via Virtual ULA Beamforming
authors: Lixin Zheng, Yafeng Zhan, Zhen Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper addresses robust direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation with unknown source number, under challenging conditions including colored noise, array imperfections and source power imbalance. We employ a minimum redundancy array (MRA) to synthesize a virtual uniform linear array (ULA), which facilitates the generation of high-gain, narrow beams through effective aperture extension. These high-quality beams partition the surveillance region into non-overlapping sectors, enabling isolated detection of source presence within each sector. High-precision DOA estimation is performed within identified active sectors, leveraging the beam-guided spatial prior for reliable and accurate estimation. In mobile tracking simulations under non-ideal conditions, the proposed method achieves 19.30% and 33.99% higher successful estimation rates than conventional subspace-based and sparse-based methods, respectively.

title: Belief Propagation Via Stochastic Transport Mapping
authors: Jiazhen Song, Zhuoyue Shi, Girim Kwon, Yuhan Dong, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Zhenyu Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Belief propagation (BP) is a versatile inference methodology widely applied in domains such as information fusion, channel decoding, and cooperative localization. The core task of BP is to compute variable node beliefs. However, BP involves intractable integrals in nonlinear and non-Gaussian scenarios, thereby limiting the accuracy and incurring high complexity for existing methods. To address these limitations, we propose a framework named belief propagation via stochastic transport mapping (STMBP). This framework reformulates belief update as computing marginal posteriors from priors. A stochastic transport mapping is then constructed to push prior samples to posterior samples, from which variable-specific components are extracted to obtain the marginals. We showcase the application of STMBP in a cooperative localization scenario, where simulations show that STMBP achieves consistently lower estimation root-mean-square error (RMSE) with reduced computational time, compared to particle BP.

title: Comparative Study of Modeled and Measured Generation Data for Ground-Mount PV Power Plants Regarding Commercial and Free Software
authors: Ivan Ramljak, Marko Blažević
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: In this paper a comparison of real PV power plant generation and software modeled generation is presented. Three PV power plants in various locations in Bosnia and Herzegovina were analyzed. PV power plants are different regarding module type, rated power, DC/AC ratio etc. PV power plants were modeled in four (4) different software: PVGIS, SMA SUNNY DESIGN, PVSYST and HELIOSCOPE. The software is quite different regarding modeling flow and input data. Real terrain data of PV power plants were available with technical details. Independently of the software, special attention was given to PV power plants modeling regarding software capabilities. The goal was to analyze differences between real generation and software predictions. One year of real data was available. One-year period and monthly values were analyzed. Additionally, comparison of real generation data for three presented PV plants was performed regarding uniform generation parameter MWh/MWp.

title: Blind Online Neural Recovery of Radar Waveforms from Linear Projections of Interleaved ADC Measurements
authors: Subrahmanya Teza Bhamidi, Sri Rama Murty Kodukula
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose using a 2-layer GRU to recover denoised RADAR waveforms online from streams of linear projections of their noisy measurements by an interleaved ADC. Our approach jointly learns a measurement projection and a recurrent neural network to perform blind, denoised, online recovery of radar signals for ELINT and passive RADAR applications. We trained two sets of models, each for baseband waveforms and passband waveforms, respectively. We benchmarked the GRU against LISTA adapted for these settings and present qualitative and quantitative results demonstrating the GRU’s superior performance for this reconstruction task.

title: Chromouvqa: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models under Chromatic Camouflaged Images
authors: Yunfei Zhang, Yizhuo He, Yuanxun Shao, Zhengtao Yao, Haoyan Xu, Junhao Dong, Zhen Yao, Zhikang Dong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have advanced multimodal understanding, yet still struggle when targets are embedded in cluttered backgrounds requiring figure–ground segregation. To address this, we introduce ChromouVQA, a large-scale, multi-task benchmark based on Ishihara-style chromatic camouflaged images. We extend classic dot plates with multiple fill geometries and vary chromatic separation, density, size, occlusion, and rotation, recording full metadata for reproducibility. The benchmark covers nine vision-question-answering tasks, including recognition, counting, comparison, and spatial reasoning. Evaluations of humans and VLMs reveal large gaps, especially under subtle chromatic contrast or disruptive geometric fills. We also propose a model-agnostic contrastive recipe aligning silhouettes with their camouflaged renderings, improving recovery of global shapes. ChromouVQA provides a compact, controlled benchmark for reproducible evaluation and extension. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Chromou-VQA-Benchmark/Chromou-VQA.

title: Localization Data Fusion for Near-Field Bistatic MIMO Radar Systems
authors: Liana Khamidullina, Martin Haardt
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As next-generation networks adopt increasingly large antenna arrays, the far-field and Fresnel approximations of spherical wavefronts no longer hold. This has led to growing interest in exact spherical wavefront-based localization techniques for communications and radar systems, including bi- and multistatic radar, where multiple antenna arrays are used for sensing. While such approaches provide high-resolution parameter estimation, they often yield separate location estimates from different array perspectives, which must be fused to obtain accurate and reliable target positions. In this paper, we propose a location parameters fusion frameworks for near-field bistatic MIMO radar systems that combine estimates from the transmit and receive arrays using distance-based and statistical fusion methods. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed fusion approaches improve the localization accuracy compared to individual estimates from either the transmitter or receiver side.

title: Uncertainty Factorization with Linear-Time Sequential Modeling for Speaker Embedding
authors: Yijia Chen, Junjie Li, Kong Aik Lee, Man-Wai Mak
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker verification aims to determine a speaker’s authenticity from a given speech utterance, but the contribution of individual speech frames in the utterance varies significantly due to noise, channel distortions, and linguistic variability. Existing pooling methods address this by computing attention scores based on uncertainty to highlight informative frames. However, these methods typically estimate uncertainty with simple linear layers, which limits their expressiveness. To overcome this limitation, we provide an alternative perspective by modeling frame-level uncertainty using probabilistic distributions, a dual-path architecture designed to separately estimate means and variances, leading to more accurate statistical representations. To capture temporal dependencies more efficiently and effectively, we incorporate state space duality (SSD), a scalable alternative to Transformer-based models. Furthermore, a mixture-of-experts (MoE) mechanism is introduced to the dual-path architecture, where each expert is encouraged to learn a complementary sub-feature of the probabilistic distribution. Our experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements over baselines, with notable gains exceeding 8% on the VoxCeleb1-O dataset.

title: MeanVoiceFlow: One-Step Nonparallel Voice Conversion with Mean Flows
authors: Takuhiro Kaneko, Hirokazu Kameoka, Kou Tanaka, Yuto Kondo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In voice conversion (VC) applications, diffusion and flow-matching models have exhibited exceptional speech quality and speaker similarity performances. However, they are limited by slow conversion owing to their iterative inference. Consequently, we propose MeanVoiceFlow, a novel one-step nonparallel VC model based on mean flows, which can be trained from scratch without requiring pretraining or distillation. Unlike conventional flow matching that uses instantaneous velocity, mean flows employ average velocity to more accurately compute the time integral along the inference path in a single step. However, training the average velocity requires its derivative to compute the target velocity, which can cause instability. Therefore, we introduce a structural margin reconstruction loss as a zero-input constraint, which moderately regularizes the input–output behavior of the model without harmful statistical averaging. Furthermore, we propose conditional diffused-input training in which a mixture of noise and source data is used as input to the model during both training and inference. This enables the model to effectively leverage source information while maintaining consistency between training and inference. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of these techniques and demonstrate that MeanVoiceFlow achieves performance comparable to that of previous multistep and distillation-based models, even when trained from scratch.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: BOATT: Unified Bayesian Online Tracking and Adaptation for Dynamic Tensor Streams
authors: Zikun Quan, Weilin Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Modeling dynamic tensor streams is challenged by non-stationarity, where both latent patterns and model complexity (i.e., tensor rank) evolve. Existing online methods often rely on fixed-rank assumptions or heuristic rank adjustments, limiting their real-world applicability. We propose BOATT (Bayesian Online Adaptation and Tracking for Tensors), a unified probabilistic framework that addresses these limitations. BOATT models rank dynamics via Markovian binary activation variables and prunes irrelevant components using Group-ARD priors, all within a robust Student-t state-space model. An efficient online inference algorithm based on Assumed Density Filtering (ADF) [1] makes the framework tractable. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show that BOATT achieves competitive and frequently superior performance over representative baselines in tracking accuracy and anomaly detection, while providing improved robustness and interpretability.

title: LipsAM: Lipschitz-Continuous Amplitude Modifier for Audio Signal Processing and its Application to Plug-And-Play Dereverberation
authors: Kazuki Matsumoto, Ren Uchida, Kohei Yatabe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The robustness of deep neural networks (DNNs) can be certified through their Lipschitz continuity, which has made the construction of Lipschitz-continuous DNNs an active research field. However, DNNs for audio processing have not been a major focus due to their poor compatibility with existing results. In this paper, we consider the amplitude modifier (AM), a popular architecture for handling audio signals, and propose its Lipschitz-continuous variants, which we refer to as LipsAM. We prove a sufficient condition for an AM to be Lipschitz continuous and propose two architectures as examples of LipsAM. The proposed architectures were applied to a Plug-and-Play algorithm for speech dereverberation, and their improved stability is demonstrated through numerical experiments.

title: Free2frame: A Training-Free Framework for Video Understanding with Memory Boosting
authors: Shiqiang Lang, Peiwen Sun, Hao Jiang, Shuyuan Zhu, Huiying Zhao, Lan Yang, Honggang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The development of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has become a fundamental research direction, paralleling the progress of standard large language models. Owing to the abundance and accessibility of image-text data, image-based MLLMs have matured more rapidly than their video counterparts. In this paper, we propose a training-free framework that leverages pre-trained image-based MLLMs to enhance video understanding. To preserve spatio-temporal information while reducing computational redundancy, we introduce a dynamic keyframes sampling strategy that adaptively selects frames from the video input. Furthermore, to address the distinct challenges of short and long video sequences, we design a memory-based mechanism that samples keyframes from short videos and merges semantically similar frames in long videos. This design improves storage efficiency and reduces representational drift. Extensive experiments on three widely-used video-language benchmarks validate the effectiveness and generalizability of our approach.

title: Incomplete Vocabulary Learning for Fine-Grained Visual Recognition
authors: Gaurab Bhattacharya, Riya Sadrani, Jayavardhana Gubbi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing recognition systems mostly consider the closed-set assumption, where all testing classes are to be present in the training data. Open-vocabulary learning alleviates this problem by considering test data from any class present in the vocabulary. However, it cannot recognize classes beyond vocabulary, hence it is closed in the vocabulary space. To address this problem, we propose Incomplete Vocabulary Learning, which is capable of handling novel classes beyond vocabulary by leveraging the advantages provided by the large language models. To this end, we propose IVLNet that uses a ViT backbone along with the proposed hybrid feature selection block and cross-modal attention fusion block to capture fine-grained features. For inference, we have proposed a novel pipeline to perform incomplete vocabulary learning. The performance on two large-scale fine-grained datasets showcase its ability to detect and recognize classes in and beyond vocabulary.

title: Privacy-Aware Design of Distributed MIMO ISAC Systems
authors: Henrik Åkesson, Marco Gomes, Diana P. M. Osorio
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems raise unprecedented challenges regarding security and privacy since related applications involve the gathering of sensitive, identifiable information about people and the environment, which can lead to privacy leakage. Privacy-aware measures can steer the design of ISAC systems to prevent privacy violations. Thus, we explore this perspective for the design of distributed massive multiple-input multiple-output ISAC systems. For this purpose, we introduce an adversarial model where a malicious user exploits the interference from ISAC signals to extract sensing information. To mitigate this threat, we propose an iterative privacy-aware framework of two blocks: precoder design and access point selection. The precoder design aims to minimize the mutual information between the sensing and communication signals by imposing constraints on sensing and communication performance and maximum transmit power. The access point selection also aims to minimize the mutual information between communication and sensing signals by strategically selecting access points that transmit ISAC signals, and sensing receivers. Results show a reduction in the effectiveness of the attack measured by the probability of detection of the attacker.

title: IoT-Enabled Hybrid Solar–Grid Power Supply with Battery Storage for Bench-Scale EV Charging
authors: Vignesh R, Simeon Giftson Herbert I, M Ramesh Babu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: This growth in transportation electrification comes with huge challenges in the quest to ensure reliable, efficient, and sustainable energy supply for electric vehicle charging. Conventional systems that rely heavily on the grid suffer from issues such as instability, high operational costs, and limited integration of renewables. This project presents an IoT-enabled hybrid power supply system comprising solar PV generation, battery energy storage, and grid backup under an ANN-based control strategy. The objective of the proposed system is intelligent energy management through dynamically optimizing the power flow and predictive source selection. The system includes a bidirectional DC-DC converter for effective battery management and an ESP32-based IoT layer using Node-RED for real-time monitoring, fault detection, and analytics. Validation has been performed through MATLAB/Simulink simulations and prototype testing. The novelty of this work lies in combining ANN-driven adaptive control with IoT-enabled supervision for efficient, scalable, and sustainable EV charging infrastructure.

title: HNCOP: Designing a Robust Medicinal Plant Identification Using Hybrid Neural Classification and Optimization Principle
authors: R. Vijayarajeswari, N. Pushpa, N. Devakirubai, S. Janani, M. Vichithra, P. Periyasamy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The identification of medicinal plants is essential in traditional medicine, pharmaceutical research and ecology conservation. However, the identification of medicinal plant species is a complicated task because of similarity of visual appearance of the species, differences within the species, and interference with real life conditions in the form of noisy backgrounds, uneven lighting, etc. To address these challenges, this paper presents HNCOP- a Hybrid Neural Classification and Optimization Principal framework that can aid with the robust medicinal plant recognition. The model uses a combination of deep convolutional neural networks with handcrafted features such as texture, shape and color allowing comprehensive visual comprehension. These characteristics are combined with the help of the attention-enabled layer that highlights significant characteristics selectively. Besides, Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is used to optimize model parameters and ensemble weights. The HNCOP has shown superiority over the classical classifier and separate deep models to benchmark data sets with an impressive accuracy of 99.7 % and high precision, recall and ROC-AUC scores. The ablation analysis and the cross-validation indicate the stability of the model and the good-quality generalization on different types of plants. HNCOP has the potential to be useful in mobile devices and digital herbal databases with its effective inference velocity and scalable design, which provides a powerful instrument to identify plant species with unparalleled accuracy to botanists, researchers, and conservationists.

title: Fusionedit: Semantic Fusion and Attention Modulation for Training-Free Image Editing
authors: Yongwen Lai, Chaoqun Wang, Shaobo Min
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Text-guided image editing aims to modify specific regions according to the target prompt while preserving the identity of the source image. Recent methods exploit explicit binary masks to constrain editing, but hard mask boundaries introduce artifacts and reduce editability. To address these issues, we propose FusionEdit, a training-free image editing framework that achieves precise and controllable edits. First, editing and preserved regions are automatically identified by measuring semantic discrepancies between source and target prompts. To mitigate boundary artifacts, FusionEdit performs distance-aware latent fusion along region boundaries to yield the soft and accurate mask, and employs a total variation loss to enforce smooth transitions, obtaining natural editing results. Second, FusionEdit leverages AdaIN-based modulation within DiT attention layers to perform a statistical attention fusion in the editing region, enhancing editability while preserving global consistency with the source image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our FusionEdit significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/Yvan1001/FusionEdit.

title: EICA: An Emotional Inertia-Contagion-Aware Alignment for Emotion Recognition in Conversations
authors: Chenmin Ke, Huijuan Zhao, Shuangjiang He, Xuanhe Fan, Xinling Zhang, Li Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Emotion Recognition in Conversations (ERC) is fundamental to advancing human-machine interaction, as it requires understanding how emotions evolve and propagate across dialogue turns. Existing studies often consider emotional inertia and contagion in parallel, however, they may overlook the potential prediction conflicts between them, which in turn hinders recognition accuracy. To solve this problem, we propose a novel Emotional Inertia-Contagion-aware Alignment for Emotion Recognition in Conversations (EICA). Specifically, EICA conceptualizes emotional inertia and contagion as relational constraints rather than auxiliary cues. The emotional inertia alignment assesses the extent to which an utterance’s emotion is governed by the coherence of the speaker’s affective trajectory, while the emotional contagion alignment examines its susceptibility to emotional propagation across participants. Embedding these dynamics into a unified contrastive space enables consistent yet distinct representations and boosts emotion recognition performance. Experimental analysis on four publicly available datasets reveals the superiority of our proposed method as compared to all baseline methods.

title: PromptMAD: Cross-Modal Prompting for Multi-Class Visual Anomaly Localization
authors: Duncan McCain, Hossein Kashiani, Fatemeh Afghah
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Visual anomaly detection in multi-class settings poses significant challenges due to the diversity of object categories, the scarcity of anomalous examples, and the presence of camouflaged defects. In this paper, we propose PromptMAD, a cross-modal prompting framework for unsupervised visual anomaly detection and localization that integrates semantic guidance through vision-language alignment. By leveraging CLIP-encoded text prompts describing both normal and anomalous class-specific characteristics, our method enriches visual reconstruction with semantic context, improving the detection of subtle and textural anomalies. To further address the challenge of class imbalance at the pixel level, we incorporate Focal loss function, which emphasizes hard-to-detect anomalous regions during training. Our architecture also includes a supervised segmentor that fuses multi-scale convolutional features with Transformer-based spatial attention and diffusion iterative refinement, yielding precise and high-resolution anomaly maps. Extensive experiments on the MVTec-AD dataset demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art pixel-level performance, improving mean AUC to 98.35% and AP to 66.54%, while maintaining efficiency across diverse categories.

title: SLM-SS: Speech Language Model for Generative Speech Separation
authors: Tianhua Li, Chenda Li, Wei Wang, Xin Zhou, Xihui Chen, Jianqing Gao, Yanmin Qian
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech separation (SS) has advanced significantly with neural network-based methods, showing improved performance on signal-level metrics. However, these methods often struggle to maintain speech intelligibility in the separated signals, which can negatively affect the performance of downstream tasks such as speech recognition. In this work, we propose SLM-SS, a novel approach that applies speech language models to SS, aiming to enhance the intelligibility and coherence of the separated signals. We frame SS as discrete multi-codebook sequence generation, using Encoder-Decoder models to map quantized speech mixtures to target tokens. In addition to the autoregressive modeling strategy, we introduce a non-autoregressive model to improve decoding efficiency for residual tokens. Experimental results on the LibriMix dataset demonstrate that our approach shows significantly better preservation of speech intelligibility, leading to improved linguistic consistency in a variety of downstream tasks compared to existing approaches. <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: MECap-R1: Emotion-Aware Policy with Reinforcement Learning for Multimodal Emotion Captioning
authors: Haoqin Sun, Chenyang Lyu, Xiangyu Kong, Shiwan Zhao, Jiaming Zhou, Hui Wang, Aobo Kong, Jinghua Zhao, Longyue Wang, Weihua Luo, Kaifu Zhang, Yong Qin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech Emotion Captioning (SEC) has emerged as a notable research direction. The inherent complexity of emotional content in human speech makes it challenging for traditional discrete classification methods to provide an adequate representation. Consequently, utilizing natural language to describe speech emotions presents a novel avenue for more effectively capturing and expressing affect. In this paper, we propose MECap-R1, a pioneering emotion-aware policy with reinforcement learning for multimodal emotion captioning. By employing Group Relative Policy Optimization with emotion-aware reward (Emo-GRPO), the framework precisely captures the emotion and semantic features, thereby addressing the shortcomings of rigid rules in handling the dynamic and flexible nature of captions. Experimental results on the EmotionTalk dataset demonstrate that MECap-R1 performs well in generating emotion descriptions and achieves substantial gains in both accuracy and diversity.

title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning with Contextual Bias Reduction
authors: Siming Lan, Jiaming Guo, Rui Zhang, Shaohui Peng, Yunkai Gao, Fan Wu, Zihao Zhang, Xing Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Context-based Meta-Reinforcement Learning (Meta-RL) is a powerful approach for training a generalizable agent by using context variables derived from historical data. However, due to the uneven distribution of these historical data in different tasks, the context variable tends to memorize trajectory features from training rather than capturing essential task information, introducing bias and impairing the agent’s generalization ability. In response to this challenge, we examine this problem from a causal perspective and identify that contextual bias arises from the indirect effect via the indirect path from task to context variable. In causal inference theory, such effects transmitted through indirect paths are proven to introduce biases into the estimation of target variables, which forms a well-established conclusion in the causal domain. Leveraging this insight, we propose the Contextual Bias Reduction Meta-RL (CBRM), a novel off-policy method addressing biases in the context variable from two perspectives: Firstly, we employ a policy initialization approach, in tandem with the auxiliary reward, to reduce biases in trajectories. Secondly, we use contrastive learning to penalize the context encoder that extracts trajectory information. Experimental results show that CBRM achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: Large Language Models for Learning Technologies: Structural and Functional Evaluation of Automated Q-Matrix Generation for Educational Assessment
authors: Arcanjo Miguel Mota Lopes, José Francisco De Magalhães Netto
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Linking assessment items to knowledge components (KCs) is essential for adaptive learning and cognitive diagnosis but remains a labor-intensive and expert-dependent process. This study investigates the use of Large Language Models (LLM) to automate item–KC association through Q-matrix generation as an interpretable representation of item–attribute mappings. In contrast to prior studies that focus solely on tagging accuracy or linguistic classification, we propose a dual-level computational evaluation framework that jointly assesses (i) structural alignment with expert-defined Q-matrices using formal similarity and discrepancy metrics, and (ii) functional impact on predictive performance within the DINA cognitive diagnostic model using RMSE and MAE under controlled experimental settings. The results show that few-shot prompting substantially improves structural alignment compared with zero-shot configurations, whereas chain-of-thought provides marginal refinements. Despite structural variations across prompting strategies, the predictive performance remains stable and comparable to expert-defined Q-matrices, indicating the functional robustness of the diagnostic model. The main contribution of this study lies in providing an integrated structural–functional evaluation protocol for LLM-based knowledge tagging, offering empirical evidence that automated item–KC linking can support scalable assessment design without degrading the diagnostic accuracy.

title: Image Geolocalization Via Combined Classification and Regression
authors: Andrei-Iulian Saceleanu, Irina Mocanu, Serban Radu
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: This paper presents a geolocation system to assist in identifying the location in images associated with illicit activities, thereby reducing investigation time for authorities. The system is implemented as a multi-agent system. The geolocalisation is performed based on image analysis, which involves both image classification and regression of the geographical coordinates. The final result is obtained by fusioning both results. The classification model is based on the EfficientNet architecture. The evaluation is performed on a dataset with images from Romania collected from Google Street View. Moreover, the best version of the model succeeds in solving difficult classification tasks, correctly identifying the answer among two or more similar counties in 80 % of the cases. Alternatively, from a regression point of view, the predicted coordinates are at most 80 km away from the ground truth location in over 80 % of the evaluation cases. By employing various data augmentation techniques, such as noise addition or severe compression, a model robust to potential adversarial interference was achieved.

title: HMD: Enhancing Vision Transformer Distillation via Mask Reconstruction
authors: Xiaodong Shi, Keju Pu, Jingwen Yan, Bowen Zheng, Zirui Cheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current knowledge distillation methods are primarily designed for convolutional networks and often fail to fully exploit the unique strengths of Vision Transformers (ViTs). ViTs inherently excel at mask reconstruction due to their flexible input processing and global attention mechanisms. To leverage this capability, we propose Hierarchical Mask Distillation (HMD), a novel distillation framework that integrates a mask reconstruction objective. Specifically, the student model adopts a hierarchical input strategy: shallow layers process the full image to learn foundational features directly from the teacher, while deeper layers receive highly masked inputs, enabling the student to reconstruct global features under teacher guidance. Extensive experiments show that HMD significantly outperforms DeiT on ImageNet-1K, achieving accuracy gains of 2.31%, 1.99%, and 2.96% for ViT-Tiny, ViT-Small, and ViT-Base, respectively. Moreover, our model significantly outperforms existing approaches on downstream tasks including object detection and semantic segmentation, demonstrating stronger generalization capability.

title: Factorial Powers
authors: Robert M. Corless, David J. Jeffrey, Qingze Li
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: Stirling numbers were originally introduced in order to relate monomial powers and factorial powers. The notion of factorial powers has been generalized beyond integer powers, and Stirling numbers have been generalized beyond integer arguments. Here we explore the transformations between monomial and factorial powers by identifying special values of the generalized Stirling numbers.

title: Probing Content and Channel in Speaker Verification Models
authors: Eleni-Konstantina Sergidou, David Bikker, Charlotte Pouw, Johan Rohdin, Zeno Geradts, Marcel Worring
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: To understand the performance of speaker verification models and to optimize their performance, explainability and interpretability are gaining attention. Ideally, a speaker verification model relies solely on voice characteristics for its output and ignores unwanted features related to content and channel. Therefore, we investigate to what extent current models are capable of doing this. We analyze the representations of several speaker verification models using Minimum Description Length probing with compression as our main metric. To interpret the probing results of each model, we compare them to their baselines and two upper bound models. Our analysis shows differences in the ability of various speaker verification models to suppress irrelevant information. Further, it seems that the models explore available information on content and channel before they suppress it. These findings help us to understand various robustness aspects of current speaker verification models.

title: Interval-Aware Retrieval Framework For Speech-Based Automatic Alzheimer’s Detection
authors: Mingyang Gu, Zunsheng Tan, Kai Li, Xiaobao Wang, Bin Wen, Tianrui Wang, Gaoyan Zhang, Jianwu Dang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Detecting Alzheimer’s disease (AD) from spontaneous speech offers a scalable and non-invasive alternative to conventional diagnostics, but remains challenging due to limited datasets and pipelines that underrepresent temporal cues. Existing systems typically insert symbolic pauses or attach acoustic features, followed by simple fusion, which weakens token-level alignment and lacks a normative reference for healthy timing. To address these issues, this paper proposes an interval-aware retrieval framework that explicitly incorporates temporal knowledge into speech-based AD detection. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module retrieves word-level timing priors from healthy speech. A Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC)–guided alignment head preserves fine-grained speech–text correspondences without relying on oracle durations. An interval-aware enhancer then conditions token representations on deviations from retrieved priors, ensuring rhythm anomalies such as long pauses, local lengthening, and disfluencies remain salient. Ablation studies confirm that each component—RAG priors, CTC alignment, and interval-aware conditioning—contributes additive gains.On the ADReSS and ADReSSo benchmarks with official splits, our method achieved 94.79% and 88.73% accuracy, with error reduction rates of 13.4% and 11.1%, respectively, and provides lightweight interpretability signals to facilitate further expert review.

title: K-Function: Joint Pronunciation Transcription and Feedback for Evaluating Kids Language Function
authors: Shuhe Li, Chenxu Guo, Jiachen Lian, Cheol Jun Cho, Wenshuo Zhao, Xiner Xu, Ruiyu Jin, Xiaoyu Shi, Xuanru Zhou, Dingkun Zhou, Sam Wang, Grace Wang, Jingze Yang, Jingyi Xu, Ruohan Bao, Xingrui Chen, Elise Brenner, Brandon In, Francesca Pei, Maria Luisa Gorno-Tempini, Gopala Anumanchipalli
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Evaluating young children’s language is challenging for automatic speech recognizers due to high-pitched voices, prolonged sounds, and limited data. We introduce K-Function, a framework that combines accurate sub-word transcription with objective, Large Language Model (LLM)-driven scoring. Its core, Kids-Weighted Finite State Transducer (K-WFST), merges an acoustic phoneme encoder with a phoneme-similarity model to capture child-specific speech errors while remaining fully interpretable. K-WFST achieves a 1.39% phoneme error rate on MyST and 8.61% on Multitudes—an absolute improvement of 10.47% and 7.06% over a greedy-search decoder. These high-quality transcripts are used by an LLM to grade verbal skills, developmental milestones, reading, and comprehension, with results that align closely with human evaluators. Our findings show that precise phoneme recognition is essential for creating an effective assessment framework, enabling scalable language screening for children. Our K-Function framework demo is available at https://chenxukwok.github.io/K-function/.

title: Dafmr: Dual-Side Attribute-Aware Fusion with Mixture-of-Experts and Regularization for Recommendation Systems
authors: Shanqing Yu, Mengzhen Wang, Shuxin Song, Meng Zhou, Jintao Zhou, Songtao Peng, Zeyu Wang, Qi Xuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Data sparsity remains a fundamental challenge that limits the performance of recommender systems, and incorporating side information such as user demographics and item categories has emerged as a promising solution. However, existing approaches either rely on simple feature concatenation, which causes redundancy and overlooks attribute interactions, or knowledge-graph modeling, which fuses information at a coarse granularity and often leads to over-smoothed features. To address these challenges, we propose a Dual-side Attribute-aware Fusion framework with Mixture-ofExperts and Regularization, termed DAFMR. Specifically, DAFMR introduces separate expert modules for user and item attributes with an input-aware gating mechanism to enable fine-grained semantic fusion, while orthogonality regularization reduces redundancy and enhances representation diversity. Extensive experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that DAFMR consistently outperforms competitive baselines, achieving an average relative gain of 6.6% in Recall@10 and 5.3% in NDCG@10. Our code is available at https://github.com/Hanyuzhen0826/ SideInformation_MoE.

title: AutoVQA-G: Self-Improving Agentic Framework for Automated Visual Question Answering and Grounding Annotation
authors: Rongsheng Hu, Runwei Guan, Yicheng Di, Jiayu Bao, Yuan Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Manual annotation of high-quality visual question answering with grounding (VQA-G) datasets, which pair visual questions with evidential grounding, is crucial for advancing vision-language models (VLMs), but remains unscalable. Existing automated methods are often hindered by two key issues: (1) inconsistent data fidelity due to model hallucinations; (2) brittle verification mechanisms based on simple heuristics. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoVQA-G, a self-improving agentic framework for automated VQA-G annotation. AutoVQA-G employs an iterative refinement loop where a Consistency Evaluation module uses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for fine-grained visual verification. Based on this feedback, a memory-augmented Prompt Optimization agent analyzes critiques from failed samples to progressively refine generation prompts. Our experiments show that AutoVQA-G generates VQA-G datasets with superior visual grounding accuracy compared to leading multimodal LLMs, offering a promising approach for creating high-fidelity data to facilitate more robust VLM training and evaluation. We release codes publicly<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: MR-TEB: A Scalable Multi-Robot Trajectory Planning Framework with Adaptive Priority Scheduling
authors: Niharika Chaudhary, Yogesh Dasgaonkar, Radhe Shyam Sharma, Parimala Kancharla
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Multi-robots navigating in narrow, congested spaces often encounter collisions and deadlocks. State of the art local planners, such as Timed Elastic Band, optimize single robot trajectories but do not provide coordinated motion planning between multiple robots. In this paper, we present the Multi-Robot Timed Elastic Band (MR-TEB), a scalable trajectory planning framework that combines TEB related trajectory optimization and an adaptive priority scheduling system. In this approach, we rank robots based on their progress and emergency priorities. We use a global A* planner, to allow robots to share paths and provides both reactive and predictive avoidance. Experimental results of our method in warehouse simulation environments show that MR-TEB significantly reduces the waiting times and avoids deadlocks when compared to standard TEB. As a result, navigation in dense multi-robot scenarios (where coordination is complex and critical) are completed in less time and more reliably.

title: Reference-Coupled Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Coordination with Real-Time Adaptive Perimeter Field Navigation
authors: Darshankumar Prajapati, Pushkar Kumar, Surya Prakash S.K., Ankit Mehra, Ashish Rana, Jaganath Prasad Sahoo, Thugu Jaswanth Reddy, Amit Shukla
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Autonomous industrial inspection in unstructured environments requires coordinated UAV-ground systems for aerial perception and ground-level task execution. Coordinating heterogeneous 3D aerial and 2D ground dynamics requires control frameworks with adaptable coordination strategies. Existing decoupled or fixed hierarchical approaches lack optimal coordination and runtime strategy adaptability. This work presents a reference-coupled Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) framework with parameterized coordination achieving optimal state synchronization across heterogeneous 3D aerial and 2D ground dynamics.
