The formulation enables continuous coordination strategies from leader-follower to symmetric modes via single-parameter adjustment, with adaptive altitude control maintaining perception coverage. For real-time trajectory generation, we introduce APF-Nav (Adaptive Perimeter Field Navigation) employing moving perimeter charge representation with 0.012 s computational latency. The system integrates RGB-D perception, deep learning terrain classification, and vision-based state estimation. Multi-terrain experiments confirm successful autonomous navigation with terrain-adaptive locomotion, establishing the viability of reference-coupled LQR coordination for heterogeneous multi-agent systems in resource-constrained embedded deployment.

title: SSRDWater: A Robust and Secure Watermarking of Large Language Models via Sentence-Level Semantic Relational Dependencies
authors: Xiaofan Deng, Tao Zhou, Dirui Min, Gan Zhu, Kexin Yu, Yongtao Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models offer impressive text generation capabilities, but they also pose risks, including the spread of misinformation and academic dishonesty. Current watermarking methods face challenges: token-level approaches are prone to paraphrasing attacks, while sentence-level methods struggle against spoofing attacks. We propose SSRDWater, a sentence-level semantic watermarking technique that tackles both issues. By utilizing inter-sentence semantic similarities and cryptographically controlled rejection sampling, our approach embeds watermarks in pseudo-random intervals, ensuring robust detection against paraphrasing and precise localization of spoofing attempts. Experiments on OPT-2.7B and LLaMA-3-8B demonstrate that SSRDWater achieves an average ROC-AUC of 97.8% against paraphrasing attacks, while maintaining text perplexity comparable to natural language. This advancement in watermarking enhances robustness and security verification for AI content authentication. Our code is available at github.com/maplefan/SSRDWater.

title: Loose Coupling of Spectral and Spatial Models for Multi-Channel Diarization and Enhancement of Meetings in Dynamic Environments
authors: Adrian Meise, Tobias Cord-Landwehr, Christoph Boeddeker, Marc Delcroix, Tomohiro Nakatani, Reinhold Haeb-Umbach
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sound capture by microphone arrays opens the possibility to exploit spatial, in addition to spectral, information for diarization and signal enhancement, two important tasks in meeting transcription. However, there is no one-to-one mapping of positions in space to speakers if speakers move. Here, we address this by proposing a novel joint spatial and spectral mixture model, whose two submodels are loosely coupled by modeling the relationship between speaker and position index probabilistically. Thus, spatial and spectral information can be jointly exploited, while at the same time allowing for speakers speaking from different positions. Experiments on the LibriCSS data set with simulated speaker position changes show great improvements over tightly coupled subsystems.

title: Comparison of Tabular and Multidimensional OLAP Models in SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS)
authors: Nejra Rizvić, Esad Kadušić, Elmin Marevac, Nataša Živić, Tamara Cvijanovic
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: In the context of business systems, efficient data analysis through various Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) models represents a key challenge for performance optimization and timely decision-making. This study examines tabular and multidimensional OLAP models within the SQL Server (MSSQL) and Visual Studio environments to determine which model facilitates more effective data processing in a specific business context. An experimental analysis was conducted using the Stats dataset, where the same business question was addressed through both models, comparing their response times and query execution efficiency. Particular attention was paid to execution speed, data processing methodologies, and resource optimization strategies. Results indicated that the tabular model, which relies on in-memory technology and the Data Analysis Expressions (DAX) language, reduces data processing time by approximately 38 % and offers simpler modeling capabilities, making it suitable for analyses where rapid result retrieval is critical. In contrast, the multidimensional model, utilizing Multidimensional Expressions (MDX), provides advanced analytical features and greater scalability, rendering it more appropriate for complex analyses involving large datasets and predefined aggregations. Based on this comparison, it was concluded that the choice between tabular and multidimensional OLAP models depends on specific analytical requirements. If speed and flexibility are prioritized, the tabular model enables faster execution, whereas the multidimensional model offers enhanced control over analytical processes.

title: IBPCodec : A Low-Bitrate Lightweight Speech Codec With Inter-Band Prediction
authors: Peng Zhou, Xiaojiao Chen, Pincheng Lu, Jing Wang, Shenghui Zhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the advancement of deep learning, neural speech codecs have far surpassed traditional codecs in speech reconstruction quality. However, achieving such superior performance often comes with very high computational complexity, making it difficult to deploy on devices with limited computational resources. In this paper, we propose a low-bitrate lightweight speech codec with inter-band prediction, called IBPCodec. Under low bitrate conditions, efficient transmission of low-frequency information is crucial for perceived quality than high-frequency details. Therefore, our model first performs feature extraction and quantization of the low-frequency information from the input signal to ensure low complexity and effective low-frequency transmission. At the decoding stage, the Inter-Band Prediction Module (IBPM) leverages the correlation between high and low frequency bands to predict high-frequency information from the low-frequency band, enabling full speech reconstruction. Experiments show that IBPCodec delivers competitive quality at 16 kHz and 1–3 kbps while keeping computational complexity at just 0.35 Giga Multiply-Add Operations per Second (GMACs).

title: Physio-Mamba: Physiologically-Guided Mamba Network for Continuous Calibration-Free Abp Reconstruction from PPG
authors: Sung Woo Kim, Junmo Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Continuous Arterial Blood Pressure (ABP) monitoring requires invasive arterial catheterization, while non-invasive cuff-based devices only provide intermittent spot readings and are unsuitable for continuous monitoring. We introduce Physio-Mamba, a physiology-informed framework for reconstructing continuous ABP waveforms from photoplethysmography (PPG). The model encodes physiology at the input by processing PPG together with the acceleration photoplethysmogram (APG), and at the objective by using a Physiological-Dynamic loss that aligns second-order pressure dynamics to preserve clinically salient features. A Mamba-based MaskNet offers bidirectional long-context temporal modeling with linear time and memory complexity. In subject-independent splits of the Sensors dataset and cross-dataset testing on BCG, Physio-Mamba achieves competitive accuracy, preserves key morphology such as the systolic upstroke and the dicrotic notch, and generalizes across datasets. These results show that embedding explicit physiological priors is a practical and effective route to cuffless ABP reconstruction. Furthermore, they motivate causal real-time variants, uncertainty-aware outputs, and broader clinical validation.

title: BPMF: Bidirectional Prediction by Multi-Scale Features for Multimodal Industrial Anomaly Detection
authors: Yupeng Zhao, Xufei Zhuang, Yuanyuan Zhi, Jie Lv, Xishuang Yang, Ningyu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal industrial anomaly detection aims to simultaneously localize visual and geometric defects. Existing cross-modal prediction methods often rely solely on global semantic features, neglecting the fine-grained local details essential for accurate defect localization, leading to suboptimal performance, especially for small and multi-scale anomalies. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-scale feature bidirectional prediction network (BPMF) with an asymmetric architecture. The 2D-to-3D branch utilizes target-specific optimization and residual alignment mechanisms, leveraging multi-scale RGB features to simultaneously reconstruct FPFH handcrafted features and PointMAE learned features, encouraging the network to learn comprehensive representations. The 3D-to-2D branch directly reconstructs multi-scale RGB features from the fused point cloud features, providing auxiliary supervision. Additionally, we introduce a non-anomalous region suppression module (NRSM), which refines the initial anomaly map by reducing false positives in non-defective areas. Experiments show that BPMF outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both MVTec 3D-AD and Eyecandies datasets.

title: Learning Latent Space for Multi-Order / Resolution Graph-Regularized Image Denoiser
authors: Tam Thuc Do, Gene Cheung
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We build an interpretable and lightweight transformer-like neural net for image denoising from a graph signal processing (GSP) perspective. First, via an autoencoder setup, we transform an image (patch) from the input signal space to a norm-preserving latent space learned directly from data. Then, we denoise the signal’s latent representation via a split-Bregman (SB) algorithm minimizing a combination of Graph Laplacian Regularizers (GLR) and Graph Total Variations (GTV) of different orders and spatial resolutions—one that parametrizes a general Gaussian and Laplacian mixture model. We unroll iterations of our linear-time SB algorithm into a transformer-like network for data-driven parameter tuning. Experiments show that our image denoiser provides interpretability while achieving competitive performance as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, at a reduced parameter count.

title: WaterFlow: Explicit Physics-Prior Rectified Flow for Underwater Saliency Mask Generation
authors: Runting Li, Shijie Lian, Hua Li, Yutong Li, Wenhui Wu, Sam Kwong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Underwater Salient Object Detection (USOD) faces significant challenges, including underwater image quality degradation and domain gaps. Existing methods tend to ignore the physical principles of underwater imaging or simply treat degradation phenomena in underwater images as interference factors that must be eliminated, failing to fully exploit the valuable information they contain. We propose WaterFlow, a rectified flow-based framework for underwater salient object detection that innovatively incorporates underwater physical imaging information as explicit priors directly into the network training process and introduces temporal dimension modeling, significantly enhancing the model’s capability for salient object identification. On the USOD10K dataset, WaterFlow achieves a 0.072 gain in S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</inf>, demonstrating the effectiveness and superiority of our method. https://github.com/Theo-polis/WaterFlow.

title: NEDGCN: High-Quality Sample Selection and Noise-Tolerant Graph Neural Network Via Differentiated Edge Weighting
authors: Yucong Zhang, Junhang Wu, Yabo Yin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Graph-based fraud detection is of paramount importance to the security of modern digital ecosystems, including financial services and online content platforms. Its primary goal is to identify anomalous nodes within large, complex networks. However, the performance of existing graph learning methods deteriorates significantly when faced with both extreme class imbalance and label noise. To address these challenges, we propose the Noise-tolerant Edge-differentiated Graph Convolutional Network (NEDGCN). The NEDGCN employs a dual strategy to purify both node and edge information. First, an edge differentiation module mitigates feature dilution caused by heterophilous connections. Second, a node selection module reduces the impact of label noise by identifying reliable training samples. Finally, these modules are optimized together in a joint iterative framework. Experimental results on the Amazon and Elliptic benchmark datasets demonstrate that our NEDGCN outperforms competitive methods.

title: MERI-VL-MIL: Multi-Expert Reasoning-Informed Vision-Language Multi-Instance Learning for Few-Shot Cancer Subtyping
authors: Dibaloke Chanda, Nasim Yahya Soltani, Masoud Ganji
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vision language models (VLMs) in computational pathology (CPath) have limited capability in cancer diagnosis due to an information bottleneck in the language modality. This makes it difficult to adopt them in real clinical settings where complex but reliable diagnostic decisions need to be made. In this work, information from the chain-of-thought (COT) process in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is utilized as a supplementary source of information to aid in cancer subtyping for TCGA dataset. Three separate MLLMs are prompted to generate language descriptions for class names in cancer subtypes with four different structured prompts, each focusing on a specific aspect of cancer diagnosis. The generated response, along with the intermediate COT responses, is used to guide the visual features from whole slide images (WSIs) in a vision-language embedding space. The final embeddings carry rich semantic information that amplifies the prediction capability. Simulations are carried out on the TCGA-LUNG dataset for cancer subtyping under a few-shot setting.

title: RLSP-NER: Reinforcement Learning of Soft Prompts for NER with Large Language Models
authors: Yue Gao, Liuchi Jiang, Chunrui Zhang, Shimin Cai, Tao Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in LLMs have spurred their application in Named Entity Recognition (NER). However, existing methods are sensitive to prompt design, and standard token-level fine-tuning doesn’t align with NER evaluation metrics, limiting model performance. To address these issues, we propose RLSP-NER, a new two-stage framework. In the first stage, we use parameter-efficient LoRA with manually crafted prompts to teach the model task formats. In the second stage, we freeze the model and replace entity descriptions with soft prompts. We then use Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to optimize these soft prompts by designing a reward function based on F1 score, thereby aligning our training objective with the evaluation metric. Experiments on four public datasets show that RLSP-NER improves the average F1 score by 1.46% and generalizes well across models and datasets. To our knowledge, this is among the first works to optimize soft prompts for NER via GRPO, offering a scalable and parameter-efficient RL-based strategy for LLMs.

title: SPGR: Source-Path Guided Repair for Deep Neural Networks
authors: Liming Bao, Yan Wang, Tao Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Neural network repair aims to correct erroneous behaviors through local parameter modifications, thereby enhancing the safety assurance of deep neural networks (DNNs) in safety-critical applications. However, existing methods lack accurate localization of fault sources and modeling of propagation paths, often requiring large-scale parameter modifications that not only limit the effectiveness of repair but also risk compromising correct behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose a source-path guided repair method, SPGR. This method identifies source neurons that trigger erroneous behaviors through dual-source contrastive relevance backtracking and a source scoring function, and constructs repair paths accordingly. Starting from the source neuron, SPGR employs optimization methods to progressively adjust the parameters of faulty neurons along the repair path, correcting errors with minimal intervention while maximally preserving correct behaviors. Experimental results demonstrate that SPGR can significantly reduce the repair scale, while also improving repair efficiency and generalization capability, and further enhancing the overall safety and robustness of neural networks.

title: Query-Specific Context-Enhanced Representation Learning for Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
authors: Yu Gao, Qingtian Zeng, Weijian Ni, Cheng Cheng, Hua Duan, Minghao Zou, Ziyang Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning (TKGR) aims to leverage historical information to predict future facts. However, most existing methods learn entity embeddings shared across all queries at the same timestamp, without considering query-specific context. To tackle these problems, we propose Query-Specific Context-Enhanced Representation Learning (QSCEL) model for TKGR. Specifically, we construct an extended graph based on the query set to provide a structural basis for learning query-specific context. On this graph, we design a query-aware dual-attention graph neural network to learn both shared and query-specific contexts. Moreover, we design a global repeated history encoder that encodes fact frequency and temporal decay to better exploit historical information. Extensive experiments on four public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of QSCEL. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/yugao9892/QSCEL.

title: InsightRec: Enhancing Sequential Recommendation through Reasoning-Aware Preference Optimization
authors: Hua Huang, Qiyao Peng, Minglai Shao, Hongtao Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: For sequential recommendation, most existing LLM-based recommenders rely on supervised fine-tuning over the target item. However, these models primarily focus on surface-level patterns, failing to develop a genuine comprehension of underlying user preferences and the reasoning processes. Inspired by the current advancement of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, we propose InsightRec to comprehensively align both the model’s thinking processes and final recommendations with human preferences. Specifically, we generate think-answer pairs for LLMs to fine-tune. Then, we design an in-distribution DPO training data construction strategy and perform preference optimization using a modified DPO variant to refine its reasoning process. Through our approach, LLMs learn from comparisons of chosen and rejected samples while refining their understanding of user preferences, drawing insights from both implicit recommendation processes and explicit results. Extensive experiments demonstrate InsightRec’s effectiveness and superiority. Our code is available at https://github.com/Palpitate18/InsightRec.

title: UJCodec: An End-to-end Unet-Style Codec for Joint Speech Compression and Enhancement
authors: Pincheng Lu, Peng Zhou, Xiaojiao Chen, Jing Wang, Zhong-Qiu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: End-to-end speech codecs enable low-bitrate communication but most of them typically lack integrated enhancement, limiting performance in noisy conditions. Although recent efforts have explored integrating speech enhancement into neural codecs, the decoded speech often exhibits noticeable distortions, with word omissions particularly pronounced, leading to significant degradations in intelligibility. To address this, we first propose a UNet-style model with skip connections designed to retain speech details throughout the coding process. We then employ a three-stage training strategy that builds on the codec’s compression capability while rapidly enhancing its robustness to noise. To further mitigate word omission distortions, we simulate latent frame corruption during training. Experiments show that UJCodec achieves strong noise suppression and high intelligibility under low-bitrate, noisy scenarios.

title: An Adaptive Sampling Method Based on Reinforcement Learning for Wind Power Forecasting Under Extreme Weather
authors: Ruibo Guo, Lei Liu, Mengjun Xu, Rui Yang, Hongwei Zhao, Tengyuan Liu, Bin Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate wind power forecasting (WPF) is essential for reliable energy management, especially during extreme weather events. However, existing WPF methods often compromise overall model performance when adapting to extreme events, as they tend to overfit to rare, extreme samples. To address this challenge, we propose an adaptive sampling method (ARS-WPF) based on reinforcement learning (RL), which enhances forecasting for extreme events without sacrificing accuracy under normal weather. Specifically, ARS-WPF formulates the selection of training samples as an RL problem, optimized using a novel fine-grained reward function. This reward encourages the selection of informative samples to enhance forecasting, penalizes overfitting performance and supports precise timing of crucial turbine shutdowns. Experimental results show that ARS-WPF achieves error reductions of 4.3% and 11.7% in MAE under cold wave and typhoon events, respectively.

title: ADH-VA: Adaptive Directed-Hypergraph Convolution with VA Contrastive Learning for Multimodal Conversational Emotion Recognition
authors: Ziqi Shu, Rongzhou Zhou, Xiaodong Wang, Qingfeng Wu, Lu Cao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Conversational multimodal emotion recognition remains difficult due to cross-modal heterogeneity, inconsistent emotional cues, and strong context- and speaker-dependent dynamics. We introduce Adaptive Directed Hypergraph with Valence–Arousal contrastive learning (ADH-VA). A valence–arousal–guided contrastive objective aligns representation geometry with affective distance, producing stable and discriminative unimodal features. An adaptive directed hypergraph convolution then models higher-order intra-and inter-modal relations and conversational dynamics, enforces causal information flow to prevent future-to-present leakage, and suppresses redundancy and over-smoothing. On IEMOCAP and MELD, ADH-VA consistently outperforms prior methods across standard metrics, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness.

title: PSCC-Net: A Siamese Network Framework for Pseudo-Video Temporal Modeling and Spatiotemporal Fusion in Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Hongyi Dong, Yan Wang, Xiang Li, Yanqi Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Although deep learning has advanced remote sensing change detection, effectively modeling temporal information and achieving robust spatiotemporal fusion remain challenging. We propose PSCC-Net, a novel framework that reformulates change detection as a video understanding problem. Bi-temporal images are transformed into pseudo-video sequences via forward and backward linear interpolation, enabling explicit temporal modeling beyond static 2D features. A Siamese network extracts temporal dynamics, while a Cross-Modality Compression and Attention (CMCA) module refines features and alleviates information loss during 3D-to-2D compression. The refined temporal features are fused with spatial representations to generate globally expressive features. Experiments on multiple public datasets show that PSCC-Net surpasses existing methods in terms of accuracy and robustness, with particularly notable performance compared to convolution-based SNUNet and Transformer-based HCGMNet models.

title: Robust Drone Swarm Identification Through Wavelet Scattering Transform and Hybrid Machine Learning
authors: Feten Slimeni, Tijeni Dalleji, Ahmed Siala
publish: IT Professional
abstract: The exponential growth of drone usage necessitates reliable radio-frequency-based detection systems capable of identifying swarm characteristics in congested spectral environments. In this work, a robust solution is developed based on three main contributions: 1) a feature extraction method based on wavelet scattering transform (WST) that demonstrates clustering accuracy of 83. 44%, high improvement over conventional approaches; 2) a hybrid unsupervised–supervised architecture that combines the mean-shift cluster with the eXtreme gradient boosting classification that achieves 99.82% accuracy; and 3) a comprehensive evaluation across heterogeneous datasets that reveals critical insights into noise resilience. The experimental results show that our WST-based solution outperforms benchmark methods in multidrone scenarios, particularly in handling overlapping signatures and unseen drone types. The system’s practical viability is demonstrated through real-time implementation on software-defined radios with low latency, offering security professionals a robust tool for spectrum monitoring with high computational efficiency (3× faster than deep neural network alternatives).

title: Learning to Intervene: Optimized Soft Intervention Selection for Causal Discovery
authors: Chen Peng, Sambit Mishra, Urbashi Mitra
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Understanding causal structure is fundamental for scientific discovery, yet most existing methods rely on observational data or passively use available interventional datasets. While these approaches have advanced the field, they do not actively target the most uncertain or poorly represented parts of the structure. We address this challenge by optimizing intervention design under soft interventions and integrating it into causal subgraph learning. A key ingredient is a utility function that balances reconstruction accuracy, exploration, and diversity: it highlights poorly estimated subgraphs, revisits under-explored distributions, and promotes diverse coverage to distinguish correlation from causation. Experiments show that optimized intervention selection enables more efficient and reliable structure learning than purely observational approaches and random-intervention baselines, underscoring the importance of principled intervention design in advancing causal discovery.

title: Refining Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation via Regional Semantics and Visual Prototypes
authors: Aijing Yu, Zhengbo Wang, Lijun Sheng, Jian Liang, Xiaoyu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has shown superior performance in the image-level open-vocabulary classification task, benefiting from its powerful alignment of visual features and text embeddings. However, dense prediction tasks require models to perform pixel-level predictions, necessitating modifications to CLIP for region-text alignment. In this paper, we adjust the attention layers within the vanilla visual encoder of CLIP. With a novel attention calculation method for selective attention features, the model retains more regional semantic information. To further mitigate the negative impact of the modality gap, we introduce additional visual prototypes to refine the original predictor solely based on text embeddings. Specifically, patch features with confident predictions are selected to form an additional auxiliary memory-based classifier for more accurate prediction. We further integrate an optimizable feature extractor with an exponential moving average updating strategy to enhance prediction quality. Extensive experiments on 5 semantic segmentation datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

title: DemoReranker: Enhancing the in-Context Learning Capability of Multi-Modal Large Models VIA Demo Ranking
authors: Yuheng Li, Taoyu Wu, Jiechao Gao, Wei Zhu, Wenwen Ouyang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In contemporary large multi-modal model (LMM) deployments, in-context learning (ICL) typically relies on ad hoc demonstration selection, such as reusing a fixed demo pool across diverse queries or retrieving examples via CLIP-based similarity. Although simple, these heuristics fail to consider whether the chosen demonstrations actually facilitate the model’s reasoning and generation processes. To overcome this gap, we introduce DemoReranker, a principled demo selection framework that explicitly models demonstration utility for LMMs. The core idea is to assess demo quality through its observable influence on model outputs, yielding a supervision signal that goes beyond surface-level similarity. Leveraging this signal, we equip a frozen CLIP encoder with a lightweight scoring head to predict the compatibility between a query instance and candidate demonstrations. The reranker is optimized end-to-end with a list-wise ranking objective, allowing it to select sets of demonstrations that are jointly effective for ICL. Experiments on seven benchmarks spanning three multi-modal tasks show that DemoReranker consistently boosts LMM performance by reordering candidates to expose the most beneficial demonstrations.

title: Noise-Robust Video Salient Object Detection in Spike Streams
authors: Amin Mao, Yuming Fang, Jiebin Yan, Patrick Le Callet, Zhenyu Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In spike camera shooting, stray light accumulation, thermal noise, and light intensity loss make pixel-generated spike intervals inaccurately reflect original light source info, posing two video salient object detection (VSOD) challenges in spike streams: 1) Noisy spikes hinder long-term video info capture; 2) Noise makes sparse spikes struggle to capture salient object texture details. To address these issues, we propose a noise-robust VSOD model based on spiking neural networks (SNNs) with a gradual fusion strategy: the spatial-channel cross-perception module (SCPM) enhances attention to salient regions and filters spatial noise; the local deformable cross-attention module (LDCM) strengthens local feature correlations for temporal denoising; the global information-enhanced self-attention module (GISM) models global context and extracts fine-grained textures. Extensive experiments show that it achieves an average relative improvement 9.5% across all metrics compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: The Capabilities and Limitations of Applying Large Language Models in Process Mining: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Nina Kozma, Đorđe Pržulj, Dušanka Dakić, Teodora Vučković
publish: 2026 25th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA (INFOTEH)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to support and enhance business process management. However, their ability to support Process Mining (PM) tasks, such as interpreting event logs, generating process models, identifying bottlenecks, and process deviations, lacks sufficient academic research. The question arises whether Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools can perform process mining tasks just as well as trained business analysts. Therefore, the following research represents a systematic literature review conducted with the aim of presenting key benefits and challenges of utilizing LLM tools for process mining analysis. The literature review shows that general-purpose LLMs can perform process mining tasks, but with significant limitations in handling semantic-aware tasks and consistently identifying process anomalies. Hence, the main contribution of this paper presents an overview of related work on the potential and limitations of LLM-based process mining, as well as suggestions for future research.

title: A Closed-Loop and Data-Driven Decoding Algorithm for Optical Pulse Position Modulated Uplinks in Neural Implants
authors: Yumin Zheng, Rui Jiao, Sunwoo Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Advanced implantable microsystems (IMs) employ pulse position modulation (PPM) for data transmission. However, identifying PPM pulses whose signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) have been heavily attenuated and fluctuating within biological media is challenging. An efficient decoding algorithm can not only relax the IM design equirements, but also increase its operational depth. We present one such PPM decoding algorithm that is adaptive and closed-loop, utilizing a proportional-integral feedback control, and leverages a microsystem’s periodic clock for reference enhancement. For verification, we model a PPM channel based on experimental data from a neural recording autonomous microsystem, microscale optoelectric tetherless electrode (MOTE). Across test datasets with low and varying SNRs, the algorithm provides higher recall and F1 scores compared to the traditional Otsu’s Method and Otsu-based thresholding. This enhanced pulse detectability translates to a 24 % reduction in total power requirement, demonstrating how our algorithmic improvement directly translates into improved system performance.

title: ILSA: Information Loss-Guided Sparsity Allocation for Pruning Large Language Models
authors: Lin Li, Yan Wang, Zhuopeng Wang, Feilong Bao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pruning is an effective approach to accelerate large language models (LLMs), yet uniform sparsity often neglects inter-layer heterogeneity, leading to excessive pruning of sensitive layers. We propose ILSA, an Information Loss-guided Sparsity Allocation framework that employs virtual pruning to estimate perturbations and evaluates layer sensitivity via KL divergence, cosine similarity, and L2 distance. A dynamic Sigmoid-based function then adaptively calibrates sparsity across layers. Integrated with existing pruning methods, ILSA consistently improves performance: on zero-shot benchmarks, it improves mean accuracy by up to 2.0 points over OWL and DSA; under extreme sparsity, it reduces perplexity by an average of about 45% across multiple models compared to the same baselines. These results demonstrate that ILSA effectively balances compression and performance, significantly enhancing pruning efficiency in LLMs.

title: Introduction to the Special Issue on LLM Empowered Internet of Things Part 2
authors: Wei Dong, Jiliang Wang, Stephan Sigg, Luca Mottola
publish: ACM Transactions on Internet of Things
abstract: None

title: SE-DiCoW: Self-Enrolled Diarization-Conditioned Whisper
authors: Alexander Polok, Dominik Klement, Samuele Cornell, Matthew Wiesner, Jan Černocký, Sanjeev Khudanpur, Lukáš Burget
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker-attributed automatic speech recognition (ASR) in multi-speaker environments remains a major challenge. While some approaches achieve strong performance when fine-tuned on specific domains, few systems generalize well across out-of-domain datasets. Our prior work, Diarization-Conditioned Whisper (DiCoW), leverages speaker diarization outputs as conditioning information and, with minimal fine-tuning, demonstrated strong multilingual and multi-domain performance. In this paper, we address a key limitation of DiCoW: ambiguity in Silence–Target–Non-target–Overlap (STNO) masks, where two or more fully overlapping speakers may have nearly identical conditioning despite differing transcriptions. We introduce SE-DiCoW (Self-Enrolled Diarization-Conditioned Whisper), which uses diarization output to locate an enrollment segment anywhere in the conversation where the target speaker is most active. This enrollment segment is used as fixed conditioning via cross-attention at each encoder layer. We further refine DiCoW with improved data segmentation, model initialization, and augmentation. Together, these advances yield substantial gains: SE-DiCoW reduces macro-averaged tcpWER by 52.4% relative to the original DiCoW on the EMMA MT-ASR benchmark.

title: Detecting Abnormal Vehicle Behavior with a Diffusion-Transformer Generation Framework
authors: Zhiyu Wang, Linheng Li, Xu Qu, Bin Ran
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the rapid development of intelligent transportation systems (ITS), ensuring traffic safety has become a critical societal priority. Abnormal driving behavior detection has emerged as an essential approach to mitigate potential traffic risks, as even minor abnormal behaviors can lead to severe safety hazards through cascading effects under specific environmental conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an innovative framework for the detection of abnormal vehicle behavior, built on the deep integration of a diffusion model and the Transformer architecture. The proposed framework adopts a two-stage process: a generation stage, where a classifier-free denoising diffusion model reconstructs the normal trajectory distribution from Gaussian noise iteratively, and a comparison stage, where deviations between observed trajectories and generated normal trajectory distributions are quantified to detect anomalies. This method eliminates the need for hand-made thresholds, overcoming the subjectivity and rigidity of traditional anomaly detection approaches, and effectively addresses the severe class imbalance between normal and abnormal samples often observed in real-world traffic scenarios. In addition, extensive evaluations of real-world traffic datasets demonstrate the superiority of the framework, achieving a precision of 0.948, a precision of 0.317, a recall of 0.765, and an F1-score of 0.448, significantly exceeding state-of-the-art benchmarks. These results underscore the potential of the proposed framework as a robust foundation for proactive early-warning systems in ITS, contributing to safer and more efficient transportation networks.

title: Contrastive Learning for Terrain Identification of Earthworm-Like Robots with Limited Labeled Proprioceptive Data
authors: Hongsen Pang, Hanzhuo Zhang, Yunfei Zheng, Hongbin Fang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Accurate terrain perception is essential for autonomous decision-making in earthworm-like robots, which navigate complex environments using retrograde peristaltic gaits. During locomotion, these robots generate proprioceptive feedback through ground interaction, making visionless terrain recognition possible. However, conventional learning-based methods typically rely on large labeled datasets, which are costly and impractical for field-deployed earthworm-like robots. In this study, we develop an inertial sensor system to capture the robot’s proprioceptive signals and propose a contrastive learning framework for efficient terrain identification with minimal supervision. The model employs data augmentation strategies—both strong and weak—to generate diverse views of the same data, facilitating robust feature extraction. A Temporal Convolutional Network and autoregressive Transformer are used to capture multi-scale temporal dependencies from raw IMU signals. To further enhance performance under limited labeled data, we introduce a semi-supervised fine-tuning stage, which generates pseudo-labels for unlabeled data and applies supervised contrastive loss to refine the learned representations. Extensive experiments across varied terrains and gaits show that our method consistently outperforms existing supervised and self-supervised baselines, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal labeled data. This approach addresses a key challenge in proprioceptive terrain recognition and offers a scalable, efficient solution for real-time deployment in resource-constrained earthworm-like robotic platforms.

title: IRKAN: Wavelet-based Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Peng Ding, Xiaolin Wei, Weihua He, Wuming Wu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Infrared small target detection is fundamentally challenged by the poor generalization of deep learning models across diverse sensors and imaging resolutions. To address this limitation, a novel framework named IRKAN is proposed, which reimagines the network’s core feature extraction mechanism. The proposed approach moves beyond conventional architectures by adopting the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network paradigm, replacing fixed, node-based activations with learnable functions on the network edges. This design endows the model with the intrinsic capability to learn optimal, data-driven transformations for complex infrared signals. To tailor this adaptive framework for the specific physics of infrared small target detection, wavelet basis functions are employed to parameterize these activations. This innovation infuses a powerful signal-processing prior into the network, leveraging the time-frequency localization properties of wavelets to function as a learnable matched filter, adept at isolating the faint, high-frequency signatures of targets from background clutter. By strategically embedding these specialized wavelet-parameterized Kolmogorov-Arnold Network modules into a U-Net backbone, IRKAN achieves strong performance on the public WideIRSTD-Full dataset. Evaluated over ten independent runs, it attains a mean normalized Intersection over Union of 68.89% and a low False Alarm Rate of 8.33 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup>, while maintaining computational efficiency (3.96M parameters). The synergy between the architectural flexibility of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks and the signal-aware inductive bias of wavelets provides a robust solution to the challenges of modern infrared small target detection. Zero-shot evaluations on the unseen NUDT-SIRSTD (512 × 512) and IRSTD-1K (256 × 256) datasets indicate that IRKAN possesses strong cross-resolution generalization capabilities, particularly under resolution shifts, without requiring fine-tuning.

title: A High Gain and Fully Metallic Ku-Band Antenna Design based on High-Order Mode Method
authors: Zhaoqi Bian, Yuanxi Cao, Fei Yang, Hengfeng Wang, Sen Yan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a fully metallic high-gain antenna operating in Ku-band. A high-order mode (HOM) method is employed to calculate the dispersion curves of periodic structure accurately. This approach enables convenient acquisition of the radiation efficiency of slot antennas, and provide a guidance for slot array design. A single-ridge waveguide is adopted as the transmission waveguide of the slot array, which effectively expands the operating bandwidth and narrows the array element spacing, thus greatly improving the aperture efficiency. Additionally, to accommodate the small spacing caused by the ridge waveguide, a compact H-plane ridge waveguide T-junction with a matching slab is designed for the feeding network. This T-junction requires no complex ridge deformation or additional impedance matching lines, thus effectively saving the space of feeding network. The measured peak gain of the antenna prototype reaches 33.45 dBi, with an operating bandwidth of 7.3%. Its radiation efficiency and aperture efficiency reach 85.2% and 81.4%, respectively.

title: Sub-30 nm Silicon Nanopatterning: Toward Reduced Reflectance and Enhanced Sensitivity in CMOS Image Sensors
authors: ByoungGyu Kim, Yongkeun Lee
publish: Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems
abstract: This work investigated how subwavelength silicon patterning and dielectric passivation influence the optical and electrical performance of advanced CMOS image sensors (CIS). Hole arrays with sidewall angles of 73°, 79°, and 86° were fabricated using industrial plasma-etch processes, with and without aluminum-oxide/hafnium-oxide passivation and antireflective (AR) coatings. Reflectance measurements from 360–1000 nm showed that etch geometry and dielectric filling substantially reduced Fresnel reflection, with the 79° profile achieving the lowest broadband reflectance and film-filled structures providing additional suppression in the blue–green region. Device-level evaluations on 0.64 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m-pixel CIS sensors demonstrated RGB sensitivity improvements of 0.7–3.3% under low illumination (23 lux, 30 ms), with no increase in crosstalk. These trends correlated closely with wafer-level reflectance, confirming reflectance as a reliable early predictor of device performance. Although certain etch conditions increased dark current due to plasma-induced damage, the aluminum-oxide/hafnium-oxide passivation stack effectively mitigated interface traps, enabling stable electrical behavior for optimized profiles. Overall, the results demonstrated that co-optimizing etch geometry, dielectric materials, and process conditions enhances both optical efficiency and electrical robustness, providing a manufacturable path toward higher-sensitivity CIS devices in submicron-pixel technology.[2025-0151]

title: Tensor Wheel Decomposition: Theory and Application to Tensor Completion
authors: Zhong-Cheng Wu, Liang-Jian Deng, Ting-Zhu Huang, Hong-Xia Dou, Gemine Vivone, Yu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Recently, tensor network (TN) decompositions have gained prominence in computer vision and contributed promising results to tensor recovery for their capability of compactly and efficiently representing high-order tensors. However, current TN topologies are rather being developed towards more intricate structures to pursue incremental improvements, resulting in a drastically increased number of TN ranks, which requires laborious hyper-parameter selection, especially for higher-order cases. In this paper, we propose a novel TN decomposition, dubbed tensor wheel (TW) decomposition, in which a high-order tensor is represented by a set of latent factors mapped into a specific wheel topology. Such a decomposition is constructed starting from analyzing the graph structure, aiming to more accurately characterize the complex interactions inside objectives while maintaining a lower hyper-parameter scale, theoretically alleviating the above deficiencies. The comprehensive analysis of the mathematical properties fully demonstrates that TW decomposition can be more potential in representation capabilities and more flexible in controlling both parameter storage and computational costs. To compute the TW-format decomposition, the sequential singular value decomposition (SVD)-based and the alternating least squares (ALS)-based learning algorithms are developed. Furthermore, to investigate the validity of TW decomposition, we provide its one numerical application, i.e., tensor completion (TC), yet develop an efficient proximal alternating minimization-based solving algorithm with guaranteed convergence. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data reveal that TW decomposition significantly outperforms other state-of-the-art tensor decompositions for incomplete-tensor inference, especially under solely few observations, thus substantiating the superiority and reliability of TW decomposition.

title: High Gain Observer Based Simultaneous Identification of Stator Resistance and Rotor Speed for Sensorless Induction Motor Drives
authors: Lisi Tian, Lijuan Wang, Tiantuo He, Lei Xu, Yu Wang, Yujie Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: To address the issues of system instability and control performance degradation in sensorless induction motor drives caused by stator resistance mismatch under low-speed conditions, this paper proposes and investigates a dual-parameter simultaneous identification method based on an Adaptive High-Gain Observer (AHGO). By constructing an augmented system, the proposed method achieves real-time, synchronous online identification of both rotor speed and stator resistance. Consequently, it effectively mitigates the risk of system instability induced by the excessive parameter sensitivity inherent in conventional single-parameter identification strategies. Experimental results fully validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method under various complex low-speed operating conditions.

title: Hidden Markov Model-Based Approach For Adaptive Learning Path Recommendation
authors: Miftah Farid Adiwisastra, Irfan Darmawan, Dade Nurjanah.
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Adaptive learning systems require effective mechanisms to model students’ evolving learning states and provide personalized learning path recommendations. Traditional rule-based approaches are limited in capturing temporal learning dynamics and often fail to adapt to individual student progress. To address this limitation, this study proposes a Hidden Markov Model (HMM)-based framework for adaptive learning path recommendation. The proposed approach models students’ learning progression as a sequence of latent states inferred from discretized performance scores derived from learning activities. The HMM is trained using the Baum–Welch algorithm and employs the Viterbi algorithm to estimate the most probable sequence of learning states. These inferred states are then integrated into a recommendation engine that generates personalized next-item learning recommendations based on a probabilistic ranking mechanism and curriculum constraints. Experimental results on a real-world educational dataset demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms a traditional rule-based baseline, achieving an accuracy improvement of up to 15%. The model also shows improved robustness under varying learning behaviors and noisy data conditions. In addition, qualitative evaluation through learning satisfaction indicates positive user feedback toward the personalized recommendations. The findings suggest that the proposed HMM-based framework provides an interpretable and effective approach for modeling learning dynamics and generating adaptive learning paths. This work establishes a foundation for future extensions incorporating hybrid models, including deep learning and reinforcement learning, to further enhance adaptability and scalability in intelligent learning systems.

title: An Explainable Multimodal Vision–Language Framework with Adaptive Mixture of Experts and Optimized Learning for Remote Sensing Image Captioning and Visual Question Answering
authors: M Balakrishna Mallapu, Deepthi Godavarthi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The provision of accurate and explainable captions and answers to visual questions about remote sensing images is essential in areas such as disaster management, land-use supervision, and military analysis, where system interpretability is a critical factor for decision-making. However, existing vision–language models, powered by transformers and multimodal encoders, often struggle to generalize across diverse scene types and lack transparent reasoning mechanisms, thereby limiting their applicability in critical domains. To address these challenges, thisresearch aims to develop a unified and explainable vision–language framework that improves both caption generation and visual question answering performance while ensuring interpretability. Although the current vision-language models using the transformer architecture, multimodal encoders, and attention-based decoders achieve promising results, the standardization of diverse scene types and transparent decision-making remain to be addressed. Therefore, this research presents the application of the explainable vision-language model with the incorporation of the Mixture of Experts-based Robustly bidirectional encoder representations from the transformers AutoEncoder (MoERAE) block to the caption generation task. The MoERAE model utilizes the dynamically routing mechanism for the routing of the multimodal features using different expert paths to capture the context-based representation of the different scenarios in remote sensing. The framework further incorporates an Attention-based Vision Transformer (AVT) for robust spatial feature extraction and GPT-4 for effective multimodal alignment and semantic reasoning. The fine-tuning of MoERAE is carried out by the Rabbit and Turtle Algorithm (RTA), which is an adaptive optimizer that ensures the optimal selection of experts by striking a balance between rapid exploration and convergence. Thus, the quality of the captions generated is very high. Furthermore, the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) technique has been employed to ascertain the importance of specific visual and textual tokens in the generation of captions and answers, thus promoting transparency. The framework is tested and validated on several benchmark datasets such as UCM-Captions, Sydney-Captions, and RSICD. The metrics for evaluating the framework are also provided. The experimental results prove that the proposed framework performs better than existing ones by achieving a high of 99% and a cross-validation accuracy of 99%, thereby proving the robustness and generalization of the framework. The results also prove that the integration of multimodal alignment, adaptive expert routing, and explainability mechanisms into the framework significantly enhances its performance and interpretability for practical use cases in the field of remote sensing.

title: NASchecker: Automatically Identifying the Performance, Security, and Privacy Issues of NAS Devices
authors: Guangyue Ren, Jincheng Wang, Le Yu, Liping Han, Mingzhe Hu, Wei Chen, Tingting Liu, Xiapu Luo, Guozi Sun
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Network attached storage (NAS) devices are widely deployed for personal data storage. However, their distributed architecture and limited inspection interfaces pose significant challenges for comprehensive performance, security, and privacy analysis. In this paper, we first establish a threat model for NAS ecosystems. Then, we present a systematic framework NASchecker for discovering performance optimization mechanisms, security threats, and privacy leakage in NAS devices. By analyzing traffic generated during varied file operations on crafted files, NASchecker infers implemented optimizations and identifies security flaws within the traffic (e.g., susceptibility to passive sniffing and replay attacks). NASchecker also integrates NAS-specific protocol fuzzing and firmware reverse engineering to uncover deep-seated command injection, memory corruption, and improper access control vulnerabilities. NASchecker compares personally identifiable information (PII) leaked in traffic against declarations in privacy policies to detect privacy compliance issues. We evaluated NASchecker on twelve commercial NAS devices. Our results reveal that none of the tested devices employ file compression or deduplication. From a security standpoint, ten devices are vulnerable to passive sniffing and seven to replay attacks. Moreover, seven devices are affected by command injection, four by memory corruption, and eleven by improper access control. From a privacy perspective, four devices leaked PIIs that were not disclosed in their respective privacy policies. After reporting the findings to the manufacturers, we have been acknowledged by several manufacturers, resulting in the assignment of 20 CVEs and 6 NVDB entries (16 of them are rated as high severity). These findings validate NASchecker’s effectiveness and underscore the urgent need for improved design and testing practices of NAS.

title: Reliable Visual Perception and Reasoning via False Positive Detection and Correction
authors: Ben Zhang, Lulu Yu, Ke Yan, Jing Liu, Chong Mu, Qian Huang, Hui Gao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In Internet of Things (IoT) scenarios, vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly employed for visual perception and reasoning. However, their inherent tendency toward hallucinated and post-hoc rationalized reasoning often produces false positives (FPs), where the final answer is correct but supported by unreliable reasoning paths, typically manifested as self-inconsistent reasoning. Existing methods primarily focus on forward enhancement of reasoning ability, while neglecting reverse verification of reasoning consistency. To address this issue, we propose ViFP, a training-free framework for visual FP detection and correction. ViFP identifies FPs through consistency analysis between direct reasoning and structured multi-step reasoning, uncovering self-inconsistent reasoning without relying on superior supervisory models. Based on detected FPs, ViFP adaptively optimizes reasoning paths by refining question types and reasoning chain templates. Furthermore, we introduce a novel reliability metric, Value of Correction (VoC), which quantitatively measures the benefit of FP correction by jointly considering accuracy improvement, true negative rate, and FP reduction. VoC provides an interpretable indicator of reasoning reliability beyond conventional accuracy metrics. ViFP is designed as a cloud/edge-side reasoning reliability service for IoT systems and is compatible with leading closed-source VLMs. Experiments on A-OKVQA, OK-VQA, and FVQA demonstrate that ViFP significantly improves reasoning accuracy and reduces FPs, achieving up to 5.4% accuracy gain and surpassing previous state-of-the-art performance.

title: CaMT-iTire: Context-aware Multi-Task Learning for intelligent Tire Framework
authors: Suwoong Heo, Hyewon Moon, Seunghyun Chung, Kwonwoo Lee, Sunghan You
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Intelligent tire systems (ITS) enable direct sensing of tire and road interaction using sensors mounted inside the tire, providing information that is difficult to obtain from conventional vehicle-mounted sensors. However, acceleration signals measured from tire-mounted inertial measurement unit (IMU) sensors are influenced by multiple physically coupled factors, including vertical load, wheel alignment, and varying operating conditions. This coupling limits the robustness of conventional single-task estimators and multi-task learning approaches in real-world driving environments. In this paper, we propose CaMT-iTire, a context-aware multi-task learning framework designed to jointly estimate wheel load and wheel alignment parameters (toe and camber angles) from a single tire accelerometer sensor mounted on inner liner of a tire. The proposed framework combines a learnable Sinc-based filterbank with a condition-aware convex band-mixing layer to perform adaptive frequency-selective feature extraction from raw acceleration signals. In addition, a Progressive Layered Extraction (PLE) backbone with context-aware gating is introduced to mitigate inter-task interference by dynamically routing features according to operating conditions. Extensive experiments conducted on both controlled drum-test and real world on-road driving data demonstrate that the proposed CaMT-iTire framework consistently outperforms conventional single-task and standard multi-task learning baselines. The results show improved estimation accuracy and robustness, particularly across the operating conditions covered in our datasets, highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed context-aware multi-task learning approach for intelligent tire.

title: STPNet: A Dual-Channel Spatiotemporal Network for Reweighting-Based Smartphone Positioning in Complex Urban Environments
authors: Li Liu, Hua Chen, Jian Wang, Zhao Li, Ran Lu, Kai Liu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) provides absolute positioning for Internet of Things (IoT) devices, with smartphones being the most pervasive user terminals. However, in urban environments, non-line-of-sight (NLOS) interference severely degrades GNSS positioning on smartphones. Machine-learning (ML)-based NLOS mitigation has attracted increasing attention, but the existing methods often rely on single-level feature representations. Moreover, detected NLOS signals are commonly excluded or assigned relatively low weights, thus ignoring their contributions in the observation equation and leading to degraded positioning accuracy. To address these issues, this study proposes STPNet, a dual-channel spatiotemporal model for NLOS-aware observation reweighting in smartphone-based GNSS positioning. STPNet learns per-satellite spatial representations and multi-epoch temporal dependencies from smartphone raw GNSS observations, and the predicted outputs are then employed for pseudorange correction and observation reweighting. Experimental results show that STPNet achieves an NLOS classification accuracy of 92.7%, markedly outperforming baseline ML methods. In terms of horizontal and vertical positioning accuracy, the proposed method achieves improvements of 81.4% and 83.8%, respectively, compared with traditional filtering methods. Analysis of the model window length indicates that longer temporal sequences improve positioning accuracy but increase inference latency, with a window of 12–16 epochs exhibiting an effective trade-off for smartphone deployment. This method provides a robust and practical solution for smart mobility and real-time navigation in urban GNSS-challenged environments.

title: Entropy Injection in Fused Market Networks: HSBM–MDL Quantification of News-Driven Structural Reconfiguration
authors: Jinze Yang, Kaira Sekiguchi, Yukio Ohsawa
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Understanding how semantic information flow reorganizes market topology remains an open challenge in financial network analysis. Prior work typically emphasizes price correlations or sentiment polarity, but rarely quantifies the structural response of the market network to information inflow within a unified coding framework. We propose a fusion-based approach to measure topology-aware information impact. Specifically, we construct a Price Network GP from return co-movements and a Semantic Network GN from LLM-based text embeddings, and integrate them into a Fused Network GF . Using the Hierarchical Stochastic Block Model (HSBM) under the minimum description length principle, we estimate structural entropies for GF and GP (HF and HP), and define the marginal structural cost of information injection as ΔH = HF − HP. Empirically, using S&P 100 constituents over 2023–2025, we find that ΔH is consistently positive, indicating that semantic information inflow acts as an entropy injector relative to the price backbone. High-risk regimes (periods of elevated volatility) exhibit markedly larger ΔH together with a reorganization of the inferred hierarchy, characterized by deeper partitions and increased fine-grained communities, consistent with micro-fragmentation of market structure. At the macro level, normalized modularity ZQ declines on high-volatility days, suggesting weaker sectoral separability and intensified cross-community coupling. Finally, permutation-based counterfactual tests that disrupt semantic alignment show that the observed regime separation inΔH is not attributable to arbitrary mixing, supporting the interpretation that information shocks induce organized structural reconfiguration rather than random disorder. Overall, our framework provides a principled, topology-aware link between semantic information flow and market-wide risk states.

title: Multimodal haptic object recognition: Can kinesthetic inference compensate for the lack of tactile sensing resolution?
authors: Francisco Pastor, Da-hui Lin-Yang, Alfonso J. García-Cerezo, Juan M. Gandarias
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Haptic perception arises from the integration of cutaneous and kinesthetic cues, yet achieving human-level tactile performance in robotic systems remains technically demanding and economically costly. This raises a fundamental question: how much does spatial resolution truly matter for robotic touch, and can kinesthetic inference compensate when tactile resolution is limited? To investigate this, we conduct a study on haptic object recognition using an underactuated, sensorized gripper equipped with a high-resolution tactile array and joint angle sensors. We evaluate a dataset of 36 objects collected under a squeeze-and-release exploratory procedure (EP). Tactile sequences are downsampled to four resolutions (100%, 75%, 50%, and 25%) using bicubic interpolation, and ConvLSTM-based models are trained to quantify accuracy degradation as spatial detail decreases. We then use two fusion strategies from our previous work: Bayesian inference and neural-based inference, which combine tactile and kinesthetic modalities, and assess whether kinesthetic cues can offset the loss of tactile precision. The results quantify the performance drop as tactile resolution decreases and uncover promising evidence that multimodal fusion can partially recover this lost capability. Interestingly, the two fusion methods exhibit distinct behaviors in terms of robustness and consistency. As discussed comprehensively in the manuscript, these findings suggest that, under the right conditions, reliable object recognition may not depend solely on maintaining high tactile resolution, opening new possibilities for designing more scalable and cost-efficient haptic perception systems.

title: Multi-Scale Residual Denoising Network for Robust InSAR Phase Reconstruction
authors: Qi Zhang, Houjun Jiang, Teng Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) phase denoising is essential for deformation monitoring and topographic mapping, yet it remains challenging due to the inherent trade-off between denoising strength, structural preservation, and computational efficiency. Classical filters maintain phase integrity but oversmooth fine details, non-local methods deliver effective noise suppression at high computational cost, and existing deep learning approaches often alter low-SNR regions, introducing artifacts. To overcome these limitations, we propose the Multi-Scale Residual Denoising Network (MSRD-Net), a hierarchical phase reconstruction framework that integrates the nonlinear recovery capability of deep learning with the robustness of frequency-domain filtering. MSRD-Net decomposes interferometric phase into low-frequency and corresponding residual components, preserving dominant structures while progressively restoring fine-scale details. A self-boosting training strategy using real-world noise extracted from Sentinel-1 and TanDEM-X data further improves the network's robustness and generalization. Evaluations across four representative scenarios—earthquakes, landslides, rainforests, and urban areas—show that MSRD-Net achieves a balanced improvement in noise suppression, structural fidelity, and efficiency. Furthermore, its scalable design allows adaptive adjustment of denoising strength, while its iterative nature ensures reliable performance under extreme noise. These characteristics establish MSRD-Net as a practical and versatile solution for both large-scale operational monitoring and high-precision geoscience studies with InSAR.

title: A Physics Parameter-Guided Metalearning Framework for Temperature Prediction of Double-Sided Double-Glass TopCon Photovoltaic Modules
authors: Huajian Ding, Zhongyuan Su, Jun Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Photovoltaics
abstract: Aiming at the need for accurate prediction operational temperature of N-type TOPCon bifacial double-glass photovoltaic modules, this article proposes an integrated forecasting method combining physical principles with metalearning. A metalearner model based on a Stacking ensemble framework is constructed, which integrates predictions from a thermodynamic model and a dual-branch long short-term memory (LSTM) network through a dynamic weighting mechanism. Cross-validation strategies are employed to train deep neural networks, enabling multiscale feature integration and weight self-adjustment. Experimental results achieve a root mean square error (RMSE) of only 0.95°C, representing a 66% reduction compared to the physical model (RMSE = 2.81°C) and a 14% reduction compared to the LSTM model (RMSE = 1.11°C), substantially improving the model’s adaptability in nonlinear and dynamic scenarios.

title: Young Professionals: Learning to Learn: Reflections from ISDEIV 2025 and a Career in Science
authors: Moein Borghei
publish: IEEE Electrical Insulation Magazine
abstract: The city of Chengdu, home of giant pandas and spicy Sichuan cuisine, was the host to the electrical insulation community for the 31st International Symposium on Discharges and Electrical Insulation in Vacuum (ISDEIV) from September 21 to 26, 2025. The event, impeccably organized by Sichuan University (chaired by Shenli Jia) and technically sponsored by the IEEE Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation Society (DEIS) along with sponsorship from key industry leaders, brought together 288 researchers, engineers, and students from across Asia, Europe, and the Americas.

title: CF-LRDM: An Efficient Channel Fingerprint Construction Approach Assisted by Latent Residual-based Diffusion Model
authors: Wenya Fan, Zhenzhou Jin, Li You, Chenjie Xie, Xiqi Gao
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Accurate acquisition of channel state information (CSI) for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems is of paramount importance for next-generation wireless networks. An emerging approach termed channel fingerprint (CF) can store essential site-specific channel knowledge information within the target communication area, which is conducive to the acquisition of CSI. However, limited by hardware cost and storage overhead, most available CFs are coarse-grained, which fail to provide sufficient and precise channel-related knowledge. To tackle this challenge, we propose an efficient generative approach for CF construction, namely latent residual-based diffusion model (CF-LRDM). Specifically, different from traditional generative diffusion models (GDMs) with ill-posed generating targets and limited inference efficiency, the proposed CF-LRDM establishes a Markov chain between fine-grained and coarse-grained CFs via residual-shifting and can sample from a given coarse-grained CF to a fine-grained CF with only 15 sampling steps. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance of the proposed method against existing baselines.

title: IGBT Open-Circuit Fault Detection and Localization Method for Cascaded H-Bridge Stage of Power Electronic Transformer
authors: Tao Zheng, Chong Wang, Yufei Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: The rectifier stage of power electronic transformers (PETs) commonly employs either a cascaded H-bridge (CHB) or a modular multilevel converter (MMC) topology. Due to its high modularity, scalability, and ease of redundancy design, the CHB topology is widely adopted in PETs. However, the use of numerous power electronic devices in PETs increases the probability of faults. In this paper, a novel fault detection and localization method is proposed for the rapid identification of IGBT open-circuit faults in the CHB stages of PETs. A mathematical model of the CHB grid-side voltage is established, which contains abundant harmonic information. The harmonic characteristics of the grid-side voltage under different IGBT open-circuit faults are analyzed, revealing that the amplitude and phase of even-order carrier frequency harmonics depend on the location of the faulty H-bridges. Based on this characteristic, the fault region is identified. By adjusting the modulation scheme of specific H-bridges and analyzing the resulting variations in the grid-side voltage, multiple IGBT open-circuit faults can be accurately localized. The proposed method utilizes the characteristic harmonics of the H-bridge port theoretical voltage as a reference, thereby reducing fault-identification time and remaining unaffected by load conditions. Its effectiveness is validated through PSCAD/EMTDC simulations and hardware-in-the-loop experiments.

title: Transient DC Bias Suppression Strategy and Zero-Voltage Switching Boundary Analysis for Three-Level Dual-Active-Bridge Converter
authors: Tao Jin, Lingxiang Chen, Minxin Lin, Chang Qiu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The dual-active-bridge (DAB) converter is widely used in DC-DC applications due to its high efficiency and power density. However, the modulation of the DAB converters presents challenges in terms of efficiency and reliability. One key issue is the requirement for a wide zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) range to minimize switching losses, which significantly impact efficiency. Another is the occurrence of transient DC-bias currents in the transformer, which can lead to core saturation and component damage during dynamic operations such as load changes or power reversals. This article addresses the challenges of operating three-level dual-active-bridge (DAB) converters by introducing strategies for ZVS optimization and transient DC-bias suppression. The ZVS boundaries are analyzed using a harmonic decomposition method, which leads to an optimized phase-shift strategy for achieving the widest possible ZVS range. The paper also presents a method to eliminate transient DC-bias by balancing the volt-second integrals during dynamic shifts. This integrated control method can be implemented by using a unified optimization method without the need for additional auxiliary hardware. The effectiveness of these methods is confirmed through simulation and experiments.

title: Collaborative Design of Conductor Plate and Switching Pattern for SiC Three-Level ANPC Inverter in PMSM Drives
authors: Guanzhou Ren, Guangtong Ma, Hong Xie, Hong Wan, Shuai Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: In recent years, the silicon carbide (SiC) power devices have been widely applied in permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives. However, their application in three-level inverters introduces critical design challenges, including low stray inductance busbar configurations and balanced thermal distribution. To overcome these challenges, this article presents a collaborative design of conductor plate and switching pattern for SiC three-level active neutral-point-clamped (ANPC) inverters. First, a four-layer laminated conductor plate is developed to minimize switching overshoot and reduce stray inductance. The conductor plate geometries are optimized for balanced thermal distribution. Then, to mitigate common-mode current and enhance dielectric reliability, a multimode space vector pulse width modulation (SVPWM) is proposed. Through the switching patterns optimization, the common-mode voltage and switching frequency are suppressed simultaneously. Additionally, the optimized switching pattern achieves precise neutral-point voltage balance by dynamically adjusting the dwell times of four trapezoidal-shaped vectors. The experimental verification with PMSM drive and resistive-inductive load demonstrates the efficiency and robustness of the designed configuration and the optimized modulation scheme.

title: Towards adaptive optimal personalization control of robotic hip exoskeleton assistance for individuals with paretic stroke_supp2-3685273.mp4
authors: Qiang Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Neurological disorders, like stroke, can re duce the mobility and quality of life for millions of indi viduals across the world. As promising gait assistive de vices, powered lower-limb exoskeletons have gained wide attention for their potential to improve walking function for individuals with mobility impairments. However, due to the complex human-robot interaction, highly nonlinear dynamics modeling, and person-to-person variability, it is challenging to provide optimal customized assistance from these exoskeletons. In the current study, we showed that a reinforcement learning (RL)-based control framework pro vided adaptive and optimal personalized assistance across the hip joint to improve walking function for individuals with and without mobility deficits. We selected hip kinematic spatial gait symmetry as the control objective for five par ticipants with hemiparetic stroke, and found three optimal control parameters over an average of 201.7±36.1 sec. The kinematic spatial gait symmetry was defined as the joint excursion symmetry on both hip joints within the same gait cycle. By adding optimal assistance, we observed that the joint excursion during gait cycles at the affected hip joint was increased from 37.09° to 48.03° by 29.5% (p = 0.003) and the joint excursion gait symmetry was improved from 7.55% to-0.63% (p = 0.002) compared to transparent mode (no assistance). The findings suggest that the application of an RL-based assistance personalization control scheme to a wearable hip exoskeleton may positively impact gait rehabilitation training outcomes in a clinical population.

title: On the Study of Integrated Sensing and Communication: From an Information Timeliness Perspective
authors: Chao Jia, Zhongyuan Zhao, Like Sun, Tony Q. S. Quek
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: In integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, the timeliness performance is pivotal to support high-precision sensing and reliable high-rate data communication, particularly for real-time applications. However, there are two critical challenges in the research on the timeliness of ISAC systems: joint characterization of sensing-communication time-liness tradeoffs and unified resource allocation optimization. To address these challenges, a timeliness-centric analysis and optimization framework is proposed for ISAC systems. First, the age of information (AoI) of sensing tasks and the delay of communication tasks are analyzed to evaluate the timeliness performance of ISAC systems. Then, the closed-form expressions of the outage probabilities for sensing and communication tasks are derived, quantifying their reliability under dynamic channel conditions. Next, a joint sensing-and-communication time-allocation optimization problem is established to minimize the weighted sum of sensing AoI and communication delay.
