To tackle the non-linear integer programming problem, a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm is designed, which can be verified to converge to an optimal solution with polynomial computational complexity. Simulation results are presented to validate the accuracy of the analytical results, and demonstrate the optimality and performance gains of our proposed DP algorithm.

title: Multipath Component Power Delay Profile Based Joint Range and Doppler Estimation for AFDM-ISAC Systems
authors: Fangqing Xiao, Zunqi Li, Dirk Slock
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems combine sensing and communication functionalities within a unified framework, enhancing spectral efficiency and reducing costs by utilizing shared hardware components. This paper investigates multipath component power delay profile (MPCPDP)-based joint range and Doppler estimation for Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing (AFDM)-ISAC systems. The path resolvability of the equivalent channel in the AFDM system allows the recognition of Line-of-Sight (LoS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) paths within a single pilot symbol in fast time-varying channels. We develop a joint estimation model that leverages multipath Doppler shifts and delays information under the AFDM waveform. Utilizing the MPCPDP, we propose a novel ranging method that exploits the range-dependent magnitude of the MPCPDP across its delay spread by constructing a Nakagami-m statistical fading model for MPC channel fading and correlating the distribution parameters with propagation distance in AFDM systems. without requiring additional ranging-specific time synchronization beyond standard receiver coarse synchronization, since the method exploits relative delays and MPCPDP statistics. We also transform the nonlinear Doppler estimation problem into a bilinear estimation problem using a First-order Taylor expansion. Moreover, we introduce the Expectation Maximization algorithm to estimate the hyperparameters and leverage the Expectation Consistent algorithm to cope with high-dimensional integration challenges. Extensive numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of our MPCPDP-based joint range and Doppler estimation in ISAC systems.

title: AnyUser: Translating Sketched User Intent into Domestic Robots
authors: Songyuan Yang, Huibin Tan, Kailun Yang, Wenjing Yang, Shaowu Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Robotics
abstract: We introduce AnyUser, a unified robotic instruction system for intuitive domestic task instruction via free-form sketches on camera images, optionally with language. AnyUser interprets multimodal inputs (sketch, vision, language) as spatial-semantic primitives to generate executable robot actions requiring no prior maps or models. Novel components include multimodal fusion for understanding and a hierarchical policy for robust action generation. Efficacy is shown via extensive evaluations: (1) Quantitative benchmarks on the large-scale dataset showing high accuracy in interpreting diverse sketch-based commands across various simulated domestic scenes. (2) Real-world validation on two distinct robotic platforms, a statically mounted 7-DoF assistive arm (KUKA LBR iiwa) and a dual-arm mobile manipulator (Realman RMC-AIDAL), performing representative tasks like targeted wiping and area cleaning, confirming the system's ability to ground instructions and execute them reliably in physical environments. (3) A comprehensive user study involving diverse demographics (elderly, simulated non-verbal, low technical literacy) demonstrating significant improvements in usability and task specification efficiency, achieving high task completion rates (85.7%-96.4%) and user satisfaction. AnyUser bridges the gap between advanced robotic capabilities and the need for accessible non-expert interaction, laying the foundation for practical assistive robots adaptable to real-world human environments.

title: Research on the Mechanisms of Abnormal Heating and Temperature Rise Distribution of the Decay-like Degraded Composite Insulators
authors: Shuqi Liu, Zhou Zuo, Chao Wu, Qian Wang, Haohan Zhou, Te Li, Ruixiang Tao, Yu Deng, Xinzhe Yu, Songsong Zhou, Xidong Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Online infrared inspection has become a powerful approach for identifying faulted composite insulators; however, the mechanisms responsible for their abnormal heating and temperature distribution remain unclear. In this study, 16 decay-like degraded composite insulators were obtained for investigation after ceasing operation. Their heating and partial discharge characteristics were studied by full-scale energization under AC voltage, along with their leakage currents. Local electrical parameters of the decay-like degraded Fiber-Reinforced-Plastic (FRP) core rods were measured in short segments, which were subsequently imported into a FEM simulation model for electric field and heating analysis. The spatial coincidence of abnormal heating and partial discharge is confirmed in the transitional region of the degraded composite insulator, where local electrical parameters change most significantly. Moreover, the mismatch of AC leakage currents measured from both sides of the degraded composite insulators manifests that the leakage current is not continuous along the composite insulator string. A resistance-capacitance coupling AC equivalent circuit model is introduced, which explicitly interprets how the spatial distribution of electrical parameters results in the concentration of electric field and heating regions.

title: Dielectric Performance and Reprocessability of Boronic-Ester Vitrimeric Epoxy with Nanoscale Phase Separation
authors: Li Zhang, Yiwei Wang, Liang Zou, Zhiyun Han, Guan Wang, Rakhmonov Ikromjon Usmonovich, Kurbonov Nurbek Nurullo Ugli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Epoxy resins are widely employed as insulating materials in ultra-high-voltage (UHV) power equipment thanks to their excellent electrical properties, yet their permanently cross-linked networks are insoluble, infusible and difficult to recycle, and the electrical behavior of their dynamic-bonded counterparts remains poorly understood. Here, Bisphenol A diglycidyl ether (BADGE) was co-cured with a boronic-ester diamine and a flexible monoamine to generate a vitrimer network (EP-NBN) featuring 10 nm hard/soft periodic domains. The electrical behavior of EP-NBN was systematically evaluated at 20 ℃ to 60 ℃: Dielectric analysis and Havriliak–Negami fitting show that increasing temperature weakens interfacial polarization while activating local boronic-ester–related relaxation. The fitted apparent dc conductivity (σdc) decreases with temperature, accompanied by reduced low-frequency dielectric loss, due to diminished electrical contrast between hard and soft phases. Space-charge peaks at both electrodes decrease in magnitude and broaden with temperature. The current density-electric field (J-E) curves evolve from a two-regime lnJ-E behavior consistent with field-assisted interfacial hopping to an approximately single-slope behavior at 60 ℃. EP-NBN shows high AC breakdown strength (38.32 kV/mm) and retains 69.2 % of this value after reprocessing, outperforming the non-dynamic EP-ref. EP-NBN can also self-heal surface scratches and breakdown channels at elevated temperature. DFT calculations indicate a bipolar localization tendency in boronic-ester units, where electrons preferentially localize near boron sites and holes near O/N sites, providing a microscopic basis for interfacial trapping behavior associated with boronic-ester units, while the wide band gap (7.3 eV) supports the high intrinsic insulation level of the network. Overall, the microphase separation + boronic-ester dynamic bonds strategy enables high insulation performance together with reprocessability and self-healing.

title: RhythmBERT: A Self-Supervised Language Model Based on Latent Representations of ECG Waveforms for Heart Disease Detection
authors: Xin Wang, Burcu Ozek, Aruna Mohan, Amirhossein Ravari, Or Zilbershot, Fatemeh Afghah
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is crucial for diagnosing heart disease, but most self-supervised learning methods treat ECG as a generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Existing contrastive methods utilize augmentations that distort morphology, whereas generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation, which misaligns cardiac cycles. To address these limitations, we propose RhythmBERT, a generative ECG language model that considers ECG as a language paradigm by encoding P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations. These discrete tokens capture rhythm semantics, while complementary continuous embeddings retain fine-grained morphology, enabling a unified view of waveform structure and rhythm. RhythmBERT is pretrained on approximately 800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings with a masked prediction objective, allowing it to learn contextual representations in a label-efficient manner. Evaluations show that despite using only a single lead, Rhythm-BERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines. This generalization extends from prevalent conditions such as atrial fibrillation to clinically challenging cases such as subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction. Our results suggest that considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

title: CANDY-Net: Cross-Modal Aligned Dual-Stream Network with Hierarchical Spatial-Frequency Features for Alzheimer’s Disease Classification
authors: Zhi Yang, Lei Cheng, Haitao Gan, Ming Shi, Zhongwei Huang, Bo Cheng, Ji Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) classification based on brain network data remains challenging due to complex multi-modal neuroimaging features and the need for effective integration strategies. This paper proposes CANDY-Net, a dual-modal deep learning framework enhancing AD classification through joint spatial-frequency analysis. We utilized 411 samples from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) data covering AD, MCI, and NC classifications. The framework employs: (1) Spatial-Frequency Feature Extraction (SFFE) using spatial pyramid extractors and DB4 wavelet analysis; (2) Dual-Domain Feature Fusion (DFF) with attention-based cross-modal alignment; (3) Tri-Domain Feature Alignment (TDFA) ensuring complementarity across domains. Through 5-fold cross-validation, CANDY-Net achieves strong performance across accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, demonstrating superior performance for AD classification.

title: Improving Interpretability in Generative Multitimbral DDSP Frameworks via Semantically-Disentangled Musical Attributes
authors: Francesco Ardan Dal Rì, Nicola Conci
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose a generative multitimbral DDSP framework focusing on acoustic instruments modeling. Traditional DDSP architectures rely on frame-wise latent encoding, limiting semantic interpretability; the acoustic realm is further challenged by the inherently interdependency of musical descriptors. Our approach introduces disentangled global embeddings for timbre and dynamics, achieved via a Triple-VAE architecture, and a DDSP model to learn temporal dependencies from such reduced representation along with normalized envelope curves. Experimental results demonstrate optimal reconstruction accuracy while enabling flexible and realistic generation capabilities, with the disentangled representation promoting interpretable control over musical descriptors.

title: Planning-Oriented Adversarial Attack Against End-to-end Autonomous Driving Systems
authors: Hao Tan, Ruonan Li, Junjian Zhang, Huan Zhang, Di Shao, Zhaoquan Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep neural network (DNN)-based end-to-end autonomous driving (E2E AD) models are vulnerable to adversarial threats, where imperceptible perturbations can lead to dangerous collisions. Existing attacks focus on explicitly disrupting perception modules or simply applying classical adversarial methods to E2E AD scenarios. However, these attacks lack temporal coherence and fail to misguide the planning stage, resulting in limited attack capabilities. To address this problem, we propose a planning-oriented adversarial attack (PAA), which is a temporal-aware and trajectory-guided attack framework designed to maximize future collision rates. Specifically, we first propose time-dependent perturbations propagated across frames by exploiting the temporal dependence property. Then, we leverage bird’s-eye view (BEV) features to corrupt critical scene semantics implicitly. Finally, we generate planning-oriented perturbations by constructing adversarial drivable trajectories. Extensive experimental results show that PAA significantly increases the L2 error and collision rate of the advanced E2E AD models in the open-loop attack, with an average collision rate of 5.59% on five models, 19 times more than in the no-attack test. In the closed-loop attack, PAA reduces the driving score by an average of 49.98%.

title: CADM: Cluster-Customized Adaptive Distance Metric for Categorical Data Clustering
authors: Taixi Chen, Yiu-Ming Cheung, Yiqun Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: An appropriate distance metric is crucial for categorical data clustering, as the distance between categorical data cannot be directly calculated. However, the distances between attribute values usually vary in different clusters induced by their different distributions, which has not been taken into account, thus leading to unreasonable distance measurement. Therefore, we propose a cluster-customized distance metric for categorical data clustering, which can competitively update distances based on different distributions of attributes in each cluster. In addition, we extend the proposed distance metric to the mixed data that contains both numerical and categorical attributes. Experiments demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method, i.e., achieving an average ranking of around first in fourteen datasets. The source code is available at https://github.com/Taixi-CHEN/CADM

title: AltProj-Min: A Projection-Based Alternating Minimization Algorithm for Low-Rank Matrix Recovery
authors: Gaojun Dong, Shuang Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-rank matrix recovery from linear measurements is a fundamental problem in signal processing and machine learning. A recent approach, AltGD-Min, achieves provable recovery by alternating between coefficient estimation and gradient-based subspace updates, but requires specifying a fixed step size and performing repeated QR decompositions. In this work, we propose AltProj-Min, a projection-based alternating minimization algorithm that replaces QR-based retractions with a rank-one correction, thereby avoiding step size tuning and reducing the subspace update complexity from ${\mathcal{O}}\left({n{r^2}}\right)$ to ${\mathcal{O}}(nr)$. We further introduce a noise-robust variant, R-AltProj-Min, which incorporates a regularized update to handle measurement corruption. Experiments on synthetic data show that AltProj-Min matches or surpasses AltGD-Min in accuracy while converging much faster, and R-AltProj-Min preserves comparable accuracy under noise with significant efficiency gains.

title: GAP-URGENet: A Generative-Predictive Fusion Framework for Universal Speech Enhancement
authors: Xiaobin Rong, Yushi Wang, Zheng Wang, Jing Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We introduce GAP-URGENet, a generative-predictive fusion framework developed for Track 1 of the ICASSP 2026 URGENT Challenge. The system integrates a generative branch, which performs full-stack speech restoration in a self-supervised representation domain and reconstructs the waveform via a neural vocoder, along with a predictive branch that performs spectrogram-domain enhancement, providing complementary cues. Outputs from both branches are fused by a post-processing module, which also performs bandwidth extension to generate the enhanced waveform at 48 kHz, later downsampled to the original sampling rate. This generative-predictive fusion improves robustness and perceptual quality, achieving top performance in the blind-test phase and ranking 1st in the objective evaluation. Audio examples are available at https://xiaobin-rong.github.io/gap-urgenet_demo.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Secrecy Optimization in Underwater Energy Harvesting Relay Network
authors: Shalini Tripathi, Ankur Bansal, Chinmoy Kundu
publish: 2026 National Conference on Communications (NCC)
abstract: This paper explores secure communication in an underwater energy-harvesting (EH) relay network that supports hybrid optical-acoustic transmission. The optical hop is modeled using a Gamma-Gamma turbulence channel with pointing errors and may occasionally be blocked by underwater obstacles. At the same time, an eavesdropper is assumed to monitor the acoustic hop, creating a secrecy concern. To address this, we formulate the relay power allocation problem as an infinite-horizon Markov decision process (MDP). A model-based reinforcement learning (RL) driven optimal power allocation (OPA) strategy is proposed to maximize long-term cumulative secrecy performance until the network stops functioning. To offer lower-complexity alternatives, we also develop a Greedy Algorithm (GA) and a Naive Algorithm (NA). Simulation results show that the RL-based OPA adapts effectively to battery dynamics, varying channel conditions, and optical link availability, achieving the highest secure data transmission, while GA performs reasonably and NA performs poorly due to its short-sighted decisions.

title: A Convex Demixing Approach for Hybrid-Field Channel Estimation of XL-MIMO Systems via Atomic Norm Minimization
authors: Dehui Yang, Feng Xi, Yanxian Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Channel estimation is a critical task in extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems for 6G wireless communications. A hybrid-field channel model effectively characterizes the mixed far-field and near-field scattering components in practical XL-MIMO systems. In this paper, we propose a convex demixing approach for hybrid-field channel estimation within the atomic norm minimization (ANM) framework. By promoting sparsity of the far-field and near-field components directly in the continuous parameter domain, a demixing scheme that minimizes a weighted sum of two atomic norms is proposed. We show that the resulting ANM is equivalent to a computationally feasible semi-definite programming (SDP). Numerical experiments on simulated data demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches for hybrid-field channel estimation.

title: An Information Geometric Approach to Fairness with Equalized Odds Constraint
authors: Amirreza Zamani, Ayfer Özgür, Mikael Skoglund
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: |We study the statistical design of a fair mechanism that attains equalized odds, where an agent uses some useful data (database) X to solve a task T. Since both X and T are correlated with some latent sensitive attribute S, the agent designs a representation Y that satisfies an equalized odds, that is, such that I(Y;S|T) = 0. In contrast to our previous work, we assume here that the agent has no direct access to S and T; hence, the Markov chains S−X−Y and T −X −Y hold. Furthermore, we impose a geometric structure on the conditional distribution P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S|Y</inf>, allowing Y and S to have a small correlation, bounded by a threshold. When the threshold is small, concepts from information geometry allow us to approximate mutual information and reformulate the fair mechanism design problem as a quadratic program with closed-form solutions under certain constraints. For other cases, we derive simple, low-complexity lower bounds based on the maximum singular value and vector of a matrix. Finally, we compare our designs with the optimal solution in a numerical example.

title: Big Data-Driven Intellectual Property Management and Innovation in Traditional Chinese Medicine
authors: Jiaqi Yan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The research introduces a Big Data-based framework for intellectual property management together with innovation strategies of Traditional Chinese Medicine that safeguards traditional knowledge through innovation development. The framework uses five artificial intelligence-enabled procedures to operate. TCM texts become accessible through Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) so users can search them easily and prevent Biopiracy. The GCN-based TCM Knowledge Graph creates a visual representation that illustrates the relationships between herbs, treatments, and diseases, facilitating more efficient detection of novel patterns. The identification of novelty based on deep-learning-based patent standards compliance is achieved by the use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) during the third step. Decision Trees uses IP suitability assessment based on legal criteria scoring that involves novelty and industrial applicability scoring on innovations. The system applies the Explainable AI (XAI) methods to establish openness to allow the knowledge providers to maintain ethical relationships and comply with global IP regulations. The big data approach supplements the security of TCM innovations in cultural heritage preservation along with international traditional medical knowledge blending.

title: Toward Conversational User Interface via Voice Command Correction
authors: Shi-Jie Ding, Chia-Hui Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite recent advances in AI, ASR systems still struggle with real-world errors from pronunciation and homophones. We propose a speech-command-based correction system that enables users to issue natural-language instructions to refine recognition outputs with minimal effort. The system consists of three modules: an input classifier, a command classifier, and a correction labeler. To support training and evaluation, we simulate ASR errors via TTS/ASR pipelines and generate realistic correction commands using LLMs and linguistic features. Experiments show that individual models achieve over 80% correction accuracy, and a combined model delivers stable performance. Compared to manual correction, this system also demonstrates highly competitive correction speed, which sufficiently indicates its feasibility for practical deployment.

title: A Novel Array Design with Increased Degrees of Freedom by Employing the Vertical Motion of Uniform Circular Array
authors: Chengjin Kang, Jianhong Chu, Zhi Zhang, Ruizhe Zhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The physical cylindrical conformal array (CCA) has garnered significant attention in the field of direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation due to the special conformal property. However, the shadow effect caused by the structure seriously degrades the degrees of freedom (DOF). In this paper, a virtual cylindrical conformal array (VCCA) is designed by employing the vertical motion of uniform circular array (UCA) to address this limitation. Specifically, we make the UCA move along the vertical direction and take advantage of the synthetic aperture process to design the VCCA. Then, an optimal configuration with increased DOF is proposed by introducing a sparse sampling scheme. Meanwhile, the closed-form expression of the DOF is analyzed in detail. Simulations validate that the designed VCCA can effectively mitigate the shadow effect as well as achieve higher DOF and better estimation accuracy.

title: Improving the Speaker Anonymization Evaluation’s Robustness to Target Speakers with Adversarial Learning
authors: Carlos Franzreb, Arnab Das, Tim Polzehl, Sebastian Möller
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The current privacy evaluation for speaker anonymization often overestimates privacy when a same-gender target selection algorithm (TSA) is used, although this TSA leaks the speaker’s gender and should hence be more vulnerable. We hypothesize that this occurs because the evaluation does not account for the fact that anonymized speech contains information from both the source and target speakers. To address this, we propose to add a target classifier that measures the influence of target speaker information in the evaluation, which can also be removed with adversarial learning. Experiments demonstrate that this approach is effective for multiple anonymizers, particularly when using a same-gender TSA, leading to a more reliable assessment.

title: Measuring Prosody Diversity in Zero-Shot TTS: A New Metric, Benchmark, and Exploration
authors: Yifan Yang, Bing Han, Hui Wang, Long Zhou, Wei Wang, Mingyu Cui, Xu Tan, Xie Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Prosody diversity is essential for achieving naturalness and expressiveness in zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS). However, frequently used acoustic metrics capture only partial views of prosodic variation and correlate poorly with human perception, leaving the problem of reliably quantifying prosody diversity underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProsodyEval, a prosody diversity assessment dataset that provides Prosody Mean Opinion Score (PMOS) along-side conventional acoustic metrics. ProsodyEval comprises 1000 speech samples derived from 7 mainstream TTS systems, with 2000 human ratings. Building on this, we propose the Discretized Speech Weighted Edit Distance (DS-WED), a new objective diversity metric that quantifies prosodic variation via weighted edit distance over semantic tokens. Experiments on ProsodyEval show that DS-WED achieves substantially higher correlation with human judgments than existing acoustic metrics, while remaining highly robust in speech tokenization from HuBERT and WavLM. Leveraging DS-WED, we benchmark state-of-the-art open-source TTS systems on LibriSpeech test-clean and Seed-TTS test-en, and further explorations uncover several factors that influence prosody diversity, including generative modeling paradigms, duration control, and reinforcement learning. Moreover, we find that current large audio language models (LALMs) remain limited in capturing prosodic variations. Audio samples are available at https://prosodyeval.github.io.

title: Distributed Multichannel Active Noise Control with Asynchronous Communication
authors: Junwei Ji, Dongyuan Shi, Boxiang Wang, Ziyi Yang, Haowen Li, Woon-Seng Gan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distributed multichannel active noise control (DMCANC) offers effective noise reduction across large spatial areas by distributing the computational load of centralized control to multiple low-cost nodes. Conventional DMCANC methods, however, typically assume synchronous communication and require frequent data exchange, resulting in high communication overhead. To enhance efficiency and adaptability, this work proposes an asynchronous communication strategy where each node executes a weight-constrained filtered-x LMS (WCFxLMS) algorithm and independently requests communication only when its local noise reduction performance degrades. Upon request, other nodes transmit the weight difference between their local control filter and the center point in WCFxLMS, which are then integrated to update both the control filter and the center point. This design enables nodes to operate asynchronously while preserving cooperative behavior. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed asynchronous communication DMCANC (ACDMCANC) system maintains effective noise reduction with significantly reduced communication load, offering improved scalability for heterogeneous networks.

title: Clue2Emo: A Brain-Inspired Framework for Open-Vocabulary Multimodal Emotion Recognition
authors: Ziyun Zhang, Jian Chen, Yuxuan Hu, Zhen Zhang, Xiaoyan Yuan, Min Yang, Xiangyu Zhao, Edith C. H. Ngai, Chengming Li, Xiping Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Open-vocabulary multimodal emotion recognition integrates audio, video, and text to capture nuanced emotions beyond fixed labels, offering a more flexible and fine-grained alternative to traditional categorical approaches. Nevertheless, current methods typically feed features directly into large language models to generate results, lacking explicit reasoning steps, which leads to insufficient interpretability and robustness. To address this issue, we propose Clue2Emo, a brain-inspired two-stage framework grounded in the theory of constructed emotion. The first stage, guided by our newly constructed MER-CLUE dataset, equips the model with explicit multimodal clue generation to fill the gap in intermediate reasoning. The second stage integrates these clues into open-vocabulary reasoning, enhancing both robustness and interpretability. Experimental results show that Clue2Emo achieves state-of-the-art performance on both MER-Caption+ and OV-MERD, while also demonstrating smoother convergence and greater robustness.

title: AI-Driven Supply Chains: Revolutionizing Efficiency and Risk Management
authors: Naveen Kumar Kotha, Monisha Athi Kesavan Premalatha, Sharath Kumar Kanniyappan, Omoshola Owolabi, Ramachandra Handaragal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Today's supply chains are more complex, globalized and subject to more disruptions, but they need new approaches to efficiencies and changing risk levels: A great number of traditional supply chain strategies don't efficiently and dynamically respond to changing demand, inventory balances, and external disruptions to create greater efficiency at lower costs and service levels. This research seeks to establish an artificial intelligence-based framework that combines predictive modelling, reinforcement learning and network-based risk detection in order to optimise the supply chain operations. The framework takes advantage of historical and operating data sets, and uses LSTM for demand forecasting, RL agents for prescriptive decision making, and graph neural networks for disruption detection. Comparative analysis shows that there is a significant improvement in some of the following aspects: lead time: 32 hours, inventory cost: $ 8.6 M, order fulfillment accuracy: 96.8%, disruption probability: 3.2%, expected loss: $ 1.2 M, resilience index: 0.89, superior model performance metrics <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{MAE}=4.5$</tex>, F 1 -score =0.91, cumulative RL reward =1340). The proposed AI framework is a robust data-driven framework for supply chain efficiency and resilience enhancement that can offer actionable insights to operation managers and policy-makers in such a dynamic industrial context.

title: Solving the Helmholtz Equation Via Physics-Informed Neural Networks with an Adaptive Weighting Strategy
authors: Yanan Guo, Junqiang Song, Xiaoqun Cao, Hongze Leng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) integrate governing physical laws into deep learning architectures to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Despite their versatility, PINN optimization for complex physical systems is frequently impeded by gradient pathologies and slow convergence, which result in suboptimal solution accuracy. This study introduces a pointwise adaptive weighting strategy to dynamically balance residual decay rates across training coordinates, thereby mitigating the disparate convergence rates that often constitute a primary bottleneck in PINN training. The proposed methodology is applied to the forward Helmholtz equation in acoustic wave modeling. Numerical evaluations demonstrate that this adaptive framework surpasses conventional PINN implementations in terms of numerical precision, convergence velocity, and computational efficiency. The high consistency between the predicted solutions and analytical benchmarks validates the efficacy of the proposed approach for acoustic field reconstruction and provides a robust foundation for future research involving complex inverse parameter identification tasks.

title: Adaptive Compressed Integrate-and-Fire Time Encoding Machine
authors: Vered Karp, Aseel Omar, Alejandro Cohen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Integrate-and-Fire Time Encoding Machine (IF-TEM) is a power-efficient asynchronous sampler that converts analog signals into non-uniform time-domain samples. Adaptive IF-TEM (AIF-TEM) improves this machine by adapting its process to the characteristics of the input signal, thereby reducing the sampling rate. Compressed IF-TEM (CIF-TEM) reduces bit usage by performing analog compression before quantization. In this paper, we introduce a combined Adaptive Compressed IF-TEM (ACIF-TEM) – a new sampler that leverages the two machines, AIF-TEM and CIF-TEM, where each reinforces the effectiveness of the other. We propose an efficient adaptive clockless time-to-digital converter (TDC) architecture for the novel sampler that integrates the compression stage within the TDC, facilitating the realization of the intended integrated system. Via an evaluation study, we demonstrate that the proposed ACIF-TEM sampler achieves lower Mean Square Error (MSE) with fewer bits for real audio signals from the 3GPP standard, offering compression gains of at least 3-bit out of 9-bits over AIF-TEM and 65% compression over IF-TEM, for fixed recovery MSE.

title: Octopus: Enhancing Distributional Reinforcement Learning Through Regularization
authors: Jinyuan Zhang, Pengqian Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distributional reinforcement learning (RL), which models the full distribution of future returns rather than just the expected value, has achieved significant progress in recent years. Concurrently, regularization techniques have proven highly effective in enhancing the stability and generalization of RL agents. However, the interplay between these two paradigms remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce Octopus, a novel reinforcement learning framework that unifies distributional RL with regularized RL. Our theoretical results provide guarantees for both policy evaluation and control, offering insights into the benefits of regularization within the distributional RL setting. Empirically, Octopus consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the Arcade Learning Environment, achieving superior performance. These findings highlight the robustness and practical viability of Octopus, laying a solid foundation for its application in real-world reinforcement learning tasks.

title: PASTA-YOLO: An Enhanced Detector for Small Object Detection in UAV Imagery
authors: Yaqi Xiao, Bohan Guo, Yongpeng Wei, Chenze Xiao, Bingcai Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Small object detection in UAV imagery is particularly challenging due to low resolution, dense distribution, and strong background interference. To address these issues, we propose PASTA-YOLO, an enhanced detector built upon YOLOv9 and tailored for aerial small object detection. Specifically, it incorporates three key innovations: (1) a high-resolution detection head to preserve fine-grained spatial details; (2) a High-Resolution Assisted Feature Pyramid Network (HR-FPN) that facilitates multi-scale feature interaction with lightweight computation; and (3) the Position-Aware Sparse Token Attention (PASTA), which integrates semantic similarity with spatial priors and employs a Top-k sparse routing strategy with precomputed distance matrices to efficiently highlight small objects. Extensive experiments conducted on the VisDrone and UAVDT benchmarks demonstrate that PASTA-YOLO substantially improves detection accuracy while reducing model size, achieving a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. These results underscore its potential for real-world deployment in UAV-based applications.

title: Physigen: Integrating Collision-Aware Physical Constraints for High-Fidelity Human-Human Interaction Generation
authors: Nan Lei, Yuan-Ming Li, Ling-An Zeng, Liang Xu, Zhi-Wei Xia, Hui-Wen Huang, Fa-Ting Hong, Wei-Shi Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite substantial progress in text-driven 3D human motion synthesis, generating realistic multi-person interaction sequences remains challenging. Notably, body interpenetration is a pervasive issue from both data acquisition to the generated results, which significantly undermines the realism and usability. Previous generative models either ignored this issue or introduced computationally expensive mesh-level loss functions to alleviate inter-body collisions. In this paper, we propose a general-purpose and computationally efficient optimization strategy named PhysiGen to explicitly integrate collision-aware physical constraints for human-human interaction generation. PhysiGen is plug-and-play and can be readily integrated into existing human interaction generation models. Extensive cross-dataset and cross-model experiments show that our method can effectively reduce interpenetration and significantly improve visual coherence and physical plausibility compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

title: DA-VLM: Data Factory with Minimal Effort Using VLMs
authors: Jiaojiao Ye, Jia-Xing Zhong, Qian Xie, Yuzhou Zhou, Niki Trigoni, Andrew Markham
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diverse and sufficient data are crucial for semantic segmentation; however, collecting and annotating pixel-wise labels is a time-consuming process. Diffusion-based augmentation offers a promising solution by allowing image-to-image transformations, enabling manipulation of high-level semantic attributes, such as materials and textures. However, it often requires costly training or compromises performance. We address these limitations by proposing a novel automated pipeline that combines pre-trained ControlNet and Vision-Language Models to generate pixel-level labelled realistic images without additional training or manual annotations. Our approach includes a Multi-way Prompt Generator, a Mask Annotator, and a High-quality Image Selection module to enhance fidelity and diversity. Extensive experiments show consistent gains across various segmentation tasks, underscoring the potential of high-quality synthetic data to improve downstream tasks.

title: CAMEO: Collection of Multilingual Emotional Speech Corpora
authors: Iwona Christop, Maciej Czajka
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents CAMEO – a curated collection of multilingual emotional speech datasets designed to facilitate research in emotion recognition and other speech-related tasks. The main objectives were to ensure easy access to the data, to allow reproducibility of the results, and to provide a standardized benchmark for evaluating speech emotion recognition (SER) systems across different emotional states and languages. The paper describes the dataset selection criteria, the curation and normalization process, and provides performance results for several models. The collection, along with metadata, and a leaderboard, is publicly available via the Hugging Face platform.

title: Self-Supervised Depth Map Super-Resolution via Spectral-Bias-Aware Kolmogorov-Arnold Network
authors: Ruobing Jian, Yunqiu Lyu, Yuchao Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Depth map super-resolution (DSR) targets at reconstructing high-resolution depth maps from low-resolution input. The supervised DSR methods require high-resolution ground-truth depth maps which are often unavailable, motivating self-supervised DSR approaches based on implicit neural representations (INR). However, MLP-based INRs struggle to recover fine details due to the limited capacity for high-frequency content, while Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) can better capture these components. However, KAN introduces their own spectral bias that blurs sharp edges. In this paper, we propose Spectral-bias-Aware KAN (SA-KAN), a novel self-supervised framework that resolves the inherent spectral bias in KAN for DSR. First, we formulate DSR as fitting a continuous INR by leveraging a KAN. Then, we address the spectral bias of KAN by introducing a patch-wise empirical neural tangent kernel (pNTK) that reshapes the loss function’s gradient energy. This accelerates learning of high-frequency details, effectively mitigating blur. Experimental results demonstrate that SA-KAN enables arbitrary-scale super-resolution, with performance rivaling that of supervised state-of-the-art methods.

title: A CNN-LSTM Framework for Predicting and Streamlining Healthcare Service Delivery
authors: Om Prakash, Sahana Venkatramana Hegde, Jayanth Gowda S, Manjunath H M, Dilip M, T P Sankeerthana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: This study is a CNN-LSTM model that is used to forecast and optimize medical service provision using state-of-the-art deep-learning methods. The architecture is a combination of convolutional layers to detect the spatial pattern and LSTM networks to detect the temporal variations in patient-flow and service-utilization data. The experimental assessment proves that the model is quite specific, with testing accuracy of 94.7% and F1-score of 92.7, and AUC-ROC of 0.964, which beats classical machine-learning models. The system is able to learn the multi-dimensional operation factors successfully to be able to predict the delays in service provision, patient load, and workflow bottlenecks with a high degree of reliability. The efficiency of training is also confirmed by the repeated convergence after fifty epochs which demonstrates that model can be used in real time or near real-time analytics. The suggested solution will provide an expandable and evidencebased basis of improving operational intelligence in hospitals and assist in the informed decision-making process. Results indicate that deep learning has the potential to enhance the quality of services, shorten wait time, and enhance the coordination of healthcare resources in dynamic clinical settings.

title:
A
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    D: Advanced Adversarial Attack as Detection Framework for Edge Devices
authors: Yun-Hao Yang, Wan-Jung Chen, Ya-Chi Peng, An-Yeu Andy Wu, Shih-Hsu Huang, Mladen Berekovic
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that deep neural networks (DNNs) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. While adversarial detection is a recognized defense strategy, it often incurs substantial detection time to achieve high accuracy. Moreover, the high computational complexity of existing methods poses significant challenges for deployment on edge devices. To address these issues, we propose advanced adversarial attack as detection (A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>D) framework for edge devices. A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>D improves detection performance by employing more precise evaluation strategies to effectively distinguish between adversarial and clean data. For edge deployment, A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>D reduces computational overhead by pruning low-frequency pixels and shortens inference time by adaptively processing inputs based on their confidense. Compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) detection method, A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>D achieves superior performance across multiple benchmark datasets, improving AUC by up to 0.0446. During inference, A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>D reduces FLOPs by 0.35× and inference time by 0.49× relative to SOTA.

title: VNODE: A Piecewise Continuous Volterra Neural Network
authors: Siddharth Roheda, Aniruddha Bala, Rohit Chowdhury, Rohan Jaiswal
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper introduces Volterra Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (VNODE), a piecewise continuous Volterra Neural Network that integrates nonlinear Volterra filtering with continuous-time neural ordinary differential equations for image classification. Drawing inspiration from the visual cortex, where discrete event processing is interleaved with continuous integration, VNODE alternates between discrete Volterra feature extraction and ODE-driven state evolution. This hybrid formulation captures complex patterns while requiring substantially fewer parameters than conventional deep architectures. VNODE consistently outperforms stateof-the-art models with improved computational complexity as exemplified on benchmark datasets like CIFAR-10 and Imagenet-1K.

title: EEG-Based Visualization of Brain Perceived Stimulation Images
authors: Wuxia Zhang, Xiaoyan Zhang, Jianmin Jiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Over recent years, visualizing EEG signals via advanced deep learning is becoming a hot research spot [1], as EEGs represent brain perceptions of the stimulating images. In this paper, we propose a novel two-stage framework to address this challenge. In the first stage, we enhance the Spatio-Temporal Thought Mapper (ISTM) by incorporating a late temporal window branch and an adaptive temporal weighting mechanism. This allows for improved high-level semantic decoding by emphasizing the critical time points across EEG sequences. To overcome the instability in decoding low-level structures from EEG sequences, we introduce a prototype retrieval-based guiding strategy in the second stage. By leveraging the high-level semantic features extracted from EEGs, we semantically retrieve similar visual prototypes and uses them as structural priors for EEG visualization. Extensive experiments are carried out to evaluate the proposed framework. In comparison with the representative existing state of the arts, our proposed has achieved significant performance improvement, including higher semantic metrics measured in Top-1 accuracy rates, CLIP, and lower SwAV (lower is better), and 15.4% improvement in SSIM values.

title: Leveraging Large Speech Language Models as Evaluators for Expressive Speech
authors: Bismarck Bamfo Odoom, Philipp Koehn
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Expressive speech generation aims to produce speech that conveys not only linguistic content but also nuanced emotional and stylistic information. However, evaluating the expressiveness of the generated speech remains a challenging problem, often relying on expensive human listening tests. We propose using large speech language models (SLMs) trained on speech and text data, as automatic evaluators for various aspects of expressive speech, such as emotion, gender, emotional intensity, valence, dominance, arousal, accent, and speak rate. We leverage the speech perception and understanding capabilities of existing large SLMs and fine-tune them to produce natural language evaluation of expressive attributes in speech, providing a scalable alternative to traditional evaluation methods.

title: Reflective Confidence: Correcting Reasoning Flaws via Online Self-Correction
authors: Qinglin Zeng, Jing Yang, Keze Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks with techniques like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Self-Consistency. However, these ensemble methods, especially Self-Consistency which relies on multiple reasoning trajectories, often incur high computational overhead. To improve efficiency, researchers have leveraged internal confidence signals, where early stopping strategies such as DeepConf save resources by terminating low-confidence paths. Yet this discards incomplete paths and wastes computation. We introduce Reflective Confidence, a novel reasoning framework that transforms a low-confidence signal from a termination symbol into a reflection trigger. When confidence drops below a threshold, instead of stopping, the model generates a reflection prompt to analyze current reasoning, identify errors, and continue with a corrected trajectory. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, including AIME 2025, show significant accuracy gains over advanced early stopping strategies at comparable cost, validating the efficiency of proactive correction over passive discarding.

title: LEEM: A Large EEG-based Emotion Model with Brain-Inspired Sparse Experts
authors: Zihan Zhang, Xiao Ding, Tianyi Jiang, Junhao Gao, Xia Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Reliable emotion diagnostics are critical in clinical psychology, particularly when patients have difficulty communicating. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers a promising basis for such applications, yet current EEG-based models largely rely on opaque deep-learning architectures that overlook the unique biological properties of EEG signals, thereby limiting diagnostic accuracy. Here, we introduce Large EEG-based Emotion Model (LEEM), inspired by neuronal firing patterns underlying emotional processes in the human brain and integrating pretraining strategies from deep learning. On the EAV dataset (five-class classification), it achieves SOTA accuracy of 77.18%. On the DEAP dataset (binary tasks), it attains accuracies of 84.71%, 86.54% and 80.02% for valence, arousal and dominance, respectively. These results suggest that incorporating neurophysiological priors into large models can improve EEG emotion decoding and improve interpretability.

title: Efficient Solutions for Mitigating Initialization Bias in Unsupervised Self-Adaptive Auditory Attention Decoding
authors: Yuanyuan Yao, Simon Geirnaert, Tinne Tuytelaars, Alexander Bertrand
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Decoding the attended speaker in a multi-speaker environment from electroencephalography (EEG) has attracted growing interest in recent years, with neuro-steered hearing devices as a driver application. Current approaches typically rely on ground-truth labels of the attended speaker during training, necessitating calibration sessions for each user and each EEG set-up to achieve optimal performance. While un-supervised self-adaptive auditory attention decoding (AAD) for stimulus reconstruction has been developed to eliminate the need for labeled data, it suffers from an initialization bias that can compromise performance. Although an unbiased variant has been proposed to address this limitation, it introduces substantial computational complexity that scales with data size. This paper presents three computationally efficient alternatives that achieve comparable performance, but with a significantly lower and constant computational cost. The code for the proposed algorithms is available at https://github.com/YYao-42/Unsupervised_AAD.

title: ConfMamba-SAM: Structured State Space Modeling with Memory-Augmented Prompting for Automatic Brain Lesion Segmentation
authors: Zhenyu Cheng, Yanbo Hu, Yue Du, Long Zhang, Chang Lu, Boyuan Zhang, Shoujun Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate and consistent brain lesion segmentation from clinical MRI and CT volumes remains challenging due to microscale lesions, low contrast, anisotropic resolution, and interslice discontinuities. To address these issues, we propose ConfMamba-SAM, an end-to-end, fully automatic segmentation framework that leverages a frozen foundation model backbone with lightweight, trainable adapters for efficient adaptation. The ConfMamba encoder introduces a structured 3×3 corner→edge→center scanning mechanism within a state space model, prioritizing central regions and reinforcing directional features along anatomical axes—enhancing sensitivity to small and low-contrast lesions. To enable interaction-free prompting, a memory-driven prompt generator maintains a learnable prototype bank across adjacent slices, synthesizing structural prompts with temporal coherence. A multi-scale, memory-augmented decoder progressively refines segmentation masks under deep supervision, selectively attending to historical feature states to preserve boundary fidelity and ensure 3D consistency. Evaluated on a composite benchmark including BraTS2021, ISLES2022, FCD2023, ICH2020, and Instance2022, ConfMamba-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and robustness, while maintaining high computational efficiency.

title: OiLSAM2: Memory-Augmented SAM2 for Scalable SAR Oil Spill Detection
authors: Shuaiyu Chen, Ming Yin, Peng Ren, Chunbo Luo, Zeyu Fu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Segmenting oil spills from Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging due to severe appearance variability, scale heterogeneity, and the absence of temporal continuity in real-world monitoring scenarios. While foundation models such as Segment Anything (SAM) enable prompt-driven segmentation, existing SAM-based approaches operate on single images and cannot effectively reuse information across scenes. Memory-augmented variants (e.g., SAM2) further assume temporal coherence, making them prone to semantic drift when applied to unordered SAR image collections. We propose OilSAM2, a memory-augmented segmentation framework tailored for unordered SAR oil spill monitoring. OilSAM2 introduces a hierarchical feature-aware multiscale memory bank that explicitly models texture-, structure-, and semantic-level representations, enabling robust cross-image information reuse. To mitigate memory drift, we further propose a structure–semantic consistent memory update strategy that selectively refreshes memory based on semantic discrepancy and structural variation. Experiments on two public SAR oil spill datasets demonstrate that OilSAM2 achieves state-of-the-art segmentation performance, delivering stable and accurate results under noisy SAR monitoring scenarios. The source code is available at https://github.com/Chenshuaiyu1120/OILSAM2.

title: Behind the Scenes: Mechanistic Interpretability of Lora-Adapted Whisper for Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Yujian Ma, Xikun Lu, Jinqiu Sang, Xianquan Jiang, Ruizhe Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large pre-trained speech models such as Whisper offer strong generalization but pose significant challenges for resource-efficient adaptation. Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become a popular parameter-efficient fine-tuning method, yet its underlying mechanisms in speech tasks remain poorly understood. In this work, we conduct the first systematic mechanistic interpretability study of LoRA within the Whisper encoder for speech emotion recognition (SER). Using a suite of analytical tools, including layer contribution probing, logit-lens inspection, and representational similarity via singular value decomposition (SVD) and centered kernel alignment (CKA), we reveal two key mechanisms: a delayed specialization process that preserves general features in early layers before consolidating task-specific information, and a forward alignment, backward differentiation dynamic between LoRA’s matrices. Our findings clarify how LoRA reshapes encoder hierarchies, providing both empirical insights and a deeper mechanistic understanding to design efficient and interpretable adaptation strategies in large speech models.

title: How to Label Resynthesized Audio: The Dual Role of Neural Audio Codecs in Audio Deepfake Detection
authors: Yixuan Xiao, Florian Lux, Alejandro Pérez-González-de-Martos, Ngoc Thang Vu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Since Text-to-Speech systems typically don’t produce waveforms directly, recent spoof detection studies use resynthesized waveforms from vocoders and neural audio codecs to simulate an attacker. Unlike vocoders, which are specifically designed for speech synthesis, neural audio codecs were originally developed for compressing audio for storage and transmission. However, their ability to discretize speech also sparked interest in language-modeling-based speech synthesis. Owing to this dual functionality, codec resynthesized data may be labeled as either bonafide or spoof. So far, very little research has addressed this issue. In this study, we present a challenging extension of the ASVspoof 5 dataset constructed for this purpose. We examine how different labeling choices affect detection performance and provide insights into labeling strategies.

title: Discrete-Continuous Fusion With Adaptive Hierarchical Features For Audio Deepfake Detection
authors: Jianqiao Cui, Bingyao Yu, Shun Qin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generative AI technologies, including text-to-speech (TTS) and voice conversion (VC), frequently become indistinguishable from genuine samples, posing challenges for individuals in discerning between real and synthetic content. Existing detection methods often rely on unimodal acoustic features, failing to capture nuanced synthetic patterns or generalize to unseen neural attacks. To address these limitations, we propose an end-to-end framework integrating Hybrid Audio Tagging (HAT) and Hierarchical Residual Connection (HRC) modules into the Whisper architecture. HAT fuses discrete semantic taggings from GLM-4-Voice with Whisper’s continuous acoustic features to detect semantic–acoustic mismatches, while HRC adaptively preserves multi-level spoofing cues across Transformer layers by selectively integrating initial and high-level encoder outputs. We fine-tune Whisper’s decoder (with some layers of encoder frozen) using structured prompts, combining full speech transcripts and task-specific prefixes to maintain pre-trained acoustic knowledge. Experiments on ASVspoof2021 DF, LA, and CodecFake datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: our best model (with HAT&HRC) achieves an average Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.67%, reducing EER by up to 46% compared to strong baselines. Ablation studies confirm HAT’s role in enhancing cross-dataset robustness (e.g., 40.4% EER reduction on LA) and HRC’s effectiveness in preserving hierarchical features (26.1% EER reduction on CodecFake validation).

title: Testing The Efficient Coding Hypothesis Beyond Humans: The Auditory Kernels of Bat Vocalizations
authors: Aleksandra Savova, Dimme de Groot, Jorge Martinez
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The efficient coding hypothesis posits that biological sensory systems maximize information transfer to the brain while minimizing neural resources. Although extensively studied in humans, its role in non-human auditory perception remains relatively unexplored. Here, we apply sparse coding to bat echolocation calls to test whether their vocalizations are intrinsically optimized for efficient representation. Unlike prior bat studies using black-box models, our approach examines how acoustic selectivity can emerge in early auditory structures from call structure alone, independent of higher-level neural processing. The learned kernel representations are compact, sparse, and functionally specialized, with distinct activation profiles encoding specific call shapes. These findings suggest that bat auditory systems are tuned to conspecific vocalizations and underscore the advantages of sparse coding over traditional signal representations. They also improve the interpretability of animal auditory processing and provide a computational basis for modeling animal signals, supporting future research in interspecies communication and decoding animal vocalizations.

title: Improving Multimodal Brain Encoding Model with Dynamic Subject-Awareness Routing
authors: Xuanhua Yin, Runkai Zhao, Weidong Cai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Naturalistic fMRI encoding must handle multimodal inputs, shifting fusion styles, and pronounced inter-subject variability. We introduce AFIRE (Agnostic Framework for Multimodal fMRI Response Encoding), an agnostic interface that standardizes time-aligned post-fusion tokens from varied encoders, and MIND, a plug-and-play Mixture-of-Experts decoder with a subject-aware dynamic gating. Trained end-to-end for whole-brain prediction, AFIRE decouples the decoder from upstream fusion, while MIND combines token-dependent Top-K sparse routing with a subject prior to personalize expert usage without sacrificing generality. Experiments across multiple multimodal backbones and subjects show consistent improvements over strong baselines, enhanced cross-subject generalization, and interpretable expert patterns that correlate with content type. The framework offers a simple attachment point for new encoders and datasets, enabling robust, plug-and-improve performance for naturalistic neuroimaging studies.

title: FinHuBERT: Hierarchical Feature Imitating Networks for Low-Resource Speech Recognition
authors: Kavan Fatehi, Amir Shirian, Erfan Loweimi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised models like HuBERT require substantial fine-tuning data, limiting their effectiveness in low-resource scenarios. We propose FinHuBERT, enhancing HuBERT through hierarchical Feature Imitating Networks (FINs) pretrained on synthetic signals. Three specialized FINs approximate features at distinct abstraction levels: acoustic (78-dim MFCCs), prosodic (12-dim pitch/energy features) and linguistic (39-dim phone posteriors). These FINs, trained exclusively on Gaussian noise, provide structured initialization that embeds domain knowledge without requiring speech or labeled data. Integration occurs at transformer layers 4, 8, 12 through attention-based weighting optimized via canonical correlation analysis. On UASpeech, FinHuBERT achieves 13.5% WER, significantly outperforming HuBERT (14.9%) and WavLM (14.0%). Most critically, with only 1 hour of labeled data, FinHuBERT demonstrates 36% relative improvement over HuBERT, validating that structured feature initialization substantially reduces sample complexity.

title: Self-Supervised Deep Convolutional Reconstruction for Low-Light X-Ray Fluorescence Ghost Imaging
authors: Nicola Viganò, Dmitry Karpov, Kees Joost Batenburg, Sharon Shwartz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We recently developed a new self-supervised deep-learning-based Ghost Imaging (GI) reconstruction method, which provides unparalleled reconstruction performance for noisy acquisitions among unsupervised methods. Self-supervision removes the need for clean reference data while offering strong noise reduction. This provides the necessary tools for addressing signal-to-noise ratio concerns for GI acquisitions in emerging and cutting-edge low-light GI scenarios. Notable examples include micro- and nano-scale x-ray emission imaging, e.g., x-ray fluorescence (XRF) imaging of dosesensitive samples. Here, we will analyze the performance of our method against the state of the art in unsupervised reconstruction methods for the reconstruction of XRF-GI data.

title: SafeTR: Verifiable Semantic Tree-Ring Watermark for Diffusion Model Against Forgery Attacks
authors: JinFeng Xie, Peipeng Yu, Jianwei Fei, Xiaoyu Zhou, Zhihua Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Modern text-to-image (T2I) models require robust watermarking for content provenance. However, current methods are vulnerable to "semantic-forgery" attacks that exploit their static, global secret keys to transplant or reuse watermarked latents, breaking the assumption that detection implies authenticity. We propose SafeTR, a hardened framework that counters these attacks by replacing the static key with a dynamic, per-image index embedded in the latent space. This index seeds a unique watermark pattern, and a spatial permutation is further applied to obfuscate the embedding structure. This creates an immutable link between the watermark and its original semantic context. Our key insight is a mandatory two-stage verification. While an attacker can transfer a latent to pass Stage 1 (presence detection), our crucial Stage 2 uses the decoded index to retrieve the image’s original prompt and performs a semantic consistency check. Experiments demonstrate that attacked images, despite containing a statistically valid watermark, exhibit a massive drop in CLIP score when compared to their original prompts. This unambiguous signal allows SafeTR to identify them as tampered. SafeTR thus demonstrates that for modern generative models, true content authenticity can only be guaranteed by verifying both the watermark’s presence and its semantic integrity.

title: Spectro-TwinNet: Dual-Stream Temporal Deep Learning for Robust Hyperspectral Land-Cover Classification
authors: K. Anitha Devi, R. Priya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Spectral noise, high dimensionality, and limited time modeling in hyperspectral data render adequate prediction of Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) very difficult. To solve these problems, it applies Spectro-TwinNet, a spectral-spatial deep learning architecture that combines adaptive preprocessing and enhanced temporal representation. The proposed ANSER module considerably improves the quality of data, decreasing MSE to 0.061, raising PSNR to 61.27 dB, and SSIM to 0.944, which is better than traditional filters. To extract features and classify 3DSpectro-TwinTCN architecture is designed to combine 3D spectral encoding and dual-stream temporal learning with maximizing 97.6 % accuracy, 94.8 % sensitivity, and 95.2 % specificity, where the application of high spectral robustness and temporal consistency is provided. Experimental analyses attest to the fact that unified preprocessing and a deep temporal modeling method provide better discrimination ability of complex land-cover patterns. The findings make Spectro-TwinNet a useful tool forhigh-precision and reliable prediction of hyperspectral LULC.

title: Vector Quantized Stain Disentanglement Network For Stain Normalization Of H&E-Stained Pathological Images
authors: Jiawei Luo, Yuanpin Zhou, Shuanlong Che, Xiangdong Ding, Zhikui Zhang, Yao Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Stain normalization aims to harmonize the staining styles of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) pathology images across different medical centers to improve the generalizability of deep learning methods. However, existing models often capture data-specific and content–stain entangled representations, thereby limiting cross-center adaptability. To overcome this limitation, we restructure the feature space into a content subset and a stain subset and posit a center-specific content-to stain mapping. In this framework, the extracted features are content-specific and domain-invariant, so adapting to a new medical center requires only its staining style without retraining the network. Specifically, we design two independent encoders to extract content and stain features, introduce a vector quantization module to construct a global content codebook and center-specific stain codebooks capturing staining styles, and employ a generator to reconstruct images from these features. Furthermore, we develop a center-specific gradient-driven discriminator to jointly evaluate images and their gradients, enhancing structural fidelity and cross-center stainning style discriminability. Extensive experiments on public and private pathology datasets demonstrate that our proposed Vector-Quantized Stain Disentanglement Network (VQ-SDN) substantially outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of structural similarity and distributional consistency.
